Document 1 of 219

Sault election candidates grilled on business, environment

Author: Della-mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sault-election-candidates-grilled-on-business/docview/3171115113/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sault+election+candidates+grilled+on+business%2C+environment&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Several organizations are offering voters the opportunity to do their own homework and hear from local candidates seeking the Sault Ste. Marie seat at Queen's Park in the next provincial election.
Election Day in Ontario is Thursday.
The Sault Ste. Marie Chamber of Commerce invited all six local provincial candidates to participate in one-on-one interviews with Chamber CEO Robert Burns.
Participants included Liberal Gurwinder Dusanjh, the NDP's Lisa Vezeau-Allen, PC Chris Scott and the New Blue's Arnold Heino.
The full interviews are available on the Chamber's YouTube channel for public viewing.
Here are the links: Liberal - Gurwinder Dusanjh: https://youtu.be/Ntp9JZtEZps NDP - Lisa Vezeau-Allen: https://youtu.be/BMlI483mVOY New Blue - Arnold Heino: https://youtu.be/6kzVA51s7kw PC - Chris Scott: https://youtu. be/VHKPFsCVBro "The decisions made at Queen's Park have a direct impact on businesses in Sault Ste. Marie," said CEO Robert Burns "By sitting down with each candidate, we aim to provide our members and the public with insight into their platforms and priorities, particularly regarding the local economy."
Meanwhile, an environmental coalition that includes Sault Climate Hub, Sault Naturalists, The Kensington Conservancy and Lake Superior Watershed Conservancy, also surveyed candidates and their positions on key environmental issues.
The candidates who submitted responses to the written questions have their answers posted here: https://drive.google.com/ drive/folders/1HfhMyvxS7nFORHo7uRUPgfv4TSMkB7gC?usp=sharing.
You Vote At cards are being delivered by Canada Post but voters can also find their assigned polling stations by entering a home postal code on the Election Ontario Voter Information Service website.
The website (https://www.elections.on.ca/en.html) also shows examples of which ID can be used to vote and lists the candidates in each riding.
There will be six people on the ballot. Green candidate Jaycob Jacques and Ontario Party Paul Frolich will also be on the ballot.

Ford vows to protect Algoma Steel's jobs from tariffs

Author: Kelly, Brian

Publication info: Kelly, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-vows-protect-algoma-steels-jobs-tariffs/docview/3170270683/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+vows+to+protect+Algoma+Steel%27s+jobs+from+tariffs&amp;title=The+Sault+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Kelly%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Sault+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Progressive Conservative government would do "whatever it takes" to protect jobs at Algoma Steel and other Ontario steelmakers if the United States brings in tariffs on their products sold south of the border, leader Doug Ford says.

Document 5 of 219

Border cities will be hit hardest by tariffs: Mayors; Leaders vow to safeguard communities

Author: Della-mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/border-cities-will-be-hit-hardest-tariffs-mayors/docview/3169671721/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Border+cities+will+be+hit+hardest+by+tariffs%3A+Mayors%3B+Leaders+vow+to+safeguard+communities&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Border city mayors are standing together in a newly formed alliance to protect local economies and the interests of communities from U.S. threat of tariffs.
The Border Mayors Alliance includes 35 mayors from communities along the Canada-U.S. border who know they'll be the first economies to suffer if President Donald Trump makes good on his threat to impose 25-per-cent tariffs on all Canadian imports, including steel and aluminum.
Chaired by Windsor, Ont., Mayor Drew Dilkens, the alliance is ultimately designed to ensure mayor advocates stand together to safeguard communities and counteract the staggering pressures tariffs will place on the economy by citing the same facts.
Mayor Matthew Shoemaker said Sault Ste. Marie has been the cornerstone of the North American steel industry, built on open and fair trade between Canada and the U.S.
Similarly, auto workers in Windsor and Detroit are bound by an international bridge that binds their two economies.
The threat of tariffs put the mutually beneficial trade relationship at risk for both countries, Shoemaker said.
Studies showed that in 2018, tariffs then resulted in 75,000 job losses, increased production costs and higher consumer prices.
"It is important to recognize that this happened in Canada and the United States," Shoemaker said.
Proposed tariffs on the steel and aluminum industry, the auto industry and energy exports, among others, will also result in a weakening of both countries' economies, he said.
"This is a national and international issue. The impacts of tariffs will be felt across the industries that supply and employ Canadians . . . and we will see rising costs as a result of those tariffs," he said.
The Border Mayors Alliance will advocate for policies to strengthen trade relationship and push back on tariffs to protect border communities.
Dilkens, who visited Sault Ste. Marie Thursday, toured Algoma Steel and Tenaris.
Both Windsor and Sault Ste. Marie industries are critical to the local economy and GDP.
Dilkens said while the focus and timing of threats have changed since Trump's inauguration, the situation is disconcerting and ever-evolving. Mayors have joined together to fight back on what could create massive economic damage to border communities.
"We represent communities that are different, those that have different input to the Canadian economy, but one thing we have in common is that we are border Canadian communities and threat of a tariff means we'll be the first, and hit the hardest," he said.
Dilkens said the goal is to leverage relationships developed with political leaders, business leaders, associations and organizations in the U.S. is important.
"I think it is fair to say prime ministers talk to presidents, premiers talk to governors and mayors talk to mayors," he said.
Dilkens said U.S. counterpartsunderstand the devastating impact tariffs will have on their own communities and that voice needs to be amplified.
"We share the longest, undefended border with fortified gates and that is something we should celebrate as we've had a relationship that has gone on for such a long time and we found pathways to work together. I'm sure this time will be Ino different," he said.
Dilkens said, to date, the focus has been to bring the communities together and further meetings are being held to develop a slew of tangible action items. The priority is to make sure all mayors are using the same fact sheet and opening the conversation with U.S. contacts.
Dilkens said it is important that the message travels up through the ranks to the president and proper consideration is given before any tariffs are imposed.
Dilkens said the Border Mayors Alliance are not looking for a seat at the federal table. The uncertainty with the disconnect of the federal government and even the Ontario government in the midst of an election campaign, is difficult.
Shoemaker said he supports the alliance because common concerns require a common response.
He said he hopes federal and provincial governments take the alliance's suggestions on what the response ought to be to heart and that it be seriously considered. Ultimately, Shoemaker, like all mayors, hopes there are no tariffs.
Shoemaker said the provincial election and the federal Liberal Party leadership race and expected spring election -- are not helping things.
"We can't start our message five or six weeks from now. We need to start it now, and repeat it over and over," Shoemaker said.
Shoemaker has had discussions on the issue with Sault Ste. Marie, Mich., Mayor Don Gerrie, who agrees the tariffs will negatively impact his community, especially when Canadians withdraw spending in the sister city.
Meanwhile, a readout from the Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's office says he updated the premiers on Canada's fight against fentanyl and the continued implementation of Canada's border plan. They also discussed the evolving tariff threat from the U.S., including on aluminum and steel and the possibility of reciprocal tariffs, the release reads.

Document 6 of 219

Put your money where your mouth is; Sault candidates give their two cents on affordability challenges

Author: Della-mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/put-your-money-where-mouth-is-sault-candidates/docview/3169671714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Put+your+money+where+your+mouth+is%3B+Sault+candidates+give+their+two+cents+on+affordability+challenges&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This is the third and final story in a weekly series The Sault Star is publishing to keep voters informed about candidates' views on major issues. The provincial election is Feb. 27.
There is no doubt inflation has had an impact on Sault Ste. Marie residents.
Many Saultites are having a hard time making ends meet, despite a fairly strong local economy. Prices on goods - from groceries to fuel - are on the rise and there is a fear that the implementation of tariffs will see prices soar even more.
Provincially, affordability is one of the key topics in the Feb. 27 provincial election.
In Sault Ste. Marie, candidates touted their party views on how they will ensure Ontario returns to an affordable province.
The PC's Chris Scott and NDP's Lisa Vezeau-Allen weighed in on health care. The Sault Star has reached out to Liberal candidate Gurwinder Dusanjh but has not received a response.
The NDPs and the PCs are citing different approaches on how they would tackle the affordability cri- sis and improve lifestyles of Sault Ste. Marie residents.
Lisa Vezeau-Allen said ensuring families can afford to put food on the table is critical to the NDP. That's why they've developed a plan that will see a monthly grocery rebate provided to low-income earners, based on family size. The rebates would max out at $120 and be deposited directly into bank accounts.
"This is something we will implement as soon as we form government," Vezeau-Allen said. "I think it will be a real game changer for some folks, especially the seniors who are living on a fixed income and are having difficulty buying fresh fruits and vegetables."
The NDP also wants to bring transparency back to grocery pricing, forcing larger grocers to publicly post when they raise prices more than two per cent in a week.
"That's going to combat price fxi - ing and also create more accountability by the grocers," Vezeau-Allen said.
As founder of Grocer 4 Good, Vezeau-Allen said she knows wholesale prices have increased 20 to 30 per cent "and we're still keeping our pricing fair, and we do let our customers know when prices are being increased."
She wants to see large retailers - those with the buying power - follow suit and be accountable to customers.
Scott counters that the PCs, under Doug Ford's leadership, have been hyper-focused on putting money back into the pockets of people to help with affordability - and will continue to do so.
For instance, Scott said, the gas tax has been cut by 10 cents per litre, and the plan is to make that permanent should the PCs form the next government.
"We've also scrapped license plate sticker fees for over eight million drivers on the road, saving an annual $240 for the average family and we are freezing the fees for driver's licenses and photo cards, saving people $66 million over the next five years," he said.
Scott said other affordability items recently implemented, such as increasing the base amount of ODSP by five per cent and then tying it to inflation moving forward, is a big step to help people on fixed incomes.
"We're also the only government that has never raised a tax while in office, and have no plans to raise taxes," he said. "In contrast, we've cut income taxes for 1.1 million low-income workers already, and plan to keep that up."
The PCs have also sent out $200 taxpayers rebate cheques to individuals, he noted.
"We're the only party who is putting money back in taxpayers' pockets and not trying to get our hands into people's pockets. We believe in keeping costs down, and the other guys are always looking to increase taxes and make life more expensive with more government fees," he said. HOUSES HARDER TO AFFORD BY MANY It's not just food prices that have increased in recent years. Housing prices have also soared. Young people fear they'll never be able to afford their own home ifthe status quo remains.
Scott said Ontario is growing at an unprecedented rate, something that is driving the housing supply crisis. But the PCs are on track to achieving a solution, Scott said.
While Ontario's population grew by 500,000 last year, and statistics show the province is on track to add about five million more people in the next decade, a plan is in place to encourage more housing growth.
"One of the largest increases of costs of building a new home over the last 15 or 20 years has been the institution creep of government costs and permitting, and so I think our government is working to reduce red tape, get shovels in the ground quicker, and find smart ways to get houses built, get money fowing," he said.
Scott said the PCs are the only government that has created a scoreboard to monitor new housing growth, with the ambitious plan to create 1.5 million new units over the next decade.
Reducing red tape and ensuring funding envelopes are available to increase new builds is important, he said.
Scott said he wants to see a resurgence of the CO-op housing model, something that supports entry into the housing market and creates a sense of ownership, which fosters pride.
Vezeau-Allen vowed it's time to get back to building affordable homes through Homes Ontario.
"We're going to double the supply of permanently affordable housing by providing funding, lowcost financing," she said. Offering low-interest mortgages, similar to Habitat for Humanity's model, will make home ownership more affordable for many, she said.
The NDP plan is to also free up public land for non-profit and coop housing providers, a choice many young families and seniors turn to, she said.
Vezeau-Allen is in favour of building more homes where people want to live by increasing the density at municipal levels and ensuring the legislation is there to allow for community improvement plans to permit second units and nanny flats in homes.
It's also imperative, she said, that the provincial government protects renters by bringing back rent control, stopping unethical evictions and ensuring that families don't face unfair rent hikes.
"We have to take a look at both home ownership and folks that are renting, and how we can enhance their quality of life and their access to affordable places to live in. Both are equally important," she said.
Those who do own homes have their municipal taxes calculated on the Municipal Property Assessment Corp. (MPAC) 2016 value and fear a re-assessment will see taxes soar.
Vezeau-Allen said that would be completely unfair to all property owners.
"There needs to be a cap created on the percentage amount that can be increased in those values, otherwise, people would not be able to stay in their homes. I will fight for that," she said. "You can't just double the value of a home and then be taxed on it. It wouldn't be affordable for families, and especially seniors who want to stay in their homes."
Scott didn't comment on MPAC. Instead, he said it is important to target the bad actors, not the families and homeowners who shouldn't have to bear the brunt of land speculators, people who purchase prime pieces of property and sit on it, letting the buildings become dilapidated and unusable.
KEEPING M UNICIPAL TAXES AFFORDABLE Affordability also trickles down from the provincial government to the municipal governments. Without adequate grants from the province, municipalities are often forced to raise their taxes. Many have been calling on the province to review and update the municipal tax system and the funding relationships between the provincial and municipal governments.
Scott said there are avenues for a municipality to get more funding.
For example, the Building Faster Fund helps municipalities pay for upgrades or new infrastructure needed to support housing developments.
Scott said he likes Mayor Matthew Shoemaker's idea to reward municipalities that are high achievers and have exceeded their annual housing start targets.
The PCs, he said, have increased Ontario Municipal Partnership Funding to municipalities in recent years and the plan is to keep funding areas of growth to support municipalities.
Vezeau-Allen said the issue is a result of the province downloading services - and costs of those services - to municipal governments, creating a harder burden for them.
"The NDP will upload the shelter funding back to the province, and that will make a huge impact in terms of reducing the municipal tax burden to the individual," she said.
As a city councillor, Vezeau-Allen said she's witnessed the rising costs of levy boards at budget time for emergency services, policing and shelter costs all of which are traditional provincial services but have been downloaded to municipalities without adequate funding.
"Those service costs need to go back to the province, and I think that's a better way of making it more affordable for people within the municipal tax burden," she said. HOMELESSNESS, MENTAL HEALTH, ADDICTIONS NEED MORE SERVICES Creating a path to affordability also means focusing on homelessness, mental health and addictions - all of which are socioeconomic issues that have an affordability factor.
In Northern Ontario, the proximity between communities is vast, and services not as readily available to residents spread out across a large geographical area.
Vezeau-Allen agrees the issue is pressing in the North.
She said she believes that by developing 60,000 more supportive housing units, the burden on the shelter system will be lessened. It will help move individuals from the shelter system to the bridge unit, and with additional support, eventually to their own housing.
"You can't just take an empty church or school and put people there. They need support," she said.
Vezeau-Allen said under the current system, Social Services is responsible for the asset while the Canadian Mental Health Association and John Howard Society provide support within the institutions.
"We need to take a look at that funding model. It shouldn't be that municipal delegations have to go in front of the ministers and beg and plead for more supports. All of that should be across the board," she said. "We need to give people housing, but they need mental health support, and that should be part of our health-care system," she said.
The NDP has also announced a plan for universal youth mental health care. With only three clinicians across Algoma, and two licensed psychologists at Algoma Family Services and one at Sault Area Hospital, more is needed, she said.
While Algoma University attempts to integrate a youth mental health-care system across the region, Vezeau-Allen promises to work on the provincial level to make that idea a reality.
Scott said the focus of mental health and addictions is two-fold. Itis to protect public safety and help people get their lives back through treatment and recovery.
"We're the first government in Ontario's history to actually create a portfolio specifically to deal with mental health and addictions, and we're the only government that has ever treated these issues with the seriousness that they actually deserve," he charged.
Funding HART Hubs including two in the Sault and Algoma District means first-class treatment for those who need it most.
Scott said he is confident the model will ensure people get all the supports and services they need under one roof.
He wants to expand partnerships between Social Services and the province to tackle issues head-on and explore innovative solutions something that has been occurring regularly in the Sault.
The provincial election is Feb. 27. edella-mattia@postmedia.com

Document 7 of 219

Sault candidates talk seniors issues to packed house; Health care, aging in place and affordability top discussion

Author: Della-mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sault-candidates-talk-seniors-issues-packed-house/docview/3169671659/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sault+candidates+talk+seniors+issues+to+packed+house%3B+Health+care%2C+aging+in+place+and+affordability+top+discussion&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Health care, aging in place and affordability, topped concerns raised during an all-candidates meeting focusing on seniors Wednesday.
Hosted by the RTOERO (formerly the Retired Teachers of Ontario), the packed room at the Royal Canadian Legion heard from NDP candidate Lisa Vezeau-Allen, Liberal Gurwinder Dusanjh and New Blue candidate Arnold Heino.
PC candidate Chris Scott and Green Party candidate candidate Jaycob Jacques both sent their regrets and declined the invitation.
The question-and-answer format included a number of prepared questions by the RTOERO, followed by an open question period by participants.
It was the first time residents heard from Dusanjh in an open, public forum.
The business owner and self-described, long-time community advocate said he wants Sault Ste. Marie's next MPP to host monthly town hall meetings, both to keep residents informed and to ensure accountability to constituents.
"That hasn't been done with the current administration," Dusanjh said.
He said he had reached out to the MPP office on several occasions and had never received a response.
"I believe an MPP needs to represent the people of Sault Ste. Marie to the government," he said, citing past representatives, including Liberal David Orazietti and NDP Tony Martin.
Even more so, Dusanjh said he's hoping that all elected MPPs in Northern Ontario form a coalition, setting party stripes aside, to advocate as one voice for the North on common issues like highway safety and physician shortages.
Vezeau-Allen, a city councillor for more than six years, said she's been involved in multiple negotiations and delegations with provincial members, always advocating for Sault Ste. Marie.
In fact, Vezeau-Allen said she attended the last Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference and sat in on 13 delegations. The outgoing MPP was not at the meeting.
"I will be there with every delegation and advocate for this community," she said. "I know what we need in the community and I will fight for you."
New Blue's Heino said he believes in "the supremacy of country and rule of law" where government needs to return to "we the people" instead of "we the government."
"It will be up to me to listen to you and fight for you at Queen's Park," he said.
In addition to hearing about plans to reduce the physician shortage crisis, reduce hallway medicine and ensure hospitals are properly funded, candidates were asked what they would do to ensure seniors could age in place, affordably in their own homes.
Vezeau-Allen said the NDP's plan is to establish a care-giver benefit to help seniors stay in their home longer and have a better quality of life.
She noted 48 per cent of Sault Ste. Marie's population over 55, and the issue is one that affects many.
The NDP also want to create more culturally appropriate longterm care facilities for Indigenous people and Francophones and work with Sudbury's Laurentian University to make that happen.
A grocery rebate with a maximum of $120 per month would be provided, based on income and family size, and the NDP will advocate for what if calls fair food prices and transparency in grocer pricing.
Dusanjh pinpointed the need not only for more physicians and practitioners, but more home-care services that would ultimately reduce the backlog in the hospital system.
"We need to divert funding to the right places, where it makes the most sense, for the most people," he said.
The Liberal's plan to oppose the federal carbon tax, implement business tax relief for seniors which would trickle down to savings for consumers and cut land transfer and development taxes for new housing would all help seniors live more affordably, he said.
"There is nothing specific in the (Liberal) platform for seniors, but I believe advocacy is the best thing," he said.
New Blue's Heino said he believes there is a place for public and private health care, wants to see more accountability with government's spending of health-care dollars and wants the north to get its fair share from Queen's Park.
The provincial election is Feb. 27.

Subject: Aging in place; Candidates; Elections; Accountability

Document 8 of 219

Ford's hot-mic moments revealing, not endearing

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-hot-mic-moments-revealing-not-endearing/docview/3169671618/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+hot-mic+moments+revealing%2C+not+endearing&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Twenty years ago, the future King Charles was caught on a hot mic during a ski trip with his sons, William and Harry. Sitting for a few photos, the prince bristled at a question about his upcoming wedding, muttering: "Bloody people. I can't bear that man."
The unscripted moment of irritation was broadcast around the world. Instead of sparking a public relations crisis, people found it relatable. Endearing, even.
Doug Ford doesn't need help being relatable; the folksy premier speaks from the heart and governs with his gut, searching for evidence later. A smattering of hot-mic mishaps on the campaign trail have revealed the conservative leader in his less-guarded moments. The results are not endearing.
Ford likes to position himself as the leader who can deliver us from U.S. President Donald Trump. The entire rationale for this preposterously early election rests on the shaky premise that, with 15 months left in his term, Ford needed a new mandate to defend Ontario against Trump's threatened tariffs.
Set aside, for the moment, that every other premier in Canada is working to address the same threat, without the self-imposed distraction of running for re-election. Ford's tough-on-Trump credibility took a hit early in the campaign, when the premier was taped backstage chatting about the U.S. election. "Was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was," he said, until the mercurial president "pulled out the knife."
Ten days later, a video surfaced from a London police chiefs gala. In it, Ford mused about bringing back the death penalty (abolished a half-century ago) for deadly home invasions. "I send 'em right to sparky," the premier boasted.
The law-and-order audience of about 1,000 people probably seemed like a safe bet for Ford's tough-guy rhetoric. Instead, the comment drew audible gasps.
It took six hours for Ford's campaign to walk back the remarks, calling the electrocution reference a "poortaste joke."
That's plausible, right? Ford's closed-door campaign speech had all the hallmarks of comedy.
A standard three-part joke contains a setup, a build and a punchline. Ford set up his remarks: "I was warned by my campaign, 'Do not go off script.' ... Well, that's just not who I am." The premier then described a four-part framework of escalating punishments, starting with 10 years "automatic" for a break-in, and building to "sparky." Classic.
A punchline is usually the end of a joke, but Ford kept the gag going. "Hopefully when a new government comes in, I think the provinces should take over some of the Criminal Code," he said, displaying the same masterful grasp of civics that has propelled the premier's for- ays into municipal bike lanes and federal interest rates set by the Bank of Canada.
"We need to straighten out these criminals ... Sorry for sidetracking, I just had to vent a little bit," Ford said. What a kidder!
The incident was an apt reminder, the man in front of the cameras doesn't always match the man behind the scenes. It echoed the infamous hot-mic mishap during Ford's first campaign for premier, in 2018. During a secret meeting with developers, Ford promised: "We're going to open up the Greenbelt for development. Not all of it. We're going to open a big chunk of it up, and we're gonna start building."
Facing significant voter pushback, Ford pledged to leave the Greenbelt untouched; however, once in office, the story changed.
Ontario's Feb. 27 election conveniently comes before the results of the RCMP's criminal investigation into the $8.28 billion land-swap scandal that would have benefited select developers. No joke. write.robin@baranyai.ca

Subject: Political campaigns; Scandals

Location: United States--US

Document 9 of 219

Voters miffed at PC, Liberal debate no-shows; USW Local 2251 endorses NDP's Lisa Vezeau-Allen

Author: Della-mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/voters-miffed-at-pc-liberal-debate-no-shows-usw/docview/3169671595/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Voters+miffed+at+PC%2C+Liberal+debate+no-shows%3B+USW+Local+2251+endorses+NDP%27s+Lisa+Vezeau-Allen&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sault Ste. Marie voters wanting to hear from political candidates looking for their vote were angry that two mainstream candidates were a no-show.
NDP candidate Lisa Vezeau-Allen was the sole hopeful to participate in the USW Local 2251 all-candidates forum on Wednesday at the Royal Canadian Legion. It was the second all-candidates forum hosted in Sault Ste. Marie where she was the only candidate to show. The first was held by the Ontario Secondary School Teachers Association.
PC Chris Scott and Liberal Gurwinder Dusanjh did not respond to an invite from the union to attend, said Adam Guizzetti, chair of Local 2251 election committee.
Guizzetti said he's been chair of the election committee since 2015 and the union has been holding all-candidates meetings for all federal and provincial elections since that time - except during COVID.
He told the attendees that all candidates were invited to attend, but there was no response to invitations sent to the PCs or Liberals.
"I don't remember a time when candidates didn't show up," he said.
Dusanjh said his Liberal Party email was only set up two days ago and messages were not forwarded. He said he's offered to meet with the USW to discuss their concerns.
Ross Romano, campaign manager for Scott, said the PCs didn't receive an invitation.
"I can assure you there was no invitation sent to any other address and there is no doubt in my mind that people know how to reach our office," he said.
Romano didn't respond to suggestions that the PC Party has given candidates a directive not to participate in local debates.
Guizzetti vowed the union will continue to hold all-candidates meetings for its members and the public.
"I don't know if we're just in weird times or new times, but we want to give our members a chance to get informed on what the party platforms are," he said.
The no-show of Scott and Dusanjh didn't sit well with some voters, there to hear views of candidates prior to the Feb. 27 election.
"It's disappointing the other candidates are not there, especially when one is right across the street," a member of the public said, referring to Scott's campaign office.
"It shows a lack of commitment," another chimed in.
Another member of the public wanted to know why the other candidates failed to show, pushing Vezeau-Allen for an answer.
"This is not democracy. If they wish to be elected, how can they not come and explain their platform? This is not fair."Vezeau-Allen said she couldn't comment on why other candidates chose not to attend.
"I can tell you I care about this community. I want to be a good leader. And I believe that you need to be a good listener, be around people and know what their needs are to be a good leader. I will be present at all the events that are held," she said.
Following the event, Local 2251 president Mike Da Prat said, "It's obvious that they either thought we're not worth it or that this is a labour arranged meeting and it's not worthwhile."
"The reality is, and if you look at our history, we've supported different parties than what people might think is ideologically our view," he said.
Da Prat said the union publicized the meeting on radio, in media ads and the information was disseminated in several media stories.
"We did exactly what we did the last time when we endorsed Ross Romano and when we endorsed Sonny Spina. They received the information then, and participated. For Ross to say the PCs didn't receive the information, well, I don't believe it," DaPrat said.
Da Prat said Vezeau-Allen has his personal support.
"For Sault Ste. Marie, she's the only one that makes sense. I don't care what the promises are by the other ones," he said, noting that she has an identity in Sault Ste. Marie, cares about the community and is not a parachute candidate.
"And the other party, you can see they are arrogant. They need to recognize that everybody is important, not only some kind of event where you have hundreds of thousands of people. These people count, and especially in Sault Ste. Marie," DaPrat said.
USW Local 2724 president Bill Slater said the no-show is not a surprise.
He said he's never been able to meet face-to-face with the former MPP, despite repeated attempts in the past. "I'm glad we had a candidate show up and I'm glad people see that, and I hope they base their votes on that," he said.
Local 2724 has never endorsed a candidate since it was formed in 1995, but Slater said he's happy to share his opinion and show his support for the NDP because Vezeau-Allen shows up and has completed some good community work.
"The other candidates, who don't show up, and I don't know them, shows me where their heart is. Whoever is in office, even if they're not part of the government that is formed, at least there is somebody there who cares about our community and will try to help us out," Slater said. Vezeau-Allen said her family has a long history with Algoma Steel, and at the time she worked her first job, understood the importance of unions, labour and commitment.
She said workers need the protection of their livelihood, safe working conditions, a healthy family and family supports, all of which she said are included in the NDP platform.
With the prospect of U.S. tariffs to hit the important steel and aluminum industries, Vezeau-Allen said it is also important to listen to workers and their needs, including the need to retrain displaced workers and connect them with existing gaps in the community.
Government, unions and industry leaders, are needed to come to the table to develop a plan that will see the diversification of exports and the expansion of supply chains, she said.
Vezeau-Allen was asked what she has learned as a city councillor that will help her in provincial politics.
As a member of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario and the Rural Ontario Municipalities Association, Vezeau-Allen said she has the experience being in front of provincial government ministers. She has an understanding of the funding system and has vast experience drawing up those applications in the non-profit sector.
"Council has given me the tools needed to represent you at Queen's Park," she said.
Vezeau-Allen was also asked about the party's health-care platform, the need to improve services for young people with autism and the need to help volunteer fire services replenish aging equipment.
The membership endorsed Vezeau-Allen by way of a private vote.
The provincial election is Feb. 27.

Subject: Provincial elections; Candidates; Voters; Elections

Document 10 of 219

Ford survives 3-on-1 attack in election debate

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-survives-3-on-1-attack-election-debate/docview/3168907475/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+survives+3-on-1+attack+in+election+debate&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In hockey, you'd call it a 3-on-1 breakaway. Doug Ford stood on a TV studio stage in downtown Toronto Monday night as three people trying to take his job took their best shots on net.
For an incumbent like Ford who is leading in the polls, the optics of being attacked from all sides is always good, as long as the leader can keep their cool. Voters don't respond well to someone being ganged up on and Ford was definitely ganged up on by NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner.
In the end, Ford kept his cool and neither Crombie, Stiles nor Schreiner did much in the way of getting shots on net. They were as effective as Team Canada against Team USA on Saturday night - entertaining at times, but ineffective.
On health care, all four parties had policies that sounded similar. Ford's plan to connect every Ontario resident with a family doctor was written by Dr. Jane Philpott, Justin Trudeau's former health minister who Crombie had speak at the Liberal party convention just before Ford tapped her to devise a provincewide plan.
On crime, none of the opposition parties sounded credible as they repeated the federal Liberal talking points on the "root causes" of crime without calling for violent criminals to be locked up.
The debate was an interesting exercise, but the outcome is unlikely to change anything.
Ford and his Progressive Conservative party have held a strong lead in most public opinion polls since the campaign began with most putting him above 40 per cent support, while some have said 50 per cent of voters are backing him. The closest challenger has been Crombie, who's taken her party as high as 31-32 per cent in some polls and as low as 24 per cent in some recent polls.
Barring any last-minute surprises in the last nine days of this election campaign, the real battle appears to be for second place. In the 2022 election, the NDP and Liberals each received 23 per cent support - the Liberals even took a few thousand more votes - but the NDP took 31 seats, while the Liberals took eight.
So while Crombie and the Liberals are ahead of the NDP in most polls right now, the Liberal vote is widespread and not concentrated and the NDP vote is what politicos call efficient. The Liberals haven't been able secure official party status in the last two elections, so to be able to be a recognized party once again would be a big step; to take over the mantle of official Opposition would be a major step.
What Crombie and her team have to worry about as we get closer to Feb. 27 is that the NDP takes a smaller percentage of the vote, but still comes out with more seats.
If the NDP is able to maintain its official Opposition status, it still likely will be with fewer seats. The New Democrats haven't been able to capitalize on their position of strength since becoming the official Opposition in 2018.
Ford's time in office has not been an easy ride for him. Had the NDP played their cards better, they could be in a position to defeat Ford or at the least finish off the Liberals. In every province west of Ontario, the provincial Liberal party is essentially a non-entity.
The NDP didn't do this over the last seven years and now they may be replaced as the Opposition by the Liberals.
These sorts of scenarios, these possibilities will be the interesting part of this election. Unless a meteor strikes or Ford is arrested, it's not clear how his PC party doesn't win handily.
Which is exactly the result that Ford was hoping for when he called this unnecessary election. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 11 of 219

Sault-Algoma federal NDP riding association names candidate

Author: Della-mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sault-algoma-federal-ndp-riding-association-names/docview/3168906678/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sault-Algoma+federal+NDP+riding+association+names+candidate&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Sault Ste. Marie-Algoma Federal NDP riding association has named Laura Mayer as its candidate for the next federal election.
The Sault Ste. Marie - Algoma Federal NDP began its candidate search in October, "actively engaging" with the community to find a "strong" representative for the riding.
"With a demonstrated commitment to social justice and community advocacy, Laura Mayer emerged as the unanimous choice," said Luis Moreno, president of the SSM-Algoma Federal NDP Riding Association, in a release.
Mayer is a "dedicated" leader and "proud" Anishinaabekwe from Mississauga First Nation, committed to strengthening Indigenous governance, advancing community-led solutions, and fighting for policies that "uplift" working people, a media release states.
Mayer, a graduate of Osgoode Hall Law School, has held advisory and management roles with First Nations, working to advance Anishinaabe sovereignty, justice efforts, environmental protections, infrastructure projects, child welfare reform, and broader community development. She has not only written and championed First Nations laws that are now national models, but has also been on the ground, re-starting a community garden, organizing powwows and assisting with art and theatre outreach.
She currently serves as the executive director of the National Council of Indigenous Midwives, where she advocates for culturally safe, community-driven health care and stronger reproductive rights for Indigenous families across Canada. She is also serving her second term as a councillor for Mississauga First Nation, where she plays a role in governance, policy development, and community advocacy.
"One of my key priorities is health-care accessibility in Northern Ontario", said Mayer in the release. "Too many people in Sault Ste. Marie-Algoma struggle to access timely medical care, maternity care, and mental health services close to home. I will fight to expand health-care services in the North and ensure no one is left behind."

Document 12 of 219

'Bring back the beds'; Residents, advocates rally to 'save' Thessalon hospital

Author: Kirk, Maggie

Publication info: Kirk, Maggie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bring-back-beds-residents-advocates-rally-save/docview/3168906417/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Bring+back+the+beds%27%3B+Residents%2C+advocates+rally+to+%27save%27+Thessalon+hospital&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Kirk%2C+Maggie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mary Jane Thompson was born in the Thessalon Hospital.
Now, she fears that she won't be able to die there.
In 2020, the inpatient beds were "temporarily" removed, and now, a sole emergency clinic remains which relies on locum doctors to operate.
The emergency room has also faced several closures since the summer.
A rally took place Wednesday at 2 p.m., drawing over 100 supporters who gathered at the Thessalon Hospital before marching to the Trans-Canada Highway with signs and a banner.
Michael Hurley from the Canadian Union of Public Employees and Al Dupuis, a representative for the Ontario Health Coalition from Blind River, spoke and the rally.
"It used to be a vibrant, fully equipped hospital," Thompson told The Sault Star in an interview. "People had their babies here - if they were sick, they stayed in the hospital."
Located one hour east of Sault Ste. Marie, the Thessalon Hospital is one of three in the North Shore Health Network (NSHN). Alongside the Blind River hospital and the Matthews Memorial Hospital Association in Richards Landing, it serves communities from Echo Bay to Spanish.
The NSHN operates one inpatient unit at the Blind River site and three emergency departments across its facilities, including Thessalon. Currently, Thessalon relies on locum doctors because it lacks permanent physician-based primary care.
Thompson, a lifelong Thessalon resident and retired teacher, said the hospital has been gradually stripped of its services.
"It just seems to be changing into an emergency clinic and a transfer site," she said. "People aren't able to just stay in the hospital for any longer length of time. If it's an emergency, maybe 24 hours, but not even that."
A major concern is end-of-life care. "People that had lived their whole life in Thessalon, when they came to the end of life, could not die in the Thessalon hospital," Thompson said.
Her efof rts to "fgi ht back" against hospital cuts began last June. "I put up signs in my yard and around town that said, 'Save our hospitals. We need to bring back the beds. We need to get doctors.' But people were against me putting up signs, and they would take them down," said Thompson. With the help of the Ontario Health Coalition (OHC), Thompson got 600 signatures for a petition to bring the inpatient beds back to Thessalon.
On Feb. 8, a town-hall meeting was hosted by the Ontario Health Coalition to discuss health-care services within Thessalon, which was attended by more than 100 residents.
Natalie Mehra, executive director of the OHC and Hurley from CUPE spoke at the meeting.
"When I went to Thessalon last week, my core message was, and remains, that if each candidate for the election hears at every doorway a clear demand to restore the hospital services and to stop the closures, then they will have no choice but to make some firm commitments on it," Mehra told The Sault Star in an interview.
Tim Vine, CEO for the NSHN, did not attend the rally.
"Personally, and as an organization ... we absolutely support citizens' rights to peacefully demonstrate and bring issues of concern to the public square and to policy makers," said Vine. "But we are not connected or affiliated with the work that's being done by the Ontario Health Coalition."
The OHC has called the situation a crisis, pointing to systemic issues in Ontario's healthcare system under Premier Doug Ford's government.
"We don't tell people how to vote. That's not our role, but I think we're using the strongest language we have ever used in this election, because the situation is the worst it has ever been," said Mehra.
A release sent out by the OHC on Sunday states that Ford has not ended "hallway medicine" as he promised, but instead the number of patients waiting on stretchers in hallways has doubled.
It also states that the number of emergency department closures broke all records in 2024.
"You would think, if you had the worst staffing shortage that anyone has ever seen and emergency de- partments closing down all across the province, that the approach from the provincial government would be all boots on the ground," said Mehra. "That did not happen.
"Instead, they actually held hospital funding well below the rate of inflation. That's what economists call real dollar cut," she said. "And only in the last two to three weeks of the financial year did they give the hospital funding increase, most of which was for retroactive wage increase because the court struck down their wage gaps."
According to the OHC website, the Ford administration has tripled funding to for-profit clinics.
Mehra said she would like to see a standard set by the provincial government to define what a hospital is. "And then the hospital leadership would respond and say, 'Well, if that's the standard, this is what we need to do it. We need this much money, and we need this much support,' and then the province would have to negotiate with them and come up with a solution" Mehra said that Vine has been instrumental in getting locum funding secured. "But that is, you know, at best, a Band-Aid. A permanent solution would rely on a significant bump up in hospital funding," she said, adding that stable, multi-year funding would allow hospitals to hire permanent, local staff, including nurses and support staff, not just physicians.
Vine told The Sault Star that staffing challenges in Thessalon are compounded by its geography and limited local resources. "Thessalon is the farthest from an airport and has the shortest track record with fewer local providers (tied with Richards Landing)," he said. Unlike Richards Landing, which benefits from a well-developed network of locums, and Blind River, which benefits from an alternative funding arrangement (AFA), which provides access to a larger pool of physicians, Thessalon struggles to attract consistent coverage.
"We are not in a position to be able to review their reopening for reasons of health, human resources, of physical space and flow, due to the increase in volumes that we've seen at that site," said Vine.
"The emergency site remains open and has only suffered temporary closures because of the lack of physician and health human resources, despite provincial programs to assist with locums."
While the NSHN has a recruiter, who attends medical schools, recruitment fairs and physician conferences, Vine said the main issue is the burdensome responsibility of rural physicians to "be all things to all people at all times."
"The Thessalon Hospital is obviously the most at risk among the hospitals along the North Shore, but it impacts everybody," said Mehra, noting the overflow of Thessalon patients in the surrounding hospitals.
Vine said these issues stem from systemic funding and policy decisions at the provincial level. "This has nothing to do with the North Shore Health Network. This has to do with the decisions that have been made by the Ministry of Health," he said.
Vine called for more equitable funding and enhanced locum support to secure sustainable, permanent staffing across all sites.
Thompson said the issue with locums is the "consistency of care." She shared a story of her friend, diagnosed with cancer, who saw 17 different locums at the Thessalon clinic over a two-year period.
"And she said it was a roller coaster," said Thompson, noting that locums sometimes have different medical opinions.
While Vine said that the NSHN and the OHC share broad concerns about the stability and sustainability of health care in the province, he doesn't believe it is the role of a public hospital to support protests.
Vine said he is happy that health care is at the forefront of the election in a way that it hadn't in 2022.
"I think right now, Ontarians have the opportunity to have their voices heard at the ballot box in terms of what the future of healthcare for Ontario looks like," he added. "And I would really encourage them to pay close attention to what is being said by various political leaders and political parties and make the best-informed choice they can for the future of healthcare."
He also mentioned that protests could backfire for the community.

Subject: Workforce planning; Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Health care networks; Funding; Elections; Physicians

Document 14 of 219

Clock ticking for Singh before he loses his chance to prove himself; NDP dragged down by the supply-andconfidence agreement with Trudeau

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/clock-ticking-singh-before-he-loses-his-chance/docview/3168137445/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Clock+ticking+for+Singh+before+he+loses+his+chance+to+prove+himself%3B+NDP+dragged+down+by+the+supply-andconfidence+agreement+with+Trudeau&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After Jagmeet Singh spent the fall struggling to prove he could be the change Canadians want, time is now ticking down for the leader of Canada's New Democrats to show he can offer it, against the man expected to replace Prime Minster Justin Trudeau.
For strategists and former staffers, including his past communications director, that means rethinking Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as Singh's biggest threat.
"It would be worthwhile to spend some time defining Mark Carney before he is chosen as Liberal leader," said Melanie Richer, who left Singh's office in 2023.
"If he calls an election on March 10, it's too late."
That is the date Jennifer Howard, the NDP's national campaign director, gave in a memo circulated to candidates and campaign teams last week as to when they should be prepared to fight an election, should Carney, the front-runner of the Liberals' leadership contest, choose to trigger one instead of going to Parliament, once a leader is named on March 9.
Carney himself confirmed he was open to calling an early election.
Expectations that he may so do have only risen since polls began to show the Liberals surging in public opinion, the first signs the party may be climbing out of the hole it landed in under Trudeau.
Phillipe Fournier of the polling aggregator 338Canada.com said one survey suggests trouble could be brewing for Singh's New Democrats if they see their support drop even further as voters turn to a Carney-led Liberal party, which some polls suggest may be happening, despite the fact the contest is not yet over.
"The NDP would get really close to single digits," he told National Post in a recent interview.
The New Democrats are trailing both the Liberals and Conservatives and hold fourth-place party status in the House of Commons, behind the Bloc Quebecois. Abacus Data CEO David Coletto says New Democrats "should be just scared completely" about the idea of their support dropping and agrees they would risk party status.
U.S. President Donald Trump's threat of a tariff war, coupled with Trudeau choosing not to seek re-election and Carney emerging as a fresh face, has left the main federal parties watching the political ground shift beneath their feet.
But even before that, the NDP was struggling.
Singh found the party dragged down by the supply-and-confidence agreement he struck with Trudeau after the last federal election in March 2022. In exchange for passing government bills - ensuring the Liberals' survival in the minority Parliament - the NDP saw some of their priorities enacted into law, such as dental care, pharmacare and rental supports.
There was some hope the party's fortunes would improve once Singh announced before the start of last fall that he had "ripped up" the deal, but those expectations never materialized in a significant way.
The reason? "They kept voting for them," said Karl Belanger, a former NDP national director and longtime political staffer who left Parliament Hill in 2016. "They kept supporting them and keeping them in power, so that distance that they were trying to create was never established."
At the same time, the federal Conservatives pummelled the freshly unshackled Singh with ads and attacks about him being a "sellout" by voting with the government, which the NDP argued was to deliver programs and help to Canadians.
An NDP strategist, speaking on the condition of background in order to speak freely, said when the Liberals "coughed up the ball" the NDP simply "didn't catch it."
Richer said the pact with the Liberals ended up costing the party voters who switch between casting a ballot for them and the Conservatives, in ridings where both are competitive.
Those who switch between voting Liberal and New Democrat liked the deal, she said. Now that those voters see Carney as the Liberal option instead of Trudeau, they appear open to leaving.
"Do I think that's a solid number? Do I think we've lost those folks for sure? Absolutely not," said Richer.
Carney is running his Liberal leadership campaign as an outsider, despite having advised Trudeau and served in a special role for the party.
Besides trying to appeal to Liberals concerned that the party did not prioritize the economy enough under Trudeau, the twotime former central banker is pitching himself to progressives who think the Liberals did not live up its promises on climate change.
Emily Williams, a spokeswoman for Carney's campaign, said he hosted an event with more than 1,000 people in Vancouver last week and staged another 500 person-event in Winnipeg and 400 in Kelowna.
Belanger said the NDP must stop Carney from painting himself "as Canada's saviour," when the country is anxious because of Trump.
"They need to go after him," he said. "They can't let him ... escape from his past, from his record, from who he is, from what he's defending, from what he's saying."
Belanger pointed to comments Carney made about bringing government spending into balance within three years.
"That's Liberal austerity, he says, "that's what's going to hit you."
The Conservatives, who have also seen some of their support slide to Carney, released their first ad against him last week, while the Liberals took out one against Poilievre.
Singh has so far attacked Carney along the same lines he has targeted Poilievre, saying both are leaders who choose to "stand with the billionaires" by making promises like repealing the capital gains tax hike, in contrast to the NDP, which Singh says stands with ordinary Canadians.
He also took aim at how Carney said he disagreed with the GST holiday the NDP helped pass, which ended Sunday.
Belanger says because Conservatives have been effective in attacking Singh for his expensive tastes, he believes it may be difficult for Canadians to see much of a difference between him and Carney on the issue of wealth.
"Jagmeet Singh wears a Rolex," he notes.
For Brad Lavigne, who worked as former national campaign director when Jack Layton delivered the NDP its "orange wave" back in 2011, Singh still has time to tell Canadians who Carney is "through a progressive lens" and outline why he is the wrong choice for non-conservative voters hungry for change.
"When you're defining your opponent, it is vitally important for it to be a critique that has an impact on the voter themselves," he says.
"If Mr. Carney is out of touch with the struggles of everyday Canadians, it has to be put through the lens of how (being out of touch) affects them ... there has to be a little bit more depth."
Singh has been calling for Parliament to be recalled to pass a support package for workers and other Canadians who could be hurt by Trump's tariffs.
At the same time, he says nothing has changed about his intention to bring down the government at the first opportunity.
But with the possibility of an election call before that, Singh may have lost his chance.
National Post staylor@postmedia.com

Subject: Political campaigns; Leadership; Canadians; Voters; Parliaments; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada

Document 15 of 219

Choosing party over person is pure poison; Letter to the editor

Author: Barill, Don

Publication info: Barill, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/choosing-party-over-person-is-pure-poison-letter/docview/3168137233/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Choosing+party+over+person+is+pure+poison%3B+Letter+to+the+editor&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Barill%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I just want to point out to Canadians that we tend to see the tremendous demise of politics in the U.S. and we choose to believe that we have no similar problems with our politics in Canada.
Down in the U.S, there is such evident hate between the Republican and Democratic parties, that the U.S. democracy has begun to be dismantled by the new bully President Donald Trump.
But I really don't think Canadians should be too smug, because politics in Canada is also failing the electorate. Both provincial and federal governments tend to make important decisions without discussing and voting on the issues through the elected representatives of the Canadian population. We have more than just two parties as in the U.S., but these political parties depend greatly on political donations. Both provincially and federally, we have often seen the politicians make decisions which really only benefit the companies who have financially supported the ruling party. To me, this is a complete failure of true democracy.
I believe the average Canadian is well educated and is aware of this system of "look after the people who have supported the party" - not necessarily the majority. We know that this is not democracy, and we don't actually approve of this practice. But when the next election comes up, we choose to ignore the failures and support the party in spite of their poor democratic principles.
Ontarians have watched the last three years of this type of performance from Premier Doug Ford. He acknowledges the problems in our health-care system, but then he chooses where the money goes that was awarded by the federal government for national health care. He has been moving toward costly private services rather than supporting our national health system. He has underfunded the Ontario health-care system and our education system.
His version of solving our acute housing shortage in the province is to open the previously protected Green Belt to building contractors who support him financially.
He tends to use public money for the interests of party supporters and very rich friends.
Most voters are aware of this in Ontario, but Progressive Conservative party supporters will still vote for Ford because they are conservative. Is it not like the Republican voters in the U.S. who knowingly voted for an evil lying bully as their president?
I think every voter of any party should think about the design and important purpose of democracy to represent the majority, and the crucial need to support the democratic system. Voting party rather than for the real needs and the future of our province will propel our people down the very road that the United States has foolishly taken.

Subject: Democracy; Voters

Location: United States--US; Canada

Identifier / keyword: point; canadians; tremendous; demise; politics; choose

Document 17 of 219

Lone candidate speaks at debate; Grit, Tory hopefuls not there

Author: Della-mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lone-candidate-speaks-at-debate-grit-tory/docview/3167233359/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Lone+candidate+speaks+at+debate%3B+Grit%2C+Tory+hopefuls+not+there&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NDP Lisa Vezeau-Allen was the only candidate running in the upcoming Ontario election to appear at what was to be an all-candidates event Thursday.
Hosted by the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation, members in Sault Ste. Marie were hoping to meet local candidates, share their views and ask questions on party policies.
Jason Young, president of the teachers' bargaining unit for District 2 Algoma, said he reached out to each of the three main parties and only received a response from Vezeau-Allen.
The Ontario PCs have appointed Chris Scott as their candidate in Sault Ste. Marie and the Liberals recently appointed Gurwinder Dusanjh.
Young said while he didn't have direct contact information for Dusanjh, he had reached out by email to the Ontario Liberal Party Association, but did not get any response.
He said he also left a detailed voice mail message at Ross Romano's constituency office, knowing that Romano is Scott's campaign manager.
Young said he attempted to reach Scott and his campaign office directly, but his Google search of the Sault Ste. Marie Ontario PC riding association only offered him the options to donate to the campaign, volunteer or post support for the candidate.
"There was no phone number or email address to connect to him, so I left a detailed message on Ross Romano's voice mail at the constituency office," Young said.
Romano told The Sault Star the campaign office did not receive any request or information about the OSSTF event.
"I'm no longer the MPP so the constituency office wouldn't have been allowed to pass on anything to me," he said. "They're not in a position to pass along any type of message."
Young said making contact with the PC or Liberal candidates -- or their campaign offices -- directly has been a problem.
Romano said "it's a shame" the event is being politicized this way.
The Sault Star also asked whether Scott would be attending the United Steelworkers Local 2251 all-candidates event at Royal Canadian Legion Branch 25 on Wednesday at 5:30 p.m.. The hourlong event is open to the public.
Romano said he had no knowledge of that event either.
He wouldn't say whether Scott would have attended the OSSTF event, or whether he will attend the USW Local 2251 gathering.
Meanwhile, Vezeau-Allen told the small group that she believes education is an important building block needed to create a healthy society. "As parents, we have to work with all of you to give you all the tools you need to help our kids succeed," she said.
Vezeau-Allen noted that educators are required to do more than teach. There's the need for community closets, additional mental health services, health and safety issues and fighting for increased funding and assistance in the classroom.
"There were 1,200 students in the first three months of this school year that sought mental health services assistance," she said. "That's huge."
Health and safety of educators are also important, Vezeau-Allen said.
"Because as educators you are also dealing with the failure of our health-care system. We need to do better as a society to ensure everyone has the supports they need."
Vezeau-Allen said she believes in a more universal mental health care system, one that also needs to be brought to the North and promised to fight everyday at Queen's Park for that and a universal food program for all students.
"We know when students are nourished, they function better and participate better and can learn better," she said.
The NDP promises $830 million in infrastructure to update schools. The party is also promising to reduce classroom sizes, hire more staff and review the funding model to focus on student needs.
"It's important to set up everyone for success and help you be an advocate at Queen's Park to make things happen," said Vezeau-Allen.

Subject: Students; Candidates; Mental health care; Health services; Teachers; Elections

Document 18 of 219

Ford's impact on education defiles Davis's legacy

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-impact-on-education-defiles-daviss-legacy/docview/3167233330/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+impact+on+education+defiles+Davis%27s+legacy&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Across Ontario, college programs are toppling like dominoes. The cuts are sweeping: 18 programs at St. Clair; 40 at Sheridan; 49 at Centennial; two dozen at Loyalist; and 55 at St. Lawrence - the biggest hit so far - comprising 40 per cent of its lineup. Seneca has shut its Markham campus. This month, Algonquin will ask its board to suspend 37 programs and close a satellite campus in Perth. Meanwhile, Mohawk College sent out its third round of layoff notices this week.
The loss of wide-ranging programs, including skilled trades, are raising fears about colleges' ability to deliver on their mandate to meet local workforce needs.
The cuts are a response to new federal caps on international students. The bigger question, however, is why Ontario's publicly funded colleges have been forced to rely so heavily on revenue from international students, who pay about five times the tuition of domestic students for the same programs.
In 2019, Ontario Premier Doug Ford imposed a 10 per cent tuition cut, then froze domestic tuition, hamstringing post-secondary budgets. Ford touted lower costs as a win for students and families. It's not looking like a big win now.
The results for cashstarved colleges were predictable, and predicted. In 2021, Ontario auditor general Bonnie Lysyk called overreliance on international tuition a "risky formula," noting it had swelled to more than two-thirds (68 per cent) of tuition revenue collected by colleges, provincewide.
The community college system was introduced in the 1960s by Bill Davis, then minister of education for the Progressive Conservatives. Davis championed new education infrastructure in response to the technological revolution and disappearance of lower-skilled jobs.
Davis went on to serve four terms as premier of Ontario. Though sometimes at odds with teachers' unions, notably weathering a one- day strike in 1973, he became known as the "education premier." He once said: "There is no more important commitment that a government can make than to education."
That doesn't seem to be the philosophy of our current premier, whose attacks on education funding and delivery have provoked wave after wave of job actions, and destabilized the college system built by his own party.
Across the board, the two premiers really could not be much further apart.
Davis did not live to see Ford's bold assault on collective bargaining rights in 2022, using the notwithstanding clause to overrule job action from Ontario's lowest-paid educational support workers. Ford was forced to back down under threat of a general strike.
Davis, who had played a central role in negotiating the notwithstanding clause 40 years earlier, was notoriously tactful after retirement. However, he already had broken his customary silence to rebuke Ford for abusing the clause, conceived for "exceptionally rare circumstances."
Davis also created the first environment ministry in Canada, and intervened to protect the Niagara Escarpment from commercial development.
Ford, by contrast, infamously tried to open protected areas of the Greenbelt for housing development. The abandoned plan is the subject of an ongoing criminal investigation.
Davis invested in transportation infrastructure, but sagely halted the Spadina Expressway, memorably arguing: "We must make a decision as to whether we are trying to build a transportation system to serve the automobile, or one which will best serve people."
By contrast, Ford's "more roads, more cars" philosophy reached its zenith with the pie-in-the-sky promise to build the world's largest (uncosted) tunnel expressway under Highway 401.
Notably, the two premiers never met. Davis made it clear their versions of progressive conservatism were "quite incompatible," biographer and TVO host Steve Paikin observed shortly before Davis's death. The education premier must now be rolling in his grave. write.robin@baranyai.ca

Subject: Foreign students; College campuses; Education; Community colleges

Identifier / keyword: ontario; college; programs; toppling; dominoes; sweeping
