Document 19 of 219

Candidates promise MDs for all residents; Health care key for voters

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/candidates-promise-mds-all-residents-health-care/docview/3167233146/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Candidates+promise+MDs+for+all+residents%3B+Health+care+key+for+voters&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This is the second in a weekly series of stories The Sault Star is publishing to keep voters informed about candidates' views on major issues affecting local voters. The provincial election is Feb. 27.
Health care in Ontario has been pegged as one of the major concerns of voters.
Hallway health care, emergency room closures in rural hospitals, lengthy wait times and physician shortages, have all made headlines.
Sault Ste. Marie is no different. In fact, in 2024, concerns about physician shortages dominated news coverage for months after the Group Health Centre announced that it would deroster 10,000 patients because of a shortage of family practitioners. It's an issue that remains ongoing in the community.
The most recent statistics from the Ontario Medical Association (OMA) show there are 24 vacant physician positions in Sault Ste. Marie, six of which are family doctors.
OMA says there are now at least 12,447 people in Sault Ste. Marie without a family doctor.
A recent survey by OMA concludes that 40 per cent of physicians are considering retiring in the next five years, and provincewide, at least 2.3 million Ontarians do not have regular access to primary care. The shortage is especially acute in Northern and rural areas.
Sault Ste. Marie candidates running in the upcoming provincial election understand health care is a priority for voters.
The PC's Chris Scott and NDP's Lisa Vezeau-Allen weighed in on the health-care issue. The Sault Star reached out to recently appointed Liberal candidate Gurwinder Dusanjh but has not received a response.
With thousands of Saultites without a family health-care provider, the candidates shared their ideas on how they would reduce those numbers.
Scott said the PCs would earmark $1.8 billion to connect every Ontarians with a primary care pro- vider, in addition to $50 billion to build new and upgraded hospitals across Ontario.
To address the local need, Scott said the Sault must get plugged into the innovative and expanded training of doctors and nurses and use the local university to connect students with medical school partners.
"We want to hit the ground running, because these are highly trained, highly specialized workers," he said. "We didn't get here overnight. It takes a lot of specialization to train a doctor, but I think connecting people to places ensures a higher rate of retention and a higher rate of success. I think it also helps us grow stronger locally."
Vezeau-Allen said the NDP's plan will see the recruitment of 3,500 physicians across the province, with 350 targetted for Northern Ontario. The regional complement would include 250 family physicians and 100 specialists. A Northern Command Centre would also be established within the first 100 days of taking office to streamline online referrals and reduce red tape. Vezeau-Allen said she met with OMA president Dr. Dominik Nowak "and they love the idea of a centralized referral system as well as centralized system for paperwork, because we want doctors to be able to doctor and see patients and not spend so much time on paperwork," she said.
Vezeau-Allen called the NDP's $4-billion, four-year, multipronged approach "a very strategic, deliverable plan, and I will make sure that those 350 doctors that are getting recruited for Northern Ontario also come to our community so that everyone here that needs a doctor has a doctor." TAPPIN G IN TO N OS M UNIVERSITY, ALGOMA U The candidates understand the physician shortage in the North is much more acute than southern Ontario.
Both candidates believe that NOSM University can be better utilized to reduce the shortage.
Vezeau-Allen said NOSM needs to be expanded so more students can enter the medical-training program. But if capacity is increased, she said, then financial support for NOSM and increasing numbers of residency positions are also needed, including at Sault Area Hospital.
"We need to connect our educational system by looking at gaps in our community," she said. "We need to train our own for our own people."
Vezeau-Allen said that may not require inventing new, but instead, looking at best practices occurring elsewhere and offering incentive programs to encourage physicians to move here.
"We also need homegrown medical physicians here in Sault Ste. Marie. So, we need to nurture our students that are in high school so that they know what the pathway is to study medicine. We need to increase capacity at NOSM. We need to increase the capacity at our area hospital for residencies. All of this must occur in order to be effective," she said.
Scott said while stand-alone medical schools like NOSM are great, he wants to use his experience in post-secondary education policy and relationships with stakeholders to navigate the issue makers and deliver innovative partnerships.
"We need to make sure we're not duplicating, but turning over every stone possible to get the best possible partnerships and start connecting people to schools and hospitals and the workforce," he said.
Scott said it is imperative the workforce is built up, especially as more doctors reach retirement age and wind-down their practices.
There are other options that can also encourage more young people to pursue a medical career that may include expanding medical schools, or pre-medical programs, into other universities, such as Algoma University.
Scott said starting a new medical school takes time. It took Toronto Metropolitan University five years to getits first cohort into place, and it will take four more years before graduates emerge.
He would rather see partnerships take place between Algoma U and NOSM that will get students into medical school more quickly.
"It's a lot faster when you form the partnerships and expand the seats (atexisting medical schools) and are working from the ground up with the local doctors to ensure that that workforce is being supported on all ends," he said.
Vezeau-Allen said Algoma U already has a strong science program, particularly with biology. Attempts are being pursued to offer a master's psychotherapy program.
"I think if we had pre-med at Algoma, that would be a game changer for getting students into medical school. I don't see why we can't work with our educational systems," she said, adding that Sault College could also be a venue for a nurse practitioner program to build on the already successful nursing programs it offers.
"I think there's defnitely an opportunity for us to take a look at what we have that already exists, and that's how you get the solutions you need to come from your own community," she said. "It's going to be connecting what we already have in our educational system, and how can we improve that so that it's transformational, not only for the Sault, but for the Algoma District as well." HOSPITAL BUDGETS STRETCHED TO THE MAX The health-care crisis is more than just about the need for more physicians. Hospitals, including Sault Area Hospital, are stretching their budgetary dollars to the max and still risking deficits. Many have opted to hire nursing grads in part-time positions to save money on benefit and pension packages. The Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario says cutting back on staff leads to nursing burnout and leaves patients without the attention and care they deserve.
Vezeau-Allen agreed the issue has been ongoing for some time.
While she admits she doesn't know the ins and outs of the SAH budget, Vezeau-Allen said she believes there is a need to take a deep dive into the hospital funding model and gather the hospital board to talk about what is working, what isn't and see what needs to be changed. "I've managed budgets from $100,000 to up to $12 million, so I certainly understand how to look for efficiencies, how to really look at funding and budgets and determine where the funding should be allocated and where efficiencies can be found," she said.
"There are definitely efficiencies to be found, and the need to look at best practices in other institutions, taking a look at hospitals that are in other provinces, that are working on a better bottom line. I think we need to invest in helping hospitals so that they can service patients better," she said.
Scott said he, too, would work with specific hospitals, such as SAH and their experts to identify specific needs.
"I would lean on the people that are running the hospital to tell us what the best use of their dollars is, and what the best use of any potential influx of new dollars would be," he said. PUBLIC VERSUS PRIVATE HEALTH-CARE OPTIONS While Canada has always prided itself on universal, public health care, some are discussing the need to privatize some health care to increase efficiencies.
Both candidates say they support public health care in Ontario.
Scott said he believes in protecting public health care, but finding solutions that ease the crisis.
"It's about getting innovative solutions and building where we're spending $85 billion a year in Ontario on health care. That spending has increased over $25 billion since we took office in 2018," Scott said.
Vezeau-Allen said universal health care dates back to Tommy Douglas and the NDP and she doesn't believe there is a place for privatization of the province's system.
"The one thing we are as Canadians, is we have universal health care, and I am going to protect that 150 per cent," she said.
Vezeau-Allen said the only possible place for privatization is with "vanity procedures" or cases so acute and specialized. But, even then, the system must be supported by the existing system.
Vezeau-Allen said she lived in the UK for a short period and witnessed first-hand its two-tier system.
"It was luxurious hospitals versus your public health hospital. One was for the elite, the other for the general society," she said. "If we invest in our public health care, if we look at efficiencies in our health care, there's no reason to have any privatization whatsoever," she said.
Vezeau-Allen said the "game changer" the NDP is presenting in its plan is to create a centralized referral system to cut red tape and ensure that doctors can focus on patient care, not paperwork.
It's also about ensuring the government works with medical associations to get the 13,000 internationally trained doctors wanting to serve in Ontario the residencies they need to get their licences to practice.
"I think we will see almost immediate improvements for access to primary care," said Vezeau-Allen.
For Scott it is about creating important partnerships that build the talent pool, create community support and build a strong primary care system.
"I can't stress enough how much of an impact and long-standing resultthat delivers when done right. I think we've got aonce-in-a-lifetime opportunity to take advantage of that," he said.
The provincial election is Feb. 27. Edela-mattia@postmedia.com

Subject: Health care access; Students; Shortages; Funding; Workforce; Hospitals; Primary care; Public health; Budgets; Candidates; Nursing; Voters; Medical schools; Elections; Physicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Shortages Workforce Hospitals Budgets Physicians

Document 20 of 219

Leaders spar over housing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/leaders-spar-over-housing/docview/3167233027/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Leaders+spar+over+housing&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NORTH BAY The leaders ofOntario's New Democratic, Liberal and Green parties are going on the offensive against Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford in the first election debate of the campaign, pressing him in particular on his record on housing. The debate is being held in North Bayspecifically discuss Northern issues, but the leaders are discussing the housing supply crisis across the province.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner all say Ford is failing in his promise to build 1.5 million homes, with many of them reminding voters of the Greenbelt scandal.
Ford's government is under RCMP criminal investigation for its now-reversed decision to open up the protected Greenbelt lands to build 50,000 homes, a decision the auditor general said would have seen a few developers stand to benefit to the tune of $8.3 billion.
When the moderator turned to Ford to allow him to defend his housingrecord he immediately began talking about the fight against tariffs from the United States, which he has spoken about frequently on the provincial election campaign.
Ontario's housing starts were down 16 per cent in 2024 compared to the previous year, and the province has not met any of its interim targets toward building 1.5 million homes since Ford promised that in the 2022 election.

Document 21 of 219

Carney open to snap election

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carney-open-snap-election/docview/3167233011/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carney+open+to+snap+election&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal leadership candidate Mark Carney has signalled he is willing to go to a general election before returning to Parliament at the end of March, should he win the party's leadership and become prime minister.
Liberals will pick their new leader on March 9. The five candidates have to clear a final $125,000 fundraising hurdle by Monday, with two debates set for the end of February.
Speaking to reporters in Vancouver, Carney, the race's front-runner, says he would not commit to deciding now about whether to have Parliament recalled, suggesting he may see a need to ask voters for a mandate.
"It's a very fluid situation. The discussions with the Americans, the demands of the Americans, at least of the president, change on an almost daily basis, as do the timelines," Carney said of the tariff threat facing Canadians, adding he believes it is best to evaluate the situation come mid-March.
"If Parliament needs to be recalled for certain reasons, it will be," he said. "If it makes sense to get a strong mandate at that point, that is what will follow." Recent public opinion polls that show the Liberals enjoying an upswing have raised expectations among federal parties that a spring election could come sooner than expected.
On Wednesday, the NDP's national campaign director sent a memo calling on candidates and their campaign staff to prepare for an early election.
"It is becoming more and more likely that Mark Carney will be the next Liberal leader on March 9," Jennifer Howard wrote in an email, obtained by Postmedia.
She went on to reiterate the NDP's call for Parliament to be recalled, which Opposition Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has also demanded. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh has said he would be open to working with the Liberals to pass a support package for workers who could be impacted by U.S. President Donald Trump's threat of 25-per-cent tariffs on all Canadian imports.
"Nonetheless, Parliament stands prorogued, and no Liberal leadership candidate - including Mr. Carney - has reached out to any Opposition leaders to discuss emergency legislation coming to Parliament to deal with Donald Trump's trade war and the impact on Canadians.
"On that basis, we believe the rumours of Mark Carney calling a snap election are credible. Taking a page from Doug Ford's playbook, the Liberals believe that they can turn this crisis into a political benefit. For that reason, I am asking all campaigns to be prepared for an election call to come as early as March 10."
staylor@postmedia.com

Document 22 of 219

6 candidates run in Sault

Author: Della-mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/6-candidates-run-sault/docview/3167232928/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=6+candidates+run+in+Sault&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Six candidates have registered and are listed to be on Sault Ste. Marie's ballot for the Feb. 27 Ontario election.
NDP Lisa Vezeau-Allen, PC Chris Scott and Liberal Gurwinder Dusanjh have all been registered, along with Green Party candidate Jaycob Jacques, Ontario Party Paul Frolich and New Blue Arnold Heino.
Residents can ensure they are on the voter registration list or confirm or update their information on the elections.on.ca website, or by calling 888-668-8683.
The website also offers information such as examples of acceptable ID needed to vote, how to vote by mail and options for accessible tools and services to vote in the election.

Identifier / keyword: candidates; registered; listed; sault; ballot; ontario

Title: 6 candidates run in Sault

Publication title: Sault Star; Sault Ste. Marie, Ont.

First page: A.3

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 15, 2025

Section: Local

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Sault Ste. Marie, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 0839203X

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3167232928

Document 26 of 219

Ford cleaning up with union backing, polling

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-cleaning-up-with-union-backing-polling/docview/3166360313/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+cleaning+up+with+union+backing%2C+polling&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford secured another union endorsement Monday, his 10th of the campaign, with surely more to come. This time it was the International Union of Operating Engineers (IUOE), Local 793, a construction-focused union representing more than 20,000 workers.
This comes on top of endorsements from LiUNA, the International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers, a couple Unifor locals, sheet metal workers and more. Ford even secured the endorsement of the Ontario Professional Fire Fighters Association, the only time the public service union has endorsed the PC Party of Ontario.
It's no surprise Ford has been endorsed by so many private-sector unions, especially in manufacturing and the building trades - he did this last time. Now, though, he's collecting more support from unionized workers just by supporting their industries and the infrastructure projects they build.
There have been no secret backroom deals to capture the labour vote from the NDP, it's just Ford's PC Party supports major construction projects while the NDP, and the Liberals, are seen as the parties of no.
A recent Abacus Data poll shows the Ford PCs leading in the current election with 46 per cent of the vote, Bonnie Crombie's Liberals with 24 per cent, and Marit Stiles's NDP at 21 per cent. What is worse for both progressive opposition parties is the Ford PCs are leading among unionized voters, both private- and publicsector.
When Abacus chief executive David Coletto broke down the vote by union membership, Ford had 47 per cent among private-sector union members, Crombie had 23 per cent and the NDP had 17 per cent. Among public-sector union members, Ford had 34 per cent of the vote to 29 per cent each for the Liberals and NDP.
These numbers should be worrisome for the Liberals and devastating for the New Democrats. The NDP was founded as the party for labour, for working-class voters, and they're clearly not winning that vote anymore.
The election has been dominated by the economic challenge of the Trump tariffs, an issue which Ford has dominated, making hard for the NDP or Liberals to break through.
On the one hand. they're calling for a Team Canada approach which Ford is leading, but on the other, they're critiquing him.
Stiles was pushing on health care Monday morning, promising to hire 15,000 new nurses over three years and improve nurse-to-patient ratios in hospitals.
"Hospitals across Ontario are facing extreme pressure, with many emergency rooms having to close their doors. Patients are being treated in hallways because of a severe shortage of nurses," Stiles said.
Crombie, meanwhile, also is focused on health, pushing to add family doctors.
"This election, voters have a choice: a luxury spa, or a family doctor? A fantasy tunnel, or an affordable home?" Crombie said. Crombie even made a bizarre push-up challenge to Ford while laughing at the end of an answer to a question about her party's Super Bowl ads.
Ford on Monday was at Oakville's BWXT Technologies, a precision manufacturing facility for CANDU nuclear reactors, vowing to protect Ontario manufacturing amid economic upheaval due to the Trump tariffs.
Saying China is the real problem for the United States and President Donald Trump's administration, Ford pledged to ban Chinese firms from energy procurement.
"Canada and the U.S. need to remain united and focused on the real trade war we're fighting against China," Ford said.
And that's why Ford is winning with blue-collar workers, tradespeople, those who making things.
He is consistently pushing one main message; the threat is here, and Doug Ford will protect Ontario and your job.
And in a time of anxiety and uncertainty, Ford's message appears to be resonating. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Political campaigns; Manufacturing; Voters; Endorsements; Union membership; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Manufacturing Union membership Tariffs

Location: China; United States--US; Canada

Sault Grit candidate appointed

Full text: The Liberal Party of Ontario has appointed a candidate for Sault Ste. Marie to run in the upcoming provincial election.
Gurwinder Dusanjh is listed on the party's website as the candidate for the city.
Dusanjh has not responded to requests for an interview. He is not yet officially listed as a candidate on Elections Ontario's website.
His LinkedIn profile says he is a senior customer service representative with BMO Financial Group.
Dusanjh was a vice-president of Algoma University Students' Union from May 2016 to January 2017 and a student at the post-secondary institution from 2014 to 2019, completing his business administration degree.
Dusanjh's profile also notes he is the founder and president of Algoma's Sikh Association. It is not known whether he still resides in the Sault.
He was a recipient of the President's Appreciation Award from Algoma University in 2016. The honour recognizes the recipient's contribution to student success and student life at the post-secondary institution. Sault Ste. Marie's Progressive Conservative candidate, Chris Scott, was hand-picked by Premier Doug Ford to succeed Ross Romano, who announced in December he was taking a step back from political life to spend moretime with his family.
Lisa Vezeau-Allen is the NDP candidate. She is currently taking a leave from her position as a Ward 2 city councillor.
Arnold Heino is carrying the New Blue party banner in the Sault.
Candidates have until today to file their nomination papers with Elections Ontario.
The Ontario election is Feb. 27.

Document 28 of 219

Canada's new 'fentanyl czar' a former Mountie

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadas-new-fentanyl-czar-former-mountie/docview/3166359668/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+new+%27fentanyl+czar%27+a+former+Mountie&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: O T TA W A Canada's first "fentanyl czar" is a former senior Mountie and intelligence adviser to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
On Tuesday, Trudeau's office announced that Kevin Brosseau will be taking on the job of working with Canada's law enforcement agencies and their U.S. counterparts to eradicate the "scourge of fentanyl."
"With an over 20-year career in public safety and national security including tackling drug trafficking and organized crime, Mr. Brosseau will bring tremendous value to this position, and his work will help keep Canadians safe," said Trudeau in a press release.
Brosseau recently served as deputy national security and intelligence adviser to the prime minister, where he "navigated Canada's most sensitive security challenges" read the release. He was also assistant deputy minister in the fisheries and transport departments.
Prior to his work in the federal public service, Brosseau served in the RCMP for over 20 years, including as deputy commissioner and as commanding officer in Manitoba.
Trudeau promised to appoint a fentanyl czar last week amongst a slew of measures meant to reinforce the Canada-U.S. border in exchange for a 30-day reprieve on U.S. tariffs.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith and Doug Ford, who is running for re-election as Ontario premier, have been increasing the pressure on Trudeau to appoint a person in the role as soon as possible to mitigate any further risks of tariffs from U.S. President Donald Trump.
"It's been a whole week. This can't be the speed we operate at," criticized Ford on Monday, seven days after Trudeau first announced that he would nominate a fentanyl czar.
On top of the fentanyl czar, the government has promised to have nearly 10,000 frontline personnel working on protecting the border.

Document 29 of 219

'Buy canadian' with federal ad money

Author: Sygutek, Lisa

Publication info: Sygutek, Lisa. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/buy-canadian-with-federal-ad-money/docview/3166359435/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Buy+canadian%27+with+federal+ad+money&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Sygutek%2C+Lisa&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As a proud second-generation Canadian community newspaper publisher, who is the lead plaintiff on a class-action lawsuit to make sure U.S.-based tech giants don't rip off publishers like me, I was gobsmacked to see that the Government of Canada is using my hard-earned taxpayer dollars to resume advertising with Meta Platforms.
Anyone who watched the inauguration of U.S. President Donald Trump will know that Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg seems more interested in checking out Lauren Sanchez than he does about fact-checking the content on his platforms, which spew misinformation and disinformation faster and more broadly than anything in human history. In fact, he fired his fact-checkers, seemingly to curry favour with President Trump.
Here are the facts my fellow Canadians should know about federal ad spending. Last year, according to the government's annual report on its advertising activities, the feds spent more than $75 million on advertising. I don't have an issue with spending this kind of money to inform Canadians about what their government is doing. But I have a problem with how the government is trying to reach Canadians.
Of that $75 million or so, the feds spent nearly $40 million on digital ads, including over $8.5 million on search engine marketing and more than $6.5 million on social media. In comparison, radio got just $2.5 million and print publications received less than $1.4 million. That's about two per cent of all federal ad spending on every English, French and ethnic newspaper and magazine throughout the entire country. Yet 86 per cent of Canadians read a newspaper weekly. Newspapers, and ads in newspapers, are trusted.
Here's a case in point: Recently, Totum Research conducted an online survey on behalf of News Media Canada, which found that almost two-thirds (63 per cent) of Canadians trust advertising in newspapers and news websites, while just 28 per cent trust ads they see on Facebook and Instagram. When it comes to their preferred medium to receive information about federal programs, more than half (58 per cent) of respondents cited newspapers or news websites, while only 17 per cent chose Facebook or Instagram.
The feds need to instruct their agencies to get their advertising acts together. For inspiration, they should look to Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who came up with a simple, common-sense solution that doesn't cost Ontario taxpayers one extra cent. His government and its Crown corporations - like the LCBO, OLG, the Ontario Cannabis Store and Metrolinx - are now spending 25 per cent of their ad budgets on local publications. This supports a local Canadian industry that is losing ad dollars to American tech giants.
Local community newspaper publishers like me want the federal government to adopt Ford's common-sense approach. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, along with Liberal leadership hopefuls Mark Carney, Karina Gould and Chrystia Freeland should pledge to resume the federal ban on advertising with Meta. If their campaigns want to use Meta, that's their business, but no Canadian taxpayer dollars should be flowing to the company.
Donald Trump's tariff threats and his rants about annexing Canada are probably the biggest contributors to Canadian pride since the Montreal Canadiens won the Stanley Cup way back in 1993. Like most patriotic folks I know, when I'm at the grocery store, I'm now double-checking labels to make sure my beef is from Alberta and my apples are from British Columbia. And I'm buying Ontario and B.C. wine, too.
Likewise, our federal government has to get in sync with Canadians when it comes to its advertising spend. Come on, Canada: let's support the home team - and would an all-Canadian Stanley Cup final between Toronto and Edmonton or Winnipeg be too much to ask for? Postmedia Network Lisa Sygutek is owner and publisher of the Crowsnest Pass Herald.

Subject: Political campaigns; Leadership; Canadians; Advertising; Newspapers; Advertising expenditures; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Advertising Advertising expenditures Social networks

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J

Identifier / keyword: proud; canadian; community; newspaper; publisher; plaintiff

Document 31 of 219

Sault Ste. Marie mayor slams Trump's U.S steel tariffs, backs retaliatory action; Algoma Steel 'monitoring' the situation and speaking to government on potential impact

Author: Kirk, Maggie

Publication info: Kirk, Maggie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sault-ste-marie-mayor-slams-trumps-u-s-steel/docview/3165544327/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sault+Ste.+Marie+mayor+slams+Trump%27s+U.S+steel+tariffs%2C+backs+retaliatory+action%3B+Algoma+Steel+%27monitoring%27+the+situation+and+speaking+to+government+on+potential+impact&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Kirk%2C+Maggie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sault Ste. Marie Mayor Matthew Shoemaker calls the proposed U.S. steel tariffs "unwarranted aggression towards a friendly neighbor," and supports the federal government to respond with retaliatory tariffs that were effective in mitigating previous threats of tariffs in 2018.
Late Sunday evening, U.S. President Donald Trump unofficially announced a 25 per cent tariff on all steel coming into the U.S., including from its neighbours, Mexico and Canada.
Shoemaker said the news comes as a blow to local industry, particularly Algoma Steel, which relies heavily on exports to the U.S.
Shoemaker said he believed the two countered had resolved the issue of broad-based tariffs for at least 30 days, prior to Trump's press conference last night. "It makes it difficult when the goal posts are moving week by week and sometimes day by day or hour by hour."
He spoke to Algoma Steel early this morning to discuss the potential of tariffs. He said it is up to Algoma Steel to determine the concern for job loss.
"I think the big impact is on the bottom line of the steel plant. And every dollar that is going towards mitigating a tariff... or to paying a tariff instead of going into the economy, is one less dollar that's going to circulate in the local economy, one less dollar that's going to be spent on local contractors, local materials, local supplies that go into Algoma's operations," Shoemaker said.
"It takes money directly out of the Sault Ste. Marie economy," Shoemaker said, which is especially true given that Algoma Steel profit shares with employees, meaning employees are directly impacted from the bottom line.
Laura Devoni, director of human resources and corporate affairs for Algoma Steel, wrote in an email to The Sault Star, "We are monitoring the situation closely and have been in close consultation with the government regarding the challenges this will present to Algoma Steel and the Canadian Steel Industry."
The Canadian Steel Producers Association (CSPA) has also voiced strong opposition to the proposed tariffs. Catherine Cobden, President and CEO of the CSPA, released a statement condemning the move and urging swift retaliatory action.
"We are deeply concerned that US President Donald Trump is again threatening more tariffs on Canada, this time directly targeting the steel and aluminum sectors. While the details remain unclear, we will analyze the President's order once it is released and have more to say at that time," Cobden stated.
Cobden emphasized the significant economic integration between Canada and the U.S., noting that Canada exports $20 billion in steel to the U.S. annually, with 40% of Canada's steel imports coming from the United States.
"This is at a time when Canada has worked tremendously hard to align our trade policy with the United States to protect both markets from unfair trade that threaten jobs and communities. For example, Canada implemented its own Section 53 tariffs on Chinese steel imports and a monitoring system of all imports to ensure it can identify the country of melt and pour," reads the statement.
The CSPA urgently demands that the Canadian government respond with retaliatory measures to combat the devastating impacts of tariffs on the steel sector and its workers.
Shoemaker similarly supports the federal government in implementing retaliatory tariffs that successfully impacted the 2018 tariff dispute during Trump's first term in office.
"Your government is ready to retaliate, and to support workers and industry as well," said Sault MP Terry Sheehan.
"The last time Donald Trump imposed arbitrary tariffs on Canadian steel in 2018, our government fought back, with dollar-for-dollar counter tariffs, and we won," Sheehan said.
He noted the government also has packages ready for industry and workers, if needed.
"I've spoke to Algoma and Tenaris and the union presidents and I reminded them that by working together, we are successful," he said.
The federal government has $155 billion of U.S. goods identified for potential retaliatory tariffs.
"These tariffs will have deep impact on the U.S. people and their economy. The costs will increase for items like cars and refrigerators and a lot of other items," Shee- han said.
At the provincial level, government retailers such as the LCBO was prepared to remove American goods from the shelves last week, though the plan was paused by Premier Doug Ford as per the 30-day delay for tariffs Trump agreed to with Canada.
Ford also threatened to pull out of contracts with Starlink and no longer procure new contracts with American companies if tariffs were to be placed on Canada.
"Everyone's waiting on pins and needles to see hour by hour what the news is," said Shoemaker. "So, until they're actually implemented, I think it's business as usual and planning for the worst-case scenario."
Locally, planning for the worstcase scenario, for Shoemaker, means, "to review our capital dollars, for us to review our purchases, specifically ofthings like foods and beverages that are sold at the GFL Memorial Gardens and other venues throughout town, making sure that we are integrating Canadian products and removing American products to the greatest degree possible."
Shoemaker and Sheehan also advise that everybody should be buying Canadian and local goods.
People have already taken to Facebook and Reddit compiling lists of Canadian alternatives to common American products to help shoppers buy more consciously, following Trump's first announcement of tariffs last Tuesday.
Shoemaker isn't alone in viewing last week's initial tariff threat as an attempt by Trump to use the weight of the American economy to push for stricter border policies, which Shoemaker agrees there is value in making as safe as they can be for both sides of the border. "But, without even waiting to see the plan (that Canada is creating to tighten border control), the goal posts have moved again," said Shoemaker.
This time Trump cited strengthening American steelmaking as the reasoning behind the tariffs.
"US Steel was one of our great companies, and through a lot of stupidity and bad government, and also bad management of the company, it lost that luster, but it's going to gain it back and tariffs are going to help," Trump told reporters on Sunday on Air Force One as he flew from Florida to New Orleans to attend the Super Bowl.
While most border towns in the U.S. are larger than the Canadian cities on the other side, the Twin Saults are in a unique situation because Sault Ste. Marie, Ontario is five times the size of Sault Michigan.
While the impact of consumer traffic back and forth will be bigger on the American side of the St. Mary's River, the impact will be much larger on the industries in Sault Canada, said Shoemaker.
"The impacts on our economy overall will be, will be industria economic impacts, which affect almost 50% of the population in the Sault," Shoemaker said.
When asked about Trump's recent statement suggesting Canada should become a U.S. state, Shoemaker dismissed the idea outright.
"It will never happen - not willingly," Shoemaker said. "Canadians are Canadians, and we will not accept being part of the United States. Anyone who thinks that that's a good plan can move south of the border."
At press time, Trump's U.S. executive order had not yet been signed, but there was an expectation that it would be.

Subject: International trade; Presidents; Boundaries; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: International trade Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada

Document 32 of 219

No quick fix coming for Ontario doctor shortage

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-quick-fix-coming-ontario-doctor-shortage/docview/3165544099/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+quick+fix+coming+for+Ontario+doctor+shortage&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie's promise on the campaign trail last Thursday was pretty straightforward: more family doctors.
Speaking in Scarborough, Crombie said Doug Ford promised to end hallway health care and hasn't, and her fix for the problem will be more family doctors.
"You'll get a family doctor within four years, that's our commitment," Crombie said.
To fix the situation, Crombie's Liberals are promising to invest $3.1 billion "to attract, recruit, retain, and integrate 3,100 family doctors by 2029." It's a bold promise, but likely not one that is reasonable given that there is a shortage of doctors, nurses and other health professionals not just across Canada but around the world.
"WHO estimates a projected shortfall of 10 million health workers by 2030, mostly in low- and lower-middle income countries," the World Health Organization said in a recent report.
There are currently an estimated 2.5 million Ontario residents without access to a family doctor. As one of those residents, it isn't easy navigating the health system without that base of support. But this has been a problem years in the making, and it is doubtful any party in the current election can solve this short term.
Crombie should be lauded, not condemned, for looking for solutions. But her plan is unlikely to bear the kind of fruit we need.
In her plan, Crombie promises Ontario residents will get seamless access to care, including on evenings and weekends. That sounds nice, and we all want it, but family doctors are private businesses who set their own hours. My former GP in Ottawa, Barry Dworkin, runs what I consider the model for family practice teams. It is open Monday to Thursday 8 a.m. to 8 p.m., with reduced hours on the weekend. The practice operates its own walk-in clinic, but only for those registered with the practice. This ensures that if you need to see someone quick, it may not be your GP, but it will be someone in the practice and they will have access to your chart.
I think every clinic should follow Dworkin's model, but I can't force that on a GP - and neither can Crombie.
Just before calling the election, the Ford PCs unveiled their plan, written up by Dr. Jane Philpott. For those who don't know, Philpott is the dean of the faculty of health sciences at Queen's University and Justin Trudeau's former health minister.
The PCs were politically smart to bring in a Liberal to draw up some health-care plans. But is her plan any more realistic?
Under Philpott's plan, every Ontario resident would be connected to primary care, in some form, within the next five years, at a cost of $1.8 billion - significantly lower than Crom - bie's $3.1 billion.
The truth is, though, that primary care doesn't necessarily equal a family doctor - it could include nurse practitioner or other form of care.
Since Ford became premier of Ontario, the province's population has grown by roughly 14 per cent. That is well above what the province was prepared for and what the existing system can accommodate.
In reality, most people just want a personal touch point to look after their needs and access the system, including access to specialists.
Beyond the global shortage of doctors, nurses and other health workers, we have our own homegrown problems. Ontario spent years clamping down on the number of doctors allowed into the system, and it will take years to break free of that.
The Ford government has added seats at existing medical schools, while also building two new medical schools: TMU and York University. That's not a short-term solution, though, but a long-term one. And while it may be a solution in a decade, it isn't one now.
No one has a short-term plan to fix the doctor shortage in Ontario and anyone claiming otherwise is lying to you. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Primary care

Identifier / keyword: bonnie; promise; campaign; trail; pretty; straightforward

Document 33 of 219

Let's get it together; Sault candidates tout partnerships to keep economy strong to combat U.S. tariffs

Author: Della-mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lets-get-together-sault-candidates-tout/docview/3164731978/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Let%27s+get+it+together%3B+Sault+candidates+tout+partnerships+to+keep+economy+strong+to+combat+U.S.+tariffs&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This is the first in a weekly series of stories The Sault Star will feature to keep voters informed about candidates' views of the major issues that affect local voters. The provincial election is Feb. 27.
Now, more than ever, the economy is top of mind to citizens and businesses alike. It is important for Ontario to have a plan considering potential tariffs U.S. President Donald Trump is considering imposing on Canada.
In fact, the existing tariff pause, announced earlier this week, could end days after Ontarians head to the polls to elect their next government.
Local candidates weighed in on what tariffs could mean to both the provincial and local economy and what they and their party would do to lessen any impact.
Lisa Vezeau-Allen is carrying the NDP banner and Chris Scott is the appointed PC candidate for Sault Ste. Marie. To date, a Liberal candidate has not been announced.
Vezeau-Allen said the NDP has announced a Tariff Proof Ontario policy which would call for immediate assistance to the automobile industry.
"I think that industry will be affected the most," she said.
Identifying new supply chains and export chains needs to be immediate, she said.
"From a local standpoint, for me, it's also about how we are protecting our steel industry in terms of export markets. That's intertwined with (leader) Marit Stiles plan, which also includes the secondary supplies, like steel in that plan," she said.
Vezeau-Allen said the NDP would create a premier's task force on the economy and include business leaders, labour leaders, economic development agencies and the private sector, to work together to develop a comprehensive response to potential tariffs.
Vezeau-Allen said she also wants to examine and grow the emerging agri-sector.
"We have a lot of great manufacturers and smaller industries locally," she said.
From Hogan's Homestead to Penokean Hills Farms and the work Harvest Algoma is doing, Vezeau-Allen said she believes a Buy Ontario plan can be quickly implemented to grow the local agricultural network in Sault Ste. Marie to offer residents a chance to purchase from local markets and reduce costs.
In the long-term, Vezeau-Allen says it's important for the province to support retraining if industry job losses occur. Programs previously worked for retraining or buying into new businesses to help entrepreneurs should be implemented.
Aiding municipalities accelerate their infrastructure improvements can also help target economic growth and sustain jobs, she said.
"We know that all municipalities across Ontario have an infrastructure list, so looking at targeted infrastructure projects that will have an economic growth impact would be critical for the longer-term," she said. Scott said the election call by Doug Ford is to ensure Ontarians are all on the same page and voters provide a clear path forward for government.
"We want to put every tool we have in the province's toolkit forward and stand shoulder to shoulder with our employers," he said. "The cloud is not going away," he said of potential tariffs.
Scott said it is important the provincial government stand shoulder-to-shoulder with workers and businesses.
"It's mission critical that places like Algoma Steel and Tenaris know we have their backs and we're laser focused to ensure people are staying gainfully employed during this extremely stressful time," he said.
In the longer term, Scott wants to see Ontario become more competitive by strengthening its supply chain and growing Ontario businesses through procurement.
He notes the Ontario government spends $29 billion annually on procurement and it's time to review those directives to ensure there is a level playing field and ensuring the Ontario government supports and buys from Ontario businesses.
Scott said the province's critical mineral strategy also must be reviewed. "We need to make sure we are anchoring Sault Ste. Marie to the supply chain of the critical supply chain."
Scott wants to see local companies benefit from mining exploration and leverage post-secondary institutions to support critical mining strategy for the future and the development of the Ring of Fire, with its economic opportunities and benefits.
FEDERAL- PROVINCIAL PARTNERSHIPS ESSENTIAL The federal governmenta needs to partner with the province to reduce any economic impacts potential tariffs may have, the candidates agree.
Scott said he believes in Ford's approach to be a good partner with Team Canada to support the border and protect the economy.
"But we are crystal clear that we are going to stand up to bullies and make sure everyone knows Canada is not for sale," he said.
Vezeau-Allen said she believes the federal government's prime responsibility is to ensure the cost of living remains intact and people can have affordable homes and support for families. The NDP wants to construct new homes through Build Homes Ontario where funding and lowcost financing would be provided to aid in home building. Public lands would also be made available for non-profits and cO-op housing providers.
"If you don't have a roof over your head and security, then it's very difficult to be a participant in our economy and working towards our economy," she said. "I say that because that's aperfect connection where we, as a province, we can contribute and partner with the feds, and that can trickle down to help municipalities."
CONSTITUENTS, ENTREPRENEURS HAVE NEEDS, TOO Closer to home, a provincial government and its local representative needs to keep a focus on constituents and local businesses, including if tariffs are imposed.
Vezeau-Allen said the province must partner with employers and unions to protect jobs in a Team Canada approach that includes all union leaders around the table.
"By connecting what is happening around the province, you're creating a groundswell across Ontario to find solutions and that is what we need to do," she said.
For residents, capping rent increases, lowering energy costs and ensuring wages meet the cost of living, are all top of mind.
"We know there is going to be some impacts and that also buys into the support local, buy local campaign to help reduce costs," she said.
Scott wants to continue building on the successes local businesses and industry have had.
For example, he said, Tenaris has grown substantially since 2017, and that growth must continue. "We don't want to go back. We want to keep building; we want to keep growing."
Scott notes the Trump 2.0 tariff threat is more chaotic than that of 2018, but Canadians have their first experience and lessons learned to build and expand upon to continue keeping people gainfully employed and the economy strong. "We're not going backwards. We're going to make sure our supply chain is strong and our talent doesn't leave," he said.
It's also important that Algoma University and Sault College have an important role to play with upscaling and retraining, and provide a rapid response to the community's needs.
"They're going to be really good partners to ensure that we come out of this stronger than ever," Scott said. CANDIDATES MEET WITH STEEL LEADERS Both candidates said they were holding meetings with representatives on the steel and tariff file. Scott was to meet with Algoma Steel executives Thursday and Vezeau-Allen was meeting with the United Steelworkers Union representatives.
"Rather than be reactive, I'm a solution-based person and I admire Merit Stiles, our leader, as a solution-based person. It's not about finger pointing. It's about what we've been dealt with and how we can have less risk mitigation, especially in Sault Ste. Marie," Vezeau-Allen said.
Sault Ste. Marie's steelmaker, Algoma Steel, has a lot to lose if tariffs are imposed, both candidates agree.
Scott said he's laser focused on Algoma Steel's needs moving forward and wants to learn more about its projects and forecast.
"We've made a lot of progress being partners and supporting them at the provincial level," he said.
The electric arc furnace received large provincial and federal support in recent years and the provincial government has worked in the past with finding solutions on major issues, including providing pension relief.
Vezeau-Allen envisions the steel mill will be part of the premier's task force that would examine business needs, their supply chains and expand export markets. The commodity needs to be sold and that must be hyper focus, she said.
"We are one of the biggest steel producers in Canada and I believe we can find new markets and other things Algoma Steel can do to protect workers," she said.
Vezeau-Allen said the key is to ensure an affordable life exists in Ontario and looking at the cost of living.
"I know in my own life groceries have increased 35 or 40 per cent monthly but creating that affordable life is what has always connected me to the NDP," she said. "We grow the economy by having healthy folks and living well, eating healthy and having food on the table. It starts from those basic needs being met and I'm very mindful of that and my party is very mindful of (that)."
Scott said he's been knocking on doors and VOws to continue to hear from voters.
"There's a common thread across the city. At the end of the day, people are telling me that Doug Ford is the best person to fight for them. He's the only person with an actual plan to protect their jobs and protect their future."

Subject: Cost of living; Task forces; Infrastructure; Local businesses; Tariffs; Mindfulness; Political campaigns; Supply chains; Rents; Candidates; Economic growth; Voters; Union leadership; Entrepreneurial finance; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost of living Infrastructure Local businesses Tariffs Supply chains Economic growth Union leadership Entrepreneurial finance

Document 34 of 219

Tariffs could cripple Sault Ste. Marie: NDP leader Stiles; Tours Algoma Steel Friday

Author: Kirk, Maggie

Publication info: Kirk, Maggie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tariffs-could-cripple-sault-ste-marie-ndp-leader/docview/3164731975/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tariffs+could+cripple+Sault+Ste.+Marie%3A+NDP+leader+Stiles%3B+Tours+Algoma+Steel+Friday&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Kirk%2C+Maggie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's NDP leader warned that the looming threat of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs could devastate Sault Ste. Marie, a border city heavily reliant on its steel industry.
"This is not just about trade, the threat is about real people, jobs and theirfamilies,"saidMaritStiles,who toured Algoma Steel on Friday for the second time in the last year and a half.
"We talked a lot about the importance of tariff-proofing our future here," said Marit. "Sao not only dealing and fighting like hell to protect every single job and every single worker here but also look at what can we do to support industry."
She noted that one issue Algoma Steel workers are facing is access to child care, highlighting the broader challenges confronting the workforce.
Stiles also pointed out the transformative potential of the new Electric Arc Furnace (EAF), saying it is going to make Algoma Steel "different" and "competitive."
"Having said that, if the tarifsf go through, and if we can't resolve that situation quickly and effectively, it could have long-term impacts, for sure, because a lot has been invested in the EAF," she added.
Emphasizing the need to diversify the local economy, Stiles added, "If we can actually increase our power capacity here in the Sault, then we can attract more industry, different kinds of industry, and I think that's what the potential is. But right now, even though there's a commitment to getting it here, it's just not happening fast enough."
Local residents remain deeply concerned about their jobs and the future of the region's economy.
Stiles blames Premier Doug Ford for "quitting his job" and calling an election instead of fighting for Ontarians. At Friday's summit, called by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Ontario was not represented at the table.
"That's a really big problem when Doug Ford is not at the table with these conversations," she told reporters.
"The responsibility lies with Doug Ford and his (Progressive) Conservatives. He made a decision to call an election at a time when we knew this was coming. He made a decision to try to pull one over on the people of Ontario to con everybody and save his job for another four years," she said.
After touring the EAF at Algoma Steel, Marit spoke with customers at Wendy's and Tim Hortons on Second Line and Great Northern Road with Sault Ste. Marie NDP candidate Lisa Vezeau-Allen.
Some patrons asked Marit about the timing of the $200 rebate cheque, given to all Ontarians for completing their 2023 income tax, to which Marit agreed the timing was deliberate.
Marit and Vezeau-Allen handed out campaign information outlining a plan to fight tariffs, build homes, hire doctors, improve highway safety, and make life more affordable.

Subject: Tariffs

Document 35 of 219

NDP's Stiles promises 350 new doctors for North, more residency spots

Author: Della-mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndps-stiles-promises-350-new-doctors-north-more/docview/3164731841/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP%27s+Stiles+promises+350+new+doctors+for+North%2C+more+residency+spots&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's NDP leader is promising to recruit 3,500 new doctors provincewide, expand Northern health care and create a Northern Command Centre to ensure residents have the access they need.
Marit Stiles stomped in Sault Ste. Marie Friday with local candidate Lisa Vezeau-Allen and Algoma-Manitoulin candidate David Timeriski.
Stiles unveiled her family health-care guarantee that will see an Ontario NDP government fast-track solutions by the first 100 days in office by creating more family health teams, shorter specialist wait times and flexible care options.
The plan will connect all Ontarians with team-based primary care providers, she promised.
Over the longer term, Stiles said she will recruit and support 3,500 new doctors across the province over the next four years.
Specific to Northern Ontario, she will ensure 350 new doctors are hired, including 200 new family physicians and 150 specialists. The proposed Northern Command Centre will be used to manage capacity across the North and rural areas and co-ordinate responses to help prevent ongoing issues like emergency room closures.
"It is time the people of the Sault and across the north have the respect and the care they deserve," she said.
Cutting red tape by adding more administrators will allow physicians to spend more time with patients, fast-track internationally trained doctors and increasing residency spots across Ontario round out the program.
"We can't trust Doug Ford or the Liberals to fix what they broke," Stiles said. "Even with an MPP who sat in Ford's cabinet, Sault Ste. Marie was ignored and abandoned." Last year 10,000 Group Health Centre members were derostered due to a shortage of family practitioners, an issue that remains ongoing.
Stiles said she has been fighting for residents, steelworkers who built the GHC, and the community at large.
The most recent statistics from the Ontario Medical Association show that there are 24 vacant physician positions in Sault Ste. Marie, six of which are family doctors.
OMA says there are now 12,447 people in Sault Ste. Marie without a family doctor.
"I'm here today to say we can fix this," Stiles said. 'An Ontario NDP government will connect every Ontarian with team based, primary care."
The program will cost $4 billion over four years to fill the physician gap.
She said the major shift will make primary care easier and faster to get the care Ontarians need.
"I'll make the quickest, most effective changes from day one," Stiles promised. Stiles said she knows there are candidates wanting a spot in medical school and residency programs, but their careers have been paused due to lack of space.
"That's why we're working with the OMA and the Northern Ontario School of Medicine to make sure we have a target that is realistic and possible," she said. "We have to increase the number of spaces in medical schools and residencies and that needs to be done right here in Northern Ontario to keep people in Northern Ontario."
Stiles said she knows hospitals, including Sault Area Hospital, are cash-strapped and having difficulties staying in the black, which has resulted in reduced hirings of front-line health-care staff, including nurses.
"We are spending more now and getting less than we ever have and part of that is because we are privatizing part of the system," she said.
Stiles said nurses and healthcare providers are stressed and burned out and had to face a government that took them to court to suppress their wages. "I see this in hospitals all across Northern Ontario," she said. "We are hemorrhaging health care dollars into private nursing agencies. That costs more and it doesn't help us at all. We need health care workers here who know what they're doing, feel respected and feel supported."
Stiles said she knows, especially in border communities, that people are opting to travel into the U.S. for the care they need, when they need it.
"New Democrats built this health-care system in Canada. It's what made us and our strong values as Canadians," she said.

Subject: Primary care; Health care; Physicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Physicians

Full text: Ontario's NDP leader warned that the looming threat of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs could devastate Sault Ste. Marie, a border city heavily reliant on its steel industry.

Document 38 of 219

'I'm playing these shows with a lot of hits'; Canadian country star Steven Lee Olsen forged songwriting chops long before taking stage; plays Sault for Bon Soo Feb. 12

Author: Ougler, Jeffrey

Publication info: Ougler, Jeffrey. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/im-playing-these-shows-with-lot-hits-canadian/docview/3164142453/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I%27m+playing+these+shows+with+a+lot+of+hits%27%3B+Canadian+country+star+Steven+Lee+Olsen+forged+songwriting+chops+long+before+taking+stage%3B+plays+Sault+for+Bon+Soo+Feb.+12&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Ougler%2C+Jeffrey&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Steven Lee Olsen still laughs out loud when he speaks about his mum's efforts to embarrass him and his posse during car rides to their Newmarket-area school, blasting Garth Brooks tunes from the vehicle's sound system.
"We would sink down in our seats because she would pull right up to the school, open up the windows and blast it," the Canadian country music star recalled during a recent phone interview with The Sault Star.
Whether Olsen's mother knew it or not, she was helping sow musical seeds and nourish creative talent that would take her young fellow to the epicenter and hallowed halls of country music - Nashville and the Grand Ole Opry - and see his writing chops rewarded with a Grammy nod, one of music's highest honours.
"We grew up a little bit poor, so singing and music was a (thing) that made us feel like the lucky ones sometimes," said Olsen.
Indeed. But that was a long time and many country songs ago, tunes Olsen would pen for others and many he would record and make his own.
Now 39, he was a much younger man when he packed up in 2004 to cross the 49th parallel en route to Nashville, where he hoped to make a solid name for himself chiefly as a songwriter. Olsen apparently wasn't one to watch the clock and seems not to begrudge those years logged in Music City fostering skills. The decades paid dividends.
Olsen would, in time, break into the toppermost of the poppermost of Nashville's songwriting ranks thanks to two No.1 hits with his Grammy-nominated, double-platinum certified song Blue Ain't Your Color, co-written by Hillary Lindsey and Clint Lagerberg and performed by Keith Urban. Another smash was More Girls Like You, sung by Kip Moore. Other top American country acts, such as The Judds, Garth Brooks and Rascal Flatts, would also record Olsen's handiwork.
Songwriting success for others would not be confined to the U.S. His creation, Drop, was taken to the top up north by Dallas Smith. NASHVILLE CATS If this Ontario boy felt like an exotic bird in Nashville, he didn't advertise it. There were other Canadians there, for sure, but Olsen was keen to fit in, prove himself, and not wear national identity on his sleeve.
"(I was told to) solidify yourself as a songwriter, not a Canadian songwriter," Olsen said. He knows fellow Canucks there and points to a camaraderie in that these expats do route for one another. Some even gather for Canadian Thanksgiving.
"I just kind of hung out with everybody," Olsen said. "I really didn't have one particular group."
Although his songwriting output was considerable from the start, Olsen was not entirely chained to a desk. He did record and perform and, following a decade of writing and releasing music on a Canadian indie label, Olsen signed a record deal with Columbia Nashville and began working on a debut studio album. Its lead single, Raised by a Good Time, made waves in 2014 and marked his major-label debut as well as his first release in the American country market. It reached the Top 5 on the Canadian Country chart and was certified Gold by Music Canada.
As a solo artist, Olsen continues to make his mark with more than 78 million streams and a featured Song of the Season with CFL on TSN. He wrapped 2023 with a national tour featuring Old Dominion, made his Grand Ole Opry debut and headlined his first shows in Saskatoon and Edmonton. ALL THE WORLD'S A STAGE Has the stage evolved into a natural habitat for this seasoned songwriter?
"I was really excited to evolve on stage and to see what kind of artist I would be, what kind of entertainer I would be, not only six shows in, but 20 shows in, 30 shows and now almost 40 shows in," said Ol- sen from Lethbridge, Alta., where his Country Night in Canada tour was stopping that evening.
"I'm seeing a trajectory that I'm really proud of and the comfort level on stage and my fans interacting with them, it has grown exponentially. It's everything I'd hoped for."
Olsen conceded his route to headliner is a "little bit unorthodox" as although he'd produced product in one way or another for more than a decade, the road didn't really call until 2019.
But leaning "heavily" on composing early on was significant to this seasoned songwriter's overall development.
"It's very important to me to know how to craft a song before I was going to be the artist I always wanted to be," Olsen said.
"I'm grateful for all the opportunities I'm getting to tour this late in the game. For me, as an established artist, it's been really awesome. I'm playing these shows with a lot of hits. I'm grateful for that." INFLUENCES GALORE Packing a solid arsenal of selfpenned smashes lends lots of advantages. First of all, the artist must have a genuine affinity for the product and, perhaps most importantly, there is no need to rely on anyone else for material.
Olsen recognizes this, hailing such independence "super important," as evidenced by the 2023 smash single, Outta Yours, which he co-wrote.
And perhaps Olsen's trump card is that his influences stem from so many musical directions. Yes, country was a "constant" around the household growing up, but he also loved pop, R&B, rock and - maybe you heard it here first - Michael Bolton. Boyz II Men were also impressive, Olsen counted himself as a "big" Green Day fan and, during his "hard rock phase," Silverchair and Sevendust were go-to bands.
"All at the same time learning Garth Brooks and Keith Urban songs," Olsen said.
"Because I can write every genre and I love writing every genre of music, it opens up a massive array of options as an artist. Earlier in my career, I definitely pushed the limits of what country and pop were, bounced off that guardrail and went to the other side, bounced off that one and found something in the middle I feel my fans have really connected with."
Olsen's fri st band was a rock unit, Seven Days Late.
"I had nothing to do with the name," Olsen said with a laugh. "I just joined afterwards."
Influences were, as he puts it best, a "mixed bag."
"I'm a product of the '90s. We had such great options for music in the '90s."
The current Country Night in Canada tour sees some 40 coastto-coast dates. Festivities kicked off Jan. 15 in Cumberland, B,C., and reach the East Coast by early March. A Sault Ste. Marie show is Feb. 12.
Venues are a "mixed bag." There are big-theatre gigs and shows in rooms that hold 250, complete with a bowling alley on the premises. That was, indeed, the case the previous evening.
"It was a wild show, so fun," said Olsen, adding that hitting all sizes of cities and venues during this first cross-Canada tour was precisely the goal.
"I'm very aware a lot of these venues I'll never end up playing again, which makes me a little sad," he said. "But it also makes me want to make sure that I put on a really good show for that town." TOP BILLING Topping the marquee also adds pressure. On one hand, there are "creative control" advantages, as Olsen puts it, and being boss allows him to guide the gig's "flow" and production, and "just making sure that overall that everybody's having a great time."
"(But) I'm the one who is really risking it all," Olsen said. "It's something I take great pride in, and I know that we build an incredible show. And we picked the absolute best opener possible. We're having a party out here."
Fellow Canadian country music singer-songwriter Jojo Mason takes the stage first.
"His energy is contagious, and I just want that man to win," said Olsen, adding one of his "favourite" parts of the show is when the two join for a duet.
"And the crowd just goes nuts," Olsen said. "If that's any indicator, I'm pretty sure I picked the right one." jougler@postmedia.com On X: @JeffreyOugler IF YOU GO Who: Steven Lee Olsen's Country Night in Canada Tour, with Jojo Mason opening; When: Feb. 12, 7 p.m., part of Bon Soo Winter Carnival; Where: The Machine Shop; Tickets: www.showpass. com/steven-lee-olsen/

Subject: Songwriters; Country music; Musical performances

Location: United States--US; Canada; Nashville Tennessee

People: Urban, Keith; Brooks, Garth

Company / organization: Name: Grand Ole Opry; NAICS: 711310
