Document 39 of 219

Council prepped to support 'Team Canada' if tariffs implemented

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/council-prepped-support-team-canada-if-tariffs/docview/3164142424/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Council+prepped+to+support+%27Team+Canada%27+if+tariffs+implemented&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: City council wants to see the federal and provincial governments work with municipalities, communities, consumers and businesses, to protect Canadians in light of proposed tariffs threatened by U.S. President Donald Trump.
Council passed a resolution Monday that said if tariffs are implemented, the City of Sault Ste. Marie would stand with Team Canada to promote the message that they are not in the best interest for either country. The resolution also calls on the federal and provincial governments to remove trade barriers between provinces and endorse a "buy Canadian" policy.
Finally, it also calls on government to remove all barriers to municipalities preferring Canadian companies in procurement for capital projects and supplies.
The resolution was passed by city council just hours after it was announced Trump would "pause" 25-per-cent tariffs on Canadian goods and 10-per-cent tariffs on energy for at least 30 days as the two countries continue to talk.
In return for that pause, Canada made new border commitments, including appointing a "fentanyl czar" and adding Mexican cartels to the list of terrorist entities.
A joint strike force on organized crime, fentanyl trafficking and money laundering, will also be created, along with a new intelligence directive focused on organized crime and fentanyl with $200 million from Canada.
The preamble of the local motion, penned by Ward 3 Coun. Angela Caputo and Ward 5 Coun. Corey Gardi, notes that "municipalities have significant purchasing power through capital and infrastructure programs, with Sault Ste. Marie's capital program for 2025 being $45 million alone, while the Association of Municipalities of Ontario cite that infrastructure spending across Ontario in the next 10 years is expected to exceed $250 billion."
It notes "municipalities have traditionally been prevented by trade agreements from giving preference to Buy Canadian (but) federal and provincial leaders are encouraging all Canadians to Buy Canadian."
Caputo urged Saultites to buy local, something she says she does with her business and in her personal life.
"I believe in keeping our dollars within this community," she said.
Caputo said these unprecedented times show it is important to look out for ourselves and one another.
"I would love to see trade barriers taken down between the provinces," she said. "We need to look out for ourselves first and provide for ourselves where we can."
Ward 5 Coun. Corey Gardi, the motion's seconder, agreed that buying local is always important.
Gardi noted that the "erratic nature of (Trump's) administration can't be predicted, but the outreach from the Canadian government is likely also important in the tariff 'pause."
Gardi wants Saultities to reach out to connections in the United States and explain the seriousness of what the U.S. government may have in store for Canada with the tariffs and how they would hurt both economies. Connections should be urged to reach out to their congressman or governor to explain the Canadian position and the negative effect on the economies and that the threat of making Canada a 5lst state is non-sensical and likely not legal.
Mayor Matthew Shoemaker said he's been working with the border mayor's alliance, a group established about one month ago and meets regularly to advocate the message that Canada is not a threat. The group has developed a strategy that includes sharing the message of being a good neighbour and trade partner with American citizens, businesses and politicians.
Shoemaker said he's spoken to Sault Ste. Marie, Mich., Mayor Don Gerrie, who understands the value of a relationship with Canada.
Shoemaker said he hopes Gerrie will be able to arrange a meeting with the congressman where they will explain the importance of supporting each other.
"Sault Ste. Marie is one of the few places across the country where the Canadian side is larger than the American side, therefore we have the greater buying power," Shoemaker said.
Ward l Coun. Sandra Hollingsworth said the tariff threat has "rattled our community, Ontario and Canada."
She hopes the resolution is the start of something bigger that ensures Canadians are aware of the threat of tariffs and how they can harm the economy and the importance of borders.
"I hope this is a start to being a true Canadian and making Canada great again," she said.
Shoemaker said while outreach and negotiations are the best solu- tions, he would not hesitate to call on the community to take every step possible to keep every dollar spent in the community or country. "Ifthe tariffs are implemented, we have to go hyperlocal in our every dollar we spend."
"That would hit Americans where it hurts, their pocketbooks, and that is what they respond to most," he said.
Tariffs could create great economic turmoil on Sault Ste. Marie's steel industry, as well as other industries and businesses and raise the prices of good purchased by Canadians.
Before the pause, provinces and business groups were ready to launch Buy Canadian campaigns. Several provinces were also prepared to take action.
In Ontario, the LCBO was to pull American booze off the shelves and Premier Doug Ford was ready to cancel cross-border contracts, including one with Elon Musk's Starlink.

Subject: Organized crime; Political campaigns; Canadians; Councils; Fentanyl; Tariffs

Document 40 of 219

Canadian pride grew this winter as tariff threats surged: poll; 44 per cent of respondents described themselves as 'very proud' Canadians

Author: Knight, Chris

Publication info: Knight, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadian-pride-grew-this-winter-as-tariff-threats/docview/3164142411/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadian+pride+grew+this+winter+as+tariff+threats+surged%3A+poll%3B+44+per+cent+of+respondents+described+themselves+as+%27very+proud%27+Canadians&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=Knight%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If there's a silver lining to be found in the recent threat of an all-out trade war between the United States and Canada, it would seem to be a resurgence of pride among Canadians. That has manifested itself in positive ways, including buy-Canadian initiatives, as well as negative acts, such as the booing of the U.S. national anthem at sporting events.
On Wednesday, Montreal's Fairmount Bagel started selling a redand-white "Canada Strong" variety of the popular baked good. In Ottawa, the makers of the "Canada Is Not For Sale" hat worn by Ontario Premier Doug Ford during a meeting of Canada's premiers has seen a huge jump in sales, while the hat's motto has become a social media hashtag.
Meanwhile, a moribund website, madeinca.ca has seen a spike in traffic as Canadian producers and consumers rush to advertise and locate locally manufactured products. The site now includes a grocery shopping guide.
Loblaw, Canada's largest grocery retailer, announced Sunday that it would try to leapfrog American purchases and buy from Mexico instead.
"Where we have had to purchase products made in the U.S., we will look for alternatives including products from Mexico which has also been subjected to these unnecessary tariffs," Loblaw CEO Per Bank said, adding that the U.S. decision was "wrong-headed." On the social media site Reddit, the BuyCanadian forum, introduced in 2018 during another round of Trump trade tariffs, is seeing more comments as well.
And there are polling numbers to prove it. A recent survey by the Angus Reid Institute found that, between last December and this month, the number of Canadians describing themselves as "very proud" rose to 44 per cent from 34 per cent.
In the same period, those who said they had a "deep emotional attachment" to Canada rose by a similar amount, to 59 per cent in February from 49 per cent in December. And the number of people who thought Canada should join the United States fell to just four per cent, from an already meagre six per cent in December.
The swelling pride was not evenly distributed across the country, however. The poll found that the biggest increases were in British Columbia, Quebec and the Maritimes, which all saw rises of between 12 and 15 per cent on the "proud or very proud" and "deep emotional attachment" questions.
Smaller gains of between 3 and 9 per cent were seen in Alberta, Saskatchewan and Ontario, while Manitoba alone saw a slight drop of about 3 per cent.
The pollster pointed out that the shift to greater pride in Canada also bucks a trend that has seen pride fall gradually over the last several decades. "Deep emotional attachment" was cited by 65 per cent of respondents in a 1991 An- gus Reid poll, falling to 62 per cent by 2016 and just 49 per cent last year, before the recent rebound.
A variety of polls has shown the number of people who are "very proud" to be Canadian has fallen slowly but steadily since the 1980s.
While it's impossible to say for certain whether Donald Trump's threats of tariffs and continued talk of annexation are the reason for Canadians' suddenly glowing hearts, the poll did find that the tariff issue was the most closely followed major news event since the onset of the COVID pandemic in 2020.
Fully 56 per cent of respondents said they were following news of tariffs very closely, while another 33 per cent reported following them closely, for a total of 89 per cent. This topped other recent events such as the last federal budget (62 per cent), the Israel-Hamas War (80 per cent), Russia's invasion of Ukraine (66 per cent) and the WE Charity scandal (73 per cent).
One has to go back to February of 2020 to find higher numbers, when 90 per cent were following the emergence of COVID-19 closely or very closely.
There is fear as well as general interest in the news of possible tariffs, with 3 per cent of respondents saying they expect to lose their job if tariffs are implemented, and a further 30 per cent saying they're concerned about what it would mean for their jobs.
The new poll was conducted online from Feb. 2 to Feb. 3, and carries a margin of error of plus or minus 2 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.

Document 41 of 219

Make Ontario election a vote on health care; Doug Ford says its all about Trump. Don't believe the PC leader's ploy; Make Ontario election a vote on health care; Doug Ford says its all about Trump. Don't believe the PC leader's ploy

Author: Mohammed, Adam

Publication info: Mohammed, Adam. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/make-ontario-election-vote-on-health-care-doug/docview/3163294414/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Make+Ontario+election+a+vote+on+health+care%3B+Doug+Ford+says+its+all+about+Trump.+Don%27t+believe+the+PC+leader%27s+ploy%3B+Make+Ontario+election+a+vote+on+health+care%3B+Doug+Ford+says+its+all+about+Trump.+Don%27t+believe+the+PC+leader%27s+ploy&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Mohammed%2C+Adam&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is dragging Ontarians into a needless election in the dead of winter in pursuit of more power. The election call, more than a year ahead of time, is a cynical play for power, and on polling day, Feb. 27, Ontarians should rebuff him.
Ford says he needs a "strong mandate" to deal with the threat posed by U.S. President Donald Trump's plan to impose punishing tariffs on Canada, but he doesn't need any such mandate.
He already has it. In 2022, Ontarians handed him a huge majority, giving the Progressive Conservatives (PCs) 83 of the 124 seats in the legislature. Today, as a result of vacancies and other actions, the PCs have 79 seats, the NDP 28 and Liberals nine. The Green Party has one.
By any measure, that is as strong a mandate as any leader can get, and Ford has such control at Queen's Park, there is little he wants to do that he can't.
Besides, every political leader in Ontario fully understands the dire consequences for the province and Canada if the Trump tariffs are implemented. There is therefore every expectation that all political leaders will come together to take the necessary steps to protect Ontario. Nothing so far suggests otherwise, and there is absolutely no reason to call a snap election to seek a new mandate. Ford is merely exploiting the threat of tariffs to scare Ontarians. We should resist.
But since the election campaign is now fully engaged, we should oblige Ford by turning it into a referendum on his handling on the family-doctor crisis that has left 2.5 million people without primary care, a figure that could rise to more than four million by 2026.
Think of the hundreds of people lining up in bitter cold in Walkerton recently, hoping to find a doctor. It is therefore not surprising that a day before the election call, Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced $1.4 billion in new funding over four years to connect two million people with a family doctor or primary-care team.
Of course, the announcement is an election ploy. The crisis has been boiling since Ford was first elected in 2018. The problem has become worse - not better - under his watch, so just days before the election campaign gets underway, presto, Ford finds more than a billion to throw at the problem. Raise the issue in the campaign, and Ford will point to the money. But there are no guarantees things will actually change.
The fact is, facing a weak opposition, ahead in the polls, and armed with a full war chest, Ford has been angling for this moment, and Donald Trump's tariff threats provided a convenient cover.
Over the next month, you'll hear time and again that Ford needs to get a bigger majority so he'll be in a strong position to face Trump and save Ontario. "I am asking the people for a strong mandate - a strong, stable, fouryear mandate that will outlive and outlast the Trump administration," Ford wrote in a recent op-ed.
"President Trump needs to know that for as long as he is president, I will be on the other side of the table ready to protect Ontario."
Don't buy it. It's just talk. The tariff war, if it happens, will not be Ford's to lead. As Ontario premier, he will have significant input into whatever position the feds adopt. But this is a federal responsibility, and you will notice that all the work so far has been done by federal officials in meetings and discussions with counterparts in the Trump administration. Like other provinces, Ontario has been an interested party watching from the outside.
When the time comes to face Trump, the person "on the other side of the table," won't be Ford, but the prime minister of the day.
That will be either the next Liberal leader, whoever that is, or Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Let Ontarians understand that on voting day, when they consider Ford's request for more power.
Mohammed Adam is an Ottawa journalist and commentator. Reach him at nylamiles48@gmail.com

Sault Tory hopeful banks on experience; Chris Scott aims to shift from MPP's 'right-hand man' to successor; Sault Tory hopeful banks on experience; Chris Scott aims to shift from MPP's 'right-hand man' to successor

Author: Kelly, Brian

Publication info: Kelly, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sault-tory-hopeful-banks-on-experience-chris/docview/3163293933/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sault+Tory+hopeful+banks+on+experience%3B+Chris+Scott+aims+to+shift+from+MPP%27s+%27right-hand+man%27+to+successor%3B+Sault+Tory+hopeful+banks+on+experience%3B+Chris+Scott+aims+to+shift+from+MPP%27s+%27right-hand+man%27+to+successor&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Kelly%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Progressive Conservative candidate Chris Scott is making the move from Ross Romano's righthand man to potential successor.
Scott worked with the outgoing Sault Ste. Marie MPP since Romano's byelection win following the retirement of Liberal David Orazietti in 2017. The Carleton University political science and government graduate helmed the former city councillor's election campaigns and assisted Romano with his constituency and ministry duties. The Sault MPP was Minister of Colleges and Universities and Minister of Government and Consumer Services during parts of his nearly eight years at Queen's Park.
"There's not a file that he wasn't pushing in Sault Ste. Marie that didn't cross my desk and didn't have my full support behind it," Scott told The Sault Star following the opening of his campaign office on Great Northern Road on Saturday.
Issues he worked on included the widening of Black Road and helping the city land Connecting Links funding. Scott said he "delivered Sault Ste. Marie solutions instead of Toronto excuses for the last eight years."
The 2025 election is Scott's first campaign as a candidate. He's worked for several government ministries, including housing and environment, conservation and parks as chief or deputy chief of staff since 2020. Scott told about 35 supporters his "experience navigating government" and "advocating for Sault Ste. Marie" make him "the best person to fight for you." He's not from the city, but estimates he lived in the Sault for about half of Romano's time in office.
"Being his right-hand man has really plugged me into the Sault's priorities," said the father of three. "A real fast track on understanding the local issues and understanding how to work with community partners to deliver results."
Scott vows to be "a strong voice" to confront tariffs brought in by U.S. President Donald Trump and protect jobs in the Sault.
"Once we weather the storm we got to keep on building," said Scott, with priorities including affordable housing and jobs for youth.
He's not anticipating blow back from voters angry with Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford calling an early election.
"I think that the people are smart and understand the strategy of lining up our four years with the four years down south because this fight is going to keep coming and it's not going away," said Scott. Ford appointed Scott as the Sault candidate.
Romano is Scott's campaign manager. The outgoing MPP called the first-time candidate his best friend and "an all star and a rock star."
Lisa Vezeau-Allen is the NDP candidate. btkelly@postmedia.com On X: @Saultreporter

Sault councillor Lisa Vezeau-Allen chosen as NDP candidate

Author: Della-mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sault-councillor-lisa-vezeau-allen-chosen-as-ndp/docview/3162419027/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sault+councillor+Lisa+Vezeau-Allen+chosen+as+NDP+candidate&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Lisa Vezeau-Allen will carry the NDP banner in Ontario's provincial election.
The Ward 2 councillor will officially take a leave of absence from her council position until after the election, as required by provincial legislation.
Her councillor honorarium for the month will be donated to SOYA and its renovation project, she said.
Over the past few days, the Ontario NDP has held its nomination meetings across Northern Ontario and now has a full slate in the North for the Feb. 27 election.
Vezeau-Allen said she is honoured to represent the NDP.
A party faithful for years, she said her interest in running for the party dates back to 2010 but, at that time, she just couldn't make the commitment with a young family.
"We really need to reduce the barriers and support people with young families who want to run and serve," she said. "This is some- thing no party does well."
Vezeau-Allen said she sees the need for change to create equality across the board to ensure that the best candidates are chosen in each riding.
"This has been my life-long political aspiration and it's really amazing. I feel so energized," she said.
She gives kudos to David Mornix for also being on the nomination ballot and participating in democracy.
About 45 ballots were cast by riding association members Thursday night.
Vezeau-Allen said as the current only local candidate for Sault Ste. Marie, she can bring the knowledge about ongoing issues and needs to Queen's Park.
She said she understands how government works, how funding application processes are written and get vetted.
"I'll be ready to hit the ground running," she said.
Vezeau-Allen said she was shocked the Progressive Conservatives have not nominated a local candidate from the riding.
"I think that is an insult to the community," she said. "This is about representing a community's needs and it needs someone who lives in the community, understands the community and experiences the community to represent it."
Vezeau-Allen said her platform will be a combination of the Ontario NDP's platform that reflects provincial needs and that of local needs.
For instance, she strongly supports leader Marit Stiles's push to improve youth mental health, an issue that is also important to Sault Ste. Marie.
She said that during the first three months of the school year, Algoma District School Board had 1,200 students seeking counselling services.
"This is the kind of legislation I really want to see happen," she said.
She said she also believes there are better ways to examine physician recruitment as well as connect post-secondary education with the health-care professionals needed in a community.
The founder of Grocer4Good and Cafe4Good also wants to address issues such as accessibility, food insecurity and affordable housing.
Earlier this week, the PCs announced that Premier Doug Ford appointed candidate Chris Scott for the Sault Ste. Marie riding.
Scott had worked for former MPP Ross Romano and has held a number of senior staff positions in the Ford government.
Ontarians will head to the polls Feb. 27.

Document 44 of 219

Top court to hear challenge of Quebec's Bill 21

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/top-court-hear-challenge-quebecs-bill-21/docview/3162418969/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Top+court+to+hear+challenge+of+Quebec%27s+Bill+21&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's Supreme Court has caused a stir by agreeing to hear a challenge to Quebec's secularism law, a nasty bit of legislation more unpopular outside Quebec than within it.
Bill 21 forbids public sector employees in authority from wearing religious symbols at work. The restriction effectively closes career pathways such as teaching and law enforcement to people whose religious observance includes elements of their dress. Across the province, people have been forced to choose between their faith identity and their jobs, abruptly reducing role models in respected professions, particularly Muslim women who wear hijab.
There's no question this discriminatory law violates equality rights guaranteed by the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms. Premier François Legault pre-emptively invoked the notwithstanding clause to pass the bill in 2019, and again last year, when the clause's five-year window expired.
Nobody knows on what basis the Supreme Court has decided to hear the appeal; the parties raised multiple issues, from gender equality to "unwritten principles." The decision has sparked speculation about a looming constitutional crisis. People are getting particularly exercised about the possibility the court might place restrictions on provincial and federal use of the notwithstanding clause, as Section 33 of the charter is commonly known.
In sounding the alarm, some commentators have begun referring to Section 33 by a much less common name, the "Parliamentary supremacy clause," in what appears to be a rebranding effort, as though the clause were a founding principle of the balance of power between the legislative, executive and judicial branches of government, and not a political compromise.
It's helpful to remember the notwithstanding clause was cooked up in a kitchen. When then-prime minister Pierre Trudeau was struggling to build consensus for a Canadian Constitution, his justice minister (future prime minister Jean Chrétien) and a couple provincial attorneys general sat down in an unused space in the National Conference Centre and hashed out some broadly acceptable concessions, which became known as the "kitchen accord."
Built on these principles, the 1982 Constitution ultimately secured the support of nine provinces; Quebec alone declined to sign on, under the separatist Parti Québécois.
Quebec has been the most enthusiastic adopter of the notwithstanding clause in pursuit of its legislative agenda, particularly to protect French language and culture. Given the fraught history, the province may be especially invested in the supremacy of its national assembly over the Supreme Court's role as final arbiter of what is constitutionally permissible.
More recently and increasingly boldly, some premiers, including Ontario's Doug Ford, have taken to using (or threatening to use) the notwithstanding clause like a bulldozer. Indeed, Chretien and his kitchen accord co-architects reunited in 2018 to criticize the misuse of Section 33 to bull through legislation without proper process.
It's also worth remembering Section 33 only applies to select rights and freedoms; parliamentary supremacy is not absolute. The notwithstanding clause allows governments to pause certain legal rights, fundamental freedoms, and equality, or freedom from discrimination; however, legislatures cannot abrogate language or mobility rights, nor the right to vote.
Folks fretting over a potential re-evaluation of Section 33 may well ask what use is a notwithstanding clause, if our (unelected) courts still have the power to reign in how elected governments may use it to pass laws in conflict with the Charter of Rights and Freedoms?
It's a fair question, but you can flip it around: What good are the fundamental human rights guaranteed by the charter, if they can be so easily tossed aside by elected representatives? write.robin@baranyai.ca

Subject: Freedoms; Equality; Charter of Rights-Canada; Prime ministers; Equal rights

Document 46 of 219

National poll shows leaderless Liberals starting to creep up on Conservatives

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-poll-shows-leaderless-liberals-starting/docview/3161647724/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+poll+shows+leaderless+Liberals+starting+to+creep+up+on+Conservatives&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives have been spooked in recent days by signs of a Liberal revival under a new leader.
They didn't seem too worried until Mark Carney, the Bank of Canada candidate, came across as a regular human on a U.S. comedy show.
Now, a poll from the Angus Reid Institute shows the Liberals gaining ground.
They have climbed well out of their New Year's nightmare, the 16 per cent support just before Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said he'd quit.
Angus Reid polled two scenarios, asking people which party they'd support with either Carney or ex-finance minister Chrystia Freeland as Liberal leader.
Carney runs slightly ahead of Freeland. Both are still well behind the Conservatives, but the lift in Liberal backing is enough to give Poilievre pause.
The Liberals under Carney would get 29 per cent national support. Freeland Liberals would clock 24 per cent.
Not great, but a big jump from 16 per cent. Nonetheless, the Conservatives are still in majority territory.
They would win 43 per cent running against Carney, and 44 per cent if Freeland were the temporary prime minister.
One intriguing finding is the NDP would score four points higher with Liberal leader Freeland (17 per cent) than Carney (13 per cent).
With Carney leading the Liberals, Conservatives would win 46 per cent in B.C.; 65 per cent in Alberta; 53 per cent in Saskatchewan and Manitoba; 50 per cent in Ontario; 18 per cent in Quebec, and 37 per cent in Atlantic Canada.
The Liberals are the usual hot mess anywhere from the Rockies to Manitoba's eastern border.
"While both potential leaders struggle to generate much support in the Prairies, a potential Freeland-led Liberal party performs worse than the NDP in Alberta, Saskatchewan and Manitoba," the pollster says.
But, suddenly, all-important Ontario shows some serious Liberal gains.
The Liberals have 34 per cent with Carney as leader. Substitute Freeland, and the backing drops to 27 per cent.
The relatively weaker Ontario standing for Freeland could well cost her the leadership, as party loyalists seek the leader most likely to pull off a miracle. Those miracles only happen in Ontario.
With either Liberal leader, Conservative backing in Ontario is virtually identical at near 50 per cent.
The Liberals lead only in Atlantic Canada. They have 42 per cent compared to 37 for the Conservatives. Support is identical for Carney- or Freeland-led Liberals.
Quebec appears to be a lost cause for the Liberals, who have 23 per cent support with Freeland and 25 with Carney.
The Bloc Québécois reigns. Carney is ahead of Freeland by a couple of points, but Bloc support also rises sharply if he's in charge.
The pollster asked Carney and Freeland supporters about their key issues: "The economy in general is narrowly chosen more often by Carney Liberals, while climate change is a slightly higher priority among Freeland Liberals."
This suggests voters aren't yet aware that Carney is one of the world's most influential advocates of pressing climate action through government fiscal policy.
The Reid poll sums up: "The former Bank of Canada governor Carney appears to have an advantage among prospective voters over his main rival ... That said, both still have to contend with Liberal party members and make their case to be the next leader, and both still trail the opposition Conservative party by a double-digit margin."
This early polling doesn't account for the looming crisis with the U.S.
A trade war could yet be the factor that revives the Liberals, or flattens them for a generation. Don Braid's column appears regularly in the Herald X and Bluesky: @DonBraid

Subject: Leadership; Voters; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Document 47 of 219

Sault Ste. Marie provincial NDP association announce candidacy nomination meeting; Dave Mornix, Lisa Vezeau-Allen both want the nomination

Author: Della-mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sault-ste-marie-provincial-ndp-association/docview/3161646959/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sault+Ste.+Marie+provincial+NDP+association+announce+candidacy+nomination+meeting%3B+Dave+Mornix%2C+Lisa+Vezeau-Allen+both+want+the+nomination&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two Sault Ste. Marie residents will be squaring off to earn the nomination of the local provincial NDP riding association.
Dave Mornix and Lisa Vezeau-Allen are both vying to carry the NDP banner into the next provincial election.
A nomination meeting will be held Thurs. Jan. 30 at the Delta Hotel from 6 p.m. to 8 p.m.
Celia Ross, president of the riding association said "We are thrilled to have a contested nomination with two outstanding candidates. This process shows the strength of our local movement and the deep commitment to our community to building a brighter future," she said. "Dave and Lisa embody the val - ues and determination we need to bring real, positive change to our community."
The public is invited to attend the nomination meeting and the candidates, however, only ONDP members in good standing will be eligible to vote to choose a candidate.
Vezeau-Allen said she's excited to put her name forward on the ballot.
The Ward 2 councillor said in a press release her history, work and advocacy on housing, homelessness and autism directly connects with provincial officials the community is facing today. The mother of three boys is also the founder of two social community enterprises, Grocer 4 Good Ability Development program and Cafe 4 Good, where youth and young adults with farriers are employed.
"At a young age I found purpose in contributing to local initiatives, and since then I have 40 years of being a mentor, leader, municipal figure and change-maker."
Vezeau-Allen had earlier told The Sault Star she was seeking the federal NDP riding association nomination for the Sault Ste. Marie-Algoma riding.
She said she was approached by provincial party organizers to consider running provincially and after much contemplation and consultation, she determined the work she has done translates well in provincial government and she believes she can bring a lot of experience to Queen's Park.
Dave Mornix, a founding member of the African Caribbean Canadian Association of Northern Ontario (ACCANO), is also an Algoma University graduate in sociology.
He's described as a "dedicated advocate, visionary community leader and a driving force for social transformation."
Born in Trinidad & Tobago, and raised in St. Vincent & the Grenadines, Mornix has lived in Sault Ste. Marie since 2002.
Mornix told The Sault Star his education, lived experience, community work and family make him ready for the job.
"There is so many reasons why the time is now," he said.
Mornix believes the NDP in Sault Ste. Marie has an opportunity to win the riding and candidate selection is important.
"An established politician cannot bring you the victory you need," he said. "There's no question I'm the underdog . . . but I can bring real voters to the party with the challenges we have, where there is no middle class anymore. I am the closest to that struggle."
Mornix said he has the ability to connect and invite new voters to rally around an election win on that platform.
It's anticipated the Sault Ste. Marie provincial Progressive Conservative riding association will announce their candidate by the end of the week. Now former MPP Ross Romano announced in December that he would not be seeking a third turn.
It's highly anticipated Premier Doug Ford will use one of his 10 appointments to select a candidate for Sault Ste. Marie. That candidate is expected to be a well-known local resident.
It is not known who the local Liberal association will choose for a candidate. The provincial association has not responded to requests for information from The Sault Star.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford called a quick election, after many months of speculation, on Wednesday by visiting the lieutenant governor with a request to dissolve the legislature early. The request was accepted, and the four-week election campaign begins.
Election day will be Feb. 27.

Document 48 of 219

Sault Ste. Marie PC candidate hand picked by Premier Doug Ford; Ross Romano to serve as campaign manager

Author: Della-mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sault-ste-marie-pc-candidate-hand-picked-premier/docview/3161646847/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sault+Ste.+Marie+PC+candidate+hand+picked+by+Premier+Doug+Ford%3B+Ross+Romano+to+serve+as+campaign+manager&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Sault Ste. Marie provincial Progressive Conservative riding association has announced that Chris Scott has been appointed the candidate for the provincial election.
He's been selected as the candidate by Ontario Premier Doug Ford. The premier has the ability to personally select up to 10 candidates, taking the job away from local associations.
Former MPP Ross Romano had announced in December that he wouldn't be seeking re-election.
Romano says he is now officially Scott's campaign manager leading into the Feb. 27 Ontario election.
"I've known him throughout the entirety of my political career," Romano told The Sault Star. "I'm endorsing him. He's done a fabulous job for our city for the past eight years."
Scott, 34, hails from Kemptville, Ont., just outside Brockville.
He is not from Sault Ste. Marie but intends on making his home here, Romano said. Scott has worked in Romano's constituency office and in a variety of roles, including as chief of staff in ministry offices.
Scott has served as campaign manager for Romano in past elections. He has also held a variety of positions with the Ontario PC party, including the most recent, special advisor to the chief of staff.
He's also served as the chief of staff to the associate minister of housing, deputy chief of staff of Environment, Conservation and Parks and chief of staff of ministry of government and consumer services, among other roles, his LinkedIn profile reads.
"We've knocked on doors together, worked all my elections together and he's worked for me in both my ministries," Romano said.
Romano said he believes Scott will "continue to build on the job already done and continue with the work that needs to be done."
He said since 2016, there has been a 'connection' between the two, beginning with the need for Scott to leave home at a young age to follow his career path.
"He understands about youth outmigration. He was part of youth outmigration," Romano said.
Romano denied the suggestion that Scott is a parachute candidate.
Parachuting is a political metaphor that means sending an 'outsider' as a candidate for a particular party in an election.
"He loves this community, and this place is home to him," he said of the married father of three. "He's in the process of having a home here and he came of age in this community."
Romano called the upcoming election "extremely important" and Scott has been involved in all that has been accomplished on the provincial stage in recent years.
"I want to see that continue. I want him to continue the journey. The work doesn't end here. There is still lots to be done," Romano said.
Romano said Scott has already started knocking on local doors and will continue to do so throughout the month-long campaign.
Scott gives kudos to Romano in a LinkedIn post. "I had the privilege of working alongside Ross in various capacities, from managing successful campaigns to serving as his chief of staff. His ability to challenge the status quo, foster a culture of excellence, push his team to be its best, and deliver tangible results for his community is something I'll always admire."
Scott's official campaign office will open on Saturday.

Document 49 of 219

Young Liberals have a plan to earn youth votes; Topping the list of priorities: addressing the Canadian housing affordability crisis

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/young-liberals-have-plan-earn-youth-votes-topping/docview/3160667426/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Young+Liberals+have+a+plan+to+earn+youth+votes%3B+Topping+the+list+of+priorities%3A+addressing+the+Canadian+housing+affordability+crisis&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The president of the Liberal Party of Canada's youth wing says he believes it remains the party of youth, even as many appear to have left, flocking instead to the party's chief rival: federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Liam Olsen, who has led the Young Liberals of Canada since 2023, says Prime Minister Justin Trudeau showed he took young people and their issues seriously, from appointing himself to be the minister of youth and establishing youth councils, to prioritize fighting climate change and ending interest on federal student loans.
Now, Olsen says, it is time the party considers what's next.
"We need to build a nation for our generation," the 24-year-old said.
"Too many young people feel like we're being left behind, like no matter how hard we're working, or how hard we're studying, that we can't get out of debt, but that's because of student debt we accumulated through university or college. We can't keep up with rent or rising grocery prices, and we need leaders who hear our voices."
With the contest to replace Trudeau underway, the Young Liberals are calling on candidates to commit to championing a new policy pledge. Released on Thursday, the one-page document highlights different priorities it wants them to advance.
They include lowering the voting age to 16, restricting temporary foreign work in regions where young people don't have jobs, ensuring youth have "debt-free" access to post-secondary and halving green house gas emissions by 2035.
Topping the list of priorities is one that reads: "the housing affordability crisis is a national emergency."
Olsen said candidates ought to talk about it as such.
Poilievre has made housing affordability a central plank of his pitch for why Canadians should elect him as their next prime minister, targeting those under 40, for whom owning a home feels impossible because of the price.
While Liberals, New Democrats and others dismiss Poilievre's efforts as nothing more than social media soundbites and slogans, months of back-to-back polling suggests his message has worked.
Phillipe Fournier of the polling aggregator, 338Canada, says Conservative are leading among those aged 18 to 34, with the Liberals and NDP battling it out for a distant second.
Not only is that significant because of how Trudeau rose to power in 2015 by appealing directly to young people, but because young Canadians have traditionally veered towards the NDP.
Fournier says the Conservatives began rising in the polls around when housing prices were being hit hard by interest rate hikes coming out of the COVID-19 pandemic. Poilievre began pounding the message that people cannot afford homes under Trudeau and Conservatives would fix things.
"That I think resonated with a lot of young voters," Fournier says.
Since winning the Conservative leadership in 2022, Poilievre has ushered in a social media savvy not seen from previous Conservative leaders, which has driven his success among younger Canadians. That, and his style of being able to speak casually, despite having been a career politician.
In his most recent wide-ranging interview with right-wing commentator and host Jordan Peterson, Poilievre discussed how many of the young people he meets are "exhausted." He at one point says that all young people do now "is work."
"I am astonished when I meet university students how much they work outside of their studies just to scrape by," Poilievre told Peterson. "These kids are working 20-30 hours a week in addition to a full course load."
Fournier says it appears voters have been migrating to the Conservatives from the Liberals.
"Obviously, it's not the same voters as five or 10 years ago, but we see that, yes, the Conservatives are first among young people in almost every poll."
Whether those trying to replace Trudeau can claw back any of that support remains an open question. The winner will likely have little time to do so, given how they may find themselves campaigning in a general election in only weeks. All opposition parties have vowed to bring down the minority Liberals as soon as Parliament resumes on March 24.
The Liberals pick their new leader on March 9.
Pointing to a newly released poll suggesting the Liberals may be experiencing a bump since U.S. President Donald Trump's election, Olsen expressed optimism. He added that he is not "focused on the past" and that life has become more expensive for everyone. "I don't think it's just young people."
Fournier says it is still to early to tell whether Canadians feel differently about the Liberals following Trudeau's decision to resign.
Olsen said he believes all leadership candidates take youth issues seriously, having spoken with the campaigns of frontrunners Mark Carney, a former Bank of Canada and Bank of England governor, as well as former finance minister Chrystia Freeland. Olsen also said he has been in touch with Karina Gould's campaign, the only one of the three making a direct appeal to the party's young Liberals.
On Thursday, both Gould and Freeland announced that as part of their plans to renew the party they would reinstate the role of paid national director of the young Liberals group.
In her proposal, Gould, who is herself a millennial, said they need the support of young Canadians if they hope to win the next election.
At her campaign launch last weekend, the 37-year-old talked about having been a Liberal since she was a teen and celebrated the youth wing has having been the first to champion matters that later became government policy, like Indigenous reconciliation. She also recently held an event with around 40 young Liberals.
"Young people are looking for a leader who will listen and represent their voices. I'm ready to bring that change," she recently posted to X. "It's time for a new generation of leadership - one that's fresh, energetic, and focused on building a stronger, united Canada for today and tomorrow."
Housing Minister Nate Erskine-Smith told reporters on Wednesday that he was not yet sure how he was going to be involved in the race. Talking about Gould, he said she comes from "the same generational lens" as he does, and hopes "she speaks to housing and childcare."
"I have every expectation that she will."
While Gould sees her age as an advantage, Ontario MP and former cabinet minister Helena Jaczek, who is endorsing Carney, suggested young people will not necessarily vote for someone just because they are around their age.
"What I have found is that millennials actually appreciate experience." with files from Catherine Levesque National Post staylor@postmedia.com

Document 50 of 219

Provincial dollars help pave way for Sackville Road extension; Housing-Enabling Core Servicing Stream provides $5.3M

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provincial-dollars-help-pave-way-sackville-road/docview/3160667261/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provincial+dollars+help+pave+way+for+Sackville+Road+extension%3B+Housing-Enabling+Core+Servicing+Stream+provides+%245.3M&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Sault Ste. Marie is receiving more than $5.2 million in critical infrastructure money to extend Sackville Road to Third Line.
MPP Ross Romano announced the funding under the province's Housing-Enabling Core Servicing Stream at city hall Monday, just days before Premier Doug Ford is to call an election.
The money, totaling $5,253,000, comes from the Municipal Housing Infrastructure Program. It will allow the city to move forward with the Sackville extension, which would also permit about 238 homes in the area. The funding is tied to the opportunity to open up a parcel of vacant land that would allow for new housing starts.
City council had earmarked $4 million for the first phase of the project in its 2025 budget. Of that, $1.5 million was dedicated to storm sewer work and $2.5 million for roads and engineering. The project also includes a ravine crossing and installation of a large box culvert on which the road will be built on.
It was anticipated that an additional $7.4 million would be allocated to the second phase of the project in 2026.
This funding alleviates the funding pressure for the complete project and the funds provided by the province can be spent into 2026.
It allows city council to divert any additional capital spending budget money to another project or extend the road resurfacing to lower Sackville Road. Any rejigging of the city's share of the funding would need to be approved by city council.
Romano said he's pleased to provide the funding in one of his last announcements because the extension project was first raised when he was a city councillor in 2015. The project was shelved at that time to save taxpayers money during a very tight budget year. It was also deferred in 2017 and 2020.
Now, he said, the multi-year extension project from Mary Avenue to Third Line can take place, which will also unlock more housing opportunities and support economic growth to the city.
It will also free up congestion on Great Northern Road, at Second Line, which is the city's busiest intersection.
"The city has known this was needed for a very long time," he said.
"It's important and critical infrastructure investment in our community."
Mayor Matthew Shoemaker said the project is one of several that have been deferred a number of times due to the municipality's tight budget.
But, he noted, the city has the realization that when projects are delayed or deferred, it costs more in years to come to complete them.
The funding, Shoemaker added, "will bring those costs back into something that is manageable in the budget."
While it will be left to council, the funding may also mean that there is enough funding in the budget to continue with the resurfacing of the existing Sackville Road, a project that wasn't expected to occur at this time.
There is about 6.4 hectares of property that can be opened up to development with the extension, based on medium-density housing scenarios, city council was told in December.
Shoemaker said the extension will also create a new north-south connection to the Hub Trail and save the city $3-$5 million in upgrades to a pumping station.
The Sackville Road extension is expected to begin this summer with the creation of culverts. They need to settle for a year before construction can begin the following year, Shoemaker said.
Sackville Road, north of Second Line, sees between 4,000 to 5,000 vehicles per day. The extension could increase the volume to about 8,000 vehicles per day.
City staff said traffic calming measures would be explored, where needed, to control traffic speeds and increase pedestrian safety.
While sidewalks are not planned for the extension, a multi-use trail is proposed as a John Rowswell Hub Trail extension.

Document 51 of 219

Strong mandate needed to protect Ontario

Author: Ford, Doug

Publication info: Ford, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/strong-mandate-needed-protect-ontario/docview/3160667233/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Strong+mandate+needed+to+protect+Ontario&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Ford%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: President Donald Trump is threatening steep and sweeping tariffs that would devastate Ontario's economy, putting 450,000 jobs on the line - threatening the livelihoods of workers on factory floors, construction sites and in local stores across the province and the families and communities that depend on them.
We need to be clear-eyed about the risk. Whether he imposes tariffs next week, next month or waits a year, President Trump's threats are not going away and are already taking a toll on our economy. He's promised to use economic force to get his way. He's even mused about taking over Canadian territory.
Canada is not for sale. Not to President Trump, not to anyone.
At a time when the federal government has left Canada drifting, exposed and vulnerable, President Trump will use every day he's in office to exploit any weakness he can find. To protect Ontario, I'm asking the people for a strong mandate - a strong, stable, four-year mandate that will outlive and outlast the Trump administration. President Trump needs to know that for as long as he is president, I will be on the other side of the table ready to protect Ontario.
We're entering a period of unprecedented economic risk and lengthy negotiations - against President Trump and with the federal government and other provinces. Responding to this challenge will demand extraordinary action: the need to invest tens of billions of dollars in unplanned spending and make tough choices to ensure Ontario's economy is the most competitive place to invest, create jobs and do business. This includes cutting red tape and streamlining approvals to get big things built faster.
I'm prepared to make those choices. First, I need your support. The kind of action required to respond to President Trump's threats will impact the province's fiscal plan and our path to balance. As taxpayers, this is your money. Ontario is your province and this is your future. Your voice should be heard. At a time like this, in a democracy like ours, no politician should be against letting the people have their say.
With the trust and support of Ontario voters, we'll be prepared to give this everything we've got.
I'll continue to make the case to our American allies that we're so much stronger together. I will also be ready to do whatever is necessary to stand up for and protect Ontario, our workers, businesses and communities.
Make no mistake: Ontario won't start a fight with the U.S., but you better believe we'll be ready to win one.
Over the past seven years, together with workers, businesses and union partners, we've made enormous progress rebuilding Ontario's economy. After years of decline under the previous Liberal government, we're bringing back manufacturing jobs, with more than $45 billion in new investments in the first electric vehicle and battery plants in Canada, creating thousands of jobs. We're investing nearly $200 billion to build new roads, highways and the largest expansion of public transit in North America. We have a plan to deliver better health care and connect everyone in Ontario to a family doctor and primary care team. We're keeping your costs down by fighting the Liberal carbon tax, removing road tolls, scrapping the licence plate sticker fee and putting more money back into your pocket with $200 taxpayer rebate cheques.
All of this progress is now under threat from President Trump's tariffs. Team Ontario will answer the call.
I will never stop fighting to protect Ontario. With your support and with a mandate from you, the people, I will do whatever is necessary to win this fight. For our families. For our workers. For our businesses. For all of us. Doug Ford is Premier of Ontario and the Leader of the Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario.

Subject: Presidents; Roads & highways; Tariffs

Document 52 of 219

Ford is taking a risk calling an early Ontario election

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-is-taking-risk-calling-early-ontario/docview/3159750806/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+is+taking+a+risk+calling+an+early+Ontario+election&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: So, Premier Doug Ford is going to call an election Wednesday.
His comments Friday end months of speculation and will now send local riding associations scrambling to get candidates in place.
Ford says he needs a new and strong mandate to respond to the potential economic fallout if U.S. President Donald Trump imposes 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods. His plan is to spend billions in stimulus funds to counter the potential tariffs. Polls suggest he would be re-elected with a clear majority.
Does he need a new mandate? No.
Opposition parties have said they would support stimulus spending, if required, but Ford wants Ontarians to have their say at the polls.
Ontario's NDP argues Ford is working harder to protect his own job, rather than those of Ontarians.
The Ontario Liberals have also said an early election is expensive and unnecessary, calling Ford "selfish."
It is also a waste of an estimated $175 million of taxpayer money to call an election earlier than needed.
So why does he really want a new mandate? Firstly, he likely wants to beat the looming federal election, one which clearly shows that, at this point in time, Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre will win.
If Poilievre does win, that creates some risks for Ford and his government.
For starters, historical data show that in Ontario voters tend to opt for a different party at Queen's Park than they do in Ottawa. A federal Conservative win could mean the demise of Ford - or at the very least, not show as strong results as he hopes to show.
Then there are the election-weary taxpayers.
Back-to-back elections have never gone over well with voters who have to follow the campaigns, learn the policies of each party, study the candidates and head to the polls. Apathy is huge across the country, and it will definitely impact the number of voters who cast ballot.
Unfortunately, back-toback elections often create even more apathy and distrust in politicians and government than we already have.
Ford is putting his best foot forward trying to protect Ontario's economy and jobs, urging the federal government to have a strong dollar-for-dollar tariff if Trump follows through on his threats and imposes tariffs.
He's made his own threats, including to "clear off every bit of U.S. alcohol on the shelves" of the LCBO, cutting Ontario power to more than 1.5 million homes in the U.S. and imposing other measures from the provincial level.
But an early election is not without risk.
Let's remember that the Ford government has not gone unscathed during its term -and voters remember.
Homelessness and mental health and addiction issues continue to climb, and blame is squarely put on the failure of the provincial government to properly fund and implement solutions.
A primary health-care provider shortage also continues to swell, something more apparent in Northern and rural communities, including Sault Ste. Marie and the Algoma District.
Simply put, Ontario's health care is in crisis.
Then there was the Greenbelt land swap scandal that rocked the southern part of Ontario, leading to an RCMP investigation.
He wants to tear up bike lanes and he's rejected fast-tracking of fourplexes despite a desperate need to build more housing.
Ontarians remember. Consider this: if the looming tariff threat remains real, why wouldn't Ford want to continue with his existing mandate and do his job to protect Ontario's economy and fight back against the U.S. administration - instead of pausing government to travel across the province to woo voters and send them to the polls? Ford's team has been doing its homework for almost a year now, conducting polls on the temperature of an election, well before Trump was sworn into office. It appears they seem to think the timing is right.
They've also been busy behind the scenes getting candidates in place. Ford had asked his current MPPs to declare by December whether they would seek re-election. Local riding associations are ramping up their nomination and vetting process to ensure candidates are in place and ready.
At last count, Ford's team had 75 candidates in place, more than the other major parties. There are 124 ridings in Ontario. Locally, the PCs and the NDPs have said they've started their candidate selection process. Looks like they'll be ramping up those processes in the coming days.
Elaine Della-Mattia is The Sault Star's political reporter.

Subject: Political campaigns; Apathy; Candidates; Voters; Elections; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US

Document 53 of 219

Robert Peace 'considering'PC candidacy; Ontario election call expected Wednesday

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/robert-peace-consideringpc-candidacy-ontario/docview/3159750723/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Robert+Peace+%27considering%27PC+candidacy%3B+Ontario+election+call+expected+Wednesday&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Sault Ste. Marie community activist has announced he is considering running as a Progressive Conservative candidate in the next provincial election - which is expected to be announced next week.
Robert Peace says he is eager to bring his experience to the Ontario legislature.
"I know I've only been here four years and some may still consider me a newcomer but I feel so at home here, so involved in so many aspects of the community and I think I can bring my life experience to the job," he told The Sault Star.
Peace said he leans on the "progressive side" and may not be a stereotypical PC, but he's inspired to lead Ontario's fight against what might be a tough battle with the new reality in the U.S.
"We also need a strong representation here in the Sault if we want any hope of tackling our health care, education and economic issues," he said.
Peace said he understands he'll be a backbencher at the outset, but he worked at Queen's Park as an executive assistant under former premier Bill Davies. He also worked with former education minister Larry Grossman, serving as a policy advisor and Question Period strategist during his time as Leader of the Opposition.
Peace continued in that role under Interim Leader Andy Brandt and concluded his time at Queen's Park working alongside Environment critic Margaret Marland.
"I also had the opportunity to serve briefly on a Mining and Forestry Board under premier Mike Harris before shifting my focus to conservation and environmental protection. Throughout my career, I've been considered a "Red Tory" - a fiscal conservative with a strong passion for social justice," he said in a press release.
Peace hasn't been vetted and hopes to meet with local riding association president Jason Naccarato soon.
Peace, who unsuccessfully ran for mayor in the last municipal election, said he isn't sure if there are other interested individuals in the job.
"I'm connecting with residents and community stakeholders to gather their thoughts and see if there's support for my candidacy," he said. "This is very much a moment where I want to hear from the people of Sault Ste. Marie about what matters most to them."
Premier Doug Ford has now said he intends to call an early election. That's expected to occur on Wednesday.
It limits the time new candidates can be vetted by their parties. The premier can also appoint candidates in 10 ridings to simplify the process.
Sault MPP Ross Romano formally announced in December that he would not seek re-election in order to spend more time with his family.
The Sault Ste. Marie provincial NDP riding association has said it, too, has begun its process to find a candidate to run in the next election.

Subject: Provincial elections; Peace; Candidates; Elections

Document 54 of 219

Carney unconcerned about lack of experience; Liberal leadership candidate cites time at Bank of Canada, Bank of England, UN

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carney-unconcerned-about-lack-experience-liberal/docview/3159750636/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carney+unconcerned+about+lack+of+experience%3B+Liberal+leadership+candidate+cites+time+at+Bank+of+Canada%2C+Bank+of+England%2C+UN&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The idea of becoming prime minister of Canada in a matter of weeks despite having never won an election does not seem to worry former central banker Mark Carney, who says he has started at the top many times in his career.
On his way to a private event in Ottawa with his supporters on Thursday, Carney briefly stopped to speak to reporters flanked with many ministers who have endorsed him.
"I've had many jobs where I came in at the top," he said, when asked what he would say to someone who thinks being prime minister is not an entry-level job. "I came in at the top of the Bank of England, Bank of Canada, in terms of the United Nations and climate change."
He served as Bank of Canada governor during the 2008 economic crisis before becoming governor of the Bank of England when the United Kingdom withdrew from the European Union. More recently, he served as the UN Special Envoy on Climate Action and Finance.
Carney insisted that he intends to put the economy "back on track" as a team.
"You see the team here," he said, pointing to the elected MPs standing behind him.
An hour earlier, the Conservatives had dispatched MP Michelle Rempel Garner outside of a nearby restaurant where they thought the Carney event was taking place to complain that he had not addressed the media since his campaign launch in Edmonton last week.
Until then, only the Canadian Press had managed to catch up with Carney, while he was skating the Rideau Canal on Wednesday to ask him about his plans for the carbon tax.
He promised to present a plan to address climate change "that works for everyone."
"This man ... could, quite possibly, become prime minister in about six weeks," said Rempel Garner on Thursday. "He's never held elected office of any form, he doesn't sit in Parliament, and he could be making Canada's policy at a time of national crisis.
"And yet, a week after launching his campaign ... we have heard absolutely nothing, zero, crickets from this man about what he would do as prime minister."
Liberal ministers and MPs who were entering the Carney event accused the Conservatives of being "afraid" of a new Liberal leader who is not Justin Trudeau.
"There's a lot of fear," said Employment Minister Steven MacKinnon. "I think Canadians know that the challenges we face around the world and here at home are going to require complex solutions by people of experience and not just pithy slogans."
Ontario Liberal MP Ryan Turnbull said Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre should indeed be afraid of an incoming opponent who has lots of economic experience.
"Mark Carney is a highly credible individual who has a reputation around the world for being able to deal with economic crises, and Pierre Poilievre is a career politician. He's got no experience," he said.
Rempel Garner said it is not about Conservatives, and that Canadians are frustrated.
"We are at a time where ... I have people in my community that can't afford to eat, and we've got these guys shuttering Parliament. We have tariffs ... being threatened on us by the American government, and they're having a cocktail party." calevesque@postmedia.com

Document 57 of 219

Sault provincial riding associations on alert for early election call

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sault-provincial-riding-associations-on-alert/docview/3159023018/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sault+provincial+riding+associations+on+alert+for+early+election+call&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Local provincial riding associations are ramping up in case Premier Doug Ford calls an early election.
Media reports suggest that Ford will call an election in early February, possibly even earlier.
The premier himself had repeatedly said he needs a new and strong mandate to respond to the potential economic fallout if U.S. President Donald Trump imposes 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods. His plan would be to spend billions in stimulus funds to counter the potential tariffs. Polls suggest he would be re-elected with a clear majority.
Incumbent PC MPP Ross Romano announced in December that he will not seek re-election for a third term, opting instead to spend more time at home with his family and three pre-teen sons.
Jason Naccarato, president of the Sault Ste. Marie provincial PC Riding Association, was reluctant to discuss an election that has not been officially called.
He said that since Romano announced he wasn't seeking re-election, there have been "several" individuals who have indicated they would be interested in running in the next election.
"It is not going to be difficult to find a candidate," he said. "Ross has done an excellent job, the party is doing very well."
Celia Ross, president of the Sault Ste. Marie provincial NDP Riding Association, said vetting has already begun for "more than one" potential candidate.
"We are not very pleased that Doug Ford wants to spend taxpayers money one year before he needs to, to call an election," she said.
However, she noted, "We are really excited and believe we can run a really good campaign and win the riding."
"Our position on housing and affordable living and public health care is much different than the (Progressive) Conservatives and we strongly believe these are important issues in our community. There are significant differences in our position and there is a great feeling among our members and our potential candidates that people want to see change," she said.
Attempts to reach a representative of the Sault Ste. Marie provincial Liberal Riding Association were unsuccessful.
The Sault's provincial Liberal riding association brought in last-minute parachute candidate Liam Hancock after an 18-year-old Espanola candidate was dropped by the party after alleged online comments in a gaming chat room. But Hancock never campaigned for the job.
All riding associations have similar processes to elect their candidates.
Names of individuals are ultimately put forward, applications completed and the individuals are vetted by a provincial team. Nominated and vetted individuals usually identify themselves at that time and have an opportunity to sell memberships for the association before a nomination meeting and election is held (if more than one candidate is presented).
In the 2022 election, Romano won with 12,606 votes, or a 46.9 per cent share of the ballots.
NDP candidate Michele Mc-Cleave-Kennedy placed second, with 10,029 votes, or a 37.3 per cent share of the ballots.
Technically, the provincial election is not scheduled to occur until June 2026, but speculation of an early election call has been reported since last spring.
The Ontario Election Act states a writ for an election must be dated on a Wednesday. It will mark a 28-day election period.
