Document 58 of 219

Smith helps avert tariff war Liberals invited

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/smith-helps-avert-tariff-war-liberals-invited/docview/3159022513/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Smith+helps+avert+tariff+war+Liberals+invited&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's sad to feel relief when U.S. President Donald Trump doesn't mention Canada in his inauguration speech.
Mexico, yes. Cartels and gangs, you bet. Renaming the Gulf of Mexico as the Gulf of America, certainly. Taking control of the Panama Canal, very likely.
Canada was the no-name country, for this tense moment at least.
But our absence is nothing to celebrate. Canada is just the kid hiding under the bed, unnoticed for now.
Though tariffs will be studied, not immediately imposed, the president also said he loves them to death (possibly ours.)
He sees them as geopolitical tools, revenue sources and stimuli to an all-American economy that reboots manufacturing dominance.
He will cut taxes for Americans. The foreign cash from tariffs, paid to his new "external revenue agency," will make up losses to the U.S. treasury.
All that was said on inauguration day. The pause doesn't mean no heavy tariffs, only that Trump will be somewhat more alert to the impact on U.S. economic sectors.
Liberal politicians who claim any kind of victory are courting more trouble.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith said: "The worst possible response to today's news would be the federal government or premiers declaring 'victory,'or escalating tensions with unnecessary threats against the United States."
She's right. The best Ottawa has achieved is hesitancy, and a chance to do more lobbying and explaining in the U.S.
If any politician can claim positive effect, it's Smith.
She talks to U.S. politicians and media outlets. She turns up at balls and parties. She got straight to Trump at Mara-Lago on Kevin O'Leary's ticket.
She'd said she was on vacation, but there she was again, in photos with yet another state governor.
Critics carped she hadn't registered her lobbying with Global Affairs. These people would give her a parking ticket for stopping at an accident. Smith was sure to be savaged for all this, but she didn't care.
When she zips around the U.S., there's no sign she's lobbying just for Alberta. She's promoting Canada.
On CTV Monday, Smith explained how her approach benefits industries across the country.
After Trump spoke, she released a detailed plan for building a new economic relationship with the U.S.
She doesn't believe Canada should retaliate with our own tariffs, arguing U.S. consumers quickly would force Trump to back off theirs.
For that, she's called a traitor and worse.
At home, Smith refused to sign the premiers'joint declaration because she felt it didn't shield Alberta from an oil export tax.
I've expressed my unease about her failure to endorse that document. But her fear of an export tax on one Alberta industry is justified.
The real heat should be turned on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals as they scramble for rebirth with a new leader.
They've spent nine years trying to build a new economy entirely insulated from the real world.
They refused to allow pipelines that would have exported oil and natural gas to large parts of Asia and Europe, thus freeing us from the tyranny of the U.S. market.
They built non-economic goals into all natural-resource projects. This raised costs, slowed or killed approvals, and drove off domestic and foreign investors.
They tried to create a new e-vehicle industry with massive federal subsidies for Ontario and Quebec. Now, that whole shaky project is under threat from Trump.
Canada was a much healthier country when Trump first appeared in 2016. He attacked the free-trade deal, but there was no talk of 25 per cent tariffs or turning us into the 51st state.
Today, Canada is weaker, and his assault is correspondingly stronger.
That's Trump's way. But the Liberal absolutists brought the country to this point of extreme vulnerability.
It's a sign of their sorry state that one premier, wandering lonely as a cloud, does more good than the lot of them. Don Braid's column appears regularly in the Calgary Herald X and Bluesky: @DonBraid

Subject: Export taxes; Lobbying; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Export taxes Tariffs

Document 59 of 219

New LCBO to offer 'more choice'; Late February opening; no word if other city locations will be affected

Author: Ougler, Jeffrey

Publication info: Ougler, Jeffrey. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-lcbo-offer-more-choice-late-february-opening/docview/3159022445/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+LCBO+to+offer+%27more+choice%27%3B+Late+February+opening%3B+no+word+if+other+city+locations+will+be+affected&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Ougler%2C+Jeffrey&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Booze will soon be back in Sault Ste. Marie's east end.
Not to mention added selections of other adult beverages, as LCBO is set to return to Churchill Plaza, this time located in the former Aaron's Rent To Own space, just south of the previous location, near what now houses a pizzeria and cannabis dispensary.
Officials aim for a late February launch.
The new store will offer 6,700 square feet, providing customers with "even more choice," LCBO told The Sault Star via email.
The outlet, the fourth Sault LCBO, will stock more than 1,700 products, including a large selection of "large-pack" beer, 61 VQA wines and 83 Vintages Essentials, including ice wines, and 24 "linear feet" of chilled wines.
Beer drinkers are currently able to purchase product in the city's east end; Churchill Plaza houses The Beer Store, Pino's Get Fresh was, in early 2016, one of the first 68 grocery stores in Ontario to be granted a licence to sell beer and Metro Churchill sells suds and wine.
Hard stuff is harder to come by.
LCBO says the new outlet will offer an "expanded" Canadian whisky section with more than 100 products and a "large" vodka section with an increased assortment of flavoured vodkas.
Forty-eight Ontario craft beers will be offered, including one from Northern Superior Brewing Co. There was no mention if Outspoken Brewing or Soo Falls Brewing Co., fare will also be available.
LCBO will announce an opening date in the coming weeks.
Other Sault LCBO locations are Great Northern Road, Station Mall and Second Line West. The Sault Star asked -but did not get a response - if LCBO's footprint here will change once the new store opens. It also didn't provide a price tag for the new digs. LCBO has more than 680 retail stores.
Depending on what goes on south of the border in the next little while, Jack Daniel's and Jim Beam fans will not be flocking to the new location - nor any another LCBO -to whet their whistles.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Monday he has directed the LCBO to "clear off every bit of U.S. alcohol off the shelves" if U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats come to fruition.
"They will feel the pain. I will make sure I communicate this to our other premiers that they should be following suit," he said.
Trump didn't mention Canada specifically in his inaugural address, but did announce that he was creating a new agency to "tariff and tax foreign countries to enrich our citizens."
But Monday evening, Trump suggested his administration could move ahead with 25 per cent across-the-board tariffs on Canadian imports on Feb. 1. With files from The Canadian Press

Document 60 of 219

New Liberal leader must understand Sault's, Algoma's 'very special needs': Sheehan; MP consulting with business leaders, constituents

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-liberal-leader-must-understand-saults-algomas/docview/3159021272/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+Liberal+leader+must+understand+Sault%27s%2C+Algoma%27s+%27very+special+needs%27%3A+Sheehan%3B+MP+consulting+with+business+leaders%2C+constituents&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sault Ste. Marie MP Terry Sheehan knows the next two months are critical for the Liberal Party of Canada.
With the Trudeau era is coming to an end, the next leader - and prime minister - will be carefully scrutinized on his or her plans on how to lead the country.
With outside threats such as President Donald Trump's proposed tariffs on Canadian goods, the need to carefully balance an economy to bring inflation down and keep a sharp eye on environmental issues, the next leader needs to be quick thinking, proactive and sometimes reactive to ensure the best decisions are made for Canada.
For those reasons, Sheehan said he wants to learn more about candidates seeking the party's leadership before he decides whom he will support.
Sheehan said he's very familiar with all three front-runners - Mark Carney, Chrystia Freeland and Karina Gould - and Freeland and Gould have both visited Sault Ste. Marie on several occasions to meet with business, industry and organizations.
"I'll consider everything from my perspective and who I feel should be the Liberal leader and next prime minister who will be best to represent Sault Ste. Marie and the Algoma District, but I'm also consulting and hearing from others in our community and taking their views into account," Sheehan said.
"We have very special needs here and they are my top priority, and I will base my decision on that." From a community that was shrinking for decades to one that is now growing, and one where Algoma Steel was in CCAA to one that is now growing and preparing for the future, Sheehan wants to see a leader willing to make smart investments, understand the steel industry and the unique needs of the North.
He also wants to see continued support for small businesses from a leader who understands and can respond to a changing economy and protects the rights of all Canadians.
"Continued growth and prosperity is really important," he said.
Sheehan said he's already been talking to others in the community - both Liberals and non-Liberals - along with businesses, unions, volunteers and constituents at large.
"I'm getting a diverse amount of feedback and I'm still listening," he said.
Sheehan will attend the party's national caucus meeting later this week and anticipates hearing more from leadership candidates then.
He noted Carney, born in the Northwest Territories, was raised in Edmonton, another northern community similar to Sault Ste. Marie. He has a deep understanding of business and economics, Sheehan said.
Gould and Freeland are both from Ontario and Sheehan said he's worked closely with both over the past nine years.
Gould selected Sault Ste. Marie as one of the first four communities to receive a Service Canada centre and she's explored the Rural Northern Immigration Pilot Project and its benefits to businesses.
Freeland, Sheehan said, protected steelworkers'jobs and supported Algoma Steel, fighting against tariffs Trump imposed during his first term.
Both are familiar with, and understand, the needs of Sault Ste. Marie and area.
"I'm looking forward to hearing from them, dialoging with them, listening to their platforms as they roll them out," Sheehan told The Sault Star. He hasn't discounted others who have also entered the race, but said it is important that the next leader and prime minister can deal with Trump.
"The Canadian-U.S. relationship is one of the most important relationships in the world. The United States buys more from Canada than the combined countries of China, Japan and the U.K. We are a significant partner, and we need someone who can negotiate with Trump and stand up to him if he proposes any tariffs. We are ready for dollar-to-dollar counter tariffs if that happens."
Sheehan said he doesn't know when he'll decide who he will support, but said there is time to decide.
"I'm putting Sault Ste. Marie and Algoma first. That's my priority," he said.
The Liberal Party of Canada selects its next leader March 9.

Document 62 of 219

Trump holds off on hitting Canada with Day 1 tariffs; In November, Trump threatened to hit Canada with steep 25 per cent tariffs

Author: Duggan, Kyle; Anja Karade Glija

Publication info: Duggan, Kyle; Anja Karade Glija. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-holds-off-on-hitting-canada-with-day-1/docview/3157754392/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+holds+off+on+hitting+Canada+with+Day+1+tariffs%3B+In+November%2C+Trump+threatened+to+hit+Canada+with+steep+25+per+cent+tariffs&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Duggan%2C+Kyle%3BAnja+Karade+Glija&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: U.S. President Donald Trump held off on hitting Canada with damaging tariffs on his inauguration day.
Shortly before Trump was sworn into office Monday, an incoming White House official who insisted on anonymity pointed reporters to a Wall Street Journal report that said instead of tariffs Trump would instead sign an executive order launching an investigation into alleged unfair trade and currency practices by Canada, Mexico and China.
That suggests he plans to pursue a more deliberative approach to trade relations, as he moves to quickly implement his policy agenda through a raft of decrees.
In his inaugural address he did vow to boost domestic oil production and impose tariffs to "enrich our citizens." He talked about setting up an agency to collect tariffs but did not got into detail.
In November, Trump threatened to hit Canada with steep 25 per cent across-the-board tariffs in an executive order on his first day back in office.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has repeatedly said Canada is ready to respond with countermeasures if Trump makes good on that threat.
The federal government also promised to pump $1.3 billion in new resources into border security over the next six years in the hopes of convincing Trump not to impose tariffs.
Trump initially tied the tariffs to concerns about border security but later seemed more concerned with the trade imbalance between Canada and the U.S.
As Trump begins his second term as president, federal cabinet ministers are gathering in Montebello, Que., Monday and Tuesday to discuss the tariff threat.
Trudeau also met with the country's 13 premiers last Wednesday to discuss possible retaliatory actions. At the end of those meetings, he and 12 premiers released a statement pledging to present a united front to their American counterparts.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith opted out, saying she could not support any retaliation plan that included oil and gas.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Monday the tariff threat still needs to be taken seriously, despite the reports suggesting Trump will hold off.
"He's just adding more uncertainty, (saying) 'I may not do it today, I'll do it tomorrow. Maybe I'll do it in a month.'That doesn't help," Ford said, according to a CityNews report. "So we're going to take him serious."
Broad tariffs were among one of Trump's campaign tariffs but a new poll suggests there is limited support for them among Americans.
An Associated Press-NORC Center for Public Affairs Research survey found almost half of U.S. adults oppose imposing tariffs on all goods brought into the U.S. from other countries - more than were in favour or were neutral on the question.
"I do think, and maybe it's just a hope, that Trump backs off from his most extreme rhetoric, especially on the deportation and tariff fronts," said Marvin Loh, senior macro strategist at State Street Global Markets.
The head of Canada's largest private-sector union said she's skeptical of the reports saying Trump won't proceed with tariffs, given how he imposed tariffs during his first administration.
"No one should be breathing a sigh of relief at all right now," Unifor president Lana Payne said Monday morning.
Payne said the "constant threat of tariffs" undermines investment and constantly puts Canada's economy in danger. She said the solution is to invest in industrial plans for Canadian manufacturing, put supports in place to prepare for job losses and collaborate across political parties, business and organized labour.
"We have to come together in a profound way over the next four years to make sure that we're guarding against any future threat, but also making sure that we've got our act together in Canada," she said. "It's not going to stop here. The demands are going to grow." The Canadian Press, with files from The Associated Press, Bloomberg News

Subject: Political campaigns; Executive orders; Border security; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada

Document 63 of 219

Will Ford call vote over Trump tariffs? Maybe

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-ford-call-vote-over-trump-tariffs-maybe/docview/3156322283/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+Ford+call+vote+over+Trump+tariffs%3F+Maybe&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford might be kinda, possibly thinking about maybe calling an early election, but don't bet on it yet. Yes, Ford has been toying with the idea of an early election since last spring, but he's never been committed to it. Now, though, he may have no choice.
At the end of December, I wrote that despite some top advisers pushing Ford to call an early election this spring, he wasn't sold. He saw the prospect of an early vote as opportunistic and an unnecessary risk that voters may punish him over.
Smartly, I finished the column by saying, "Of course, this being politics, things could change. So, stay tuned."
So what has changed in the past two weeks? The looming threat of devastating tariffs from the incoming Trump administration, which takes office in Washington next week.
Ford believes the tariffs are coming and they will hit the province and the provincial economy hard.
"They're targeting Ontario.
They're targeting good-paying jobs in Ontario, and I'm going to fight with every tool I have in our toolbox to protect Ontario jobs or protect Canadian jobs," Ford said, even if that means spending billions to support companies while the tariffs are in place.
"I think if it comes to it, and we have to spend tens of billions of dollars, we go to the people, let the people decide. It's their money. It's not the government's."
Ford said nothing has been decided and his closest political advisers say he's not ready to push for an early election over the Trump tariffs yet, but he may get there soon.
His opposition leaders at the provincial legislature both made pitches for Ford to work with them rather than call an early election. Speaking in Windsor, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Trump's tariffs are a real threat to Ontario's economy, but that requires everyone to come together.
"We are ready and willing to sit down and talk about anything that it's going to take ... to keep workers employed, to keep people well, and to push back against this threat to Ontario," she said.
"I think it's absolutely critical. I don't think Doug Ford needs to go to the polls to get that."
Speaking to reporters at Queen's Park, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie also called for Ford to drop the idea of an early vote.
"Absolutely," Crombie said when asked if she would support the government spending huge sums to combat the tariffs.
"I've said from the very start, we need to collaborate, and we need to work together. We need a wartime effort."
There is politics at play here on this issue from both parties, but there is also a very serious issue that must be addressed.
Obviously, Ford is well ahead in the polls and going to the voters in February with a vote in March would be advantageous to him. The opposition wouldn't be able to attack Ford on the only issue that matters, the tariffs, at a time when they are all saying money must be spent.
There is also the fact that key Liberal campaigners, including some who helped Crombie win her party's leadership, are occupied with the federal Liberal leadership race.
For the NDP and Liberals, they wouldn't want to go into an election now because they are weak in the polls, don't have as much money as Ford's PCs and wouldn't be able to effectively attack the premier at a moment of unity. They also know if Ford waits until the fixed election date of June 2026 and the tariffs are still in place, the economy will be hurting, making Ford vulnerable.
Politics aside, Ford does have a point that if he's going to spend $80 billion to $100 billion over the next year or so propping up the economy, he should ask the voters.
"That's where we differ," Ford said of himself and the opposition leaders.
Ford said he believes you've got to ask the voters before you spend their money in this way. We will see if voters buy that argument, if we end up with a late winter election. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Leadership; Voters; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Tariffs

Document 64 of 219

Alberta is now the real threat to Canadian unity

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alberta-is-now-real-threat-canadian-unity/docview/3156322216/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta+is+now+the+real+threat+to+Canadian+unity&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For years, it's been assumed the biggest threat to Canadian unity comes from the province of Quebec. It hosted a separatist terrorist movement in the 1960s and is home to the sovereigntist Parti Québécois. It held two referendums on separation, the last of which saw the country hang together by less than one per cent of the vote. Today, the party tops the polls and is threatening to hold another referendum if it takes power next year.
But the game has changed, thanks to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and U.S. president-elect Donald Trump. The biggest threat to the integrity of Canada no longer lies in the east, but in the west - namely, the province of Alberta. A showdown is looming between Edmonton and Ottawa over Canada's upcoming tariff war with the United States - one that will start this week, when provincial premiers meet with the federal government to hash out a "Team Canada" response to Trump's threats.
The battle lines are clear. Over the weekend, Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly said that "everything is on the table" when it comes to countering Trump's tariffs, including withholding the four million barrels of oil Alberta ships south every day.
This caused a backlash from Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, who spent part of the weekend meeting with Trump at his Mar-a-Lago resort. "We won't stand for that and you should never, ever threaten something you cannot do," she said. Smith reminded Joly that western Canadian crude flows to refineries in Ontario and Quebec through Enbridge's Line 5 pipeline, which runs through the U.S. Threats to cut off oil are thus "empty," she added, and would fuel a national unity crisis.
Smith is not wrong. If the federal government isn't careful, it could inflame the "Wexit" movement, which, for the first time, would be feasible, because it could find a willing partner in Washington. Trump thinks it would be great if Canada became America's 51st state. But what parts would he want? Downtown Toronto, with its core of NDP and Liberal voters? The province of Quebec, which speaks a different language? Nope. The prize would be Alberta, with its oil and conservative mindset. And its border with the Northwest Territories. If Trump got Alberta, his next stop would be striking a deal with the N.W.T., then on to neighbouring Yukon, to increase the U.S.'s access to the Arctic and its undersea oil reserves.
And how would Albertans feel about joining the U.S.? In a December poll, 30 per cent said the province would benefit, the highest percentage in the country. Thirty-eight per cent of Albertans also thought the province would be better off as its own country, while only 35 per cent of Quebecers felt the same about their province. A separate poll, released Tuesday, found that when asked if Canada as a whole should join the U.S., 18 per cent of Albertans agree - far from a majority, but the highest percentage in the country.
For this, we can thank Trudeau and Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault. Over the last decade, they've been determined to put Alberta's oil industry out of business. Between their federal carbon tax, incoming emissions cap and general denigration of the resource as dirty and shameful, it's no wonder the West feels disrespected.
It feels like a rerun of Pierre Trudeau's infamous National Energy Program, which caused thousands of Albertans to lose their livelihoods and spawned the bumper sticker, "Let the Eastern Bastards Freeze in the Dark."
But Trudeau the father also was concerned about national unity and would roll over in his grave if his son's legacy was the breakup of Canada. As Trudeau the son prepares to exit stage left, our leadership cannot allow that to happen. Canada needs to present a tough, united front that respects all its provinces.
If we don't hang together, there is a real risk we will break apart. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Decades

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada

Identifier / keyword: years; assumed; biggest; threat; canadian; unity

Title: Alberta is now the real threat to Canadian unity

Document 65 of 219

Can't 'recruit our way out'of dire doctor shortage: MD; North must retain 'existing experienced'staff, health forum hears

Author: Ougler, Jeffrey

Publication info: Ougler, Jeffrey. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cant-recruit-our-way-outof-dire-doctor-shortage/docview/3155461009/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can%27t+%27recruit+our+way+out%27of+dire+doctor+shortage%3A+MD%3B+North+must+retain+%27existing+experienced%27staff%2C+health+forum+hears&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Ougler%2C+Jeffrey&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Northern Ontario is saddled with a "retention crisis" when it comes to keeping health-care professionals here, one which the region will not be able to "recruit our way out."
That was the message from Dr.
Dannica Switzer, one of several health-care professionals, administrators and regional stakeholders, who partook in a Zoom forum Sunday afternoon, Our Healthcare: What We Need.
For example, physicians, nurses and paramedics, will not stay in this underserviced region so long as working conditions remain "unmanageable," she said.
"We're not going to be able to recruit people into it. We need to focus on retaining our existing experienced staff," said Switzer, a panelist on the Algoma-Manitoulin NDP riding association-hosted event.
Switzer knows firsthand.
She worked alongside six other full-time physicians at the Wawa Family Health Team when she began her practice there in 2019. However, that group got whittled down to three by 2023.
The overwhelming strain of providing care to a region that should have a minimum of seven full-time physicians eventually took its toll. Switzer made the difficult decision to close her practice in 2023 and now works as a locum, a physician who does temporary placements in communities.
Between 2021 and 2023, there was a 21-per-cent increase in the number of doctors needed in Northern Ontario, Switzer said. There are currently 1,700 physicians, with some 400 more needed, she added.
"So, we are simply very, very short," she said. "The experienced providers in communities are really the glue that holds everything together. They provide mentorship to junior staff. They do all the administrative work, they know how things work, they're able to share that expertise."
Also, a lack of home-care resources forces patients to remain in hospital.
"The hospital is not the appropriate place (for them)," said Switzer, adding in situations when no nurse is available, patients can't even access end-of-life care at home.
"They're not able to die in their own homes," she said. "These are basic human rights that we are simply losing because of the shortage of home care."
More local health-care administration is needed, not increased centralization.
"Wouldn't it be better if we had a local person who could drop by a patient's house, see them in hospital before discharge, go through the options and have a plan in place," Switzer said.
Fellow panelist, Ontario Health Coalition executive director Natalie Mehra, contends the Ford government is repeatedly underspending the health-care budget, "worsening" the staffing crisis and forcing "unprecedented" emergency and other critical hospital service closures across Ontario. At the same time, the province is shifting hundreds of millions of public dollars into expanding private for-profit health care.
"The fact is that you cannot have a good economy without good, strong public services as well as the private sector," said Mehra, whose group advocates for publicly funded health care while representing more than 500 member organizations and individuals.
"And, in Ontario, they are literally driving health care into the ground in order to privatize it." Ontario has been shouldering the most emergency department closures "of any time in the history of this province" because hospitals do not have adequate funding, Mehra said, adding the lion's share of Ontario hospitals were thrown into deficit last year.
In fact, a feared Sault Area Hospital shortfall was averted last year when confirmation of funding for Bill 124 compensation adjustments came late in the year. For the year ending March 31, SAH reported an overall surplus of $2.1 million.
SAH incurred a $7.6-million deficit when excluding Bill 124 retroactive dollars. The overturning of Bill 124, Protecting a Sustainable Public Sector for Future Generations Act, "significantly" increased hospital costs that fiscal year.
"And it was only something like 15 days before the end of the financial year that they actually gave the increase in hospital funding," Mehra said. "Too late to hire staff. Too late to keep the emergency departments and other services open that had been closed. But in time for them to be able to claim that they gave a big increase to hospitals."
The province disputes this, and contends Ontario leads the country with the highest number of people attached to a primary care provider, the largest physician workforce, and the highest compensation rates, Ministry of Health spokesperson Hannah Jensen told The Sault Star Monday.
"The facts speak for themselves," Jensen said. "Ontario is proud to have one of the largest publicly funded health-care systems in the world.
"While the NDP has voted against every single investment our government has made in health care, our government will continue to take historic action to connect people to the care they need, when and where they need it."
This year's Canadian Resident Matching Service (CaRMs) also shows students want to become doctors in Ontario, and that doctors want to practise in Ontario with 100 per cent of Ontario's residency positions filled, the ministry said.
Since 2018, the province says it has added 15,000 new doctors and has launched the largest medical school expansion in 15 years, including adding 44 new undergraduate and 63 new residency positions at NOSM University, expanded the Learn and Stay Grant to support 1,360 students studying family medicine and created the Practice Ready Ontario program to 100 new family doctors in rural and Northern communities.
Al Dupuis, Ontario Health Coalition's Algoma District representative, spoke of the "terrible gap" in which the Ford government funds Ontario health care at the lowest rate across Canada, with per-person public sector health spending $821 less than the average of other provinces. That equates to a $12.3 billion funding shortfall across the Ontario population, OHC claims.
"People don't have to be living under these circumstances," he told the nearly two-hour forum.
Lady Dunn Health Centre CEO Kadean Ogilvie said that, in the past, hospitals were often under the assumption that "we'd always be saved in anything."
"If we had a group in the community that required whatever they required, we could meet that need because we would always be in a position to talk with our funders to share what was happening and we would be, for lack of a better term, bailed out," said the panelist.
Hospitals have been "awakened" to the fact that no longer holds true, Ogilvie said.
Approximately 90 per cent of the 28-bed Wawa hospital's funding is provided by the Ministry of Health through Ontario Health and the remaining 10 per cent comes from patient revenues and other supports. Some 75 per cent of funding supports salaries and benefits, the other 25 per cent goes to resources needed to serve the community.
Ogilvie said she would like to see the current three-to four-per-cent funding increases bump up to 10 to 20 per cent.
"That would really give us robust financial backing to do the work that we need to do," she said, adding a health system review for small hospitals would be handy to determine roles and funding.
Sue Switzer, chair of Lady Dunn Health Centre Foundation, said the public does not realize the "difficult" task of "constantly begging" for money, often to purchase "necessary" capital equipment.
"The foundations used to go out and they'd get extra things," said the Wawa senior and Dr. Dannica Switzer's mother. "It wasn't something crucial like a new ultrasound machine because the other one is going to die at any moment.
"You get doors slammed in your face or people say, 'Well, that doesn't really affect me.'That's until you get to the hospital, and you don't have that piece of equipment." St. Joseph Township Mayor Jody Wildman said municipalities are increasingly "picking up the pieces" for health-care.
"This is becoming a real problem because we don't have the capacity to fund a lot of these things," said Wildman, also a member of the Board of Health, Algoma Public Health's governing body.
The provincial government is not being held to account enough on the health-care portfolio, especially with an election likely on the horizon, Wildman said.
"The goal it seems, likely behind it, when you've got a government that doesn't really believe in public services is that you starve those services so that people start questioning whether they're valuable or not and start to consider things like public clinics, fee for service ... all those sorts of things that put us on that slope down to eating away at the system," he said.
Lise Vaugeois, MPP for Thunder Bay-Superior North, agreed that with an election call likely by early February, health woes must be brought to the front-burner pronto.
She cited wage parity as paramount, with gaps not necessarily dictated by experience or skill but by workplace.
Senior health-care workers training new staff, who are receiving incentive bonuses, creates a "real conflict and a deep feeling of being exploited for the people with experience. And it's not surprising that they don't stay," Vaugeois said.
Mike Mercieca, deputy mayor of Sables-Spanish Rivers, some 70 kilometres west of Sudbury, said two full-time MDs in Massey have given notice they are working only part-time as of March. A nurse practitioner has been recruited.
"Decreasing workloads and having support would mean better retention," he said. "I see that as a challenge in the North."
Donald Corbett, a retired federal civil servant, said he is concerned about the current political climate and what means for the health of universal health care.
"What scares the hell out of me is that we're in a very right-wing frame of mind nationally," said the Sault Ste. Marie resident.
"The (provincial) PCs are going to say, 'Don't worry. Be happy. It's all covered. If you need some help, just go to your local pharmacy and they'll give you all the help you need.'"Let's face it, we're dealing with a right-wing mentality, which is simple, simple, simple. 'I don't give a damn about anybody else. I just want it to be OK for me. I'm not sick so I don't give a damn about health care.'This is the environment that we're in." jougler@postmedia.com On X: @JeffreyOugler

Subject: Emergency medical care; Public services; Hospital costs; Health care; Funding; Public sector; Physicians; Northern communities

Business indexing term: Subject: Public sector Physicians

Company / organization: Name: Ontario Health Coalition; NAICS: 813319

Document 66 of 219

Barring Alberta oil exports to U.S. would lead to 'national unity crisis': Smith

Author: Dawson, Tyler

Publication info: Dawson, Tyler. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/barring-alberta-oil-exports-u-s-would-lead/docview/3155460999/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Barring+Alberta+oil+exports+to+U.S.+would+lead+to+%27national+unity+crisis%27%3A+Smith&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=Dawson%2C+Tyler&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith says that if the federal government imposes an export ban on Alberta oil going to the United States as a retaliatory measure against expected U.S. tariffs it would precipitate a "national unity crisis."
"Oil and gas is owned by the provinces, principally Alberta, and we won't stand for that," Smith said Monday. "I can't predict what Albertans would do."
She warned that Ottawa would have a "national unity crisis on (its) hands at the same time as having a crisis with our U.S. partners."
Smith made the remarks during a virtual press conference with reporters, which followed her meetings with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump at his West Palm Beach Mar-a-Lago resort and his golf club over the weekend.
Mélanie Joly, Canada's foreign affairs minister, was asked on CTV's Question Period talk show on Sunday if Canada would consider cutting off energy to the United States in the event of a tariff war, and responded, "Everything is on the table." Canada is preparing a list of U.S. products against which surtaxes could be levied, should Trump bring in his promised 25-per-cent tariff on Canadian goods after he's inaugurated next week. Canada made similar moves during negotiations in 2019 with the first Trump administration, adding more than $16 billion in surtaxes to a variety of American imports, including steel, aluminum, yogurt, toilet paper, dishwashers and various other items.
Joly's response echoes a pitch made by Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who threatened in mid-December to cut off energy supply to the northeastern United States, should the Trump tariffs become a reality. Smith has staunchly opposed such a measure but said Monday morning that if Trump imposes tariffs, there will need to be a Canadian response.
"We have to wait and see what he does, and then we have to have a measured and mature response to it that hopefully does not damage the relationship or damage Canadian consumers and Canadian businesses too much," Smith said.
Smith, who said she attended Trump's Saturday soirée as a guest of celebrity investor and former Conservative party leadership candidate Kevin O'Leary, described her conversations with Trump as "constructive," but also seemed resigned that, come Jan. 20, Trump will be going ahead with tariffs, regardless of what Canadian politicians do. She said that she is "not expecting" Alberta oil to be exempt from the Trump administration's tariffs, although she remains hopeful that the United States will want to purchase more oil from Canada for its refineries in the Midwest.
"I haven't seen any indication in any of the president's public commentary, or even in the comments that he had with me, that he's inclined to change his approach," Smith said.
The Alberta premier has been on an extensive hearts-and-minds campaign in the United States, meeting with governors and appearing on conservative-leaning news programs. She has been making the case directly that U.S. tariffs would increase energy costs to American consumers. She has, however, faced criticism that by boosting Alberta she may be undermining the broader Canadian response to tariffs.
"I'm hoping that by talking about the importance of energy, that we'll be able to avoid tariffs on every Canadian product," said Smith.
Since Trump first made threats of tariffs on Canadian exports, which would end more than three decades of free trade between the two countries, Canadian provinces and the federal government have scrambled to respond.
Trump has spoken of a "trade deficit" with Canada, and claimed that the United States is subsidizing Canada. He also suggested the tariffs were a pressure tactic to stop the flow of illegal migrants and drugs into the United States. Smith announced a $29-million border security plan, which would see a team of sheriffs patrolling Alberta's border with Montana.
In recent weeks, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has met with Trump at Mar-a-Lago, and a deputation of cabinet ministers has also travelled to Florida to meet with the incoming president's transition team. So far, there has been little indication that Trump has been persuaded to abandon his plan for tariffs, and it's all occurring against the backdrop of a domestic political crisis in Ottawa.
Last Monday, Trudeau announced that he would be resigning as prime minister, kicking off a race for the leadership of the Liberal party and proroguing Parliament until March 24. Smith has called for an immediate election to avoid what on Monday she called the "leadership vacuum" in Ottawa.
"We need somebody at the table who is able to engage with the (Trump) administration saying, 'You got a four year mandate. I got a four year mandate, let's deal.'And we don't have that right now," Smith said.
Canada's premiers and representatives from the federal government are meeting on Wednesday to discuss the tariff response. As well, a joint mission of provincial premiers will travel to Washington, D.C., on Feb. 12 to meet with Trump administration officials and talk trade. The Canadian economy, being orders of magnitude smaller than the United States', will be far more harmed by a prolonged trade war than the American economy will be, Smith said.
Research indicates that Canada would see tens of thousands of job losses, contractions in the country's GDP and, in the case of retaliatory tariffs, an increase in the cost of purchasing American-made goods.
"I think the solution is that we find ways to buy more American goods," said Smith. "We know the president's opening position, we've got to try to find a way to dig down and get to a resolution. And I think that will have to happen in earnest once the inauguration happens on Jan. 20."

Subject: International trade; Political campaigns; Presidents; Surtax; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Document 67 of 219

This was the moment Trudeau told us he was done

Author: Kinsella, Warren

Publication info: Kinsella, Warren. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/this-was-moment-trudeau-told-us-he-done/docview/3152310432/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+was+the+moment+Trudeau+told+us+he+was+done&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kinsella%2C+Warren&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau didn't decide to resign this past week, sources say. The decision was made weeks ago, in plain view.
And few noticed. It happened Dec. 18. On that date, Trudeau's PMO abruptly cancelled interviews with multiple media outlets: Global News, CBC, CTV, Radio-Canada, TVA Nouvelles, along with what was to be a joint interview with CityNews and Omni Television. The move was unprecedented.
Few Canadians understood the significance of that decision. But senior Liberals knew it almost certainly meant the government end of Trudeau's reign had arrived.
One very senior Liberal, who has years of experience with different prime ministers and their offices, said that is the moment when Trudeau had truly decided to go. "No leader has cancelled year-end interviews, ever," said the Liberal. "They're a tradition. They're important, because lots of Canadians watch them. And Justin cancelled."
Trudeau's behaviour in the days that followed did nothing to alter that decision, sources said. Trudeau made some canned remarks at the Liberal Christmas party on the Hill, and then just a few words to the media after he shuffled his cabinet for the last time. He then got on a Challenger jet and flew to British Columbia to ski.
Few, if any, heard from Trudeau during the crucial days when he needed to be working the phones to save his leadership. Trudeau mainly communicated only with the small circle who remain loyal to him, and with family members. Some urged him to stay and fight.
But Trudeau had no fight left in him.
Everywhere he looked, sources said, his prospects were bad and getting worse. President-elect Donald Trump was mocking him on the world stage, calling him the governor of the 51st state. Caucus members - including the crucial Atlantic, Quebec and Ontario caucuses - started to demand publicly that he step aside. And an Angus Reid Institute poll was issued, suggesting the Liberals had fallen to only 16 per cent support nationally.
That's not all: Liberal leadership campaigns started organizing, more or less openly, and talking to the media - anonymously, of course - about their prospects in the post-Trudeau era. Among them was Dominic LeBlanc, who Trudeau considers one of his closest friends, and Chrystia Freeland, the former deputy prime minister who had dramatically quit Trudeau's cabinet two days before he cancelled his year-end interviews.
"He's human, you know," said another Liberal insider. "He was down. He asked his circle (of advisers) if there was any way to hold on and avoid a full caucus revolt. They came up with nothing."
"It had nothing to do with (Conservative Leader Pierre) Poilievre," said one Liberal. "He still thinks that Poilievre is a pipsqueak."
But the parliamentary holiday recess was like bankruptcy, another Liberal said. The process of unravelling is very slow, and then it suddenly reaches its grim conclusion very, very fast.
"He knew it was all over," said the senior Liberal. "His kids were saying to him, 'Dad, it's time to go home.'" Don't now expect an avalanche of patronage appointments, the sources agree. That was the main factor that defeated former Liberal leader John Turner in 1984. Liberals have been mad enough at Trudeau as it is, they say, and he doesn't want to make them any angrier.
As for the future, Trudeau is not worried.
His family's wealth have made him a multimillionaire, and he will be paid handsomely on the book and speech circuit. He has no shortage of job prospects, either. Some suggest a soft landing at the United Nations, but other sources doubt that.
Whatever happens, Justin Trudeau has finally taken what his father memorably called a "walk in the snow." He loved the job of prime minister, and he felt he was doing right by Canadians.
But Canadians clearly felt otherwise. And in mid-December, when no one was really looking, Justin Trudeau finally took note, and made his decision. Warren Kinsella is a Canadian lawyer, author, musician, political consultant, and commentator.

Subject: Leadership; Canadians; Prime ministers

Document 68 of 219

'The silence is deafening'; More Liberal MPs are demanding Justin Trudeau make a decision

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/silence-is-deafening-more-liberal-mps-are/docview/3151475819/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27The+silence+is+deafening%27%3B+More+Liberal+MPs+are+demanding+Justin+Trudeau+make+a+decision&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2025-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Prime Minister Justin Trudeau returning from vacation on Friday, some Liberal MPs say the time has arrived for him to face Canadians and say whether or not he intends to resign.
Trudeau has spent the past two weeks since former finance minister Chrystia Freeland's stunning resignation largely out of the public eye, at first staying behind closed doors and then setting off on his annual Christmas vacation, this time to B.C. Trudeau had said he would use the time to reflect on his future.
In speaking to fellow Liberals since Parliament broke for its holiday recess, Helena Jaczek, an Ontario MP who has called for Trudeau to quit, said what has become noticeable now is his silence.
"We want to keep our party together, and at this point, we're not hearing from the prime minister," she told Postmedia in an interview.
"I think everyone understood the need for some reflection, but it is what, more than two weeks now since Chrystia Freeland resigned. And so what I'm hearing that's a little different is, 'well the silence is deafening. Where's the leadership in a time of crisis?'" Another Ontario Liberal MP, who spoke on the condition of anonymity, said the calls for to Trudeau to resign are coming from every corner, whether it's from residents who are not usually close followers of politics or "dyed-inthe-wool Liberals."
That MP said time is running out before others in caucus get prepared to add their name to the roster of roughly 20 Liberal MPs who have gone public with their desire for Trudeau to resign, adding the fact that the prime minister has yet to make a decision "is almost as bad" as the blow Freeland dealt by resigning on the day she was set to deliver the government's fiscal update, announcing her move publicly in a scathing letter.
"This isn't just about Liberal political intrigue," the MP said. "Canadians are looking at a situation where they genuinely have a concern about who will be leading the country and what the plan is going forward on confronting the Trump threat."
Regional caucus chairs were set to meet on Friday to discuss the requests to hold a national caucus meeting.
Of the 51 out of 75 Ontario Liberal MPs who met after the House of Commons broke for the holidays, most said that Trudeau should resign - a message that Jaczek says has been communicated to the Prime Minister's Office, citing information from the group's chair. Ontario Liberal caucus chair Michael Coteau had yet to respond to a request for comment from Postmedia.
"As has already been widely reported, there was a consensus. Obviously, that's not unanimity, but there was a consensus that the prime minister should resign," said Jaczek.
A Quebec Liberal MP confirmed the sentiment that Trudeau must go is also shared by members within its provincial caucus, although its chair, Stéphane Lauzon, clarified in a statement posted to X that no letter has been signed or circulated by its caucus since they last met on Dec. 11, which was several days before Freeland resigned.
New Brunswick MP Wayne Long shared the letter sent by the Atlantic Liberal caucus chair, Nova Scotia MP Kody Blois, asking that Trudeau step aside, telling Postmedia that caucus members feel "disillusioned" that the prime minister has not yet responded to the growing calls.
"People are beyond mad now," Long says, "I think they're just really sad that the prime minister and in particular (the Prime Minister's Office) and those around him have allowed this to happen."
"Good leadership, of course, should have a lot of reflection, but good leadership also acknowledges when his caucus and, more importantly, Canadians, want to hear from him."
Long, who was one of the earliest to publicly call for Trudeau to quit following the party's surprise byelection loss in the former Liberal stronghold of Toronto-St. Paul's last June, said he expects Trudeau ultimately will resign.
"There is no other option," he said. "It's time to get out of fantasyland and get back to reality and reality clearly states that the prime minister's time is up."
Other Liberal MPs believe he will do the same, including B.C. MP Ken Hardie who said he believes Trudeau will do so "for the good of the country," as the Liberals enter an election year where they are set to face Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who has been riding high in public opinion polls since summer 2023, while Trudeau has been unsuccessful in his attempts to gain back any favour among Canadians.
Hardie pointed to a retreat the Liberal caucus had that year in London, Ont., where MPs "were assured at that time that there was a path forward."
"The leader and the Prime Minister's Office, they're pretty smart people so (we) said, 'OK, fine, let's see what that path forward is.'Well, so far we haven't seen it."
While Hardie said he believes Trudeau deserves to take the time he needs to reflect on his next steps, "the sooner the better" it would be to present a plan to Canadians.
Taleeb Noormohamed, who chairs the B.C. caucus, confirmed it met on Dec. 23 and "had a very healthy discussion about the future of the party," which remains confidential, but added those views have been communicated to the Prime Minister's Office.
On Thursday, Toronto MP and former cabinet minister Marco Mendicino added his name to the list of MPs who are not seeking re-election. Others include Nova Scotia MP Sean Fraser, who previously served as housing minister.
In a statement shared on the platform X, Mendicino did not address Trudeau's leadership, but noted how he disagreed with the Liberal government's foreign policy direction, particularly when it comes to its relationship to Israel, which he said had "deteriorated."
"In political parties, there must be room for different views." staylor@postmedia.com

Document 69 of 219

Trudeau insulted Trump for years, now their roles are reversed

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-insulted-trump-years-now-their-roles-are/docview/3150440691/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+insulted+Trump+for+years%2C+now+their+roles+are+reversed&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-12-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's funny to watch Canadians in the commentariat class clutch their pearls at Donald Trump's insults aimed at Justin Trudeau. Not because the insults and online trolling are funny themselves, but because it comes after two years of Trudeau insulting Trump for his own political gain.
Trudeau has denounced Trump's MAGA movement as a threat to Canada, as dangerous, as something to be avoided, and has tried to paint his main opponent, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, as a "maple-syrup MAGA Conservative."
Since 2015, when Trump announced his bid to Make America Great Again (MAGA) by seeking the U.S. presidency, the term MAGA has been used in Canada's Parliament 127 times. Of those, 122 have been since the start of the 2023 fall session of the House of Commons and 115 of them have been by Liberal MPs.
In the Commons, Trudeau has accused the Poilievre Conservatives of adopting MAGA policies on Ukraine, housing and climate change. He's also taken shots at the MAGA movement in news conferences and media interviews.
"What we're seeing from these MAGA Conservatives is an approach on going back on fundamental rights in ways we shouldn't be seeing," Trudeau told The Canadian Press in a 2023 year-end interview.
He also tried to link the U.S. Supreme Court decision on Roe v Wade to the MAGA movement and Canada's Conservatives.
"We may think to ourselves, 'This will never happen in Canada and this is just the Liberals bringing up the usual fear that they do.'I'm sorry, it wasn't ever supposed to happen in the United States, either, and yet it did because of MAGA conservatism. The threat is real," Trudeau said.
That was Trudeau taking shots at MAGA and Trump on Canadian soil to try to score points with voters in this country. Unfortunately, he also took shots at Trump on U.S. soil.
In April 2023, Trudeau was speaking to the Council on Foreign Relations in New York when he took a veiled shot at Trump "You guys are the greatest democracy in the world," Trudeau said. "Right now, it's not just that it's being taken for granted by so many citizens, it's actually being devalued."
He took more shots at Trump, as reported by Reuters, while also praising Joe Biden's time as president. Biden took several hardline protectionist stances in areas like softwood lumber and the auto industry, and cancelling the Keystone XL project on his first day in office, but Trudeau never criticized Biden the same way.
About two weeks ago, Trudeau lamented that U.S. voters rejected Kamala Harris and chose Trump as a failure of feminism - a move noticed in Washington and by those near Trump.
Chrystia Freeland, until recently Trudeau's deputy PM and finance minister and the woman Trudeau wanted to put in charge of Canada-U.S. relations, had a habit of calling Poilievre's party "MAGA maple syrup Conservatives."
It wasn't just columnists like me warning Trudeau this was a bad tactic in case Trump won a second term.
David MacNaughton, 2015 Liberal Ontario campaign co-chair and ambassador to Washington from 2016-19 advised the Trudeau government, via the Toronto Star in February, to avoid blow-ups in the Canada-U.S. relationship.
"We need to be seen by the Americans as a trusted friend, ally, partner and, you know, right now, I don't think that feeling is as strong as it has been," he said.
They didn't listen to me or people like Mac-Naughton. Instead, Trudeau spent the last two years campaigning against Trump and MAGA and trying to link both to Poilievre's Conservatives in a bid to boost Grit poll numbers.
It hasn't worked. Instead, Liberal support among voters has fallen further.
But team Trump noticed what Trudeau has been doing and now are responding in kind. Yet, while Trudeau's power base and popularity have shrunk, Trump's power base is growing.
Trudeau has put Canada in a difficult position due to his shameless politicking and foolish actions. He will surely lose his job at the next election - how many Canadians, though, will lose their jobs because of Trudeau's stupidity before then? Sadly, it won't just be his cabinet ministers.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Political campaigns; Voters; International relations

Business indexing term: Subject: International relations

Location: United States--US; Canada

Document 70 of 219

Canadians ready to move on, Trudeau won't go

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadians-ready-move-on-trudeau-wont-go/docview/3149807003/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+ready+to+move+on%2C+Trudeau+won%27t+go&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-12-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau was off to B.C. for a ski vacation after spending Christmas in Ottawa. He shouldn't expect a warm welcome. In fact, as the latest Leger Poll shows, there are few places Trudeau would get a warm welcome because Canadians are done with him.
You've seen the numbers, 72 per cent of Canadians are dissatisfied with his government, 69 per cent think he should resign, only 11 per cent think he'd make the best prime minister, with 13 per cent picking Jagmeet Singh and 31 per cent choosing Pierre Poilievre. In party voting intentions, the Liberals are at 20 per cent nationally just ahead of the NDP at 19 per cent and far behind the Conservatives at 43 per cent.
Trudeau's Liberals are now third in Quebec, Alberta and B.C. and if Leger didn't measure Manitoba and Saskatchewan as one group, you could likely add Saskatchewan to that group. Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are at 50 per cent popular support or higher in Ontario, Manitoba, Saskatchewan, Alberta and above 40 per cent support in B.C. and Atlantic Canada.
Poilievre's Conservatives have a commanding lead in every demographic group looking at gender, age, or location. Any Liberal strategist or campaign worker knows these numbers spell disaster when the election comes.
And yet, Trudeau hangs on. Why wouldn't he think that, given how many times Canadians have forgiven him? Trudeau has been forgiven for interfering in criminal prosecutions to help a Quebec firm, for dismissing women who disagree with him, for groping a woman, for wearing blackface, for weaponizing vaccinations after vowing not to, for dividing Canadians while preaching unity.
If you're a trust fund baby who has never had to play by the same rules as anyone else, who has survived the scandals Trudeau has - and there are many more than I've listed - why wouldn't you think you could survive the internal party struggle now underway? So what if he lost a cabinet minister, who issued a scathing public letter denouncing his feckless policies as she quit. As Trudeau showed, there were enough greasy pole climbers in his caucus willing to look past his flaws to take up new titles and positions.
One of them, Nathaniel Erskine-Smith, Liberal MP for Toronto's Beaches East-York riding, went from being an outspoken critic of Trudeau, to a minister, to a secret critic of Trudeau with a whiplash inducing speed.
If he loses a minister, he'll find another.
He's probably also thinking that despite Jagmeet Singh's rhetoric, the NDP leader will back the Liberals with the right bribe when the time comes. If not, there is plenty of chatter the Liberals will simply prorogue Parliament to avoid any confidence votes in February or May.
Sure, Trudeau denounced prorogation as an opposition MP, but he's used it effectively in the past including to shut down committee hearings into the WE Charity scandal in 2020 and he will use it again to save his political life.
That's the entire focus of this government right now: protecting Justin Trudeau.
Canadians are ready to move on, Canada desperately needs new leadership at a time of crisis, but Trudeau and his enablers are doing all they can to cling to power. blilley@postmedia.com

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Manitoba Canada; Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Singh, Jagmeet; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 71 of 219

Dealing with derelict hospital property council's top triumph

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dealing-with-derelict-hospital-property-councils/docview/3147828097/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dealing+with+derelict+hospital+property+council%27s+top+triumph&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-12-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It didn't take Mayor Matthew Shoemaker long to identify what he believes was city council's greatest achievement in 2024.
"The hospital," he replied before the question was even completed.
"It took the whole year to get it done, from the time the process was started in February or March with negotiations, then getting the direction of council, making the final decision in late fall and awarding the RFP," he rhymed off.
I agree. It's my top choice for council's greatest achievement, as well.
Renewal, rejuvenation and growth, are always exciting.
But even more so for this project, given the length of time it has taken to get to this point.
It's been over a decade since the first developer purchased the property and not a thing has been done to it. Correction. It's been left vacant, entered, vandalized and crumbled to the point of no return.
I said it before, and I'll say it again: Mayor Matthew Shoemaker and city council get kudos for their innovative approach to get something done to a property on the city's waterfront that has been nothing more than an eyesore for the past decade.
Only one thing could be better, Shoemaker quipped. And that would be having the derelict building torn down prior to year-end.
While that may not happen, we can hope the final paperwork to be completed by year-end, paving the way for demolition to begin early in 2025, something many Saultites will look forward to in the new year.
Just as important as the council's greatest achievement, is its disappointment or failure.
There is no doubt that the failure to have a supervised safe consumption site established in the city tops that list.
And while city council and staff were preparing an application to do just that, their efforts were cut short in August when Premier Doug Ford announced that the province would not approve or fund any more safe consumption sites and, in fact, would be scaling them back - 10 in fact.
Instead, the Ontario government announced it would replace supervised consumption sites with HART Hubs, whose applications are being vetted and approved. Sault Ste. Marie's bid is among those in that process and city council continues to keep its fingers crossed that it will be one of the most successful applications.
While city council can't be faulted for failing to establish a safe consumption site - the option was pulled from its control - Shoemaker muses if council should have tried to move more quickly to launch a site without funding authorization by the province.
I doubt that would have been possible.
And we can't fault city council for its lobby efforts to higher levels of government to do more to help solve the mental health and addictions crisis. We can lay blame on the higher levels of government for simply not doing enough - for Sault Ste. Marie and communities across the country - as the crisis worsens.
So, it is no doubt that one of the mayor's top priorities for 2025 is to secure HART Hub funding, establish a Hub and work at reducing the crisis.
Shoemaker said he has a second priority. He anticipates that there will be both a federal and provincial election in 2025 and said it is critical to the municipality and city council to develop a good working relationship with those elected to those positions to ensure the community gets the support it needs for its priority projects.
"I'll be watching eagerly," he said.
There were several other noteworthy items that council dealt with this year.
It approved a vacant home tax, a way to take action on derelict property owners who do not take proper care of their own property and impact the enjoyment of properties from other neighbours.
Council has also heavily invested in the revitalization of the downtown, specifically with millions of dollars of upgrades recently completed in the first phase of the renewal of Queen Street. Further work will be done in 2026.
Progress will also be noted on the Gateway site with the approval and final unveiling of the next phase of development on the former fish hatchery and Mill Market site.
A renewed spark in the Port of Algoma project has also been launched, something we will likely hear more about in the coming year.
Council also lent their support to non-profits.
It provided a new lease on life for Harvest Algoma, where operations have been transferred to the Sault Ste. Marie Innovation Centre.
It also backstopped the lease agreement between the Sault Ste. Marie YMCA and the Dr. Lou and Mae Lukenda Foundation, which purchased the McNabb Street building.
The end of 2024 marks the half-way mark for city council's four-year term. Elaine Della-Mattia is The Sault Star's political reporter.

Document 72 of 219

Vezeau-Allen 1 of 4 seeking nod to carry federal Sault- Algoma NDP banner

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/vezeau-allen-1-4-seeking-nod-carry-federal-sault/docview/3147272427/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Vezeau-Allen+1+of+4+seeking+nod+to+carry+federal+Sault-+Algoma+NDP+banner&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-12-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: City councillor Lisa Vezeau-Allen is seeking the federal New Democrat nomination for the Sault Ste. Marie-Algoma riding.
She confirmed Tuesday to The Sault Star that she is one of four individuals who have completed candidate packages and being vetted by the national NDP office to carry the banner in the next federal election.
The election may occur sooner than expected after news this week that deputy prime minister and finance minister Chrystia Freeland resigned from cabinet, a significant blow to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberal government.
Luis Moreno, president of the Sault Ste. Marie-Algoma NDP Riding Association, said the association began inviting members in October to come forward if they are interested in seeking the nomination.
Six candidates did come forward and four of those have completed the candidate packages and are currently being vetted by the national office, he said.
Of the four candidates, two are in Sault Ste. Marie, one in Blind River and one in Thessalon. Two are women, and two are men.
He could not disclose the name of the other candidates until they come forward on their own.
Vezeau-Allen said seeking the nomination is something she's always considered. She was approached years ago to do so, but said the timing was not right with a young family.
Vezeau-Allen as served on the provincial riding association in the past and worked on former NDP MP Tony Martin's last federal campaign.
"Right now, the timing is right for me," she said.
The second-term Ward 2 city councillor said the new expanded riding that covers Sault Ste. Marie and much of Algoma to the north and east of the Sault has a large diverse population, diverse culture and different needs, all of which she recognizes.
Vezeau-Allen said she is interacting with key counterparts on issues that affect different parts of the riding and is a member of the Northern Women's Conference, which networks among the smaller communities in the area. She said she knows the top issues and differences between communities.
"I'm also caring and an empathetic listener," she said.
Vezeau-Allen said she also has government experience as a city councillor and understands how each level of government works, how it intertwines and gaps in funding.
Vezeau-Allen has vast experience in the not-for-profit sector. She has also worked with area First Nations, has been a member of the District Social Services Administration Board, understands economic development and has worked with Autism Ontario, among others.
"I have the energy and experience, and I can be a strong voice for the North," she said. "I will take what I've learned in my many roles and what each community needs and work to make it happen," she said.
Moreno said he's hoping the national NDP office will have vetting of the riding association's candidates completed by year-end.
The local association wants to launch a campaign to grow its membership and fundraise for the next federal election.
Policy dictates that 30 days' notice must be provided to members before a nomination meeting is held, but that may need to be changed if a speedy election is called, he said. The association's current plans are to hold a nomination meeting in late February.
"I'm extremely happy with the movement and the process we are conducting," he said. "Our association is growing, all our executive positions are filled, and we have engagement from across the riding."
Moreno said he believes there is growing discontent with the federal Liberal government and even more people who don't want to see a Conservative government in office.
Speculation is rising that the Liberal government could fall in light of Freeland's announcement earlier this week. It's expected a cabinet shuffle will also occur to fill vacancies on the growing number of Liberal ministers who have said they will not seek re-election.
The next federal election is slated for October 2025, but under a minority government, it could occur sooner.
