Document 73 of 219

No-nonsense Ford ready to rumble with Trump

Author: Bell, Rick

Publication info: Bell, Rick. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-nonsense-ford-ready-rumble-with-trump/docview/3145920150/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No-nonsense+Ford+ready+to+rumble+with+Trump&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Bell%2C+Rick&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two can play this game.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford looked like he had it up to here pussyfooting around with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump and the man's threats against Canada.
He obviously was not willing to stick to talking points coated in generalities. You know, we're going to have do something, but we're not saying what.
It sounded like Ford wanted to get down to the meat and potatoes, the bread and butter.
And when he spoke, Ford couldn't have said what he had to say more directly. Pull no punches would be the apt description.
Ford wants Trump to know Ontario, Canada's largest province, will go to the wall against a 25 per cent tariff charge on Canadian goods heading stateside.
Ford says he will "go to the full extent," if necessary.
What does that mean? "We will go to the extent of cutting off their energy going down to Michigan, going down to New York state and over to Wisconsin."
Say what?! You heard him right. Ford adds he doesn't want this showdown to happen. He doesn't want some cross-border game of chicken. He would rather skip this real-life high-stakes political poker.
He considers Americans our friends. He has good relationships with people in the U.S.
But the Ontario premier is also 100 per cent certain Trump will slam Canada with tariffs when he takes over as president.
He is asked if Trump is all bluster. Ford does not hesitate to say no.
So now he sees his job as protecting the people of Ontario and all Canadians.
"We need to be ready. We need to be ready to fight. The fight is coming 100 per cent on Jan. 20 or Jan. 21. 100 per cent these tariffs are coming," says the Ontario premier.
"Let's see what happens as we move forward.
We'll use every tool in our toolbox, including cutting them off energy we're sending down there.
"Folks, this is coming. It's not if it is. It's coming. We need to be prepared."
Just so you're picking up what he's laying down, Ford spells it out.
"We know it's coming," says Ford, of the Trump tariffs.
"Hopefully it's not going to come as hard as we think. If he comes out and he mentions he's going to tariff everything that's a problem.
"We'll use every tool in our toolbox to fight back. We can't sit back and roll over."
Ford does his talking after the virtual gabfest Prime Minister Justin Trudeau had with the country's premiers and there was plenty of talk of beefing up security at the Canada-U.S. border.
He is asked about Trudeau, who said when Trump defeated Kamala Harris in the election there were "regressive" and "reactionary" forces at play and women's rights and progress were under attack "overtly and subtly."
Trudeau's mouthings were brought up a couple of times by premiers.
Why does he become a dime-store analyst of American politics at this time of all times? "It's not helpful at all," says Ford. "Donald Trump was elected democratically.
You like him or you don't like him. That's not our issue. It was not helpful whatsoever."
Chrystia Freeland, the deputy prime minister, is questioned more than once about cutting off Canadian energy to the United States.
Freeland, in her not-willing-to-say-toomuch tone of voice, does mention how a number of premiers want Canada to play tough.
But what about cutting off energy? The deputy PM said some premiers named "critical minerals and metals their provinces produce and are exported to the (U.S.)" So why was Ford talking so tough? He says Trump is now "a little more aggressive." He is "a different type of cat."
One thing is also not in dispute if tariffs come to pass. "Canadians will get hurt but I assure you of one thing. The Americans are going to feel the pain as well." Rick Bell is a Calgary Herald columnist

Document 74 of 219

Northern Ontario facing paramedic crisis, Nickel Belt MPP says; NDP's Health critic says it's a problem across Ontario, but more acute in North

Author: Clutchey, Carl

Publication info: Clutchey, Carl. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/northern-ontario-facing-paramedic-crisis-nickel/docview/3142728633/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Northern+Ontario+facing+paramedic+crisis%2C+Nickel+Belt+MPP+says%3B+NDP%27s+Health+critic+says+it%27s+a+problem+across+Ontario%2C+but+more+acute+in+North&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-12-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Clutchey%2C+Carl&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province should create an all-party task force to try and solve a chronic paramedic shortage that has hit remote Northern communities particularly hard, union and NDP officials say.
NDP health critic and Sudbury-area MPP France Gelinas said on Wednesday the task force would be directed to come up with advice in a year's time to find ways to boost service funding, training, recruitment and retention.
"We need to act now," said Gelinas, noting many Ontarians have been waiting too long for ambulances to arrive due to the paramedic shortage.
The call for a task force follows a request earlier this fall by Northwestern Ontario municipal leaders for strapped Northern land-ambulance services to be fully funded by the province.
Full funding "will allow (Northern) regions to offer competitive salaries and benefits, upgrade outdated equipment and (ambulance) bases, and invest in workplace health and safety programs," Northwestern Ontario Municipal Association president Wendy Landry said in a letter to the Ministry of Health. Landry is also Shuniah's mayor.
In an NDP news release, London, Ont.-area paramedic Stephen Adlington said the staffing shortage "has been considered OK for too long."
"Stress, burnout, addiction and suicide are just seen as occupational hazards," Adlington said. "This ugly reality means fewer paramedics to respond to public emergencies."
Northern ambulance departments like Superior North Emergency Medical Services (EMS) are currently funded by a combination of provincial and municipal money, with the latter component proportionally based on population.
In an earlier response to concerns about the paramedic shortage, a Ministry of Health spokeswoman referenced $910 million the province earmarked this year "to support ... critical operation(s) and to increase the availability of ambulances across the province."
She noted that in 2023, the province increased the number of spaces in Ontario colleges for students wanting to train as paramedics to 300.
Still, many land-ambulance services continue to struggle to fill significant vacancies.
Superior North EMS Chief Shane Muir last month said he could use at least 30 paramedics sooner than later. Part-time positions are the hardest to fill at the moment, Muir said.
Muir said his service, which responds to emergencies in Thunder Bay and surrounding municipalities and First Nations, has about 230 paramedics "on paper."
But Muir said the number actually available to work is less than that due to factors like illness and stress-related leaves. The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government.

Document 75 of 219

Even Liberals running away from carbon tax

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/even-liberals-running-away-carbon-tax/docview/3142727711/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Even+Liberals+running+away+from+carbon+tax&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-12-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon tax has become such a stink bomb to Liberal fortunes everywhere that even Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie is denouncing it.
At a Liberal fundraiser on Tuesday night she was reported to say: "I'm not here to tell the prime minister how to do his job. But I promise you, I will tell him when he's wrong. Like on the carbon tax."
That was in response to months of Ontario Premier Doug Ford and the Progressive Conservatives labelling her "the queen of the Carbon Tax" because of her previous support for it when she was a Liberal MP, plus describing her "as one of the only provincial Liberal leaders in Canada who won't speak out against the carbon tax."
Crombie previously pledged not to introduce a provincial carbon tax if she becomes premier.
Her credibility problem is the history of Ontario and federal Liberals saying one thing and doing another.
During the 2014 Ontario election that brought Kathleen Wynne to power, Wynne said nothing about imposing a provincial carbon tax.
Shortly after her victory, she said she had no plans to introduce a carbon tax Then, she introduced capand-trade.
Prior to the 2019 federal election, then-Liberal environment minister Catherine McKenna said the Liberals wouldn't raise their carbon tax beyond $50 a tonne of emissions in 2022.
After the 2019 election, in which the Liberals were reduced from a majority to a minority government, Trudeau announced he would raise the federal carbon tax every year after 2022 until it reached $170 a tonne of emissions in 2030.
The Trudeau Liberals have claimed the rebate system leaves 80 per cent of families with more money than they pay in carbon taxes.
Independent, non-partisan Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux subsequently pointed out this is true if one considers only the fiscal impact of the carbon tax.
But if you added in its drag on the Canadian economy, plus the fact the Liberal rebate system doesn't return revenue generated by their imposition of the GST on top of the carbon tax to Canadians, 60 per cent of families end up with less money in their pocket.
Trudeau says he introduced his carbon tax as an alternative to less efficient and more expensive ways to reduce emissions such as government regulations and government subsidies.
In reality, there are 149 government programs aimed at reducing emissions.
Finally the Trudeau Liberals said no political considerations went into the creation of their carbon tax.
But Gudie Hutchings, Trudeau's minister for rural economic development and the Atlantic Canada Opportunities Agency, said last year if provinces like Alberta wanted special deals to defray the costs of carbon pricing, as they had just announced for Atlantic Canada, "Perhaps they need to elect more Liberals on the Prairies so that we can have that conversation, as well." lgoldstein@postmedia.com

Document 76 of 219

Doug ford is leading the way; Opinion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/doug-ford-is-leading-way-opinion/docview/3141997133/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+ford+is+leading+the+way%3B+Opinion&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford has had a successful run since being elected in 2018, but perhaps more than anything else, he deserves credit for expanding the subway system in Canada's largest city.
The Ontario Line will introduce nearly 16 kilometres of track to the fastest-growing city in North America, but it wasn't easy. Initially conceived as the Downtown Relief Line, it has been mired in politics and bureaucracy for decades - until Doug Ford came along and made it happen.
Without Ford's initiative, the Ontario Line would still be aspirational rather than under construction, although that seems lost on Toronto's chattering liberal class, for whom swallowing cyanide might be preferable to bestowing even a soupçon of credit upon a Conservative.
The Ontario Line is no doubt a legacy project for a premier who will be remembered for building the most significant infrastructure project the city has seen in decades.
Ford is presently being chided from all angles for his stance on bicycle lanes in Toronto, even though it's one of the most congested cities in the world. It doesn't take an urban planner to figure out that augmenting mass transit, not expanding underused bike lanes (which are used by a mere 1.2 per cent of the residents, according to census data), is the solution.
Nearly two decades after the Ontario Liberals introduced the Places to Grow Act, in part to curtail congestion, the tribulations of navigating Toronto's factory farm-like conditions have exposed kinks in the legislation's armour.
Bloor Street is down to a single lane in either direction along the hyper-dense expanse west of Spadina Avenue. Further to the north, Eglinton Avenue has been saddled with a bike lane that virtually no one uses but has somehow managed to make traffic around the notoriously congested Allen Road intersection even worse than before.
Ford's initiatives have nevertheless done precious little to stem the barrage of criticism he receives on a quotidian basis. And the Toronto Star, which dropped the mitts against the Ford family long ago, is unsurprisingly front and centre.
It recently ran a column accusing Ford of trying to hoodwink voters with trivialities like permitting grocers and convenience stores to sell alcohol. It's a shrill rebuke that reeks of the same patronizing paternalism that's mugged the province's hard-working taxpayers of the same consumer liberty that neighbouring Quebec has enjoyed from what seems like time immemorial.
The Star's petty, low-rent reprimand ignores the fact that Ford is merely adhering to arguably the most salient principle of free-market capitalism: competition between vendors results in lower consumer prices.
The Star must have forgotten that its columnists have also advocated for the exact thing Ford codified into law, having noted in the past that the Beer Store is a foreign-owned "cartel-cum-monopoly" and that its stores feel like "Stalinist relics ... designed by Communist apparatchiks for the beer-swilling lumpenproletariat."
The Star might need a primer on its own founding principles and mission statement, which says that it believes in "individual and civil liberties," and works to "focus public attention on injustices of all kinds." Those "injustices" apparently don't include corporations colluding to pricegouge increasingly immiserated consumers.
The reality is that such free market policies augment razorthin profit margins for entrepreneurs, many of whom pour their life savings into ventures like neighbourhood convenience stores, and in case the chattering liberal class in Toronto hasn't noticed, everything is more expensive.
That includes small businesses'overheads, but alcohol sales will help - and considering that you'd be hard-pressed to find a pint anywhere in Toronto for under $9, Ford's push to increase competition and choice is great news for consumers and businesses alike.
The portion of Highway 401 running through Toronto is North America's busiest and the bane of the city's drivers. Weekday or weekend, day or night, drivers can expect a slow commute.
Ford has proposed building a tunnel below the highway to augment traffic flow. It's an ambitious project that comes with considerable risk - just look at Boston's "Big Dig," which suffered years of delays and billions in cost overruns. Yet Ford's army of media critics have only admonished his idea instead of offering their own solutions to the city's traffic woes.
Ford is a lightning rod for Canadian media, most of which has a liberal bias that perhaps explains why they're more interested in tearing him down than proffering policies of their own. They offer criticism without any real solutions of their own, which reeks of partisanship and does little to further public discourse.
Urban planners and left-wing commentators are fond of gaslighting Canadians by pointing to bike-lane usage in cities such as Beijing, Paris and Amsterdam, where temperatures rarely dip below freezing, and using the "If you build it, they will come" argument.
But the fact remains that Canada does not have the bike culture of a country like China, and no matter how bad traffic gets, the prospect of riding a bicycle through the snow when it's -20 C outside will remain unappealing to most Canadians.
Unfortunately, Ford's critics seem completely devoid of solutions of their own, repeating tired clichés about how bike lanes are the answer to all our problems and building new roads will only invite more traffic.
Toronto fancies itself Canada's answer to Manhattan - and in a way it is, if exorbitant rent and gridlock factor into the equation - but if that dream ever becomes reality, Ford's push to modernize transit and ease congestion will have played an outsized role. Postmedia

Subject: Consumers; Convenience stores; Roads & highways

Business indexing term: Subject: Consumers Convenience stores

Location: Canada; North America

Document 77 of 219

Obeying autocrat in advance a dangerous game

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/obeying-autocrat-advance-dangerous-game/docview/3141996660/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Obeying+autocrat+in+advance+a+dangerous+game&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A lot has happened since historian Timothy Snyder published his concise handbook, On Tyranny: Twenty Lessons from the Twentieth Century. The tiny book exploded in 2017. Today it's more relevant than ever. Among the lessons Snyder draws from our fraught history with authoritarianism, the most important is: Do not obey in advance.
So far, we're doing a terrible job.
When wealthy U.S. newspaper owners decided not to endorse a presidential candidate, they were obeying in advance - avoiding the ire of a grudge-holding nominee, in case he returned to power - thus making it more likely he would.
Seeking to appease an authoritarian by anticipating what he might want, and offering it before he asks, merely conditions us for more and greater concessions, Snyder warns. U.S.
senators who have pledged to support dangerously unqualified cabinet picks, long before confirmation hearings, are simply laying bare their obsequence.
Appallingly, we're doing the same thing in Canada.
A conga line of anticipatory obedience greeted the president-elect's threat of a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian imports, which he threatened as retaliation against the "Invasion" of fentanyl and "illegal aliens." Ontario Premier Doug Ford was first in line with a pre-emptive sacrifice, proposing to cut Mexico out of the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement. Alberta Premier Danielle Smith promptly lined up behind him.
The premiers'clunky attempts to curry favour only show the once and future president how easily he can push us around. More on point was Mexico's former chief free trade negotiator, Kenneth Smith Ramos, who told CBC's Power and Politics killing the trilateral deal would just play into the hands of a leader whose consistent approach to his trading partners is "dividing and conquer."
Meanwhile, federally, Opposition Leader Pierre Poilievre made up a new podium sign with the toadying slogan "fix the broken border." His new rhetorical target, "people who lie to come into our country" (while claiming to love "real" refugees), echoes the exaggerations and deflections of the president-elect. Such eagerness to obey in advance rather takes the air out of his criticism the prime minister returned from dinner at Mara-Lago without winning any concessions.
Plans for the visit, proposed by Trudeau, were hatched quietly behind the scenes. The leaders'dinner conversation touched on trade, border security and shared concern over fentanyl, along with a joke about Canada becoming the "51st state." Ha, ha.
Even less amusingly, on Nov. 29 - the same day Trudeau and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc quietly flew to Florida - Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) announced an immediate "pause" on the private sponsorship of refugees. Claims already submitted will progress through the queue; however, no new sponsorship applications by community groups or groups of five will be accepted until after Dec. 31, 2025.
Of course, this could be a coincidence. IRCC did not cite a change in American leadership as a reason for the change. Rather, it referred to the backlog of applications from private sponsors, which, not surprisingly, exceed the shrinking targets set out in Canada's immigration levels plan.
Still, one wonders why it had to happen so abruptly, extinguishing hope for vulnerable refugees, along with groups of Canadians tirelessly pursuing legal channels to help them to safety. IRCC could have tapered the flow of applications, or given private sponsors a 48-hour window to submit paperwork near completion (as it has done in the past) before the new rules took effect. Instead, the pause was declared effective as of midnight the day before.
Another factor in the decision, IRCC noted, was "stakeholder feedback on potential application intake controls." It could be unrelated, but it sure sounds perilously close to obeying in advance. write.robin@baranyai.ca

Subject: Leadership; Fentanyl; Immigration; Corporate sponsorship

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Corporate sponsorship

Location: Mexico; United States--US; Canada

Identifier / keyword: happened; historian; timothy; snyder; published; concise

Title: Obeying autocrat in advance a dangerous game

Publication title: Sault Star; Sault Ste. Marie, Ont.

First page: A.4

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Dec 7, 2024

Document 78 of 219

Push to generate nuclear power on the moon

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/push-generate-nuclear-power-on-moon/docview/3141293895/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Push+to+generate+nuclear+power+on+the+moon&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-12-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nuclear energy is undergoing a bit of a renaissance in Ontario as the government of Doug Ford looks to expand the energy source for the first time in decades. It's even spurring a push by the private sector to build out on an energy source that is getting a second look.
The moves by the Ford government come as part of an expected surge for electricity demand in the province during the coming years. It's coupled with a desire by the premier to turn Ontario into an energy powerhouse that exports green energy across North America.
During the last several months, the province has announced a massive procurement for new energy sources, promoted the continuing use of the Pickering and Darlington nuclear generating stations and introduced legislation that prioritizes nuclear energy. The province also suggested former coal plants in Nanticoke and Lambton, plus a site in Wellesleyville owned by Ontario Power Generation, could be the sites of new nuclear power plants.
Since at least 2019, Ford has been promoting the use of small modular reactors, a new technology with great promise, as a way to move energy into remote areas. They also could be used to add additional capacity for domestic use and export.
"Energy policy will be fundamental to the future of our province and Ontario has a choice: A pro-growth agenda that attracts investments, creates jobs and keeps energy costs down for people and businesses, or ideological dogma that offers false choices," Ford said in a mid-October speech to the Empire Club.
Since being appointed minister of energy and electrification, Stephen Lecce has been pushing the idea of nuclear as a key anchor.
"To meet soaring energy demands, we're working with communities to plan ahead and build for our future so that we can generate more power that is reliable and affordable for our families today and tomorrow," Lecce said last week.
The Independent Electricity System Operator has predicted that the province will see an increase in demand of by 75 per cent by 2050. To meet that demand, which is driven by an increase in population and industries such as artificial intelligence and server farms - and an expected increase in the use of electric vehicles - the province will need to increase production capacity.
While once Ontario fully embraced nuclear, a new plant hasn't been commissioned in Ontario since 1985. A combination of opposition from environmental groups and fear after the Chernobyl disaster in 1987 made nuclear unpalatable for many years.
Now, the Ford government is putting nuclear forward as a clean and green alternative.
All of this activity from the Ford government has led to interest from the private sector in exploring nuclear reactors. A Toronto-based company, Canadian Space Mining Corp. (CSMC), announced a deal with Canadian Nuclear Laboratories to try to commercialize the Slowpoke nuclear reactor technology first developed by the federal government in the 1960s.
The plan is to not only use the technology for domestic uses but also to try to deploy a small nuclear reactor to the moon.
"We are confident in our ability to deploy the world's first reactor on the moon, contributing to international space exploration as part of NASA's Artemis missions," said Daniel Sax, chief executive of CSMC.
"At the same time, we see immense potential for commercializing this technology here on Earth, offering a sustainable and reliable energy solution to replace diesel generators in remote and Indigenous communities across the Arctic."
While nuclear energy provides more than half of Ontario's electrical energy supplies, plus much needed medical isotopes, the industry was on its way out. Plans to decommission Pickering were moving ahead until the Ford government changed course.
One thing is clear, that plan to change course has kicked off a rebirth for nuclear that no one would have seen coming five years ago. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Nuclear energy; Nuclear reactors; Coal-fired power plants; Moon; Nuclear power plants

Business indexing term: Subject: Coal-fired power plants Nuclear power plants

Document 79 of 219

Ontarians want booze tax cuts, may get them

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontarians-want-booze-tax-cuts-may-get-them/docview/3138986920/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontarians+want+booze+tax+cuts%2C+may+get+them&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-12-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Consider me shocked. People across Ontario would like to pay less tax, in particular for their beer - craft beer, that is.
With the Ford government considering a realignment of provincial taxes and fees for all alcohol in the province, the Ontario Craft Brewers decided to take the pulse of the province on taxes.
A poll conducted last month shows that 71 per cent of Ontario residents believe that taxes on craft beer should be lowered and 75 per cent believe lowering taxes will help ensure craft brewers are able to compete.
The government will have trouble disputing the poll results as the brewers smartly hired Campaign Research. That's the same polling firm the government and the PC Party use and it's owned by Doug Ford's political adviser, Nick Kouvalis.
The questions detailed how Ontario charges the highest tax rates of any province for craft beer and that means "local craft beer companies in Ontario pay eight times more in taxes than craft beer companies in Alberta." The poll then asked if taxes should be lowered and, not surprisingly, 44 per cent strongly agreed taxes in Ontario should be more in line with other parts of Canada, 27 per cent somewhat agreed with that, eight per cent somewhat disagreed and seven per cent strongly disagreed.
Who are the seven per cent of people who want Ontario's taxes significantly higher on any product? If they like paying taxes that much, maybe they can pay my tax bill.
In addition to 75 per cent of Ontario residents saying it was important to ensure craft brewers were competitive, 64 per cent said the government should look at more measures to help "such as increasing shelf space for local beer." That policy idea may be popular, but is tricky for the government due to trade deals, even the language in their policy of currently advocating 20 per cent shelf space for local products has to be extremely precise to avoid trade challenges.
The craft brewing industry is likely to get their wish on an overhaul of the province's current system, which is antiquated and overly complex. When the province announced it was speeding up the sale of beer and wine last May, they also announced a total review of taxation.
"The government will also conduct a broader review of taxes and fees on beer, wine and alcoholic beverages with the aim of promoting a fairer and more competitive marketplace for Ontario-based products and consumers," a news release said at the time.
The Ford government has taken some heat with claims that it is needlessly obsessed with alcohol - odd given Ford doesn't drink - but that criticism missed the fact that booze is a major contributor to the province's coffers. The LCBO provided the province with nearly $2.6 billion last year, while excise taxes on beer, wine and spirits brought in another $593 million for a combined total of about $3.2 billion.
That's before the sales taxes on booze sold in bars and restaurants is considered, or all the other taxes generated by that industry.
This means the government will have to be careful that any move it makes on changing taxes and fees doesn't cut off revenue too deeply. That said, a study on Ontario's alcohol expansion commissioned by the Convenience Industry Council found provincial tax revenue could increase by as much as $147 million annually due to expanded sales.
While the government hasn't made its position known at this point, industry insiders expect that sometime in the new year, possibly in a pre-election budget, the government will announce tax changes on booze. Transparency is a key word that keeps being thrown around, so is simplicity.
"The problem is the government wants to make changes that don't cost them anything," said one industry rep.
Another said they expect the Ford government to drop some fees in coming months, in particular at the LCBO.
The poll also asked about support or opposition to the expansion of where alcohol can be sold. It found that 23 per cent strongly support the changes, 31 per cent somewhat support them while 16 per cent somewhat oppose, 23 per cent strongly oppose and seven per cent declined to answer. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Taxes; Tax rates; Alcohol; Beer

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxes Tax rates

Identifier / keyword: consider; shocked; people; ontario; particular; craft

Document 80 of 219

Concerns mount as Presidentelect Trump threatens steep tariffs on Canada; Feds will protect industries, workers: MP Sheehan

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/concerns-mount-as-presidentelect-trump-threatens/docview/3134429063/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Concerns+mount+as+Presidentelect+Trump+threatens+steep+tariffs+on+Canada%3B+Feds+will+protect+industries%2C+workers%3A+MP+Sheehan&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fears are mounting as U.S. President-elect Donald Trump threatens major tariffs on Canadian products.
Trump has threatened to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all goods entering the United States from Canada and Mexico unless the countries tighten their boards to prevent drugs and illegal migrants from entering the U.S.
Canada's federal government has said it will take a united Team Canada approach when dealing with the United States.
Sault Ste. Marie MP Terry Sheehan said the Canadian government has been preparing for some time for the potential of the new president to use executive orders to impose the tariffs.
Sheehan said he will be part of the Team Canada that heads to Washington in January to meet with government, industry, business and chamber of commerce to explain the importance of trade between Canada and the United States.
Sheehan said Canada's border is not the issue.
"Canada is not the issue and our job now is to educate Americans on that. We are only 1.2 per cent of the issue. Mexico's border is 98.8 per cent of the issue and we need to educate Americans," Sheehan said.
Sheehan said it was the former Conservative government that cut millions from the border agency and slashed staff while the Liberal government has reinstated funding, personnel and modernized the border.
But the potential for tariffs still concerns business and industry.
Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland met with representatives from Algoma Steel and Tenaris during her recent visit and promised open communication and collaboration, Sheehan said.
"We have been successful in the past and will be in the future," he said. "We can demonstrate how important our border and trade is to both countries, to both economies."
Sault Ste. Marie Chamber of Commerce president Jason Naccarato said members are very concerned about the uncertainty in Trump's statement and how the federal government will react.
"It's a significant challenge for all of them," he said.
There was an expectation that the U.S. would abide by the United States Mexico Canada Agreement (USMCA) until it expires in 2026 and prior to that, negotiate a new fair-trade deal.
"Now business doesn't know if (Trump) will circumvent USMCA and the unknown is very frustrating to our members and local industries," Naccarato said.
He argues that while Trump is concerned about illegal immigrants entering the United States, that concern likely focuses on the U.S. southern border with Mexico, not Canada.
Naccarato said Canadians are also concerned about the growing addiction issues, and closer to home, businesses are facing additional problems and costs associated with the local opioid issue.
"We don't know where Trump is getting his facts from, but if Canada does have a drug problem and we have a problem with manufacturing synthetic drugs, then the government needs to do more about it," he said.
Last month, RCMP in B.C. reported they dismantled the largest and most sophisticated drug superlab in Canadian history where an organized crime group were producing and distributing massive quantities of illicit drugs "If drugs are crossing the border from Canada into the U.S., then Canada needs to do something about it, not only for the U.S., but to help Canadian communities and the Canadian problem as well," he said.
Tariffs could have major implications for the steel and aluminum industry, including Algoma Steel.
The North American steel market is highly integrated and steel crosses the border several times as it is transformed from raw materials to finished product.
USMCA was inked to integrate the North American steel market.
Algoma Steel did not return requests for an interview.
Algoma CEO Michael Garcia told The Sault Star in October that it was important for the Canadian government to impose 25 per cent tariffs on cheap, Chinese-made steel and aluminum to ensure that Canadian steelmakers can compete fairly and not be faced by unfair trade and subsidy measures.
"We're not looking for an advantage. We want to compete fairly -the best product with the most competitive price and we're ready for that if we have a fair and level playing field," he said.
The steelmaker is keeping abreast of the U.S. presidency transition.
"At the end of the day it's important that the U.S. use Canada as an important, and critical and fair-trading partner. If you look at everything the Canadian government has been doing recently, that's the way they are acting, so I'm encouraged by that," he told The Sault Star.
The Canadian Steel Producers Association works to ensure policymakers at the provincial and federal level understand the industry challenges and perspective so they can make the best decisions.
Sheehan said he will continue to work with the industry, as co-chair of the all-party steel caucus.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford issued a statement earlier this week following a First Minister's meeting, urging the federal government to show that Canada understands, cares and is responsive to U.S. security and economic concerns.
He wants the federal government to match U.S. tariffs on China, ban Chinese software in cars on Canadian roads, delay the implementation of the digital services tax and present a credible plan to meet our NATO defence spending commitments.
"It's also why I called out Mexico for allowing itself to become a backdoor for cheap Chinese transshipment and am urging Canada to negotiate a bilateral trade agreement directly with the U.S.," the release states.
"Ontario will continue to engage with U.S. lawmakers to demonstrate how our province is uniquely positioned to help America bring jobs back home as it decouples from China. Ontario can help build up Fortress Am-Can by building out Ontario-U.S. critical mineral supply chains and provide more clean, nuclear power to fuel America's economic growth and promote our shared security."
The Ontario Chamber of Commerce says the tariffs would have devastating impacts on manufacturing, agriculture and tech sectors, put supply chains at risk and decline investor confidence. Job losses would also occur, said CEO Daniel Tisch.
The Canadian Federation of Independent Business says any disruption to the flow of goods between the U.S. and Canada would be a major economic hit.
"Small-and medium-sized businesses account for approximately 40 per cent of exports to the U.S.," the CFIB says in a statement. "Tariffs would not just affect our exporters as their effect on the value of the Canadian dollar would increase the cost of U.S. imports, affecting small businesses and consumers alike."
The CFIB also calls for a united front and action to respond to the threat and take it seriously, said Dan Kelly, president of CFIB.
"Our governments must take all actions within our control to ensure we are a good and reliable trading partner for the US and the world. These include a stronger focus on crime, stabilizing our supply chains such as ports and railways, promoting our energy sector and reducing the regulatory and tax burdens facing Canadian businesses," Kelly said.

Subject: Drugs; International trade; Aluminum; Federal government; Supply chains; Presidents; Trade agreements; Chambers of commerce; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: International trade Supply chains Trade agreements Chambers of commerce Tariffs

Location: China; Mexico; United States--US; Canada

Identifier / keyword: fears; mounting; donald; trump; threatens; major

Title: Concerns mount as Presidentelect Trump threatens steep tariffs on Canada; Feds will protect industries, workers: MP Sheehan

Document 81 of 219

Trudeau defends $250 rebates; PM accuses Tories and NDP of turning their backs on working people

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-defends-250-rebates-pm-accuses-tories-ndp/docview/3134428851/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+defends+%24250+rebates%3B+PM+accuses+Tories+and+NDP+of+turning+their+backs+on+working+people&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau defended his government's decision to dole out $250 cheques to working Canadians, arguing that many of them have been left out of targeted programs meant to help seniors and young families in recent years.
Speaking in Prince Edward Island on Friday, Trudeau said he had been hearing from Canadians across the country who do not qualify for any of the federal government's initiatives, whether it is a boost in seniors'pensions, child benefits or childcare spaces.
"I regularly meet a 26-year-old grocery worker, or a 32-yearold school teacher with no kids, or someone in their 40s who's working in a factory, or a 50-yearold plumber whose kids have all grown, who says, 'OK, but we're having troubles too,'" he said.
Trudeau said those Canadians are feeling "squeezed" even though they are working hard and feel like the federal government is not "showing up" for them.
"Seeing hard-working Canadians who get up, go to work every day and make our economy and our communities successful, and recognizing them specifically ... and showing that we see them, that's what this is all about," he added.
Trudeau announced last week that the government would be pausing the GST/HST on select items, including beer, takeout food, children's toys and Christmas trees in time for the holidays, but also send out a new rebate for working Canadians in the spring next year.
Those rebates are expected to be sent to 18.7 million Canadians who worked last year and made $150,000 or less. They will cost $4.68 billion to taxpayers.
In the past week, federal Liberals faced fierce pressure from the NDP, the Bloc Québécois and even some in their own caucus for the $250 cheques to include retirees, people with disabilities and other vulnerable groups who did not earn an income in 2023.
As a result, the federal government opted to table a bill to solely pass the GST/HST holiday, leaving any legislation and debate on the $250 cheques for another day.
A Liberal source said the reason for splitting the measures in two was to make sure the tax break would be in effect by Dec. 14. In comparison, the government has a lot more time to make sure the cheques go out to Canadians as they are expected next spring.
Trudeau would not say when his government would be tabling legislation for the rebates, but expects they will be rolling out in April 2025.
He also said his government will look at "different ways" of helping seniors and "all sorts of different people."
Trudeau also blasted opposition parties for not supporting his government's current proposal on the cheques, arguing that they are against working people.
"To see the NDP, which was supposedly the party of workers, turn its back on hard-working Canadians and say, 'no, we shouldn't be delivering this check unless we expand it massively,'is not recognizing working Canadians for the challenges they're facing."
He accused Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre of "pretending to care about workers and care about affordability." Poilievre has criticized the Liberals'latest ideas as "irresponsible and inflationist" and said the carbon tax increase on April 1 will raise the cost of living. P.E.I. Premier Dennis King held a press conference alongside Trudeau to announce his province is the latest to agree to join the federal government's National School Food Program after Newfoundland and Labrador, Manitoba and Ontario.
King said he and Trudeau talked about the upcoming tax holiday. He said P.E.I. would "willingly participate" in removing the harmonized sales tax for products selected by the federal Liberals during the holidays and until Feb. 15, 2025. National Post calevesque@postmedia.com

Document 82 of 219

Tariff threat needs Team Canada response

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tariff-threat-needs-team-canada-response/docview/3133884712/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tariff+threat+needs+Team+Canada+response&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump has given Canada two months'notice: Fix the border or get whacked with tariffs, huge tariffs. The U.S. president-elect announced on his Truth Social platform he will sign an executive order to bring in across-the-board tariffs on Canadian goods.
The threat is bigger than the 10 per cent to 20 per cent threat of tariffs on which Trump had campaigned, and the threat comes from issues that have nothing to do with Chinese dumping.
"As everyone is aware, thousands of people are pouring through Mexico and Canada, bringing crime and drugs at levels never seen before," Trump posted.
"On Jan. 20, as one of my many first executive orders, I will sign all necessary documents to charge Mexico and Canada a 25 per cent tariff on all products coming into the United States, and its ridiculous open borders."
While illegal immigration from Canada to the United States pales in comparison to what happens on the Mexican-American border, the numbers have been rising.
During the period from October 2023 to the end of September 2024, border patrol agents apprehended close to 20,000 people crossing in the Swanton Sector, more than in the previous 17 years combined. That sector runs from the Quebec-New Hampshire border, across the Vermont-Quebec border and over the top of New York State to where the St. Lawrence River meets Lake Ontario.
Trump's new border czar, Tom Homan, comes from western New York, just south of Kingston, and knows the Canada-U.S. border well.
"There's very little river patrol or lake patrol or road patrol, and so they had been overrun, and the problem with the northern border is a huge national security issue," Homan told a Watertown, N.Y., TV station just after he was appointed.
He also noted more than twice as many people on terror watch lists have been apprehended crossing into the United States from Canada than from Mexico. And while fentanyl isn't a drug that is often seized crossing in from the north, Canada has long been a source of cocaine, marijuana and other drugs, a fact of which Homan is well aware.
Trump clearly has an agenda with the tariff threat; he wants action.
"This tariff will remain in effect until such time as drugs, in particular fentanyl, and all illegal aliens stop this invasion of our country. Both Mexico and Canada have the absolute right and power to easily solve this long-simmering problem," Trump said, adding failure to act will result in both countries paying a big price.
If Trump were to act on this, it would cause immense devastation to the Canadian economy.
An analysis by TD economics of Trump's earlier tariff threat predicted that under a 10 per cent tariff, "Real GDP would fall around 2.4 percentage points over two years." This threat is 21/2 times bigger and would sink Canada into a deep recession.
Thankfully, there is a warning and a chance to act to avoid the tariffs, because on something like this, turning to enacting our own tariffs wouldn't have the desired effect, our pain would be much bigger than theirs.
Earlier Monday, Ontario Premier Doug Ford issued a letter to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - signed on behalf of all of Canada's premiers - asking for a meeting "to discuss a Team Canada approach to engaging with the incoming administration, including on trade, secure borders, energy, defence and the strength of our cross-border supply chains."
After Trump's threat, Ford posted to social media the need for a meeting was even more urgent.
There is a problem of drugs, crime and people crossing the border in both directions. The administration of President Joe Biden had raised issues with the Trudeau government months ago, there was no action.
Now, we face an incoming administration that isn't interested in playing nice. Trump only wants results.
What's worrisome for Canada is it's doubtful that the Trudeau government can deliver anything that will save us and our economy from the pain that is coming. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 83 of 219

Trudeau offers temporary tax cut that ends before tax hikes

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-offers-temporary-tax-cut-that-ends-before/docview/3133048124/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+offers+temporary+tax+cut+that+ends+before+tax+hikes&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-11-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau's plan to become more popular is to give Canadians a tax break just before Christmas and then raise multiple taxes on you in the new year.
Oh, and he wants to send you a cheque for $250 in March 2025 because you deserve a break today, or at least a few months from now.
Trudeau made his announcement in the small town of Sharon, about an hour's drive up Highway 404 from Toronto.
He is pledging to take the GST off a number of key items, including children's clothing and shoes, toys, books and prepared foods like the rotisserie chicken that's so popular. He's even promising to take the tax off Christmas trees - but don't rush out to buy one just yet, the tax cut won't come into effect until Dec. 14, less than two weeks before Christmas.
"These are things that people care about, that matter for them," Trudeau said. "It's time for people to get a bit of a break."
Part of the plan is also to take the GST off beer and wine - but not spirits - and restaurant bills, whether takeout or dine-in.
Of course, just 45 days after the tax holiday is over - Happy Valentine's Day - Trudeau will up a number of taxes on April Fool's Day.
On April 1, 2025, the carbon tax will go up by 19 per cent from 17.6 cents a litre on gasoline to 20.9 cents a litre. The annual escalator tax on alcohol will also go up by two per cent, making beer, wine and spirits more expensive.
This escalator tax on alcohol has been increasing the price of booze every April 1 since 2017.
Trudeau is smart to make these moves and it shows he's finally figured out that making everything more expensive isn't a good political move. But giving a two-month partial tax holiday just before increasing multiple taxes that will increase the prices of the same products doesn't show a coherent strategy to win back voters.
It's not clear how much this will cost the government, and no price breakdown was provided. The GST alone brought in more than $51 billion in revenue last year, or roughly $4.25 billion per month. But since the tax is only being removed from some items, it's hard to assess the impact on the federal bottom line - though it could be substantial.
"I don't vote for press releases and press conferences," Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said when asked if he would vote for the measure.
It's the right move, there is no bill that has been written, and details are sketchy at best.
Trudeau used the terms GST and HST interchangeably during his news conference. In Alberta, there is no provincial tax so this will be just a five per cent savings, in Nova Scotia, the combined HST is 15 per cent, it's 14.95 per cent in Quebec, 13 per cent in Ontario, and 12 per cent in Manitoba.
Depending on the details of the harmonized sales tax agreement they have with the federal government, provinces may end up giving up more revenue within their jurisdiction than the federal government while Trudeau takes all the credit.
Poilievre called the entire tax holiday plan a trick, saying Trudeau was trying to fool Canadians with a brief tax holiday over Christmas before hiking taxes in the new year.
"Nobody believes Justin Trudeau and Jagmeet Singh after they've impoverished our people and made life worse off," Poilievre said.
He promised real and permanent tax relief if elected prime minister rather than Trudeau's short-term gimmick.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, Trudeau's ongoing coalition partner said he wanted this bill - which doesn't exist - passed as soon as possible. Singh may have "ripped up" the agreement with Trudeau, but he keeps going back to him.
All of this, by the way, was announced on a day when an Abacus Data poll showed the Conservatives at 43 per cent voter support, the Liberals and NDP were at 21 per cent meaning their combined support is less than the Conservatives.
Trudeau is literally hoping to buy your vote for Christmas. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 84 of 219

Trump 2.0 threatens Liberal policy

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-2-0-threatens-liberal-policy/docview/3131638062/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+2.0+threatens+Liberal+policy&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-11-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals may be anxious about the rise of Pierre Poilievre, but their real problem isn't the Conservative leader. It's Donald Trump.
With the former president returning to the White House in January, Trudeau faces a rapid dismantling of his progressive agenda at the hands of Trump's new administration.
From immigration to the environment, trade to health care, Trump's bro-heavy cabinet is poised to upend American policy - and, by extension, rip up Canada's, before the Conservatives even get the chance.
First up, immigration. Trudeau's mismanagement of the file has already destroyed the Canadian consensus on the issue: according to polls, more than half of us now think there are too many newcomers here.
This week, Trump announced he will declare a "national emergency" and use the military to remove illegal immigrants when he takes office.
What this means isn't clear, but we do know that Trump's deportation plans could see Canada overrun with migrants fleeing ahead of their potential removal.
How is Trudeau responding? On Sunday, he released a seven-minute video blaming our immigration problems on "bad actors" who abused the system - unscrupulous colleges, greedy consultants, unethical corporations - and said he should have "acted quicker" to curb these abuses.
It's too little, too late, and unlikely to impress anyone in D.C., but it signals a sea change from a leader who preached for years that diversity is our strength.
Then there's the environment. Canada's environment policy, designed to align with international climate targets, will clash with that of a U.S. administration bent on rolling back Bidenera climate regulations.
Former Liberal finance minister Bill Morneau advised that Trudeau should pause plans for the oil and gas emissions cap announced earlier this month, as we seek to be a "good partner" to the United States.
"It will be tough for them to come around to that, but I don't think there's really a choice," he said.
Trade is no safer. Trump's threat to impose 10 to 20 per cent across-the-board tariffs would decimate the Canadian economy, which sends about two-thirds of its exports to the U.S., and our free trade deal with the U.S. and Mexico is up for renegotiation in 2026.
Bargaining chips will be essential, and one of those may be supply management in the dairy industry - a sacred cow in Quebec, but a no-go in Wisconsin.
Before Ottawa even gets to the table, though, Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith are already trying to stave off disaster, throwing Mexico under the bus to preserve the Ontario auto industry and curry favour for Alberta energy companies.
Our country's single-payer health system, long held up by progressives as a key differentiator between Canada and the U.S., will face pressure, too.
But there, Canada could find opportunity. Trump's new secretary of health, Robert F.
Kennedy Jr., is vaccine-averse and a basher of big pharma.
If Canada is smart, we will position ourselves as the last North American haven for pharmaceutical R&D - but that would mean scrapping Trudeau's national pharmacare plan, which threatens Canadians'access to cutting-edge drugs.
Finally, there's foreign policy. On this front, Trump has appointed China hawks such as Marco Rubio and Russian sympathizer Tulsi Gabbard.
To get ahead of a looming withdrawal of support for Ukraine, on Sunday, U.S. President Joe Biden greenlit the use of American-made longrange missiles for strikes inside Russia.
But the rest of the world already is turning to the new boss in town, Trump. The G20 statement put out Monday on Ukraine doesn't even mention Russia, to Trudeau's dismay.
"No, it's not strong enough for me," he said. But will anybody care? So forget about the next election. The question isn't what Poilievre will do, but what will be left to do.
In the coming year, Ottawa's biggest battles will be waged across the border, not across the aisle. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Energy industry; Immigration

Business indexing term: Subject: Energy industry

Location: Mexico; Russia; United States--US; Canada; Ukraine

Title: Trump 2.0 threatens Liberal policy

Document 85 of 219

Smith flexes more political power than any recent premier

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/smith-flexes-more-political-power-than-any-recent/docview/3130510469/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Smith+flexes+more+political+power+than+any+recent+premier&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-11-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith is not the usual kind of conservative. In some ways, she's not a conservative at all.
Traditional conservatives pride themselves on taking the best from the past and making it better. Smith is more likely to raze a traditional structure to the ground and start over.
There's more than a hint of Trumpism in this, thankfully without the ugliness and poisonous rhetoric.
Millions of Americans mistrust their federal government and want it radically rebuilt or demolished altogether. President-elect Donald Trump obliges with every new appointment of a system-wrecker.
The same dynamic is growing here. Recently, thousands of United Conservative Party members at the annual convention showed their deep mistrust of schools, teachers, health care, doctors, scientists, universities, elites of all kinds and "wokeism" in any form.
That's a partial list. Smith won 91.5 per cent support for her leadership, a thunderous mandate to effect radical change.
A premier in a majority government has a great deal of power. Much of it lies unused in normal times.
But Smith, with full support from her party, caucus and cabinet, is using power more forcefully than any premier in the modern era.
She's staging political takeovers of organizations that always have stood arm's-length from government.
That latest example is the firing of the AIMCo board, a move that could affect hundreds of thousands of Albertans whose pension funds are invested by this agency.
AIMCo's goal is to make as much money as possible for its clients, including those pension funds. Politics aren't supposed to be any part of this.
Suddenly, AIMCo is political from top to bottom.
The acting chair is Finance Minister Nate Horner. Serving as interim chief executive is Ray Gilmour, Smith's deputy minister of executive council. He's the boss of the entire civil service and works closely with the premier almost daily.
Former prime minister Stephen Harper will be the new chair. After running a government, he can surely handle AIMCo. But his mere presence suggests a bold reshaping of AIMCo as an investment vehicle for an Alberta pension plan.
Smith's most dramatic action is the takedown of Alberta Health Services.
It's being split into authorities for primary care, acute care, continuing care, and mental health and addictions (the latter under Recovery Alberta).
Whole groups of health-care workers are being shifted from their former employer, AHS, to one of the others.
There's no question AHS had become too vast to manage properly, but this is a political takeover that hands enormous power to Health Minister Adriana LaGrange. There's no longer a clear dividing line between the political wing and health-care operations.
On the civic front, Smith has assumed power to fire councillors, and forbids towns and cities from striking funding agreements with Ottawa unless the province approves.
The signpost move on the civic front is the takeover of Calgary council's Green Line LRT project, with an ultimatum to accept the province's planned route or there will no project at all.
Other premiers have done striking things.
Former premier Ed Stelmach launched AHS as a provincewide agency in 2008. The PC founding premier, Peter Lougheed, bought Pacific Western Airlines in 1974.
But we have to go back to the Social Credit era in the 1930s to find anything like the UCP urge to dramatic upheaval.
In the desperate pit of the Great Depression, then-premier William Aberhart's new government tried to print money and create banks to defy Bay Street.
But this wasn't radical enough for Aberhart's own MLAs. In 1938, he faced a formal revolt from his caucus.
It sounds familiar.
Don Braid's column appears regularly with Postmedia X: @DonBraid

Subject: Pension funds; Political power; Politics

Business indexing term: Subject: Pension funds

Identifier / keyword: alberta; premier; danielle; smith; usual; conservative

Title: Smith flexes more political power than any recent premier

Document 86 of 219

Catholic trustee wants expense policy review; Provincial audit of boards looms

Author: Kelly, Brian

Publication info: Kelly, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/catholic-trustee-wants-expense-policy-review/docview/3129080242/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Catholic+trustee+wants+expense+policy+review%3B+Provincial+audit+of+boards+looms&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Kelly%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Several Huron-Superior Catholic District School Board trustees want an early review of the policy that guides their claiming of expenses before an anticipated provincial government review of discretionary spending by boards.
John Caputo called for the assessment after trustee expenses for fiscal year 2023-24 were included in the agenda at a meeting Wednesday. The policy was last reviewed in 2021. Trustees usually review policies every five years, but can do so earlier if they want.
"I think we should have a review of that policy and update it, perhaps," said Caputo. "I'd like to be proactive rather than reactive, so we know what the content is and how that money's being spent."
Caputo later told reporters he'd "like to see" out-of-town trustees attending committee meetings online rather than travelling. He said the board has spent cash on equipment to improve virtual gatherings.
"We should take advantage of that technology," said Caputo following the meeting. "There's no issues with our technology. They can join the meeting and it's fine. It doesn't cost us anymore to bring them here."
Cash saved on mileage reimbursement to trustees can help with other costs such as special education services and library book purchases, Caputo added.
Premier Doug Ford called audits of boards earlier this month following concerns of some trustees travelling to Italy and Hawaii and making a six-figure purchase of art.
"The ministry is sort of fed up with this discretionary spending abuse," Caputo told reporters. "I feel that it's our responsibility to maybe look into it prior to being told. I'd rather be proactive rather than reactive so that we can come up with a better plan (with how money is spent)."
Tony D'Agostino backed the idea and wanted the review to be done "sooner rather than later.
"Just to see if we maybe missed something that maybe leads us open to questions," he said. "I think it would be probably a smart move on our part."
Director of education Fil Lettieri told trustees he was going to join other leaders of school boards at a meeting with Education Minister Jill Dunlop on Thursday "that will provide more insight into the auditing process of all school boards and what that will entail."
He added the English Catholic board has "a very thorough process in terms of how the approvals are granted for expenses."
In a statement, Dunlop said she stressed the ministry's "expectations" regarding spending by school boards. Discretionary expenses will be reviewed.
"Every dollar spent must go toward improving and supporting student achievement, primarily funding classroom resources, teacher support or programs that enhance academic outcomes," said Dunlop. "It is my expectation that these leaders in education exercise sound judgement and champion fiscal responsibility to maintain the trust of parents, students and taxpayers."
Trustees claimed $47,419.96 for travel and cellphone calls for the period Sept. 1, 2023 to Aug. 31, 2024. They are paid 50 cents a kilometre when they use their own vehicles for board business.
Lindsay Liske, representing Espanola, claimed the most at $11,047.23.Kathleen Rosilius, who lives in Elliot Lake, claimed $6,729. She asked why Liske's expenses were "so much more" than the other nine trustees. Liske has posted the most expenses since costs started to be reported to the board for fiscal year 2020-21.
Gary Trembinski added costs trustees claimed "are pretty much in line" ranging from John Caputo's $3,690.66 to the nearly $6,700 sought by Rosilius.
"She's about as close to the same distance as trustee Liske, so I don't know why it would be $4,000 more," said Trembinski.
Elliot Lake is 200 kilometres east of Sault Ste. Marie. McKerrow is 240 kilometres from the city.
"His expenses are mainly related to attending board and committee meetings in person," said superintendent of business Justin Pino.
He noted the distance Liske has to travel to get to the board office. Trembinski questioned driving five hours for an hour-long meeting.
Sandra Turco, who succeeded Trembinski as chair on Wednesday, noted Liske's expenses were approved when Trembinski held the chair's role.
"I'm just surprised that you're surprised at the amount," she said.
"I was shocked, actually, when I saw that number," said Trembinski of the expenses claimed by Liske. "I do know that I didn't think it would be that much to come to a couple of committee meetings."
All trustees have the option to attend the regional Ontario Catholic School Trustees'Association in the fall, an OCSTA seminar in January and OCSTA annual general meeting and conference in the spring and the Canadian Catholic School Trustees'Association AGM and conference in June. Liske said he attended three out-of-town conferences during 2023-24 "as most of the trustees attended."
Turco said Caputo's concern will be directed to the board's policy committee. That group meets monthly. The committee could make possible recommendations to have the policy reviewed by the board at a future meeting "should there be changes that require their consultation and approval," said Lettieri in an email.
Liske is a member of the audit committee. He said he quit the policy committee because he was treated disrespectfully online. Liske added the audit committee meets the day before trustees gather and he returns to McKerrow the day after the meeting of trustees.
"That's only the same mileage as a board meeting," he said. "I'm not double dipping on mileage."
Lettieri says trustees can attend committee meetings online, but can attend in person "if they choose to."
Trembinski and Liske declined further comment to The Sault Star when reached Thursday and Friday, saying only Turco and Lettieri can speak to reporters. Trustees and expenses claimed in 2023-24: Carol Amadio, $4,933.15, John Bruno, $4,954.22, John Caputo, $3,960.66, Kerri Commanda, $306.87, Tony D'Agostino, $779.68, Lindsay Liske, $11,047.23, Carol MacEachern, $4,063.27,
Kathleen Rosilius, $6,729.00, Gary Trembinski, $5,872.65, Sandra Turco, $4,773.23. Expenses claimed by student trustees: Kenzie Garson, $2,340.26, Owen Middleton, $2,472.47, Mya Jensen, $1,893.38, Alexander Orazietti, $828.20.
Policy 2007 governs trustee expenses. HSCDSB will reimburse costs "reasonably incurred in carrying out their responsibilities, while representing the interests of students, the community and/or the board," the document reads.
The board chair is responsible for the policy's implementation and "ensuring compliance" by trustees.The board must approve attendance at conferences.
Ministry of Education requires trustees to be physically present for board meetings. Online attendance is allowed if the chair gives written approval before the meeting starts.
Consent is given if the trustee meets at least one of five conditions including living at least 125 kilometres from the meeting location, poor weather conditions, physical disability or health-related issue and family responsibilities. btkelly@postmedia.com On X: @Saultreporter

Document 87 of 219

Board wants 'plan'to fight shoplifting; Nearly 1,000 incidents reported

Author: Kelly, Brian

Publication info: Kelly, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/board-wants-planto-fight-shoplifting-nearly-1-000/docview/3128485198/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Board+wants+%27plan%27to+fight+shoplifting%3B+Nearly+1%2C000+incidents+reported&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Kelly%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More ways to fight shoplifting will be identified by Sault Ste. Marie Police Service as thefts from stores keep rising.
Reported shoplifting incidents are up 32 per cent as of August compared to last year, with 940 incidents being reported compared to 713 in 2023.
"I'd like to see us adapt and change our response locally to try and bring those numbers down because they just continue to rise," said Ward 1 Coun. Sonny Spina during a Sault Ste. Marie Police Services Board meeting on Oct. 30. "We'd like to see a plan for those numbers to come down by a certain date."
Spina is a former officer with the municipal service.
Ward 4 Coun. Marchy Bruni said the police department must "protect" business owners.
"This can't continue," he said.
"The numbers can't continue to rise."
Stevenson told reporters additional strategies will start to be used by officers and reviewed with the police board when members meet next on Nov. 20.
"We'll come back with more strategies that we can put into the community at the municipal level," said Stevenson. "There's always something more we can do."
The police service's community mobilization unit can go to businesses and suggest crime prevention tips, including placement of merchandise and customer access to the building.
Break-ins to homes and businesses are down 28 per cent through the first eight months of this year with 280 burglaries reported compared to 387 from January to August 2023. Stevenson suggests shoplifting is easier than breaking into a building and could explain why break-ins are down and shoplifting is up.
He backs the Ontario government's recent call to the federal government to update the Criminal Code and tighten bail legislation. The province wants mandatory minimum sentences brought back for serious crimes, ending the possibility of bail for persons charged with major offences including drug trafficking and intimate partner violence and restricting who can get conditional sentences to serve time in the community.
"It's high time for the federal government to look at this legislation," said Stevenson. "Anything more than what we have now is a good thing."
David Eby, leader of the NDP in British Columbia, wants involuntary care for those with serious addictions and mental illness. Federal Opposition Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre backs involuntary treatment for minors and prisoners dealing with mental health and addictions, but is still considering when it comes to adults.
"I would like to see more than what we have now," said Stevenson when asked if he'd back similar action by the Ontario government. "How that transpires in the province is up to them."
- with files from Postmedia Network btkelly@postmedia.com On X: @Saultreporter
