Document 88 of 219

Beware: 'Tariff'is President-elect Donald Trump's favourite word; Canada likely won't go unscathed

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/beware-tariffis-president-elect-donald-trumps/docview/3126458808/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Beware%3A+%27Tariff%27is+President-elect+Donald+Trump%27s+favourite+word%3B+Canada+likely+won%27t+go+unscathed&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-11-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sault Ste. Marie elected officials are taking a very cautious approach to what the United States'next President means to the community -and to Canada.
While President-elect Donald Trump says 'tariff'is his favourite word and he plans to implement a minimum of 10 per cent tariffs on all imports, Sault Ste. Marie's elected officials are hoping that won't happen, opting instead to talk about the Canada-U.S. relationship as a longtime friendship with similar economic drives and a working-together attitude.
How quickly they forget. Or perhaps, want to forget. Maybe even erase the memory, if that's possible.
Let's recap. During Trump's first term, he imposed tariffs of 25 per cent on Canadian steel and a 10 per cent tariff on Canadian aluminum.
Algoma Steel, which was then Essar Steel Algoma, ships about half of its finished product to the U.S., the tax a crippling measure for the steelmaker.
Canada reciprocated with identical tariffs on U.S. imports.
Eventually, both were erased and the status quo returned.
Renegotiating NAFTA, now called the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA). was another tough one that took a long time to complete, and at times it didn't look like a deal would be inked.
And so, the word 'tariff'returns and is front and centre in many of Trump's recent campaign speeches, and Canadians should brace for his first 100 days in office where it is likely he will make good on that promise as one of his first measures to Make America Great Again.
Last month, the Canadian government imposed a 25 per cent tariff on cheap steel and aluminum imports from China, following a move from American counterparts.
While China is likely first on Trump's hit list, Canada can't expect to go unscathed without a battle.
Sault MP Terry Sheehan, co-chair of the all-party steel caucus, is hoping to ward off a repeat of four years ago by travelling to Washington on a friendly mission to remind Americans how closely the Canada and U.S. steel industries are integrated and that any tariff would negatively affect both economies.
The friendly approach may not sway the President-elect, but a tough stance with a business and economic approach could have a more positive effect. After all, Trump is a businessman. Money talks. Prosperity is his key mission.
While USMCA doesn't come up for renewal until 2026, it's likely Trump won't wait a year to push his agenda forward. Everyone needs to stay focused, especially during his first 100 days in office.
The Canadian government has repeatedly said the country survived the first time around with Trump and will do so this time. It's more prepared and knows what to expect, we are told. Possibly. But we now know that a federal government committee on Canada-U.S. relations has reconvened, likely drawing up scenarios and possible responses in preparation for the new government.
Let us remember Trump also knows the ropes now and, somehow, he needs to turn his campaign promises into results. He's taking a hard-nose protectionist stance that will change the way trade between Canada and the U.S. is done. That could put both economies at risk.
In Ontario, Premier Doug Ford is also pushing for the Buy Can-AM approach, waving his open-door, business friendly policy to Trump, noting the partnerships formed between many states and Ontario that have economic benefits for both. Time will determine how well those partnerships are etched in stone and if Trump stays away from state jurisdiction. Again, let us not forget he likes control.
The key for all levels of government and business in Canada is simple. Stay alert and expect the unexpected. Be prepared to retaliate.
Maybe it is time Canada takes on a role other than that of the nice guy. Elaine Della-Mattia is The Sault Star's political reporter.

Subject: Aluminum; Political campaigns; Presidents; Public officials; United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement Tariffs

Document 89 of 219

Time for Smith to hit federal Liberals back

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/time-smith-hit-federal-liberals-back/docview/3125918201/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Time+for+Smith+to+hit+federal+Liberals+back&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Danielle Smith launched a blazing attack on Ottawa Monday after the feds announced their emissions cap on oil and gas.
She was furious and it showed in a stream of red-hot rhetoric.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has a "deranged vendetta against Alberta," she said.
Whenever the province tries to negotiate, she added, "they hammer you on the head when your back is turned."
The premier came close to saying Alberta will invoke the constitutional notwithstanding clause to opt out of the cap.
Whether the clause works for such a purpose is questionable, but the declaration would still be a powerful message.
She'll also head to court - again - and work up a resolution under the Sovereignty Act.
Smith said she has carefully studied former Premier Peter Lougheed's victory over resource ownership in the 1980s.
That reference was intriguing. She probably wishes she had Lougheed's government tools in hand, specifically, the ability to simply stop shipping oil to central Canada.
Lougheed did that. His PC government cut supply to Ontario refineries in stages.
It worked. Ontario wept like a baby and the feds caved before the third cut came into effect.
That's much harder to do today because of regulation changes and diminished provincial control over private companies.
But we're finally at the point where the premier should launch some specific economic retaliation.
The debate in the 1980s was over who got the money. Today, it's about whether there will be any money.
As long as federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault is anywhere near government, it will be obvious the ultimate goal is to shut in Canadian oil and gas.
If this goes forward, Canada will be the only oil and gas producing country with such a cap.
That hands the advantage to countries like Venezuela, which care far less about emissions than the Alberta government and Alberta producers do.
But of course, imposing a cap convinces many people in the rest of the country that Alberta does nothing to control emissions.
That is simply false. The province has made great progress on methane. Companies know their international reputations - and sales - depend on taking serious action.
Smith said the Liberals have some nerve announcing the cap when their public support is in the tank, the NDP could defeat them any day, and even their carbon tax is under siege from other parties.
But this is an election ploy: go after Alberta, the useful climate villain, to please the progressive crowd in central Canada that has largely abandoned the Liberals.
Smith likens Trudeau to the "bad renter who wrecks the furniture" on the way out.
He would leave the economic repair job to others.
We don't hear the "just transition" phrase much any more. It became too toxic.
But the cap is just another pressure that would lead to job loss and dislocation in Alberta.
Smith argues emissions can be cut to meet targets, but by 2050, not 2026 or 2030.
The reason, she credibly argues, is technology takes years to develop and implement. Carbon capture is only now getting underway and still awaits federal support.
As a younger journalist, I covered all the major oil price and constitutional negotiations of the 1980s.
For this Ontario boy born and raised, arriving here via Quebec, the prevailing attitude toward Alberta was shocking.
To the Liberals of that day, the province was a distant place of high economic value, but even more political utility as a target that can produce masses of Liberal votes if you hit the bullseye.
Then, as now, arguing Alberta's case made you a traitor in the eyes of the national media and government.
That's exactly what's going on now. Smith should not just talk. She should act - and make it sting. Don Braid's column appears regularly with Postmedia X: @DonBraid

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: premier; danielle; smith; launched; blazing; attack

Title: Time for Smith to hit federal Liberals back

Document 90 of 219

'Working together'key to keeping Canada-U.S. relations on track; Politicians take 'safe' approach to U.S. election results

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/working-togetherkey-keeping-canada-u-s-relations/docview/3125917880/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Working+together%27key+to+keeping+Canada-U.S.+relations+on+track%3B+Politicians+take+%27safe%27+approach+to+U.S.+election+results&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Continuing to work together for the benefit of all North America is the approach Canadian elected officials are taking in the wake of the U.S. presidential election results.
Donald Trump easily won the U.S. presidential election Wednesday, claiming 292 electoral votes to Kamala Harris' 224. Under the American system, a candidate requires 270 electoral votes to claim victory. He will be sworn into office in January.
Sault Ste. Marie MP Terry Sheehan said the U.S. is the largest democracy in the world and shares with Canada many values and beliefs.
Sheehan, who also served as the city's MP during the first Trump presidency, said the renewal of the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA), now called the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA), remains important to both countries.
"We're not just the friendly cousins in the North. We are strategic partners in the success of the American people and Canadians and work together with a lot of industries, including steel," Sheehan said.
The USMCA is in place until 2026.
It was inked by Trump, and Sheehan sees no reason why the United States wouldn't want to renew it.
"Obviously it was a few years ago and things have changed... but I don't know if he'd rip up his own agreement ... but certainly it will be reviewed and modernized and we will emphasize the positive effects it has had, and will have, in the future," Sheehan said.
Sheehan is a member of Canada's trade committee and wants to start working on a pre-study to get ready for future negotiations. He's also the co-chair of all-party steel caucus and wants to visit Washington to reconfirm the importance of trade within the steel industry that exists between both countries daily.
"I was part of successful Team Canada approach when Trump was president and we will go back with a similar strategy of demonstrating our great friendship, our shared history, beliefs and values, and how important it is to work together for both nations for workers and the economies of both countries," he said.
Sheehan said Canada will stay focused on growing and modernizing its industries, all of which are part of the supply chain, including electric vehicle batteries, critical minerals for electric vehicles, steel and natural resources.
"I think there is a benefit to us because we have a lot of what the world wants and we have some of the best workers around," he said. "You're seeing unprecedented investments in Canada from other countries because they know that what is produced here is the best in the world and that will continue."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau congratulated Trump, calling the friendship between Canada and the U.S. "the envy of the world." He said he'll work with Trump on prosperity and security.
Sault MPP Ross Romano said he hopes relationships forged between Ontario and other states continues to grow opportunities for both jurisdictions.
"We've always worked very closely with anyone who wants to work with us, and we have very strong partners with all of our political levels and leaders and I anticipate that will continue," he said.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford issued a congratulatory statement to President-Elect Donald Trump, focusing on the two nations having important friendships "built on strong economic ties, shared values, national security interests and integrated supply chains."
He said a "Buy Can-AM" approach must be taken to create new job and business opportunities on both sides of the border.
Romano agreed, noting that integrated supply chains are the best way of doing things for both countries, and that is evident through the steel industry where raw materials are brought into Ontario, processed and sold across North America.
"Whenever you can have that maximum level of integration, the better it is," he said. "We do share a lot of commonalities in our supply chains, and we want to continue to facilitate that and grow it in the future."
Sault Ste. Marie Mayor Matthew Shoemaker said his city's relationship with Sault Ste. Marie, Mich., is sound -and can only improve.
"I think we have a need now, more than ever, to rely on them to be our chief advocates with their government. We have built up our relationship with (Sault, Mich.) and we should continue to build those relationships to have them voice that their economy would suffer if things like steel tariffs or protectionist policies are enacted," he said.
Shoemaker said a lot of Canadian dollars are spent in the U.S. and citizens work on both sides of the border, and the American sister city relies on a continued relationship between the two communities.
While discussions about imposing tariffs on Canadian steel were part of Trump's election campaign, Shoemaker said the strategy would be similar to that used last time - ensure American companies reach out to their government and voice their concerns, especially how it will impact their own economy.
Diligence and advocacy will be required to mitigate any different approaches of governance, he said.
Michigan Sault Mayor Don Gerrie agreed the new federal government will not affect relationships between the sister cities.
"We understand the importance of Sault, Ontario and we have several Memorandum of Understandings in place for mutual aid, both with economic development and police and fire and your community remains a partner," he told The Sault Star. "We will be able to work through any policy changes that develop on our borders, as we did in the past, during COVID, with the partnerships we created then to advocate and get our borders open."
He said the U.S. election did not really create a divisive community on the American side of the St. Mary's River.
"We're unique from much of the state because we are a decent size community and we're separated in the Upper Peninsula from the state and federal governments," he said.
More importantly, Gerrie said the community values its relationships with Sault, Ont.
"We benefit from your citizens, and I think your community benefits from ours as well," he said. "We share opportunities in education, in economic development and in tourism and we really want to see that grow."
Trump will be sworn in to office on Jan. 20.

Document 91 of 219

Minister says housing funds at risk under Poilievre's plan

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/minister-says-housing-funds-at-risk-under/docview/3124248654/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Minister+says+housing+funds+at+risk+under+Poilievre%27s+plan&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-11-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Housing Minister Sean Fraser is asking around 140 municipalities to speak out about his Conservative opponent's pledge to cut a federal housing program, a promise which one mayor says has raised questions about its impact on existing deals.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre announced last week that if Canadians were to elect his party, he would tackle housing affordability by slashing the GST on home purchases under $1 million.
He pledged that a future Conservative government would pay for it by cutting the Canada Housing Infrastructure Fund as well as the Housing Accelerator Fund.
The federal government says what Poilievre is talking about is cash Ottawa has already committed to provide cities through deals struck under the $4-billion program, which promises to give municipalities housing money in exchange for local officials reforming zoning and other bylaws to speed up construction.
Under existing agreements, municipalities must provide the Canadian Mortgage and Housing Corporation updates on how many building permits they issue, which could result in funding being held back if officials determine local governments are not meeting their agreed-to targets.
On Monday, Poilievre's office confirmed that the cash Ottawa has committed to provide municipalities for years to come would be cut under his proposal. Last year, the Conservative leader unveiled his own approach for incentivizing cities to speed up housing construction, pledging to give those that do a "building bonus" and to levy a penalty against those who fail to meet certain targets.
Under the existing accelerator program, the federal government has struck 178 agreements with 140 municipalities, 37 Indigenous communities and one for the province of Quebec.
In letters sent to each of those municipalities last week, Fraser warned how the money Ottawa has promised them is now at risk under Poilievre's pledge.
"I would also encourage you to take this opportunity to inform members of your community about the impact this cut would have to your plans to build more homes," Fraser wrote in one such letter to Markham, Ont., Mayor Frank Scarpitti.
London, Ont., Mayor Josh Morgan, who chairs the Big City Mayors'Caucus with the Federation of Canadian Municipalities, said Poilievre's proposal raises questions about how it "would work in practice."
His was the first city to strike a deal with Ottawa under the accelerator program.
"Cities like London and others have existing deals with the federal government to produce tangible results, and we expect to be measured on producing those results," he said in a recent interview.
"I think the questions are mainly around how would this actually play out with existing contracts and existing commitments we make. There would be some uncertainty there about the paths we're going down, the plans that we're making, the plans that we're currently executing."
Scott Aitchison, a Conservative MP from Ontario who serves as the party's housing critic, says in a statement that Poilievre's plan to cut the GST on home purchase under $1 million would provide "real relief directly to Canadians," compared to the Liberals'plan, which he said amounted to giving "billions of taxpayer dollars to municipal gatekeepers who block home building and raise taxes and fees."

Document 92 of 219

Police board wants better shoplifting plan

Author: Kelly, Brian

Publication info: Kelly, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/police-board-wants-better-shoplifting-plan/docview/3123491146/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Police+board+wants+better+shoplifting+plan&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Kelly%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More ways to fight shoplifting will be identified by Sault Ste. Marie Police Service as thefts from stores keep rising.
Reported shoplifting incidents are up 32 per cent as of August compared to last year, with 940 incidents being reported compared to 713 in 2023.
"I'd like to see us adapt and change our response locally to try and bring those numbers down because they just continue to rise," said Ward 1 Coun. Sonny Spina during a Sault Ste. Marie Police Services Board meeting on Wednesday morning. "We'd like to see a plan for those numbers to come down by a certain date."
Spina is a former officer with the municipal service.
Ward 4 Coun. Marchy Bruni said the police department must "protect" business owners.
"This can't continue," he said.
"The numbers can't continue to rise."
Stevenson told reporters additional strategies will start to be used by officers and reviewed with the police board when members meet next on Nov. 20.
"We'll come back with more strategies that we can put into the community at the municipal level," Stevenson said during the meeting. "There's always something more we can do."
The police service's community mobilization unit can go to businesses and suggest crime prevention tips, including placement of merchandise and customer access to the building.
Break-ins to homes and businesses are down 28 per cent through the first eight months of this year with 280 burglaries reported compared to 387 from January to August 2023. Stevenson suggests shoplifting is easier than breaking into a building and could explain why break-ins are down and shoplifting is up.
Stevenson backs the Ontario government's recent call to the federal government to update the Criminal Code and tighten bail legislation. The province wants mandatory minimum sentences brought back for serious crimes, ending the possibility of bail for persons charged with major offences including drug trafficking and intimate partner violence and restricting who can get conditional sentences to serve time in the community.
"It's high time for the federal government to look at this legislation," said Stevenson. "Anything more than what we have now is a good thing."
David Eby, leader of the NDP in British Columbia, wants involuntary care for those with serious addictions and mental illness. Federal Opposition Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre backs involuntary treatment for minors and prisoners dealing with mental health and addictions, but is still considering when it comes to adults.
"I would like to see more than what we have now," said Stevenson when asked if he'd back similar action by the Ontario government. "How that transpires in the province is up to them." With files from Postmedia Network btkelly@postmedia.com On X: @Saultreporter

Document 93 of 219

Provincial elections show Poilievre should ditch culture war politics

Author: Rahim, Mohamed

Publication info: Rahim, Mohamed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provincial-elections-show-poilievre-should-ditch/docview/3121802122/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provincial+elections+show+Poilievre+should+ditch+culture+war+politics&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Rahim%2C+Mohamed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has likely kept a close eye on this month's provincial election races in New Brunswick and Saskatchewan, where hot-button culture war issues like gender identity in schools haven't appeared to move the needle with voters.
But political strategists say it's incumbent fatigue, not disagreements over pronouns and single-sex spaces, that has weighed down conservative campaigns in both provinces.
"It's tough for incumbents right across Canada right now," said conservative strategist Fred De-Lorey.
DeLorey, who ran the last federal Conservative campaign in 2021, said he doesn't think that the decisive loss of the Blair Higgs-led New Brunswick Progressive Conservatives last Monday will slow down Poilievre's growing momentum in Atlantic Canada.
"We're comparing apples and pineapples," said DeLorey.
"Poilievre is hitting it out of the park right now, and his main message is affordability ... There's no cautionary tale for him (from Higgs's loss) other than that he's on the right track and shouldn't leave that track."
Voters in Saskatchewan seemed more interested in change than culture wars, with 55 per cent saying it's time for a new provincial government last week.
They went to the polls on Monday; the results were not in by press time.
The governing Saskatchewan Party has been in power for 17 years, by far the longest tenure of any government in Canada.
Saskatchewan Party Leader Scott Moe spent the campaign's home stretch on defence, after stumbling into a messy scandal involving the "outing" of two children of an NDP candidate following a changing room incident in greater Regina.
He brought up the incident during an impromptu mid-campaign announcement in which he said he'd immediately issue a directive banning "biological boys" from girls'school change rooms if re-elected - a promise that wasn't included in the Saskatchewan Party's platform.
Moe says he wasn't aware of the children's identities at the time.
Saskatoon-based pollster Lang McGilp said it wasn't a good use of Moe's time heading into the closer than usual election.
"The top four issues of the election have stayed pretty consistent: health care, affordability, taxes and education," said McGilp.
"You can debate whether (gender identity issues) fit in any of these boxes, but there's a lot of other stuff that's more top of mind with voters."
New Brunswick and Saskatchewan are the only two provinces with formal parental notification and consent policies on the books. Alberta is expected to become the third later this year.
Higgs sparked a national debate last year with his resolute defence of controversial revisions to New Brunswick's Sexual Orientation and Gender Identity policy, popularly known as Policy 713. Higgs said last summer that he was willing to call an early election over the changes, which established the first province-wide parental notification and consent requirements in Canada for student name and pronoun changes in K-12 schools.
Polling at the time showed strong support, in Atlantic Canada and beyond, for parental notification requirements, with Canadians more ambivalent on the question of parental consent.
Before the year was out, Moe would adopt similar guidelines, upping the ante by pre-emptively using the notwithstanding clause to push through his so-called Parents'Bill of Rights.
Poilievre himself trod lightly on the matter, declining to comment directly on Policy 713, but later told an ethnic media outlet that he believed "parental rights (should) come before the government's rights."
Jon Robinson, a progressive strategist based in greater Saint John, N.B., said that the split in the Progressive Conservative caucus over the changes to Policy 713 likely hurt Higgs more than the changes themselves.
"This became a very personal issue for (Higgs)," said Robinson, noting that Higgs stayed highly involved in his local church while premier and was largely aligned with the province's Christian right on the issue.
Robinson said Higgs's unwillingness to compromise on the contentious revisions reinforced already negative perceptions of his "autocratic" leadership style.
"Higgs was cast as someone where it's his way or the highway," said Robinson. "And this stuck with him."
Two members of Higgs's cabinet resigned, and two more were demoted, amidst party infighting over Policy 713.
Robinson added that controversy surrounding the nomination of Christian TV host Faytene Grasseschi to run for the Progressive Conservatives in a safe Saint John area riding exacerbated internal divisions heading into the election.
(Grasseschi narrowly lost to ex-Progressive Conservatives MLA John Herron, who ran as a Liberal.)
DeLorey said that a wave of resignations and retirements among incumbent Progressive Conservative MLAs further stacked the deck against Higgs.
"We have much smaller seats in the Maritimes than you do in the rest of the country," said DeLorey, who grew up in neighbouring Nova Scotia. "People get to know their local MLAs quite well.
"So when you have so many incumbents who don't reoffer, that's a huge blow to the campaign from the very start."
DeLorey said it would be unfair to pin the high turnover on Higgs's leadership style, noting that the veteran premier was going for an almost unheard of third term in a province with a long history of political swings.
"It's a major achievement to win two terms in New Brunswick, let alone three."
He added that Liberal leader and premier-designate Susan Holt's lack of a personal connection to Justin Trudeau gave her a boost by allowing her to credibly distance herself from the toxic Trudeau brand, something her counterpart Zach Churchill will have a harder time doing when Nova Scotians head to the polls next month.
Holt said during the campaign that she'd never met Trudeau in person. National Post rmohamed@postmedia.com

Subject: Parental rights; Provincial elections; Conservatism; Leadership; Election results; Negative campaigning; Incumbency; Voters; Gender identity; Consent

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

Document 94 of 219

Ford's bike lane promise so popular, opposition won't attack

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-bike-lane-promise-so-popular-opposition/docview/3121802044/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+bike+lane+promise+so+popular%2C+opposition+won%27t+attack&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's promise to stop the expansion of bike lanes and even rip out some existing ones is so popular that the opposition doesn't want to speak against it.
Sure, NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie have been dismissive of Ford's bike lane plans, but they haven't really attacked the idea.
Can you really blame them? Most people in this province, roughly 75 per cent, get to work in their own car or as part of a car pool. Just shy of 15 per cent commute via public transit, five per cent walk and just over one per cent commute to work or school by bike.
In January, in most of the province, that number is zero.
Meanwhile, we have bike lanes popping up in municipalities across the province, but especially in Toronto. Spend any amount of time watching the number of cars versus the number of bicycles and you'll see a massive, lopsided discrepancy.
Cars are stuck in traffic, while the odd bike that does show up whizzes past with no concerns.
What's worse, for much of the day, especially in Toronto, these bike lanes are mostly used by Uber Eats and DoorDash delivery riders. We're spending huge sums of money to subsidize getting people their burgers and butter chicken delivered faster.
Meanwhile, Doug Ford has been doubling down on the issue, saying at a speech to the Empire Club in Toronto last week that he wants to do more than just stop expansion.
"It isn't enough to keep an eye on future bike lanes," Ford said. "We need to and will remove and replace existing bike lanes on primary roads that are bringing traffic in our cities to a standstill."
Those should sound like fighting words to the NDP and the Liberals and yet, they aren't taking the bait. Pressed on the issue Thursday morning during an interview on Newstalk 1010, Stiles expressed sympathy with frustrated drivers.
"I think a lot of people are frustrated, I am too, you know, and sitting in bumper-tobumper traffic, like a lot of new listeners right now, I'm sure, and bike lanes and construction are a big part of the traffic," Stiles said.
She went on to note that the Bloor bike lane runs right through her riding without saying if she supported or opposed the bike lane. That saw host John Moore push Stiles for an answer he didn't really get.
"I guess I would ask you quite blankly, if the bike lane is in your riding, would you go to bat for it, or is the premier right that it needs to go?" Moore asked.
Stiles answered that there have been several iterations of the bike lane, that it has been tweaked and perhaps needs to be tweaked some more.
"I don't think that the premier should tear that out, I don't think it's helpful to people," she said in a less than full-throated defence.
Crombie, meanwhile, has spent most of her time answering questions on this issue claiming Ford wants to be mayor of Toronto. There's some merit in that, the premier spends far too much time focused on Toronto compared to the rest of the province, but Toronto is the biggest and most important city in the entire country, never mind the province.
But the claim that Ford should simply focus on improving public transit, as Stiles and Crombie have done, is laughable.
The Ford government is currently building Toronto's first major subway expansion in decades with the Ontario Line. They're extending the current subway system north and are putting billions into new cars for the existing Line 2.
They've pledged billions for Ottawa's LRT expansion, an LRT system expansion in Mississauga and Brampton and a new LRT line in Hamilton. They have dramatically expanded the GO Transit system of commuter trains and buses across southern Ontario faster than anyone expected.
The opposition parties aren't attacking Ford's plans for bike lanes for one simple reason - they know Ford's plans are popular with voters. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Automobiles; Public transportation; Traffic; Bicycling

Identifier / keyword: promise; expansion; lanes; existing; popular; opposition

Document 95 of 219

Municipalities often borne with responsibilities outside their jurisdiction

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/municipalities-often-borne-with-responsibilities/docview/3120959458/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Municipalities+often+borne+with+responsibilities+outside+their+jurisdiction&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-10-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: City council has chosen not to establish any form of public washroom downtown.
A staff report recommended a self-cleaning unit in Clergue Park, at the cost of $350,000. It was considered the best location and the most economically feasible alternative. Porta-potties, which come with a cheaper price tag, were also nixed.
Currently, there are no washroom facilities that are open or available to the city's most vulnerable on a 24-hour, seven-daya-week basis in Sault Ste. Marie.
But the cost was just too much for city council to swallow, despite the recognized need for such a facility.
"Downtown businesses and business owners across the city are dealing with human waste on their doorsteps," said Ward 3 Coun. Angela Caputo. She noted the issue is one of human dignity.
She is absolutely correct. But the underlying factor, once again, is not only just the cost of installing a public washroom, but also that, once again, the municipality is borne with a responsibility outside of its jurisdiction.
The same meeting provided council with confirmation that its HART Hub funding application has been submitted to the province, along with an attached letter from Mayor Matthew Shoemaker to Health Minister Sylvia Jones, reminding her of the city's significant need for provincial assistance to help curb the opioid and mental health crisis in Sault Ste. Marie.
The city's application to the province was completed by more than a dozen organizations and health-care providers, who came to the table to put their best foot forward in the hopes of being one of about 10 communities across the province who will be successful in receiving a share of $378 million of available funding.
Shoemaker reminded Jones of the most recent data from Algoma Public Health which show Sault Ste. Marie's opioid-related emergency department visits, hospitalizations and deaths are higher than the provincial average and that he and former mayor Christian Provenzano have written about the need for additional supports 11 times.
"As I mentioned to you at AMO, if this concept is your government's approach to the mental health and addictions crisis, Sault Ste. Marie needs to be at the top of the approval list," Shoemaker wrote.
This week Algoma Public Health also confirmed that the opioid crisis in the region is worsening.
In fact, it's now three times higher than that of the rest of Ontario, the data show.
It's not just about drug use. It's also about access to safe housing, employment and the other wraparound services required to help individuals lead full and productive lives.
A recent APH study revealed that the crisis is worse in Northern Ontario due to health and socioeconomic disparities between the North and south.
In other words, Northern Ontario communities are not getting their fair share of provincial government funding - in many health-care and social services streams - all of which play a factor in wellness.
Municipal governments have limited revenue streams. They also have limited responsibilities but are finding themselves more often using some of those resources as a Band-Aid solutions to traditional provincial responsibilities.
Social services were downloaded to municipalities years ago. But as social inequities and challenges have soared in recent years, municipalities are often tasked with taking on these challenges without adequate resources to do so. That's hurting municipalities and especially their vulnerable citizens who can't access services they need.
The situation is not unique to Sault Ste. Marie. Communities across Ontario - and Canada - are experiencing the same problems.
The Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) has repeatedly called on the province "to take comprehensive action on the opioid crisis, including investments in prevention, harm reduction, treatment and enforcement."
It has also urged the province to tackle the root causes of homelessness with solutions and funding to address housing, income security and health.
So, what do municipalities do in the meantime until the province antes up? They have limited alternatives other then to continue the lobby efforts and continue to stretch its revenues a little further.
It's time for the public to step up and lobby for change, lobby for more funding, lobby for more programs and services to meet the needs of citizens.
It's time for a true community united front - a campaign, if you will -in the hopes of getting some real results. Perhaps that's something city council can develop and lead the charge. A united voice is what may lead to results. The next provincial election is tentatively set for June 2026 and rumours suggest Premier Doug Ford may call an early election. It's time to hold the province's feet to the fire on this issue.
Elaine Della-Mattia is The Sault Star political reporter.

Subject: Municipal government; Public health; Responsibilities; Election results; Local elections; Jurisdiction; Mental health; Councils; Funding; Municipalities; Narcotics

Document 96 of 219

'That's where change comes'; Loud, united voices will help bring an end to violence against women, children: Advocates

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/thats-where-change-comes-loud-united-voices-will/docview/3119329455/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27That%27s+where+change+comes%27%3B+Loud%2C+united+voices+will+help+bring+an+end+to+violence+against+women%2C+children%3A+Advocates&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Every day is a challenge for Brian Sweeney, his family and friends, who loved his daughter, Angie.
Wednesday marks the one-year anniversary of the tragic circumstances where Bobby Haellart took the life of Angie, his three young children, and injured their mother Kim Rose before turning the gun on himself.
But Sweeney stands strong and was front and centre at Monday's Voices Against Violence rally, a cross-Canada rally taking a unified stand against violence to all, particularly against women and children.
"Getting this close (to the anniversary) and having a rally like this and putting it across the country is a pretty good feeling because we've made a lot of changes over the past year and this is just the first year out. We're just getting warmed up," he told media following the noon hour event Monday.
"In a way it is comforting because something good has to come out of this as opposed to the tragedy that it is," he said.
Angie's Angels and Caitlin's Heard were instrumental in lobbying the federal government to unanimously pass Bill C-332, which aims to criminalize coercive control in relationships and end intimate partner and gender-based violence. It is at the second reading in the senate.
Sweeney calls the move a huge success "but it's all about being the voice."
"We're not done yet. We're going to keep pushing until we get what we want. All we want is protection for our women and children and let's stop violence everywhere," he said.
Sweeney looks around the Roberta Bondar Pavilion at a number of tables from local groups and organizations focused on saving lives and offering support services to victims and survivors.
"I've got a granddaughter, so getting all these changes come through would make me feel more comfortable with her growing up. Changes have to happen. The way the system is now, it's not working and it's always the innocent people who end up with the short end of the stick."
Sweeney says larger voices can be achieved and those voices can be heard to make life better for all.
"Know your truth and be your voice," said Karen Byrd, of Nakehndan, or Knowing Your Truth.
Byrd said it is "absolutely imperative we speak our truth" about the injustices that currently exist in society, and especially on vulnerable victims.
Voices Against Violence rallies were held in 18 communities across the country, including Sault Ste. Marie. The date was selected to coincide with the return of the Ontario legislature. Toronto's rally was held at Queen's Park.
Sweeney said it is time to refocus on the provincial government and the need to declare Intimate Partner Violence and epidemic in Ontario.
"That's where change comes.
It comes through politics, so the louder and bigger voice that we are, the more attention we're going to get," he said.
Ward 3 Coun. Angela Caputo said she's disheartened that in 2024, the same conversation about ending violence against women and children is still occurring.
Caputo said the issue is very much political and she urged the roughly 40 attendees to "really listen with your head and your heart when politicians are speaking" and use your vote effectively to instrument the change you want to see.
Dan Jennings agreed. "It just shows that all these groups coming together shows a need for change and a need to keep people safe and for survivors to be heard and believed.
Jennings lost his daughter Caitlin in an act of IPV in London, Ont., just more than a year ago.
Jennings said the Voices against Violence rally is empowering for women, but also shows that men also support the safety for women and children.
"The more people that come out to speak about it, it takes away the power from the abusers and it helps people know that they're not alone and there is help for them," he said.
Jennings said more work needs to be done to publicize the services that are available so victims and survivors can easily access them to get the help they need to move away from abusive situations.
Jennings said the Queen's Park rally shows Premier Doug Ford and members of provincial Parliament that "we haven't gone away over the summer like they did. We're here and we're bringing (our message) across the country."

Subject: Gender-based violence; Domestic violence; Anniversaries

Document 97 of 219

Sault Ste. Marie to test bike shelters downtown

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sault-ste-marie-test-bike-shelters-downtown/docview/3118439314/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sault+Ste.+Marie+to+test+bike+shelters+downtown&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a move to encourage active transportation, a pilot project will be implemented to test out two bike shelters in the downtown core.
The pilot is as a result of June council resolution asking staff to research and report back on a possible pilot project that would see bike valets in key downtown areas of Sault Ste. Marie during summer.
Many municipalities are implementing forms of bike valets or services to secure bikes that provide peace of mind for users in safe locations.
Bicycle usage, a form of active transportation, has benefits to physical and mental health.
Various forms of temporary and permanent bike shelters are available - all with different costs and purposes.
One of the most common types is a weather resistant locker that is bolted to the ground and requires users to supply their own lock.
The report to city council states that a review and costing from five different firms concludes that Cycle Sitters, supplied by Greenspoke, an Ontario manufacturer, is the most economical and least costly from both a cost and operational maintenance standpoint.
City staff recommend that the pilot move forward if the city's community development fund - green initiatives fund - accepts the application for $10,625.29 -to cover the costs of the two cycle sitters and padlocks.
A second council report asks council to support four projects reviewed by the city's Environmental Sustainability Committee, including the application for funding for the bike locker pilot project.
The pilot will see one unit installed near the Sault Ste. Marie Public Library and the other near the Queen Street Commons/King Street Alley.
Each locker will accommodate two individual bikes and users will supply their own locks.
The library and Outspoken Brewing Company will support the pilot by locking them up at the end of the day and unlocking them for use in the morning.
Meanwhile, in a separate resolution, city council has been asked by the Sault Trails Advocacy Committee to pen a letter to Premier Doug Ford and Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, expressing the city's opposition to proposed legislation that would restrict the construction of new bike lanes in Ontario and limit where and how municipalities can build new bike lanes.
Under the proposed provincial legislation, municipalities would be required to get provincial approval before building any new bike lanes that reduce lanes of vehicular traffic. Municipalities would also be forced to demonstrate that the proposed new bike lanes won't have a negative impact on vehicle traffic.
The Association of Municipalities of Ontario has said "it is unclear how the Ministry of Transportation will be in a better position to make decisions about local matters."
STAC Chair Donna Hilsinger says the proposed legislation would be detrimental to the Sault and its new Soo Moves Active Transportation Master Plan, which is expected to receive council's final approval prior to year end.
"The goals are to enhance safety and accessibility, educate and encourage adoption of active transportation, create connectivity and ensure transportation equity," a letter to the mayor reads. "Communities in our province must not be restricted in how their active transportation infrastructure is designed and implemented."
STAC also requests that the letter also be sent to Sault MPP Ross Romano to oppose the attempt to override municipal authority over local transportation infrastructure and choices.

Subject: Legislation; Councils; Pilot projects

Identifier / keyword: encourage; active; transportation; pilot; project; implemented

Title: Sault Ste. Marie to test bike shelters downtown

Publication title: Sault Star; Sault Ste. Marie, Ont.

First page: A.3

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 19, 2024

Section: Local

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Sault Ste. Marie, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 0839203X

Source type: Newspaper

Document 98 of 219

Local NDP riding association seeks candidate for next federal election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/local-ndp-riding-association-seeks-candidate-next/docview/3117816659/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Local+NDP+riding+association+seeks+candidate+for+next+federal+election&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Sault Ste. Marie-Algoma NDP association has opened nominations for candidates for the next federal election.
The new federal electoral boundary for Sault Ste. Marie - Algoma marks significant changes for Northern Ontario. Stretching to the north to include Wawa and Dubreuilville, the new riding now covers St. Joseph Island, Thessalon, Blind River, Elliot Lake, Spanish, and extends eastward to parts of Sudbury.
The NDP is inviting "passionate and progressive" individuals to seek nomination as the party's candidate.
"As a community-focused, people-first party, the NDP is committed to building a more equitable and inclusive Canada by addressing the real issues Canadians face: affordability, building safer communities, and strengthening health care," a press release states.
For more information or to submit your candidacy, contact Sault Ste. Marie - Algoma Federal NDP Riding Association by emailing NDP.SSM.ALGOMA@GMAIL. COM The Sault Star

Document 99 of 219

Alberta conservatives have 'moved the dial'

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alberta-conservatives-have-moved-dial/docview/3117816503/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta+conservatives+have+%27moved+the+dial%27&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: These are triumphal times for bedrock Alberta conservatives.
Even their enemies can't doubt it: Conservatives have "moved the dial" on every key issue, from tough sovereignty-based stands against Ottawa to health-care reform and other issues.
Twenty years ago, then-PC premier Ralph Klein almost got run out of the province for suggesting a Third Way in health care through some private options.
Now, the door to private care swings wide open.
This political landscape seemed fantastical only a decade ago.
Ted Morton, ex-PC finance minister who ran afoul of then-premier Ed Stelmach, is getting a hero's welcome at events plugging his new autobiography, Strong and Free.
In the introduction, Morton wrote, "Suddenly, many of the Alberta Agenda - a.k.a. "Firewall" - policies and ideas I had championed unsuccessfully had now been supported by two successive conservative majority governments.
"... Reforms that were on the periphery of Alberta provincial politics 20 years ago are now front and centre."
Morton was referring to the "Firewall Letter" he co-authored in 2001 with Stephen Harper and other prominent conservatives. They sent it to Klein, who squirmed.
The letter shocked the country. Critics called it extremist, un-Canadian, and other names far less civil.
Today, the key measures are United Conservative Party policy, reality or aspiration: provincial pension, provincial police, massive shakeup to health care, sovereignty-based legislation barring municipalities from dealing with Ottawa, and more.
Every revolution has its sacrifices. Morton ran for the PC leadership twice in 2006 and 2011, losing both times.
The Alberta centre would hold through Alison Redford, the brief and tragic tenure of Jim Prentice, and the government of Rachel Notley's NDP.
Then conservative forces merged as the UCP under Jason Kenney in 2017. The fiercest battles were now internal. In 2022, they culminated in Kenney's resignation and Danielle Smith's shocking capture of the UCP leadership.
Smith's political life had seemed to be over after she and most Wildrose MLAs jumped to the PCs in 2014.
But she became premier by harnessing the power Morton began to unleash in 2001.
One interesting character in all this is Rob Anderson, who became Smith's chief of staff this week. A close friend of the premier, he's now the second-most powerful political person in Alberta.
As a young lawyer and PC MLA, he always advocated fierce resistance to Ottawa.
In 2010, he crossing the floor to Wildrose with fellow MLA Heather Forsyth.
Though not obvious then, they had launched a decade0-long internecine conservative battle.
But in 2014, he went along with Smith to join the PCs.
"When I first broke into politics I was very idealistic and a bit naive," Anderson says now.
"I certainly did take a lot of chances and made a lot of mistakes. The floor-crossing from Wildrose was a massive misjudgment. But you learn from these things, and hopefully I'm a better person and public servant because of it."
In 2021, he did something that proved crucial to Smith's ultimate success.
With Barry Cooper and Derek From, Anderson wrote the Free Alberta Strategy. It called for an Alberta Sovereignty Act, raising separation as a legitimate last resort if no settlement with Ottawa could be reached.
Smith latched onto the sovereignty agenda and plunged into the UCP leadership race. Her stance consumed the campaign, sharply separating her from the other candidates.
Anderson insists he didn't have that much influence.
"There were a lot of good ideas out there. How many papers have been written on this? You know, dozens and dozens.
"She took not just the Free Alberta strategy, but the Firewall letter and many other thoughts on this, combined them and made them her own, and she's been very successful with it."
Ted Morton, Danielle Smith and Rob Anderson were dismissed as fringe figures for years. Now they've won, and don't plan to lose again. Don Braid's column appears regularly in the Herald. X: @DonBraid

Subject: Conservatism; Health care policy; Politics; Sovereignty

Identifier / keyword: triumphal; times; bedrock; alberta; conservatives; enemies

Document 100 of 219

Carbon tax costs you more, PBO report shows

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carbon-tax-costs-you-more-pbo-report-shows/docview/3117816211/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carbon+tax+costs+you+more%2C+PBO+report+shows&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau's carbon tax will cost most Canadians more, lower your income and shrink our economy.
Those are the conclusions of the Parliamentary Budget Officer (PBO) after looking at the carbon tax's fiscal and total economic impact.
The PBO found that by 2030, the bottom 40 per cent of income earners will see a net benefit from the carbon tax and rebates- but everyone else will pay more.
In Ontario, the estimate is paying an extra $588 to $3,647 a year.
Since the Trudeau government announced their carbon tax plan, they've claimed eight out of 10 families will get back more in rebates than they pay in tax. While the PBO finds that generally accurate, the total economic impact is negative.
It's the second PBO report to find this.
The office's previous report in March 2023 showing a similar problem, that most were paying more. This year, the PBO said they would correct an error in the report.
The Trudeau Liberals had never really acknowledged the report. They just kept repeating that eight out of 10 got more in rebates than they paid in "carbon pricing."
"When the economic impact of the federal fuel charge is combined with the fiscal impact, the net cost increases for the average household across all income quintiles, reflecting the overall negative economic impact of the fuel charge," the PBO's revised report said.
"In 2030-31, taking into consideration both fiscal and economic impacts, we estimate the average household in each of the backstop provinces will see a net cost, paying more in the federal fuel charge and GST, as well as receiving lower incomes (due to the fuel charge), compared to the Canada Carbon Rebate they receive."
Not to sound like Chandler from Friends but, "Could they be any clearer?" Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault tried to skate past the report's findings. claiming most people were better off due to the carbon tax. But that's not what the report said.
"Now's the time to clear the air on Pierre Poilievre's big lie to Canadians," Guilbeault said to reporters before question period Thursday. "He's been misleading Canadians, the PBO is very clear. More Canadians get money back from the Canada Carbon Rebate than what they pay."
That's not what the PBO said, and once again the Liberals are lying to the public, aided and abetted by a media that won't tell the full truth. This tax is clearly costing more for the average family than they will get back in rebates.
Even the way the rebates are calculated should give people pause. Everything is calculated on StatsCan averages, meaning the rebate is calculated on the average Canadian driving 15,000 kilometres a year or less.
"That's why we need a carbon tax election where Canadians can choose between a 61 cents a litre carbon tax or axing the carbon tax altogether," Poilievre said Thursday.
The PBO report shows the Liberals are lying, and they want to claim it shows Poilievre is fudging. Trudeau and his gang can't be trusted. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Rebates; Canadians; Economic impact; Carbon; Environmental tax; Tax elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates Economic impact Environmental tax Tax elections

Location: Canada

Document 101 of 219

Timing around India affair raises flags

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/timing-around-india-affair-raises-flags/docview/3117816167/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Timing+around+India+affair+raises+flags&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Monday, Canada announced the expulsion of six Indian diplomats over allegations of foreign interference. In a news conference, RCMP Commissioner Michael Duheme warned of widespread violence, homicides and a public security threat linked to agents of the Indian government. New Delhi has rejected the allegations, and sent six Canadian diplomats packing in return.
The accusations are appalling, and no country should be targeting the citizens of another on foreign soil. But why are these revelations coming out now? On the surface, they provide a great distraction from the weekend's attempted internal coup on the prime minister. The story of a revolt among Liberal MPs, 30 of whom apparently signed a letter asking the PM to quit, hit the news cycle on Saturday. Then, presto: on Monday, the RCMP dropped its bombshell, allowing the PM to take to the airwaves and sound all grave and solemn and prime minister-like, defending Canadian sovereignty.
But that would be too obvious. So let's look at what else is going on in Ottawa, namely the Hogue commission on foreign interference. On Friday, the commission heard from Public Safety Minister Bill Blair that his office sat for 54 days on a warrant to investigate Ontario Liberal MPP Michael Chan, accused of doing China's bidding in Canada. Blair offered "no explanation" for the delay, and neither did his chief of staff.
Three days later, boom: India stands accused of being the major agent of transnational repression in Canada and the focus switches from the government's failings to the murderous machinations of New Delhi. And who is responsible for ensuring that the RCMP is "effective, accountable and addresses the government's priorities?" You guessed it: Bill Blair.
According to Chapter 5 of the 2023 NSICOP report on the federal policing mandate of the RCMP, while there exists a core concept of police independence, the minister provides direction on "national security investigations in sensitive sectors," including issuing directives to the RCMP "to implement special measures when investigating terrorist offences or activities which have an impact on fundamental institutions in Canadian society, including academia, politics, religion, the media and trade unions."
I'm not saying India isn't engaged in transnational repression, but the timing of this exposure is highly convenient for both the minister and the government. It's also highly convenient for China, which relishes any chance to bash India, its chief rival for dominance of the Indo-Pacific.
For whom is it inconvenient? Not just India.
It's also inconvenient for Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives. Left-wing media outlets made noise last month about alleged Indian efforts to build a "war chest" of funds to disparage Trudeau and help elect Poilievre. The same month, the Hindustan Times reported that Poilievre told Canadian Hindu media that, "Anti-Hindu and Hinduphobia agendas have no place (in Canada). I detest how the prime minister has divided our people. We were getting along in this country. Now look, everybody is fighting."
And then there's NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, who, until August, was Trudeau's loyal coalition supporter. When he was deputy leader of the Ontario NDP in 2015, Singh spoke at a Sikh "sovereignty rally" in San Francisco that venerated violent Khalistani leader Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale, and accused India of "trying to wipe us out." In an interview on a Punjabi station in Canada in 2018, when asked to comment on Khalistani separatist referendums, Singh said, "In any place if people seek freedom, it is their right to seek freedom."
Why is India apparently willing to kill to crush the Khalistani movement? Sikhs have been demanding a separate state in the Punjab since India was partitioned in 1947. In the 1980s, the Khalistanis engaged in major acts of terrorism, including the bombing of Air India Flight 182 in 1985, which killed 329 people and was attributed to Sikh extremists in Canada. Today, Khalistanis regularly hold referendums in the U.S. and Canada on creating a separate state, with the goal holding an independence vote in the Punjab in 2025.
None of this is an excuse for murdering Canadian citizens. But nor should it be an excuse for this government to play diaspora politics and shield its own misdeeds. Postmedia News Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Referendums; Murders & murder attempts; Politics; Prime ministers; Transnationalism; Sovereignty

Location: China; Canada; India

Document 102 of 219

UWindsor must rescind deal with protesters

Author: Cooke, Dave

Publication info: Cooke, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/uwindsor-must-rescind-deal-with-protesters/docview/3115917945/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=UWindsor+must+rescind+deal+with+protesters&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Cooke%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I have been a proud member of the University of Windsor community since 1971, when I attended as a student and graduated in 1975.
Two years after that, I was elected to the Ontario legislature as the NDP MPP for Windsor-Riverside.
Later, as minister of education and training in the Bob Rae government, I was responsible for all levels of elementary, secondary and post-secondary education.
After leaving the political world, I was invited to join the university's board of governors in 2000, serving as chair from 2007-09. While we encountered some challenging times during those years, I always felt proud of our university.
This year has been different.
I am not only deeply disappointed, but also angry and embarrassed about the University of Windsor's negotiated agreement with pro-Palestinian protesters who established an encampment on the university grounds.
That decision has outraged not just the members of our Jewish community. It also has caused people of all faiths, from all walks of life, to become angry and disillusioned.
To read this agreement is to see that not only is it one-sided, it does not even acknowledge the terrorist attack of Oct. 7, 2023, in Israel by Hamas. And while the agreement calls for a ceasefire - which has widespread support - it makes no mention of releasing the hostages taken, or the 1,200 Jews who were murdered that day.
The agreement the university struck with pro-Palestinian protesters essentially blames all Jews for decisions made by Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in the aftermath of the Oct. 7 assault by Hamas. An example of that is the sanctioning of Israeli academic institutions.
UWindsor president Robert Gordon exercised poor judgment entering into this agreement. As an afterthought, he offered to draft a similar agreement with the Jewish community. The Jewish community wisely declined.
When questioned, the university released a statement saying Gordon did not first come to the board of governors because he considered it an "operational matter."
At that point, the chair should have insisted it be brought to the board for discussion.
While he was out of the country, it was announced Gordon would resign at the end of the academic year.
A few weeks earlier, chancellor Mary Jo Haddad indicated she would be leaving in the middle of her second term.
The search for new senior leadership will be difficult because of the extreme damage that has been done to the university's reputation.
In fact, if these agreements are not rescinded by the board of governors, finding suitable candidates for the president's job might be impossible. These agreements will be an albatross around the neck, a reputational burden that will require a new president's utmost attention, with precious time spent dealing with this debacle.
As an alumnus, as well as a former board chair, and as someone who cares deeply about the future of the University of Windsor, I implore the current chair and all members of the board of governors to do the right thing.
Rescind these agreements.
Take the first steps to healing. Give the new president a chance for success. Students and the community are counting on you. Dave Cooke is a former University of Windsor board of governors chair, a former Windsor MPP and was Ontario's minister of education from 1993-95.

Document 103 of 219

Arrogance blinds feds to hunger for tax cuts

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/arrogance-blinds-feds-hunger-tax-cuts/docview/3113194830/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Arrogance+blinds+feds+to+hunger+for+tax+cuts&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-10-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It can get tiresome, watching Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre pop up in Parliament to cry "Axe the tax," and "we need a carbon tax election."
But he's smart to keep pounding the same stake into Liberal hearts. High taxes really are a huge issue countrywide, and every politician facing election knows it. The mystery is why the Liberals don't.
Signs of taxation toxicity abound in three provincial election campaigns now underway in British Columbia, New Brunswick and Saskatchewan.
New Brunswick PC Premier Blaine Higgs offers one key promise before voting day, Oct. 21.
He would lower the Harmonized Sales Tax by two points, to 13 per cent.
The Higgs tax cut would apparently cost the treasury $1.6 billion a year. That would be a direct benefit to taxpayers.
Poilievre told the Commons on Thursday federal taxes are depriving New Brunswick of funding for hospitals and schools He demanded a carbon tax election.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau gave the rote response: The Conservatives don't care about climate change, and the carbon tax works.
In B.C., NDP Premier David Eby made the most striking tax promise of all even before he called the election now set for Oct. 19.
He promised to ditch the province's consumer carbon tax, but only if Ottawa agrees not to impose the federal tax in its place.
That provincial tax, imposed in 2008, was the first of its kind in North America.
There's not the slightest chance Ottawa would give B.C. a blanket tax exemption. They'd have to do the same for Alberta and Ontario, for starters.
Eby hasn't answered the crucial question: Will he keep this promise if Poilievre wins the federal election and abolishes the federal tax? That pledge was just wind and everyone knew it. Its only value is to show how angry B.C. voters are about taxation levels.
With the campaign underway, Eby now comes up with a real-world promise. He would cut provincial income tax by $1,000 for an average family, starting next year.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith won a provincial election last year on just such a promise.
Her campaign gave the impression that the tax break would come in the next budget. But Smith reneged. The budget deferred the full break until 2027.
That made people very angry. She now promises to keep the promise soon.
The message was obvious.
In these times, a tax promise is the most dangerous one to break.
The Saskatchewan drive to an election Oct. 28 begins with duelling tax offers from both the ruling Saskatchewan Party and the NDP.
Premier Scott Moe promises income tax cuts that would save families about $1,000 a year for four years.
NDP Leader Carla Beck responds with a plan to suspend provincial gasoline tax collection for six months, and remove provincial sales tax from children's clothes and ready-to-eat foods.
The promises are all over the place, but politicians who fail to make them are doomed.
Liberal arrogance blinds them to reality. Don Braid's column appears regularly with Postmedia X: @DonBraid

Subject: Taxation; Provincial elections; Tax cuts; Sales taxes; Carbon; Income taxes; Environmental tax; Tax elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxation Tax cuts Sales taxes Income taxes Environmental tax Tax elections

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

Title: Arrogance blinds feds to hunger for tax cuts
