Document 104 of 219

Ford's tunnel plan a way to change channel

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-tunnel-plan-way-change-channel/docview/3113194714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+tunnel+plan+a+way+to+change+channel&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-10-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Changing the channel is a pretty familiar gambit. Predictable, even. When a public figure lands in the hot seat, expect them to schedule a funding announcement, unveil a big new plan or identify a threat; anything to change focus.
Remember when Donald Trump tried to "buy" Greenland? Six days before that story broke, a whistleblower filed a complaint detailing how the former U.S. president illegally pressured Ukraine to investigate his political rival, Joe Biden. By Christmas, the House would vote to impeach Trump. Nevertheless, news outlets diligently reported on Greenland's geopolitical importance, the potential for mineral extraction beneath its melting ice, and the consternation of the Danish prime minister, who seemed astonished to have to say: "Greenland is not for sale."
By now, you'd think we'd be wise to this timeworn tactic. Yet here we are, reporting on a mega-tunnel under Highway 401.
Not that we have much choice. Premier Doug Ford has proposed - indeed, promised - to tunnel under the busiest highway in North America. Like it or not, it's news. Ford suggested new vehicle lanes and transit could stretch from Mississauga to Markham. That's about 55 kilometres.
In quick succession, the premier announced both a feasibility study, and a determination to forge ahead regardless of what the study might say. This is a man desperate to change the channel.
Still, it must be reported.
Journalists are diligently putting Ford's "visionary" promise in perspective. They've plumbed comparisons to Boston's Big Dig, which delivered 12 km of underground highway for $14.8 billion. They've reported the reactions of other party leaders, from "silly" to "a complete joke." They've called on experts to test Ford's assumption that adding vehicle lanes will, in fact, relieve traffic congestion.
The argument for more and bigger highways is an enduring zombie of public policy, repeatedly shot down but very hard to kill. Former New York City traffic commissioner Sam Schwartz told CBC News what study after study has shown: "the more lanes you introduce, the more people are likely to drive."
Schwartz described proven alternatives, such as transit investments and high-occupancy toll lanes, which relieve congestion by reducing traffic. Even building transit down the centre of the 401 would make more sense than what Ford's proposing.
Meanwhile, away from the cameras, the RCMP continues its criminal investigation into the Greenbelt scandal.
Two provincial watchdogs already sharply criticized the government's improperly managed site selection process, which stood to enrich a handful of developers to the tune of $8.28 billion. The premier's office has denied any involvement in choosing specific sites for development. Ultimately Ford reversed course, leaving the Greenbelt intact.
During the summer, this government commanded a striking number of headlines, including funding announcements, alcohol in corner stores, a retreat on supervised consumption sites, and shuttering the Ontario Science Centre. Some of these actions are just the business of governing, and some may be unnecessary and ill-advised, but the premier kept that remote firmly in his grasp.
Through it all, the media reported on hints of an early election, perhaps next spring. Ontario could go to the polls ahead of the results of what is a criminal investigation. This government has no business contemplating an election before voters have answers.
A mega-tunnel under the 401 may strike voters as decisive, out-of-the-box thinking, or a boondoggle in the making. It's actually something else.
This is our Greenland, folks. write.robin@baranyai.ca

Subject: Voters; Roads & highways

Location: Greenland

Document 105 of 219

Crombie musing on taxes gives Ford fodder

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-musing-on-taxes-gives-ford-fodder/docview/3112703655/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+musing+on+taxes+gives+Ford+fodder&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Ontario Premier Doug Ford's PC Party starts running ads claiming Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie wants to hike taxes and give people money to sit at home, it will be her own fault.
Crombie gave a wide-ranging interview on TVO's Onpoli podcast last week and it's filled with the kind of statements on which her political opponents will pounce.
In her interview, hosts Steve Paikin and John Michael McGrath asked Crombie about her position on a number of provincial policies. It's her statements in favour of bringing back the universal basic income program and increasing the provincial sales tax to give municipalities more money will catch the attention of the PC Party.
"Do we give them the suite of tools? Do we look at a provincial-wide new tool like an RST?" Crombie asked when questioned about providing more funding to municipalities.
Crombie called the provincial sales tax an RST, or retail sales tax, but the province's sales tax is harmonized with the federal GST and is charged on a wide range of goods and services.
Last year, the tax brought in more than $36 billion to the provincial treasury, second only to personal income tax in terms of funds generated. The tax accounted for 26 per cent of the province's tax revenue and 19 per cent of total revenue, according to the recently released accounts.
If the province simply were to give municipalities one per cent of the existing sales tax revenue, that would cost the treasury more than $4.5 billion. That means, for this idea to work, Ontario's portion of the HST would need to increase from eight per cent to nine per cent and the total HST charged would need to increase from 13 per cent to 14 per cent.
It's doubtful most voters would be enthusiastic about an increase to the tax rate.
To be clear, Crombie didn't say she supports the idea, but did muse about it. That will be enough for Ford.
Added to the comments she made to the Empire Club a few months ago about bringing back licence plate sticker renewal fees and hiking gas taxes and there is a pattern of Crombie talking about increasing taxes.
"I've been thinking about what exactly the government is spending money on other than gimmicks and freezing the gas tax and giving you back a rebate for your stickers and making them so you don't have to pay for them and that's really reducing the fiscal capacity," Crombie said back in April.
"And I keep thinking, would people really miss that? Or is that money that could be better invested in education and our health-care system?" In her TVO interview, Crombie was asked about the Liberal government of Kathleen Wynne and its program to pilot a universal basic income program.
"I think it's a great idea, and I think it would save taxpayers money," Crombie told TVO.
By endorsing that concept, while also musing about increasing taxes paid by those who do work, Crombie is setting herself up to be a political piñata for Ford. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 106 of 219

Why we should consider Ford's tunnel idea

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-we-should-consider-fords-tunnel-idea/docview/3110869317/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+we+should+consider+Ford%27s+tunnel+idea&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford wants to build a tunnel under Highway 401 to relieve congestion.
There was no cost provided for building the highway or the feasibility study, but Ford spoke Wednesday as if the underground highway was getting built.
"We're going to get the job done, mark my words," Ford said.
The idea is for a tunnel for passenger vehicles and transit from Mississauga to Markham. Ford said one of the reasons for conducting a feasibility study was to determine the length of the project.
He said the cost of doing nothing and keeping people stuck in gridlock also comes with a steep price.
"You have to look at the cost of $11 billion a year it's costing our economy," Ford said.
The idea was immediately panned by the opposition parties at Queen's Park.
"This is a serious issue, gridlock, congestion, but this is not a serious proposal. This is not a serious Premier. It is a silly thought from a government that has run out of ideas," NDP Leader Marit Stiles said.
Stiles compared Ford's plan to Boston's Big Dig, which was years over deadline and significantly over budget. While the Big Dig had its problems, there are long, successful highway tunnels.
Norway has the Laerdal and Folgefonna tunnels, Japan has the Yamate tunnel, while Australia has the WestConnex around Sydney. These are all examples we could look to, but Stiles and other opposition leaders seem to want to throw up their hands and offer no solutions.
"We need to get transit built in a big way," Stiles said.
Perhaps Stiles didn't hear, part of the feasibility study is for a public transit component.
Beyond that, the Ford government is on a major transit expansion with a new subway line in Toronto, subway extensions north of the city, the Finch LRT, taking the LRT in Mississauga to Brampton and planning for an LRT in Hamilton. If Ottawa ever has a real return-towork plan, there will be LRT expansion there, as well.
Transit is part of the solution, but it is already being built; something needs to be done about roadways.
"I really think that this is a pipe dream of an idea," Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said.
Crombie suggested Highway 407 could be used, perhaps by allowing trucks on the highway without paying tolls. That could help, but it is by no means the solution to what is happening on highways in and around Toronto.
Crombie, like Stiles, opposes this project and they both oppose building Highway 413.
Along with Green Leader Mike Schreiner, they are the collective party of no. They don't want anything built; they believe we can add massively to the population, and we can get around with more bike lanes and subways.
That's not realistic. Perhaps Ford's tunnel plan isn't the right option - maybe there is a fatal flaw in the idea - but at least he's coming up with possible solutions.
So, let's study the idea.
blilley@postmedia.com

Business indexing term: Subject: Feasibility studies

Document 107 of 219

Sault Ste. Marie to be site of Voices Against Violence cross- Canada rally

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sault-ste-marie-be-site-voices-against-violence/docview/3107651841/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sault+Ste.+Marie+to+be+site+of+Voices+Against+Violence+cross-+Canada+rally&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-09-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Cait Alexander calls herself a victim of the Canadian justice system and she's hoping that others across Canada will join her to speak out against violence against women and children and urge the government to implement legislation that better protects victims.
Alexander's ex-boyfriend was accused of trying to kill her in 2021, but the case was ultimately withdrawn, a restraining order was granted, and Alexander has been fighting for justice ever since.
She calls Canada's justice system "one that has been broken for decades" and the statistics of increasing cases of IPV, sexual assaults and murders back up her claims.
"How can the government not take a stand against this by changing laws and making sure us survivors are safe?" she said.
Alexander argues she is only one of many women who have suffered gender-based violence and had a court case tossed with a simple peace bond while her accused continues to walk the streets of Canada.
Dismissed in July after initially being called to Ottawa to speak at a committee on the status of women, Alexander is digging in her heals and wants government to know that victims of intimate partner violence won't go away.
In fact, their voices are getting louder.
Voices Against Violence is a cross-Canada rally that will be held across the country at noon in each respective time zone on Mon. Oct. 21 to take a unified stand against all violence, particularly against women and children in Canada.
"These issues need to be brought forward again and again until government responds by changing the laws and making sure that survivors are heard, and more lives are not lost," Alexander told The Sault Star in a telephone interview. "We are not to be ignored."
Sault Ste. Marie will be one of the rally locations in Ontario, along with Ottawa, Toronto and London. The Sault rally will be held on the steps at the Ronald A. Irwin Civic Centre.
There are currently 18 scheduled rally locations for the event, which includes advocates and non-profit organizers and sponsors including Sault Ste. Marie based Angie's Angels and Caitlin's Heard.
"Sault Ste. Marie has become a voice, a hot point speaking out against IPV," said Dan Jennings, whose daughter was murdered by her partner in London more than a year ago. He is still awaiting the trial of the accused.
Angie Sweeney was killed by her former partner Bob Thomas Halleart in October 2023. Halleart then went to a second address and killed his three children, as well as injured his ex-wife Kim Rose, before turning the gun on himself.
Since the tragic incident that sent waves of shock across Sault Ste. Marie and beyond, many communities, including the Sault, have asked the province to declare intimate partner violence an epidemic.
"People in Ontario are upset the provincial government hasn't declared IPV an epidemic and we're hearing more and more of these kinds of cases, where needless deaths are occurring," Jennings told The Sault Star.
Most recently, Nova Scotia unanimously declared IPV an epidemic with lightning-fast passage of the bill introduced by the opposition NDP. The bill was passed with all three readings, and all-party support in just under 10 minutes.
Jennings said in Sault Ste.
Marie, organizers are hoping that the public rallies around victims and shows a strong sense of solidarity, which will ultimately urge the Ontario government to declare IPV an epidemic.
Alexander said this rally follows one held in Australia that resulted in the gathering of 100,000 people giving government the message "enough is enough."
"We need actionable steps, and we will do that by uniting, healing in solidarity," Alexander said.
The rally will focus on survivors sharing their stories, although it is anticipated elected government members and heads of law enforcement agencies will also be present.
"This is about survivors first," Alexander said. "We have a general, common agenda that each community can follow, and make it their own depending on what is important in their community, but survivors don't prescribe to party politics. We want the survivor side to influence and better the justice system in any platform, or party colour that will listen and take action."
Voices Against Violence is a non-profit national coalition of dedicated advocates committed to eradicating violence in all forms, but particularly against women and children in Canada.
The event is designed to raise awareness, demand action and push for legislative changes to protect vulnerable populations. It's motto is United. Protect. Love.
Supporters are invited to bring loved ones, create a sign and unite for a safer Canada.
"Your involvement will be crucial in making this rally a significant and impactful event," the notice reads. Locations of the events in each community, and registration is available on the website www.voicesrally.org. A GoFundMe campaign has also been launched to help with the costs of the rally. It can be found at https://www.gofundme.com/f/voices-against-violence-rally.

Document 108 of 219

Poilievre says he won't introduce anti-union policies; Labour chief says it's hard to take Conservative leader at his word

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-says-he-wont-introduce-anti-union/docview/3104764189/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+says+he+won%27t+introduce+anti-union+policies%3B+Labour+chief+says+it%27s+hard+to+take+Conservative+leader+at+his+word&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-09-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is pledging not to introduce the kind of union-opposed legislation he championed as a Harper government member of Parliament if he becomes prime minister.
The surprising pledge is part of what he calls an evolution in his thinking on labour unions.
And it comes as Air Canada is prepared to start shutting down its operations beginning Sunday if the Air Line Pilots Association issues a 72-hour strike notice. There has been pressure from business groups across the country for the federal government to intervene and prevent the strike.
In 2012, Poilievre and the rest of the Conservative Harper government brought in back to work legislation for those same pilots before they even took to picket lines. He said this week the pilots are negotiating for better wages and argued nothing should stand in the way of that.
In a statement to Postmedia, Poilievre said he is now pro-union and wouldn't introduce those bills again.
"Hundreds of visits to workshops, factories, union halls and training centres have taught me a lot and helped me evolve my thinking," he said. "A Common Sense Conservative Government will not introduce or pass bills C-377, C-525 or right to work laws. Period. This commitment will be written in my election platform."
Poilievre was largely silent on the threat of a work stoppage at CN and CPKC rail companies, which the Liberals sent to binding arbitration after a one-day strike last month.
Poilievre heaped blame on the Liberals and NDP for driving up the cost of living over the last nine years and said there is clearly a need for unions.
"Billionaires and multinationals have gained power and wealth from the money-printing inflation that balloons their assets while robbing buying power from workers after nine years of the NDP-Liberals," he said. "It will take strong unions to reverse these losses and to fight for wage and pension gains and better working conditions."
Poilievre also pointed to private member's bills his MPs have championed including one requiring pensions take precedence in any bankruptcy, a bill requiring airlines to play flight attendants for work before the plane takes off and his call for tariffs on Chinese made steel.
"I don't understand why it is that Canadian pilots are paid so much worse than American pilots," he said this week. "I would call on Air Canada to negotiate in good faith with the pilots. We're not going to support preempting those negotiations."
While they were in government, the Harper Conservatives also brought in legislation that was denounced by labour groups. Bill C-377 required unions to publicly disclose their finances and Bill C-525, which changed the voting process for joining a union, requiring a secret ballot and a higher threshold for success.
Those bills both passed as private member's legislation while the Conservatives were in power, but were repealed in 2016 after the Liberals came into office. Poilievre spoke out against the repeal, arguing the government was forcing people into unions by taking away the secret ballot.
"The government's problem is with the outcome. The government might not be happy that when workers are given the choice through a democratic vote, they opt not to unionize. However, that is the choice of the workers, not the choice of the government," he said in Parliament in 2016.
The Conservatives'most recent policy book, amended at last year's convention, includes commitments to those policies, but Poilievre says a Conservative government he leads won't introduce them.
He also pointed out that his party recently voted for the government's anti-replacement worker legislation - commonly referred to as an "anti-scab" bill.
Siobhán Vipond, executive vice-president with the Canadian Labour Congress, said it's hard to take Poilievre at his word on this issue, because he has such a long record of anti-union behaviour.
"We're very wary about this idea of words that are empty if you don't fundamentally understand the role that unions and workers have in a country right now," she said. "It's just really hard to trust that, knowing when you actually had the power, what you did with it."
Vipond said even just a month ago Poilievre was silent when the government sent railworkers to binding arbitration after a oneday lockout.
"We're not talking historical. It was a month ago, the railworkers weren't worth speaking up for a month ago," she said. "If that is what he's going to do, we're going to need to see more than one opportunistic statement in the media."
Vipond said when employers believe the government is prepared to legislate back to work, they are much less likely to negotiate.
Dan Robertson, a former chief strategist for the Conservative party, said unions were considered an adversary to the party and not worth pursuing previously but that changed over time.
"Five or six years ago, there was just a 'unions are bad'opinion that was pervasive in the Conservative movement and party, and it was very, very difficult to overcome," he said.
Robertson said that changed when Conservatives looked closer and realized whatever the views of union leadership, blue-collar union workers shared many of the same values as party members.
"Conservatives recognize that communities need strong institutions and among them are labour unions. They provide workers with power. They afford solidarity, mutual aid and representation all of which benefits workers and their communities," he said.
In the 2022 election, several private sector unions endorsed Ontario Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative party, Robertson said that was proof this approach could also benefit Conservatives electorally.
He said there are still Conservatives upset at the party's outreach to labour, but they can't argue with the results. rtumilty@postmedia.com

Document 109 of 219

'We are not the Justin Trudeau Party of Canada'; Liberal lawmaker thinks prime minister staying on hurts his party's chances

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-are-not-justin-trudeau-party-canada-liberal/docview/3101776598/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+are+not+the+Justin+Trudeau+Party+of+Canada%27%3B+Liberal+lawmaker+thinks+prime+minister+staying+on+hurts+his+party%27s+chances&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-09-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Liberals set to gather next week for a caucus retreat, one next week for a caucus retreat, one of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's MPs is once again calling for a change in party leadership.
New Brunswick Liberal MP Wayne Long first called for a change of leadership after the Liberals'loss in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection in June. The downtown Toronto seat had been considered a safe seat for the party and the loss to the Conservatives led to many whispered calls for change.
Long was the only sitting MP to publicly urge Trudeau to step down at the time. This week, he said he still feels it's time for the prime minister to go after nine years in office.
"I haven't changed my position one iota. The prime minister does need to step down for the good of our party and for the good of the country," he said. "We are not the Justin Trudeau Party of Canada. We are the Liberal Party of Canada."
Long was first elected in 2015, but he is not running for re-election. He has opposed his own government several times before, on the Energy East Pipeline, on proposed tax changes for private corporations and, most recently, on the government's decision to phase out salmon farms that operate in B.C. waterways.
Calgary Liberal MP George Chahal called for a broader caucus meeting soon after the byelection defeat, but one did not take place. Trudeau has spoken with individual caucus members over the summer and dropped into meetings of both Ontario and Quebec MPs. Long said he spoke with Trudeau over the summer and believes the prime minister understands his position.
He said Trudeau has served the country well, navigating the country through the pandemic, bringing in programs like subsidized child care and dental care and achieving strong economic performance post pandemic, but he said every politician's career has an end date.
"I like the prime minister. I think he has done great work, but I also recognize that we can't win the next election under his leadership," said Long.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has maintained a sizable lead in public opinion polls, as much as 20 percentage points, for more than a year. Long argues with a change in leadership, the Liberals could win a fourth term.
"I believe Pierre Poilievre is absolutely beatable, and I would argue there hasn't been a leader of the official opposition who is as disliked as Pierre Poilievre," he said.
When Liberals begin three days of meetings in Nanaimo, B.C., on Monday they will be discussing a different parliamentary landscape, now that NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh has announced he is leaving the confidence and supply agreement that kept the minority government in power for the past two years.
The move puts the Liberals on shaky ground as the House of Commons resumes sitting this month, needing the support of at least one other party to pass legislation and keep the government from falling in confidence votes.
The Liberals'national campaign director Jeremy Broadhurst also announced Thursday he was stepping down, leaving a vacancy in a key position just as a threat of an early election has been heightened. Long said that is another major setback for the party.
In a statement, Broadhurst cited the toll two decades and five national campaigns have taken on himself and his family.
He said the upcoming federal election could be the most critical federal campaign of his life, and the party deserves a campaign director who can bring more energy and devotion to the job.
Long said he knows that as a backbench MP who is not re-offering he can be ignored. But he said he has gained some political skills over nine years in office and believes Canadians have simply become tired of the prime minister.
He said he hopes his colleagues speak up because absent a change, the party could be headed to disaster.
"I continue to hope some of my colleagues will be a little more vocal and say what is needed to be done," he said. "I think we are sleepwalking to disaster if we don't read the room as a party." Postmedia News, with a file from The Canadian Press rtumilty@postmedia.com

Subject: Aquaculture; Leadership; Prime ministers; Pandemics

Document 110 of 219

Poilievre winning in the battle for workers

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-winning-battle-workers/docview/3100997739/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+winning+in+the+battle+for+workers&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Labour Day, every party wanted to show they were with "the workers" who make this country tick. It appears, though, that different political parties have different views on that and are getting different receptions from workers themselves.
On Friday, Justin Trudeau went to Algoma Steel in Sault Ste. Marie and got an earful from a worker he encountered - despite showing up with doughnuts.
"I don't believe you for a second," a steelworker, who ran into Trudeau, told the prime minister during an encounter caught on video.
The Trudeau Liberals have held the riding since 2015, but over the last 20 years, it has been held by the Liberals, Conservatives and NDP. Right now, like much of Ontario, it is expected to switch over to the Conservatives next election, which is why Trudeau was there stumping at the steel plant's gates.
"I think you are only here for another year; we won't see you around in another year," the steel plant employee told Trudeau.
"That's what elections are for," Trudeau said, trying to turn the conversation around.
It was a fruitless effort by Trudeau, and he should have known that.
The latest poll for Postmedia, by Leger, found 47 per cent of men say they will vote for Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives, compared to just 22 per cent of males who say they will vote Liberal and 13 per cent who will back the NDP. Lest anyone thinks women still back Trudeau, 40 per cent of women say they will back the Conservatives, compared to 27 per cent for the Liberals and 17 per cent for the NDP.
When it comes to people who actually work for a living, meaning people who aren't in the laptop class like me, the Conservatives are the overwhelming favourites.
"A country is built by the people who rise when it is still dark," Poilievre says off the top of the video he and the Conservative Party released in early Labour Day morning.
He paid tribute to servers and soldiers, nurses and night shift workers, and farmers and factory hands as he called them.
"Often called ordinary people, but they are extraordinary," Poilievre said.
After detailing how people are struggling with higher prices and wages that haven't kept pace, and how crime, chaos and homelessness are issues people struggle with, Poilievre's video says there is a new dawn coming "where everyone gets a good shot at a good life." The video, reminiscent of Ronald Reagan's iconic 1984 election ad Morning in America, offers the idea that there is hope ahead.
It's a message that will resonate with voters looking for a change, which they clearly are after nearly nine years of Trudeau.
Not to be left out, the NDP also released a video this week trying to appeal to workers; instead, it appeals to public sector union bosses.
The NDP video features public sector union bosses Sussanne Skidmore and Laura Walton. Skidmore is president of the B.C. Federation of Labour, and Walton is head of the Ontario Federation of Labour.
The problem with these union bosses - and the NDP politicians they align themselves with - is that they oppose infrastructure projects like highways, workplaces like steel mills and the extraction of resources like mining, oil or gas that so many private sector workers rely upon.
While the NDP in its video says that Poilievre isn't a true friend of unionized workers because he hasn't shown up at a picket line, Singh isn't a friend of unionized workers because he opposes their jobs existing.
Blue collar workers are about to vote blue en masse. Areas that traditionally would have voted NDP, or perhaps Liberal, are going to back Poilievre and the Conservatives.
The reasoning will be the same as in the 2022 provincial election in Ontario where Doug Ford's PC Party got the backing of private sector unions because he supported projects that keep them working, while progressives oppose them.
Poilievre is about to get the backing of the same kind of workers for the same reason - unlike the other parties, he doesn't want them put out of work. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Provincial elections; Workers; Strikes; Public sector

Business indexing term: Subject: Workers Strikes Public sector

Document 111 of 219

Opiates 254, Ross Romano 0: Opioid-related mortality rates soar under MPP's 'intrepid' leadership

Author: Chow , Peter

Publication info: Chow, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/opiates-254-ross-romano-0-opioid-related/docview/3100394966/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opiates+254%2C+Ross+Romano+0%3A+Opioid-related+mortality+rates+soar+under+MPP%27s+%27intrepid%27+leadership&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-09-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Chow%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Thirty years ago, I spent two months at Homewood Health Center in Guelph, Ont., Canada's premier inpatient addiction treatment center and seven months at Talbott Recovery in Atlanta, Ga., the largest treatment facility for health-care professionals in North America, doing a fellowship of sorts in opiate addiction treatment.
So, I think I know a bit about opiate addiction treatment.
The Progressive Conservative government of Doug Ford and our MPP Ross Romano are banning any new supervised drug consumption sites, ending hopes for a Sault facility and guaranteeing higher death rates from opioid overdoses.
With a death rate of 69.6 per 100,000, Sault Ste. Marie has the highest opioid-related mortality rate in all of Ontario for the first three months of 2024.
In the four years of the pandemic, 65 people have died of COVID-19 in the Sault.
Since Ross Romano became MPP in 2017, 254 people have died of drug overdoses.
Whereas COVID killed mostly senior citizens, drug overdoses killed young people, teens, people in their 20s and 30s and 40s.
Many, many more life-years lost. Former Sault mayor Christian Provenzano said, "Government must act now to prevent more harm, rather than waste time stigmatizing drug use and debating right and wrong."
In 2021, the president of the Canadian Association of Chiefs of Police said, "Over the years, we have come to realize that we cannot arrest our way out of this opioid crisis," calling for the decriminalization (not legalization) of personal quantities of illicit drugs and diverting addicts from the criminal justice system to health-care services.
Sault Ste. Marie Police Service Chief Hugh Stevenson, in 2021, supported the establishment of a supervised safe consumption site (SCS): "... safe consumption sites are methods to stop the spread of this illness ...Trained healthcare professionals are on site to prevent overdose deaths and to connect addicts with the supports they need to beat their addiction. This is a public health crisis and public health experts are advocating for this approach in our communities."
The Sault was applying for a supervised drug consumption site.
But our faithful MPP Ross Romano and his PC Party have done their very best to thwart this even as the dead bodies annoyingly keep piling up.
Instead of having a supervised safe drug consumption site, our whole city will remain an unsupervised unsafe drug consumption site.
Instead of expanding the number of safe consumption sites, the PC government announced that 10 sites are being closed, including Sudbury, Thunder Bay and Timmins, and no new sites will be allowed. The government will also prohibit any safe drug programs, which provide drug users with Fentanyl-free drugs as well as any applications for drug decriminalization exemptions from the federal government.
All this receives a standing ovation from the PC caucus. This standing ovation from the PC party is nauseatingly disgusting and shameful.
The party is showing its true colours. Conservatives have always been openly hostile to harm reduction, medication-assisted treatment with methadone and suboxone and supervised drug consumption and treatment Sites, let alone decriminalization of drugs and assuring a clean drug supply for addicts.
Ignoring the evidence-based recommendations of the medical community, the addiction medicine community, the public health community, the law enforcement community, and Health Canada.
Mayor Matthew Shoemaker vented his frustration, stating, "The announcement regarding safe consumption sites is based on ideology and not evidence."
Regressive right-wing political ideology over evidence-based medicine -a common theme of right-wing science illiteracy.
The Canadian Association of Chiefs of Police president said it's time to rethink our approach to illegal drugs to save lives. But Conservatives are unable to overcome their natural proclivity for judgement and punishment and slow to understand -on their usual timetable, about 10 years after the rest of us -that criminalizing drugs doesn't work.
The war on drugs is over and drugs kicked our ass.
But the Doug Ford PC government is hell-bent on re-igniting the war on drugs.
But their war is not just on drugs but cynically on the victims, addicts as well.
A 2020 Centers for Disease Control and Prevention study found that 75 per cent of people eventually recover from opiate addiction, but the process typically takes 8 years after multiple relapses even with high-quality expensive treatment and medical care.
But now this calculus - multiple relapses leading to eventual recovery - is upended now that street drugs are laced with Fentanyl.
Fentanyl is killing them on the first try, not giving them a chance at enough tries.
The first imperative is to stop overdose deaths.
Meta-analysis of multiple medical studies has shown that dead people don't so well in rehab but on the positive side for Conservatives, the recidivism rate is zero.
Closure of existing safe consumption sites will mean more overdose deaths, more strain on police, firefighters and paramedics, more crowded and more chaotic emergency rooms, and more public drug consumption.
A study in the medical journal The Lancet found a 67 per cent reduction in overdose deaths with SCSs in Toronto, a 70 per cent decrease in ambulance calls for overdoses and a 90 per cent decrease in rates of HIV and hepatitis C.
No one has ever died of a drug overdose at an SCS.
The first maxim in medicine is, "Primum, non nocere" -"First, do no harm."
Romano and the Ontario PCs have blown up that silly notion.
Ross Romano should be deeply ashamed, but he probably has exhausted his capacity for that after his Laurentian University dumpster fire debacle and his continued support for the ferrochrome smelter.
He has the rare ability to irrepressibly jump from one shameful failure to the next with no iota of embarrassment nor loss of enthusiasm; 56 per cent of all eligible voters didn't vote in Sault Ste. Marie in 2022 so Romano won with a landslide of 21 per cent of eligible voters.
But, far from representing the specific interests of the Sault, Romano is focused solely on selling the PC party's policies to his district, regardless of the harm such policies may have on the Sault.
To paraphrase Don Cherry, calling Romano a cynical sycophant would be to disparage cynical sycophants.
No supervised consumption site, no harm reduction with widespread medication-assisted treatment like Suboxone, no anonymous, free Fentanyl testing service in the Sault -three measures that alone would save lives right away here.
Empty cynical platitudes and mendacious prevarications are all we get in Sault Ste Marie.
Opiates 254; Romano 0. Under Romano's intrepid leadership, we are No. 1 in opioid-related mortality rate in all of Ontario.
Yay! Congratulations! Ross Romano should get up and give himself another standing ovation. Dr. Peter Chow is a retired Sault Ste. Marie physician

Subject: Drug overdose; Conservatism; Mortality; Health care; Decriminalization; Public health; Drug addiction; Voters; Pharmacists; Fentanyl; Substance abuse treatment; Narcotics; Harm reduction

Company / organization: Name: Canadian Association of Chiefs of Police; NAICS: 813920

Document 112 of 219

Unintended consequences could be avoided

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/unintended-consequences-could-be-avoided/docview/3099254737/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Unintended+consequences+could+be+avoided&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-08-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford is playing Russian roulette with the law of unintended consequences.
You've seen this law at work. It watched as Ford capped post-secondary tuition - surely a popular move - then struck when cash-panicked colleges admitted international students in greater and greater numbers. Nobody seemed to plan for the added strain on affordable housing.
Many policies with unintended consequences seem like common sense. For example: Build more highways to ease traffic congestion. It sounds perfectly sensible, except history shows the opposite is true. Induced demand makes traffic even worse: If you build it, they will come. Los Angeles has paved itself into an endless snarl of gridlock. In Houston, a 26-lane freeway expansion actually made afternoon commutes 55 per cent longer. Nevertheless, Ford is determined Highway 413 will be a magical corridor to prosperity.
An unintended outcome is not the same as unforeseen.
Take the arrival of beer, wine and pre-mixed drinks in corner stores. The last major expansion into grocery stores (by the Liberals, in 2015) resulted in a steep 17.8 per cent increase in alcohol-related visits to emergency departments. Easier access to alcohol comes with a hefty price tag of unintended consequences. The Ontario Public Health Association has warned the new rollout - nearly quadrupling sales outlets - will increase domestic violence, street violence, road crashes and thefts, not to mention chronic disease.
Here's another unintended certainty: More underage customers will be served.
The province has pledged $10 million during five years to support public health efforts and social responsibility. So far, it also has ignored the recommendations of every health organization and it's own chief medical officer of health.
Next, the Ford government plans to prevent supervised consumption sites from operating within 200 metres of a school or daycare facility. On the surface, the policy sounds sensible. Why would anyone want those two clienteles in close proximity? Of course, they already coexist. That's why supervised consumption sites opened in these neighbourhoods. The most effective place for them is where drug use is prevalent. That's why they save lives. Consumption sites can't be shuffled off to out-of-the-way locales any more than a pond can be relocated to the side of a hill.
Even if they could, it's not clear the social ills in some of these neighbourhoods - including property damage and violence - would leave with them. Some problems, such as discarded syringes, would almost certainly get worse.
Ford's new legislation, if passed, will force at least 10 out of 17 provincially regulated supervised consumption sites to close by March 31, 2025. Five of them are in Toronto, where deaths from opioid toxicity have increased 74 per cent since 2019.
Thunder Bay, which suffered the highest opioid-related mortality rates across Ontario in 2022, will lose its only supervised consumption site. Other facilities in Sudbury and Timmins already have closed for lack of funding. It's looking bleak for Northern Ontario.
Health Minister Sylvia Jones says the province will invest $378 million in 19 new homelessness and addiction recovery treatment (HART) hubs, with 375 supportive housing units and treatment beds. The added detox services are badly needed, no question. However, they're needed in addition to - not in lieu of - overdose prevention. The new hubs won't even provide needle exchange services.
The consequences of losing safe consumption sites are that more people will die from overdoses and a toxic drug supply. Fewer people will access vital health and harm reduction services, suffering worse health outcomes, such as infection from syringes.
This government should spare a thought for the unintended, but foreseeable, consequences of these policies. Unintended is not the same as unavoidable. write.robin@baranyai.ca

Subject: Public health; Drug overdose; Domestic violence; Narcotics

Identifier / keyword: ontario; premier; playing; russian; roulette; unintended

Title: Unintended consequences could be avoided

Document 113 of 219

Sault Ste. Marie to pivot for fair share of new HART Hub funding

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sault-ste-marie-pivot-fair-share-new-hart-hub/docview/3098557758/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sault+Ste.+Marie+to+pivot+for+fair+share+of+new+HART+Hub+funding&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayor Matthew Shoemaker says he'll do the best he can to advocate for new provincial government funding to help reduce opioid deaths in Sault Ste. Marie.
While Sault Ste. Marie's plan was to apply for funding for a safe consumption site, the city and its health-care partners will now pivot and work together to determine how best it can complete an application by Oct. 1 and receive its fair share of the $378 million that will be available to municipalities to establish Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment Hubs, or HART Hubs.
Shoemaker said the Northern Ontario Large Urban Mayors continue to work together to push for funding and programming that will help solve the opioid crisis in the Northern communities, but now find themselves competing against each other and other municipalities across the province for funding to establish the new HART Hubs.
Last week at the annual Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) conference, the Ontario government said it plans to ban consumption sites within 200 metres of schools and child-care centres. That means 10 existing facilities across the province will be forced to close, including one in Thunder Bay.
The province vows the new HART hubs "will reflect regional priorities by connecting people with complex needs to comprehensive treatment and preventative services." They will not offer safe or supervised drug consumption or needle exchange programs.
It's anticipated that the safe consumption sites required to close will be the first to receive funding to transition to the new hubs.
Sault Ste. Marie has the Northway Wellness Centre, a treatment facility, but advocates have been lobbying for an expansion of that site since it first opened.
The city also has the community resource centre, which provides a 44-bed facility for men, including transitional beds that offers joint services with partnering agencies like Ontario Works, a physician-led clinic, and mental health services, among others.
Shoemaker said it hasn't been made clear whether Sault Ste. Marie can apply for funding to add to the services already offered or if a new facility is required.
"Northway is treatment focused but there also is some support for housing and mental health," Shoemaker said. "And the community resource centre offers other supports."
Those supports are still not enough to reduce Sault Ste. Marie's crisis.
Shoemaker said it is important the city co-ordinates its efforts with community partners, including District Social Services, Sault Area Hospital, the Canadian Mental Health Association and others, to ensure its application for funding is complete.
But to do that, the group is awaiting the release of final criteria from the province.
Premier Doug Ford previously announced the funding criteria will be available before the end of the month. Application deadline is Oct. 1 and it is expected sites can begin operating by March 2025.
Shoemaker, who met with Health Minister Sylvia Jones at the AMO conference, said he reiterated Sault Ste. Marie's dire need for assistance and reminded her of the most recent statistics that show the Sault has the highest opioid deaths per capita in the province.
"Those statistics show we need help more desperately than anywhere else," Shoemaker said.
He's hoping an application will demonstrate the need for an increased version of mental health support.
"There's still a level of unknown until we see the specific criteria and sift through that application process," Shoemaker said. "Our intent is to make sure we can help people in our community who need it most."
Jones's announcement at AMO follows two reviews of the sites that the government ordered in the wake of the killing of a Toronto woman, who was hit by a stray bullet from a shooting near one of the sites last July.
Meanwhile, the Northern NDP Caucus members are angry that they will see safe consumption sites in their communities closed.
Thunder Bay MPP Lisa Vaugeois said her community's site "offers wrap-around services and has saved countless lives. I'm shocked that the Ford government is shutting down an essential service that is working so well.
"The Minister of Health is taking away a key tool in our fight to save lives and putting stigma ahead of support," she said in a press release.
Sudbury MPP Jamie West said the community stepped up and funded its safe consumption site when the Ontario government wouldn't.
"Instead of addressing the real issue, (Ford) gave up on Ontarians - on our most vulnerable people - and the friends and family that love them. The blame will lie with the government," he said.
Official Opposition Health Critic France Gelinas, MPP for Nickel Belt, reiterated the need for continued support.
"Not a single community in our province is asking the government to take away existing resources and programs. This government is cynically attacking health infrastructure, putting ideology over evidence. It's time to fund the treatments and support programs we need so we can stop the loss of life," she said.
But not all are unhappy with the province's news. The Ontario Chamber of Commerce says it welcomes the $378 million investment into the hubs but cautions the government that it must include all comprehensive solutions from evidence-based strategies that will save lives and connect people to the care they need.

Document 114 of 219

Feds want to be landlords

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/feds-want-be-landlords/docview/3098557531/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feds+want+to+be+landlords&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Everyone agrees that Canada faces a housing shortage. But it's not a shortage of homes: it's a shortage of homes people can afford. In Toronto, for example, there is currently a glut of condos on the market. Prices have fallen as investors off-load properties: high interest rates mean their mortgage payments exceed the rents they can get.
Homes have become unaffordable because of the disconnect between wages and prices. The average price-to-earnings ratio in Canada more than doubled from 3.2 in 1980 to 6.7 in 2020. Vancouver currently has the highest price to earnings ratio, at 12.3, followed by Toronto at 9.3. To put that in perspective, to buy an average house in those cities in July 2024, you need a household income of $208,000 and $226,000, respectively. Not exactly middle-class.
Now, the federal government claims to have an answer. The Liberals propose to convert unused public lands and buildings to housing - an idea the Conservatives have been floating for a couple of years. But here's the twist: residents won't own the land the home is built on. Instead, the government will lease the land to developers so it can maintain ownership and "keep home prices low."
In other words, it plans to get into the condo business, which private developers are abandoning because the market is saturated. In Toronto, several condo projects have gone into receivership in recent years, including The One, a supposedly "iconic" tower at the corner of Yonge and Bloor.
The Liberal leasehold project was actually announced back in April just after the federal budget. But now it's been repackaged in a shiny new video featuring the prime minister dumping on the Opposition for proposing to "sell it all to developers." Instead, Trudeau presents long-term leasing as the saviour of the Canadian housing dream, while failing to mention its downsides.
Long-term leasing doesn't build equity at the same rate, because you don't own the land your home is built on. And like in a condo, your fees, a.k.a. rent, can rise unexpectedly, not just because of need, but by government fiat. When the state is your landlord, the feeling of powerlessness is palpable.
Ask the leasehold cottage owners in Ontario's public wilderness parks, who for years felt like a "sword of Damocles" sat over their head as the provincial government decided whether to renew their leases. Or leaseholders of recreational properties in Saskatchewan forced to move when the First Nations who leased them their lands changed the terms. Or the leaseholders in British Columbia who faced a 180 per cent rent hike when their property assessment increased.
On top of this, Trudeau claims that the government will build the "right kind" of homes (i.e., "affordable" homes). The government already has several projects underway, which Housing Minister Sean Fraser showed off in another shiny video. But how will the government keep these homes "affordable?" Will it sell at below market rates? Will it cut corners in construction? Will it means-test buyers? And what happens when they sell? The shiny videos do not address these questions. They are a public relations response to the Conservatives'biggest issue: the sense that the Canadian dream is slipping away. A big part of that dream has always been home ownership, something that young people now say is out of reach. And when that dream slips away, so do the votes attached to it, as the Liberals are seeing at the polls.
Of course, the real way the government needs to address the housing crisis is on the demand side. They are finally doing this, by reducing the number of low-wage temporary workers in Canada that ballooned over the past few years, as well as cutting off a veritable deluge of international students. That announcement didn't get a shiny video, however, just a little mention.
Why? Because curtailing immigration runs counter to the Liberal narrative that diversity is our strength.
By failing to manage that key file, this government has done a disservice to all Canadians, new and old. And now it wants to lease them their house. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Housing prices; Condominiums

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing prices

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: everyone; agrees; canada; faces; housing; shortage

Document 115 of 219

'There's only one taxpayer'? Not quite

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/theres-only-one-taxpayer-not-quite/docview/3098557435/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27There%27s+only+one+taxpayer%27%3F+Not+quite&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's only one taxpayer. It's an adage that attempts to explain taxation in the simplest possible terms, but the problem is that it's neither entirely accurate nor particularly useful.
Local federal cabinet Minister Jenna Sudds recently used the phrase while deflecting the City of Ottawa's request that the federal government pay its fair share of property taxes. Sudds said, "We must remember that there is only one taxpayer," then urged the city government "to manage their money well and spend it wisely," just like the federal government does.
Citizen columnist Brigitte Pellerin recently explored the one-taxpayer concept and found it a bit silly for one level of government to seek money from another because it all comes from you and me in any case. Pellerin asked, "Is there a magic money tree under the Peace Tower that no one told us about?" It seems that there is and it's bearing an amazing amount of fruit. How else to explain the ability of Justin Trudeau's government to spend about $40 billion more this year than it will actually take in? Talk about managing our money wisely.
Things are a little different at the municipal level. By law, the city can't budget for a deficit and its problem is that there is only one tax, not one taxpayer. This year, Ottawa expects to raise just over $2.1 billion in property taxes. Its next biggest source of revenue is about $994 million in user fees.
The premise of a municipal budget is that not much will change from one year to the next. When the city takes on more, as it has with the LRT, then that equation rapidly gets out of balance. So does the city budget and there is no easy fix.
While the city has only one tax, the number of taxes levied by the federal and provincial governments is limited only by their imaginations and their political nerve.
The idea that you and I ultimately pay all taxes ignores the substantial sum that both governments collect in corporate taxes. The feds plan to rake in $90.7 billion this year, the province $24.9 billion.
Then there are all the other revenue sources. The federal government collects customs duties, excise taxes and profits from Crown corporations. The GST alone is estimated to bring in $54.1 billion this year.
The province has a health tax, Crown corporation income, a land transfer tax and its share of the HST. That sales tax will generate about $39 billion this year.
The provincial government even dips into the property tax. The average urban residential property in Ottawa pays about $4,325 in municipal property taxes annually. This same property pays an additional $635 in provincial education taxes, collected as part of your property tax bill.
Let's not forget the magic money tree. Both the federal and provincial governments routinely run deficits, something the Doug Ford government finds mildly regrettable and the Trudeau government accepts as a fact of life. In effect, there are no real restraints on federal and provincial spending.
It's a bit rich to see our city government criticized for its spending when municipal governments are the only ones that have to make difficult choices and accept fiscal discipline. The federal and provincial governments have reserved the most lucrative forms of taxation for themselves and they still can't come close to balancing their books, even when economic growth drives up the tax take every year.
The nation-wide problem with municipal transit deficits suggests an imbalance between cities'resources and their responsibilities. It's a problem that could be fixed with a sum of money that's modest by federal and provincial standards.
Without federal and provincial help, Ottawa will be faced with a transit budget shortfall that will average about $140 million a year until it's fixed. The city will have to increase property taxes substantially, make big service cuts or some combination of the two.
Surely the "one taxpayer" wouldn't want to see that, especially knowing how much money the Trudeau government squanders on things that offer the public little value at all. Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist and author. Contact him at randalldenley1@gmail.com

Subject: Taxation; Budgets; Property taxes; Government

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxation Budgets Property taxes

Identifier / keyword: taxpayer; adage; attempts; explain; taxation; simplest

Document 116 of 219

Provinces lead on Cannabis reform while feds dither; A lack of meaningful reform since legalization risks tarnishing PM's legacy

Author: Khan, Omar

Publication info: Khan, Omar. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provinces-lead-on-cannabis-reform-while-feds/docview/3097621004/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provinces+lead+on+Cannabis+reform+while+feds+dither%3B+A+lack+of+meaningful+reform+since+legalization+risks+tarnishing+PM%27s+legacy&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Khan%2C+Omar&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When the Cannabis Act became law back in 2018, it was hailed by the federal Liberals as a bold move that would keep cannabis out of the hands of youth, protect public health and safety, and reduce criminal activity. The idea was that by creating a regulated industry, illicit operators would either jump on the bandwagon and go legal or be pushed out of the market by a newly created legal sector. Some progress has been made on this front.
A 2022 study by Deloitte estimated that between 2018 (legalization) and 2021, the industry contributed $43.5 billion toward Canada's GDP while sustaining over 150,000 jobs nationally. Despite this, almost six years on, the industry continues to face serious challenges, with many feeling that the federal Liberals have washed their hands of the industry they helped to create.
According to Statistics Canada, the illicit market still accounts for about 35 per cent of total sales nationally. Another recent study by Deloitte found that the average price in the illicit market remains 20 per cent lower than in licensed stores, which deters price-conscious consumers from going legal. This poses real risks to Canadians as revenues generated by the sale of illicit market cannabis often find their way into the pockets of organized crime.
Despite this, the federal government continues to ignore the advice of the House of Commons Standing Committee on Finance, which in February recommended adjustments to the excise tax structure aimed at improving the ability of the legal cannabis sector to compete with an entrenched illicit market.
And product from the illicit market is risky. A 2022 study by the National Research Council on behalf of the Ontario Provincial Police found that a random sampling of seized illegal cannabis contained high levels of unauthorized pesticides. Add to this, the fact that many of the products they sell are packaged to look like popular junk food brands to appeal to youth, and it's clear that more work is needed.
After the creation of a Cannabis Industry Forum within the Department of Innovation, Science and Economic Development this past February, some industry players expressed cautious optimism about increased federal engagement. But the minister has yet to attend a meeting, signalling political indifference to an already skeptical stakeholder group.
The good news is that Canadian provinces, many of which have Conservative or NDP governments, have decided to fill the void created by federal inaction and have begun taking decisive steps to help ensure the long-term viability of their legal operators.
In September 2023, for instance, Ontario reduced the average wholesale mark-up it charges licensed producers. It then doubled the number of stores a licensed retailer can operate and allocated $31 million over three years to fund a Provincial Joint Forces Cannabis Enforcement Team targeting illicit market stores and websites across the province.
The Alberta government has boldly allowed licensed retailers to sell products at festivals or trade shows closed to minors - opening up another opportunity for responsible consumer engagement. In June of this year, the province announced that certain legally licensed retailers could sell private label products, usually sold at lower prices to appeal to a more cost-conscious consumer.
In 2023, Manitoba eliminated a six-per-cent levy on legal cannabis sales, which had driven prices up unnecessarily.
As helpful as these provincial changes are, they cannot fully compensate for a lack of federal action.
In March of this year, the recommendations of a statutory review of the Cannabis Act were released (more than a year late), with the federal government's expert panel confirming that "... the illicit cannabis market remains entrenched, and too many illicit retailers continue to operate both online and physical stores." Legitimate cannabis retailers, who are on the front lines of the fight against illegal operators, know this all too well with more and more cannabis companies filing for bankruptcy protection in 2024.
Cannabis legalization remains a cornerstone achievement of the Trudeau Liberals, but the lack of meaningful reform and engagement with industry since legalization risks tarnishing this legacy. With a federal election just over a year away, there is still time for federal action to help ensure the sustainability and success of an industry they helped to create. Only time will tell if they follow the lead of their provincial counterparts. Omar Khan is the Chief Communications and Public Affairs Officer at Calgary-based High Tide Inc., the second-largest retailer of legal cannabis in the world by store count. He previously served as Chief of Staff to Ontario's Minister of Health and Long-Term Care and Minister of Economic Development and Trade, and Senior Adviser to Ontario's Attorney General.

Document 117 of 219

Rail shutdown hurts economy; Algoma Steel Group, Tenaris watching dispute

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rail-shutdown-hurts-economy-algoma-steel-group/docview/3096637672/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rail+shutdown+hurts+economy%3B+Algoma+Steel+Group%2C+Tenaris+watching+dispute&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-08-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An unprecedented national rail system shutdown will have devastating impacts across supply chains for business if it continues for any length of time.
It's the first time more than 9,300 employees from both Canadian National Railway and Canadian Pacific Kansas City have been locked out at the same time.
In fact, it's estimated the labour dispute will bring about $1 billion worth of goods moved by rail daily to a sudden stop.
Two-thirds of freight is moved across Canada by train, Railway Association of Canada says.
Rail workers pushed back hard Friday against the federal government's move to get them back on the job, with a new strike notice and a regulatory challenge making it unclear when freight traffic will fully resume.
At Calgary-based CPKC, the union has challenged a directive for binding arbitration issued by Labour Minister Steve MacKinnon to the country's labour board. And at Canadian National Railway trains began to move again Friday as workers started to trickle to work -even as the Teamsters union issued a 72-hour strike notice against CN.
Derek Jackson, board chair of Sault Ste. Marie Chamber of Commerce, said a lengthy dispute will impact the economy locally and nationally.
"Individual consumers may not see it directly, but indirectly there will be impacts with the transportation of things like fuel, liquid propane, chlorine supplies, all things that are important to municipalities and large businesses. This is a concern," Jackson said.
Across Northern Ontario, the impact will likely be felt in the tourism sector, especially for remote and rural tourist operations that rely on rail to bring people to their lodges.
Food security could also become a major issue if the dispute drags on, Jackson said.
Companies, including Algoma Steel Group, are following the labour disruption.
"We are monitoring the situation closely and taking appropriate actions to mitigate the impacts to our customers," said Laura Devoni, Algoma Steel's direction of human resources and corporate affairs, in an email to The Sault Star.
"We are in close communication with our rail, road, and marine transportation providers and are keeping customers informed throughout this period." Devoni did not offer specifics about contingency plans Algoma Steel has made to transport materials and goods or how the rail dispute will affect production or staffing numbers. She also did not comment on how much alternative shipping will cost the steelmaker.
Many industries are scrambling to find alternative methods of transportation for their supplies and products.
If a labour dispute lasts more than a week, consumers across the country will begin to see food and product shortages.
Carolina Mendoza, spokesperson for Tenaris, said the company is also monitoring the shutdown.
CN is sole railway supplier for Tenaris in Sault Ste. Marie, she said.
Martin Castro, president of Tenaris in Canada, said its Sault operation "is designed to efficiently load production onto railcars" to be shipped.
"We need continuous rail service to maintain a streamlined flow of steel pipe production to support our customers and the development of Canada's energy resources," he said in a statement. "We commend the federal government's decision to take necessary action to bring Canada's railway companies back online to avoid greater impact and disruption across many industries, including manufacturing."
Long-term agreements between the two railways and their workers are necessary because CN and CPKC "play a critical role in Canada's economy and supply chains across North America."
Alternate transportation in Sault Ste. Marie and the North will likely mean additional trucks and transports on Ontario's highways, something always viewed as a health and safety concern, Jackson said.
Canadian National Railway and Canadian Pacific Kansas City are embroiled in a battle with the Teamsters Canada Rail Conference union. The union wants better wages and benefits.
The Teamsters are also demanding better crew scheduling for its workers.
The bitter labour dispute sent panic buttons ringing across Canada last Sunday after CN notified the union that it would lock out workers early Thursday, on the strike-lockout deadline.
Canadian Pacific told the unions the same.
Meanwhile, the Teamsters union also fired off its 72-hour strike notice on Sunday.
The threats became reality early Thursday.
The federal government has said it is standing by and urging all parties to continue mediated talks to reach a dispute.
MacKinnon can intervene and refer the matter to binding arbitration or pass legislation which could lead to back-towork legislation, something opposition parties contest.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford urged the parties to get "laser-focused" to get a deal done.
Jackson said the Sault chamber has joined with the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, Business Council of Canada, Canadian Federation of Independent Business and Canadian Manufacturers & Exporters, to call on the federal government to take "immediate action" to keep materials and goods moving.
The Ontario Chamber of Commerce says in a release "prolonged action would also result in serious harm at a time when our international trading partners look to Ontario and Canada as a source of critical minerals, energy resources and more.
"We call on the Government of Canada to intervene immediately, exploring all options at its disposal to limit the impact of this disruption."
Jackson said "we need the continuation of rail. We saw what the B.C. port strike looked like in 2023 and the impact it had on the economy." with files from The Canadian Press

Document 118 of 219

Scrap paid parking at hospitals; Letter to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/scrap-paid-parking-at-hospitals-letter-editor/docview/3096637410/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Scrap+paid+parking+at+hospitals%3B+Letter+to+the+editor&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-08-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re: Sault Area Hospital's paid parking should be scrapped by board, Aug. 20 Dr. Chow is right on the mark when he suggests that paid parking should be scrapped by hospitals. The Ontario Nurses'Association (ONA) - which represents more than 68,000 nurses and healthcare professionals - wholeheartedly agrees.
What may be seen as a minor issue of offering free parking is in fact part of a much larger systemic problem with our health-care system.
Since Premier Doug Ford came into power six long years ago, he has purposely underfunded our invaluable public health-care system by billions of dollars. Because of this, hospital corporations must cut their budgets to balance them, a nearly impossible feat year after year. Hospitals seek ways to supplement their bottom lines, so they introduce sneaky ways to get extra money and obtain fees from patients and staff - one of which is paid parking.
The solution is somewhat comically simple: the Ford government must fully fund our treasured public healthcare system.
Tragically, it seems that the PC government's priority is to funnel our taxpayer dollars to their greedy buddies who own private health-care corporations. This is their way to break the system then use it as an excuse for full privatization.
Free parking at the hospital is a necessary safety measure for staff who work long hours providing essential care. As a result of parking fees, staff are increasingly forced to park at a further distance from their workplace. Nurses and health-care professionals are overwhelmingly women. For those who finish their shift in the middle of the night, it is unsafe and unacceptable that they then have to travel a distance to access their vehicle. Free parking is not unprecedented. Several hospitals in the North already have unconditional free parking, so why not implement it? One of our dedicated members launched a grassroots petition on this issue and we fully support it. And close to 10,000 people agree.
We urge the hospital to do the right thing: implement free parking for all. Erin Ariss, RN Provincial President Ontario Nurses' Association

Subject: Health care; Nurses

Identifier / keyword: sault; parking; scrapped; board; right; suggests

Title: Scrap paid parking at hospitals; Letter to the editor

Publication title: Sault Star; Sault Ste. Marie, Ont.

First page: A.4

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Aug 24, 2024

Section: Opinion

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Sault Ste. Marie, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 0839203X
