Document 119 of 219

Poll finds school pronoun law will impact provincial elections

Author: Rahim, Mohamed

Publication info: Rahim, Mohamed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poll-finds-school-pronoun-law-will-impact/docview/3094982699/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poll+finds+school+pronoun+law+will+impact+provincial+elections&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Rahim%2C+Mohamed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Voters in Saskatchewan and New Brunswick are preparing to vote in fall elections and a new poll suggests that new rules on pronouns in schools brought in by governments in both provinces will be top of mind.
Two-thirds of Saskatchewan voters say that the province's law on parental notification and consent for student pronoun and name changes will impact how they vote in the province's upcoming election, according to a new poll.
Sixty-eight per cent of respondents said the school pronouns law will impact their vote "a lot" or "some," in a poll of 596 likely voters taken by Saskatoon-based Insightrix Research.
The online poll was conducted between July 23 and 26, using a representative sample drawn from all regions of the province.
Supporters of the opposition NDP are most galvanized by the signature legislation, with 72 per cent saying it will impact the way they vote in this fall's election. 64 per cent of those who favour the incumbent Saskatchewan Party say the law will affect how they vote.
The election will be held on or before October 28.
Respondents ranked school pronouns as the least important of four election issues, behind the provincial government's withholding of the carbon tax from energy bills, the government's handling of this year's teachers'strike and the recent conduct of a number of SaskParty MLAs in the province's legislature.
However, voters felt strongly about the issue compared to the others mentioned in the survey. 49 per cent of respondents said the pronouns law will have "a lot" of sway over how they vote, putting it second behind the carbon tax withholdings in terms of intensity.
Lang McGilp, research director at Insightrix, said that Premier Scott Moe raised the temperature on the issue when he pre-emptively tacked the notwithstanding clause onto the school pronouns law, also known as the Parents'Bill of Rights.
"It struck a lot of people as excessive to invoke the clause over such a narrow issue," McGilp told Postmedia, adding that Moe's use of the notwithstanding clause was especially off-putting to urban voters in Regina and Saskatoon.
"There's a lot of polarization over the issue between the cities and rural Saskatchewan," said McGilp.
If recent "culture wars" elections south of the border are any indication, the pronouns issue could drive Gen Z voters, born in the late 1990s and early 2000s, to the polls in higher than usual numbers.
One in six gen Z Canadians identify as LBGT, according to an Ipsos study published last summer, which is by far the largest proportion among any generational cohort.
Insightrix currently has the NDP leading the SaskParty by a whopping 27 points among voters under the age of 35 and up by one point among all decided voters.
Polls show that support for the Saskatchewan NDP has been growing steadily since around last September. Saskatchewan isn't the only province where pronouns will be debated at the polls this fall. New Brunswick's provincial election is also expected for October, with the 21st of the month being the last possible day the vote can take place.
New Brunswick's Progressive Conservative government set off a national debate over school pronouns last summer with controversial changes to its sexual orientation and gender identity guidelines for schools, known as Policy 713.
New Brunswick Premier Blaine Higgs has been a highly visible supporter of Policy 713, regularly appearing on political talk shows to defend the contentious guidelines.
Polls have Higgs'PC's neck-andneck with the New Brunswick Liberal Party heading into the fall.

Document 120 of 219

MPP made sex demands on staffer

Author: Kelly, Brian

Publication info: Kelly, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpp-made-sex-demands-on-staffer/docview/3092491720/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+made+sex+demands+on+staffer&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-08-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Kelly%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Algoma-Manitoulin MPP Michael Mantha pressured a female constituency worker for "sexual interactions" and wanted her to send him sexually explicit photos, a probe into his alleged behaviour found.
The veteran provincial politician also required the Elliot Lake-based employee to hug him regularly and tell him of any sexual dreams she had about him, the investigation determined. The woman was sexually harassed by Mantha "on a regular basis."
The woman "raised concerns" about an unsafe work environment in August 2022. She went on a paid leave of absence at the same time.
The Ontario New Democratic Party caucus has a collective agreement with Canadian Office and Professional Employees Union Local 343. Mantha's constituency office fell within the scope of that pact. Two grievances were filed alleging a failure to keep a workplace free of harassment and being terminated without just cause.
Mantha was removed from the NDP caucus in spring 2023 pending the probe's results. A third-party investigation by Mireille Mortimer, of MortimerKhoraych Workplace Investigations, was done between Jan. 31 and July 6, 2023. She was retained by the NDP caucus.
The constituency worker, who is only identified as the grievor in arbitrator Jasbir Parmar's release, Mantha, his lawyer and three witnesses were interviewed. Text messages, online communications and "substantial" video evidence were reviewed.
Mortimer's findings were shared with the party caucus in July 2023. A summary was shared with the worker, Mantha and Local 343 a month later. Mantha was also permanently removed from the NDP caucus in August 2023. He continues to represent his riding as an independent.
"The Mortimer investigation concluded, on a balance of probabilities, that allegations of workplace harassment, discrimination on the basis of sex, abuse of authority and creation of a toxic workplace, had all been substantiated," said Parmar. Mantha's actions contravened his party's workplace harassment, violence and discrimination policy.
The NDP caucus terminated the constituency worker in January, saying that because Mantha was no longer an NDP MPP, her position did not fall under the collective agreement with the political party. Other constituency workers in Mantha's office lost their jobs at the same time. Local 343 grieved the woman's firing.
Parmar found the worker did not lose her job for just cause.
"Rather, as with the other constituency assistants in the office, the grievor's employment with the ONDP caucus was frustrated, through no fault of her own, following the removal of MPP Mantha from the ONDP caucus based on the substantiated findings that he had engaged in sexual harassment, workplace harassment, discrimination on the basis of sex, abuse of authority and the creation of a toxic workplace in relation to the grievor during the period of her employment," said Parmar.
How the woman's job concerns and workplace harassment grievance are to be dealt with is up to the NDP caucus and Local 343.
The arbitrator will get involved if the two groups can't agree what to do. The woman wants her job back "and damages for any losses that may have occurred," said Parmar.
Mantha called the last two years "very challenging.
"But it is time to move past this matter," he said in a statement dated Saturday. "While I disagree with some of the characterizations made, on the advice of my legal counsel I will not be commenting further at this time."
Mantha will "leave the decision on my future in the hands of voters in Algoma-Manitoulin."
Mantha has served the riding since defeating long-time Liberal incumbent Mike Brown in 2011. He was a constituency assistant to NDP MP Carol Hughes for four years. Mantha did not participate in the arbitration hearing. btkelly@postmedia.com On X: @Saultreporter

Document 121 of 219

Trudeau defends billions spent attracting electric vehicle industry; PM says slowdowns in the EV market are just bumps along the road

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-defends-billions-spent-attracting/docview/3092491595/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+defends+billions+spent+attracting+electric+vehicle+industry%3B+PM+says+slowdowns+in+the+EV+market+are+just+bumps+along+the+road&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-08-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau defended his government's decision to spend billions attracting electric vehicle manufacturing, arguing some recent slowdowns in the market are just bumps along the road.
"Canada has positioned itself to be a leader in the EV industry and we will continue to be because those are where the jobs are going to be, not just a couple of years from now, but a decade from now, a generation from now," he said Monday.
Trudeau was in Napanee, Ont., where he announced $44.3 million, alongside $20 million from the government of Ontario to go toward a $575-million expansion of a Goodyear plant in the community. The expanded plant will come with 200 news jobs and will help produce tires for EV vehicles.
The Goodyear announcement is relatively small compared to the sizable amounts of money the government has spent to help companies like Volkswagen, Honda and Stelantis set up new manufacturing facilities for EV batteries and vehicles.
The government has also given money to companies in the battery supply chain, including Umicore which planned a facility near Kingston, but has since put the project on hold citing a slowdown in EV sales.
Ford recently announced plans to use an Oakville, Ont., plant that has been designated for future EV production to produce a gas-powered pickup truck instead.
Trudeau said the government knows there are going to be ups and downs as the new technology gets underway, but long-term the industry will be significant and Canada has the critical minerals that can make the project a success.
"We know that climate change is real. We know that electric vehicles are going to be essential for the future and the choices that we've made as a government have ensured that Canada will be part of it."
Trudeau called out opposition leader Pierre Poilievre as unsupportive of the EV investments saying the Conservatives would not have made any of the investments.
"I know there's a lot of people who wouldn't have made that choice. The Conservative Party of Canada has said very clearly it would not have made this choice, of investing in EVs, investing in workers."
Last week, Poilievre said a Conservative government would impose tariffs on EVs as well as steel and aluminum, from China and called out Trudeau for failing to do so.
"Canada's workers are the best in the world. They deserve powerful paycheques and a prime minister who will protect them, rather than allowing authoritarian overseas governments to steal our jobs with their low labour standards and poor environmental policies," Poilievre said.
The Biden administration has imposed tariffs on Chinese made EVs and while the Liberals launched formal consultations on tariffs, they have yet to announce any new levies.
Trudeau accused Poilievre of focusing only on the politics instead of actually trying to support Canadian workers.
"It's a bit of a joke that Poilievre is suddenly talking about workers in the auto industry. He has said repeatedly that he wouldn't be making these investments in our auto industry," the prime minister said. "For him to suddenly turn around and say, we're worried about EVs. That's baloney. He's looking for a political angle because that's all he does." National Post

Document 122 of 219

Protecting Trudeau is ruining political careers; Blaming others for PM's failings won't fool voters

Author: Snobelen, John

Publication info: Snobelen, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/protecting-trudeau-is-ruining-political-careers/docview/3090788425/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Protecting+Trudeau+is+ruining+political+careers%3B+Blaming+others+for+PM%27s+failings+won%27t+fool+voters&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-08-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Snobelen%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Ontario premier David Peterson told me he quit politics for health reasons. He said the people got sick of him.
Last week, U.S. President Joe Biden quit the 2024 election race, mere weeks before his party's nomination convention. He said he was leaving to protect democracy.
Well, maybe. But there are some other good reasons why Biden belatedly and seemingly begrudgingly left the race.
Running for office isn't a personal decision.
It starts with an individual making the commitment to begin the journey but, early in that process, it becomes by necessity a group effort.
It takes people and money to win a campaign.
Biden was bleeding support when he finally got the message his time was up. He may not have run out of ambition, but he was running out of cash.
Political leaders in Canada face a similar dilemma. They can't win on their own. Canadian elections are cheaper and raising cash isn't as crucial as it is in the United States.
But leaders need the support of people beyond the cadre of camp followers who run elections for a living. They need the party faithful to believe, win or lose, they represent the future of the party.
Justin Trudeau has lost that support. For months, Trudeau has known most Canadians will not vote for him in the next federal election. You don't need to be a political savant to figure that out.
Canadians are simply sick of him. No amount of foreign costume parties or fake cultural divides will solve that.
No doubt the politicos who have tied their futures and their income to the Trudeau brand are still plotting and scheming to find a way to some sort of victory, even if that victory is just keeping Trudeau in power for a little longer. But, it is a vain and selfish effort.
Protecting Trudeau has already ruined the political lives of some of the brightest lights in the Liberal tent. Jody Wilson-Raybould, Jane Philpott and Bill Morneau were once the best and brightest in the Trudeau cabinet. Now they are political roadkill, sacrificed to protect Trudeau.
Now the Trudeau brain trust, desperate to find someone not named Trudeau to blame for a byelection loss and sagging polls, have set their sights on Chrystia Freeland. That's right, the new target for blame is none other than the deputy PM and finance minister.
A couple of weeks ago, leaks from senior officials in the prime minister's office suggested Freeland was to blame for the government's misfortunes. Apparently, she is just not a good communicator.
This is a classic political hatchet job. First, Freeland's abilities or lack thereof aren't some new discoveries. She has been Trudeau's minister of everything for years. Second, the world's best carnival barker couldn't sell Trudeau's economic plan.
Covertly attacking Freeland demonstrates the Trudeau government has turned its fire inward. That's not a winning strategy.
It's also strong evidence Trudeau has lost his party. He is, now and forever, yesterday's man in the Liberal party.
Next week at the Democratic Party convention, people will say nice things about Joe Biden. But, if Kamala Harris loses the election to Donald Trump, Democrats will blame Biden for being the stubborn man who stayed well past his time.
Someday soon, the Liberals may feel the same way about Trudeau. His time in office will be forever marred by his dismal treatment of the best of his cabinet. John Snobelen was a cabinet minister in the Conservative government of Ontario premier Mike Harris from 1995 to 2002.

Subject: Elections; Political parties

Location: United States--US

People: Biden, Joseph R Jr

Document 123 of 219

Poll good for Ford, painful for Stiles, Crombie

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poll-good-ford-painful-stiles-crombie/docview/3087119995/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poll+good+for+Ford%2C+painful+for+Stiles%2C+Crombie&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-08-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford is more popular with voters than he was two years ago.
That's if you believe the latest poll by Abacus Data for the Toronto Star.
The latest survey of political opinion shows Ford and his PC Party would take 44 per cent of the popular vote if an election was held today. While Ford should be happy with the results, they should prompt some soul searching for the opposition parties.
Abacus pegs the Ontario Liberals under Bonnie Crombie at 26 per cent support, a full 18 points back of Ford. The NDP, which is actually the official Opposition and has the second most seats, has the support of just 19 per cent of poll respondents.
Compared to the 2022 election, Ford's PCs are up three points, the Liberals are up two points and the NDP are down five points.
This is completely detached from the rending of garments and gnashing of teeth we see in the media.
Ontario Place, the Science Centre, beer and wine in corner stores, the LCBO strike, and allowing private cataract surgery and MRI scans paid for with your health card are some of the stories used to portray Ford as being offside the Ontario public.
In reality, it seems he's right where he needs to be.
Marit Stiles has the most to worry about in these polling numbers. The NDP received 24 per cent of the popular vote in the 2022 election, taking 31 seats to retain status as the official Opposition.
Stiles has never been able to break through since taking over the leadership of the NDP and becoming Opposition leader in February 2023. Given the resources at the party's disposal in terms of budget and staff provided by the legislature given their official Opposition status, they should be well ahead of the Liberals.
Instead of using their position to try to grow their base of support, the NDP have too often looked inward. The party regularly uses their time in question period to press on issues that are important to the leadership of the province's public sector unions, but not relatable to the average voter.
As long as the NDP chooses to act as the official lobbying firm of public sector union bosses, they won't grow their voter support.
Crombie and the Liberals also should be worried by these polling results.
Sure, the party is now polling two points higher than what they received in the last election, but the NDP is down five points and the Liberals didn't scoop it all up. Neither have they dented Ford's voter support, which they must do to form government.
Time and again, the messaging coming from Crombie and Stiles is identical. It's hard to differentiate yourself from your opponent if you are saying the same thing.
Being opposed to everything Ford does without offering a realistic alternative won't win votes.
Until one of these parties and leaders starts taking on Ford in a way that resonates with voters, until they start speaking about issues that matter to voters, there won't be a real change in this polling. That suits Ford just fine. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 124 of 219

Liberal MP dismisses speculation about Freeland's political future; Former Ontario Liberal caucus chair comes to finance minister's defence

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberal-mp-dismisses-speculation-about-freelands/docview/3086182192/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+MP+dismisses+speculation+about+Freeland%27s+political+future%3B+Former+Ontario+Liberal+caucus+chair+comes+to+finance+minister%27s+defence&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-07-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Toronto Liberal MP dismissed the ongoing chatter about Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland's political future as "whispers in the shadows" and compared it to sports commentators looking to fill airtime during the off-season.
James Maloney, a former Ontario Liberal caucus chair, came to Freeland's defence on Monday during a housing announcement.
"We're standing in Toronto.
We're in a hockey town. It's the off-season. So, what do people do? They spend all their time speculating on whether John Tavares or Auston Matthews should be the No. 1 centre. The focus we have is on the team," he said.
"I can tell you - I talk to my caucus colleagues every single day - and we have complete confidence in Chrystia Freeland as the finance minister," he added.
Freeland was being asked about a recent report from the Toronto Star citing anonymous Liberal MPs who said they would want to see a cabinet shuffle before the Parliament returns in the fall, with several saying that Freeland should be shuffled out of finance.
One of them said "Freeland should definitely get moved" whereas another said they were surprised her name was coming up, calling her "very capable" despite some "mistakes."
Shortly after, a spokesperson for Freeland said several MPs would be willing to defend her. Maloney was one of them.
"These whispers in the shadows - and that's what they are - take them for what they're worth. It's like listening to talk radio, sports radio. You've got all kinds of interesting ideas, but look, we are a team as a caucus," he told reporters on Monday.
"You know what that means? It means we talk to each other, we work together, we throw around ideas."
Maloney said the Liberal caucus is "unequivocally" united behind Freeland and seemed to dare his anonymous colleagues to repeat their comments with their identities revealed.
"Chrystia Freeland has done a great job, and there isn't a single person in my caucus who would say anything to the contrary," he said.
When asked about the Star's report on Monday, Freeland said she takes the point of view of her caucus colleagues "really, really seriously" while reverting to the government's policies which she said "need to be based on conversations within caucus."
"For me, my focus is on working hard to deliver for Canadians every single day," she said.
Freeland announced that a 30-year mortgage amortization, which she announced in her last budget, would be offered by lenders as of Aug. 1. She also reiterated her government's promises on childcare, dental care, and investments in research.
"So, that's where my focus is.
It's on Canada and Canadians and working hard with all my caucus colleagues to deliver that," she said.
Freeland's political future has been in the spotlight since the Globe and Mail reported earlier this month that the prime minister's staff was unhappy with some of her economic messaging while the party is reeling from its byelection defeat in a safe Toronto riding.
Since then, Freeland has refused to say if she received assurances from Justin Trudeau himself that she would be staying on in her current role, instead insisting that she has the "confidence" she needs to do her job effectively.
She repeated that line on Monday. "I feel I have the support I need to do my job and to focus on what my job is, which is delivering for Canada and Canadians," she said.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's political future has also been the subject of intense speculation especially since the byelection defeat, although New Brunswick Liberal MP Wayne Long has been the only sitting member of the caucus to publicly call for his resignation.
Trudeau has often insisted that he is staying on as prime minister until the next election.
But it remains to be seen if he will proceed with any major changes in his cabinet or his team in the next few weeks. He said he has been trying to recruit former central banker Mark Carney for years to join his team, without success.
Until now, this summer, there has been only a one-person cabinet shuffle to replace Newfoundland MP Seamus O'Regan, who announced he will not be running again, by Quebec MP Steven MacKinnon as minister for labour and seniors.
The change came just as MacKinnon was ending his six-month replacement as government House leader during Karina Gould's maternity leave. National Post calevesque@postmedia.com

Document 125 of 219

Free Stanley Cup hockey tickets may be costly

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/free-stanley-cup-hockey-tickets-may-be-costly/docview/3084649554/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Free+Stanley+Cup+hockey+tickets+may+be+costly&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Those free hockey tickets make some of Premier Danielle Smith's allies even angrier than her enemies.
When that happens, you know a politician has badly slipped up. Smith further stokes embers of rebellion that were already flaring in the base of her own party.
Tickets get pricey during a Stanley Cup run. For Game 6 of the Oilers-Panthers series, the cheapest seat available cost more than $1,800.
Unless you're Smith, one of her cabinet ministers or staffers. Then you can accept free admission to a luxury box from eager interests. This path was greased by her United Conservative Party itself when it loosened the rules for gifts just before Christmas.
As the Globe and Mail reported, Smith and her insiders got free passes in both Vancouver and Edmonton. An Edmonton host for ministers and staffers was MHCare Medical and its CEO, Sam Mraiche.
The company was tied to the purchase of children's pain medication from Turkey, a Smith brainstorm that cost the public $75 million with little health benefit.
Without denying these details, the premier says citizens need to know their leaders are cheering for the home side.
Citizens might agree - if politicians bought their own tickets occasionally.
"Having ministers and staff sit in lobbyists'luxury boxes while we are facing an affordability crisis in this province not only looks bad, it shows they're living in a different world than the average Albertan," NDP Leader Naheed Nenshi said on X. "The revelations about multiple ministers, staff members and the premier herself attending playoff hockey games on lobbyists'dimes should come as no surprise to Albertans given the level of cronyism shown by this government."
Even stronger, and more surprising, is the word from deep inside the premier's own conservative base.
"I think it's very disappointing. It puts into people's minds what other political favours are being proposed and done in secret behind closed doors," says Nadine Wellwood of the 1905 committee, a new pressure group alternative to David Parker's Take Back Alberta.
"I think every Albertan would expect our premier to be at an event like a hockey playoff when our team is being represented. It's how she chose to do it, shrouded in secrecy.
"So I think it was a real disservice, a breach of trust.
"You combine this with things like her desire to bring the Heritage Trust Fund under the premier's office, and ... that she's appointed Alison Redford.
"It's not just one thing. All these things are building."
The committee petitioned against Smith's recent appointment of former PC premier Redford to the board of Invest Alberta Corp.
Redford was run out of office in 2014 after findings she misused government aircraft for personal purposes and planned to have a personal living space - the so-called Sky Palace - constructed in a government building.
Wellwood has history with Smith and the UCP. She was disqualified as a candidate in Livingston-Macleod for advocating alternate COVID treatments and equating vaccine passports to Hitler's Nazi regime.
The party was determined not to hand the NDP points of attack in the coming election campaign. But many UCP members agreed with Wellwood then, and still do.
Marco Van Huigenbos, a former Fort Macleod councillor convicted of mischief for his role in the Coutts border blockade, believes Smith is heading for a serious challenge in the UCP leadership review vote Nov. 2.
Many conservatives are "disgusted" by the ticket episode, Van Huigenbos says. "There's trouble in conservative land, and if they don't take these concerns seriously it could mean a big problem for her in November."
It's been obvious since the UCP's creation the uprisings start in this active, aggressive, deeply conservative corner of the party base. These people hate cronyism, undue influence and politicians exploiting their elite status.
Now they're starting to see Smith that way. Those hockey tickets may carry a price after all. Don Braid's column appears regularly in the Calgary Herald

Subject: Lobbyists; Nepotism; Politics; Hockey

Business indexing term: Subject: Nepotism

Reg Niganobe seeking office as Liberal MPP

Author: Darbyson, Kyle

Publication info: Darbyson, Kyle. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reg-niganobe-seeking-office-as-liberal-mpp/docview/3084646424/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reg+Niganobe+seeking+office+as+Liberal+MPP&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Darbyson%2C+Kyle&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The former chief of Mississauga First Nation and the Anishinabek Nation is hoping to become the Ontario Liberal Party nominee for the Algoma-Manitoulin riding.
Reg Niganobe made this announcement last Friday through his personal Facebook page, revealing that he decided to seek this nomination after "much thought, consideration, and conversation with family, friends, colleagues, mentors and other trusted leaders."
"For those who have supported me in the past, I seek your continued support and confidence," Niganobe wrote.
"As I seek the nomination bid and complete the required paperwork and processes, I will update everyone on the outcome of my application later. Thank you all for your time, and wish me luck!" Niganobe is no stranger to campaigning for public office, first becoming a member of the Mississauga First Nation band council in 2009 and later being elected chief in 2011.
After serving as Mississauga chief for a decade, Niganobe decided to step down to become the head of the Anishinabek Nation, a political advocacy group that represents 39 Indigenous communities throughout the province of Ontario.
Niganobe's time as Anishinabek Nation Grand Council Chief came to an end in early June when members voted former M'Chigeeng First Nation chief Linda Debassige into this role.
The current representative for Algoma-Manitoulin is Michael Mantha, who's held this seat since 2011.
While Mantha initially won his seat as a member the Ontario NDP, he was ousted from the party last summer following a misconduct investigation.
Now sitting as an independent MPP, Mantha told The Sault Star late last year that he intends to run in the upcoming provincial election, which is tentatively scheduled to be held in June 2026.
"I'm not going to permit anyone to write my final chapter. I have many other chapters in my story to write," Mantha said in late 2023. kdarbyson@postmedia.com

Document 128 of 219

Ford pushing ahead on booze sale expansion

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-pushing-ahead-on-booze-sale-expansion/docview/3082469727/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+pushing+ahead+on+booze+sale+expansion&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The government of Doug Ford moved up the sale of ready-to-drink beverages in grocery stores by two weeks in response to the strike by LCBO workers. Now, it's considering whether it can speed up the timeline for booze in convenience stores as well.
The announcement, made Monday morning, said readyto-drink beverages will be available for sale in the 450 existing grocery stores that sell alcohol starting Thursday. The initial plan was for this change to take effect Aug. 1, but the shutdown of LCBO retail stores due to the strike changed that.
"As the province implements its plan, the government will continue to constantly evaluate how it can deliver choice and convenience," a government release said, hinting they'd like to move sooner on corner stores as well.
The government's plan for expanding to convenience stores originally called for allowing the sale of alcohol in licensed stores after Sept. 5. Allowing expansion ahead of that date might be a government goal now, but it will be hampered by licensing, training requirements for staff and, most importantly, distribution.
Some stores have their licences in place already, others are in the final stages and all will need to put their staff through government-approved training for selling alcohol, but that training is still in development.
Being able to access products would be the main issue with expanding beyond what was announced Monday. While it is retail workers who are on strike, they have been successful in picketing warehouses that supply retailers, bars and restaurants.
Many LCBO convenience outlets - often located outside major urban centres - have been ransacked by those looking for booze. The outlet located at of Hurontario Road and King Street in Caledon, one of the closest outlets to Toronto, was completely sold out of wine and spirits on Sunday.
"Maybe Tuesday," the man working the counter said when asked when they would have stock other than beer.
Many bars and restaurants are also feeling the pinch.
While some were able to stock up, others with low cash flow or those with very high volumes weren't able to do so.
"They've gone from 3,000 products to 125," one bar owner said Monday, expressing frustration with the situation.
And with so few products available, specialty products are in short supply. Any bar or restaurant relying on the sale of high-end whisky, vodka or tequila soon will be running dry on those products unless something changes.
The LCBO was slated to open more than 30 retail stores to the public this week, but that plan was put on hold. Now managers who were slated to operate those retail outlets on a limited schedule will be deployed to find ways to service the bar and restaurant sector.
With the hiccups of online consumer sales that were experienced last week subsiding and with so many additional places to get alcohol, most residents won't notice this strike in our day-to-day lives. If the LCBO and the Ford government can solve the problems of bars and restaurants, even fewer of us will notice.
The strike is based almost entirely on the Ontario Public Service Employees Union wanting the government to reverse course on allowing sale of ready-to-drink beverages in grocery and convenience stores. By moving up the date for grocery expansion, even by just two weeks, the Ford government is signalling that it won't back down.
Ford has been clear; he has no intention of selling or privatizing the LCBO. If the unionized workers decide to stay on strike, they will only damage their future as more people realize they don't need the government liquor store and change their shopping habits.
If the union membership was smart, they'd demand their leadership put the latest contract offer to a vote. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Alcohol; Beverages; Convenience stores; Strikes; Restaurants; Union membership; Grocery stores

Business indexing term: Subject: Convenience stores Strikes Restaurants Union membership Grocery stores

Document 129 of 219

Ontario government's groundbreaking plan; Local media needs support at all levels

Author: Taube, Michael

Publication info: Taube, Michael. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-governments-groundbreaking-plan-local/docview/3082469595/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+government%27s+groundbreaking+plan%3B+Local+media+needs+support+at+all+levels&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=Taube%2C+Michael&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Recently, Ontario's Progressive Conservative government announced a plan to help provide financial support for newspaper publishers in the province. The best part about it: it doesn't come at any cost to the taxpayers.
Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy sent letters to several provincial Crown corporations, including the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corp., Ontario Cannabis Retail Corp., and the Liquor Control Board of Ontario. These agencies were directed to allocate one-quarter of their annual advertising budget, which is reportedly over $100 million, to Ontario's news publishers by Sept. 3.
This means there will be roughly $25 million available for news organizations in Ontario, including the National Post's parent company, Postmedia Network Inc., to fund local journalism.
"These significant advertising budgets should help support Ontario-based publishers and the Ontario-based workers creating local news content for people in our province," reads Bethlenfalvy's letter. It also highlights the Ontario government's belief there is a "compelling opportunity to leverage the advertising spends of Ontario's major government agencies to support Ontario workers and content."
This is a creative and forward-thinking economic plan by Premier Doug Ford and the Ontario PCs. It will help ensure that a strong focus on local news content will be maintained, and significant job opportunities will be available for young journalists and established veterans alike. Most importantly, it will play a vital role in the survival of Ontario newspapers.
The reality we all have to face is that there's going to be some level of financial involvement by federal and provincial governments in Canada's news industry, both now and in the future.
The vast majority of our newspapers, magazines and news websites currently receive some type of public funding. That being said, the use of direct subsidies can't be the only funding lifeline for Canadian publications, including newspapers. Ontario's directive to allocate part of the advertising budget of a number of provincial Crown corporations is, therefore, a step in the right direction.
This advertising budget is, unlike the introduction of direct subsidies, already earmarked and accounted for in annual government expenditures. It's an existing sum rather than an additional source of taxpayer money. Hence, the $25 million allocated for Ontario publications simply involves shifting one-quarter of the budget, while the remaining three-quarters can be used as the Crown corporations see fit.
The Ford government's policy will allow government agencies to continue to get their messages across to Ontarians, while investing in local journalism. The money is to be used for Ontario-based newspapers, news content and journalists working in the profession.
This funding directive isn't being handed out with no strings attached, either. A review will apparently be conducted on a quarterly basis.
If the Ontario government feels that the main objectives of "promoting local content and culture and supporting Ontario jobs" aren't being met, it will consider withdrawing the funding altogether. Hence, it's up to Ontario newspapers to ensure that the money is being used effectively, wisely and, above all, properly.
Andrew MacLeod, Postmedia's president and chief executive officer, said, "I'm certainly extremely grateful to the leadership of Premier Ford and his team. My hope is this will serve as an example, a north star, not just for other provincial governments, but for the federal government and for major corporations." I agree with MacLeod's sentiment wholeheartedly. There are ways for the public and private sectors to help and support Canadian journalism that don't involve direct subsidies. Ford and the Ontario PCs have identified one financial path to success. There are many others still to be considered and utilized.

Subject: Journalism; Government agencies; Advertising; Newspaper industry; Funding; Subsidies

Business indexing term: Subject: Advertising Newspaper industry Subsidies

Identifier / keyword: recently; progressive; conservative; government; announced; provide

Title: Ontario government's groundbreaking plan; Local media needs support at all levels

Document 130 of 219

Crowd the extremists out of our politics

Author: Pellerin, Brigitte

Publication info: Pellerin, Brigitte. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crowd-extremists-out-our-politics/docview/3081471950/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crowd+the+extremists+out+of+our+politics&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Pellerin%2C+Brigitte&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You need a lot of valuable skills to manage an organization like the Carp Agricultural Society. Nobody would expect that list to include "being able to spot political extremists from a mile away." Yet it is increasingly necessary to equip ourselves for this new reality.
As you may have heard, the Carp organization inadvertently rented a hall to Diagolon. For the hitherto blissfully unaware, this is a loose group of extreme right-wingers whose leader once "joked" about raping Pierre Poilievre's wife, which prompted the Conservative leader - despite having previously been photographed perhaps unknowingly shaking the group leader's hand - to call the lot of them "dirtbags" and "losers."
I heartily sympathize. There are plenty of well-documented reasons to avoid shaking hands with this character, even before he laughed on camera about sexual assault. But I wouldn't expect people whose lives are spent in productive pursuits outside the sewers of Twitter to be as extremist-aware as your favourite columnist is.
"We didn't know who they were when they booked," Jean Sullivan, co-president of the Carp Agricultural Society, told the media. "It's not within our values or mission. It was an unfortunate series of events."
Nobody can blame Sullivan for having been hoodwinked, especially as the Diagolon group didn't go out of its way to disclose who they were or what their purpose was. "From my understanding, they did not tell us what the organization was and I had never heard of them before Saturday night," Sullivan added.
I'm not as ready to absolve Goldie Ghamari, MPP for Carleton, who was kicked out of Doug Ford's Conservative caucus over a meeting with U.K. far-right extremist Tommy Robinson. Politicians have staff to do research for them and warn them not to meet someone like Robinson who, as Randall Denley reminded Citizen readers recently, has earned "various criminal convictions" for his activities.
At this point, you may be reading this and wondering, "Is it me, or is there a noticeable increase in run-ins with unsavoury extremists?" It's not you, and it's not just in Canada either.
In France, voters got a nasty scare after the first round of legislative elections yielded a victory for Marine Le Pen's extremist National Rally party, with 33 per cent support. After the second round of voting this past Sunday, the result was entirely different. The far-right party was not the winner; French voters gave first place to a brand-new coalition of four left-wing parties. Voters held their noses and cast their ballots for whichever candidate had the best chance of beating the National Rally contender.
As David Broder noted in the New York Times, one of the most remarkable developments in that second round of voting was how left-wing voters in contests where the two leading candidates were President Emmanuel Macron's centrist candidate or the far-right candidate, backed the centrist or abstained from voting.
The result may prove to be an ungovernable mess, but at least in the immediate future, French voters avoided electing a far-right party. Not that left-wing extremists would have been better, in case you were wondering. Extremism is always bad in politics, and the trick is for the rest of us who are scattered across the regular political spectrum to occupy as much space as possible so that extremists have nowhere to grow. The reaction of the people of Carp, who objected strenuously and publicly to Diagolon being in their lovely community, is exactly what we need to do.
Despite our disagreements on the best ways to achieve desirable goals, the vast majority of us agree on the fundamentals that make for a good and decent society. Individual rights (very much including reproductive rights), the rule of law and respect for our shared institutions of governance. And a baseline of properly financed social services such as health and education.
What we agree on is what makes this country such a desirable place to live. It's worth protecting it against extremists of all kinds. Brigitte Pellerin (they/them) is an Ottawa writer.

Subject: Extremism; Sex crimes; Voters; Rape; Society; Elections

Identifier / keyword: valuable; skills; manage; organization; agricultural; society

Document 131 of 219

Continuing to fund Catholic schools is a sin; Placing public dollars into Catholic schools has no place in 21stcentury Ontario, teacher hiring 'explicitly discriminates'

Author: Chow, Peter

Publication info: Chow, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/continuing-fund-catholic-schools-is-sin-placing/docview/3077560168/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Continuing+to+fund+Catholic+schools+is+a+sin%3B+Placing+public+dollars+into+Catholic+schools+has+no+place+in+21stcentury+Ontario%2C+teacher+hiring+%27explicitly+discriminates%27&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-07-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Chow%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It doesn't matter if you are Catholic, Protestant, Jewish, Muslim, Hindu, Buddhist, atheist, agnostic, or another religion. If you live in Ontario, you are forced to pay for Catholic schools with your tax money.
It is time to reconsider the funding of Catholic schools, given the fact that Ontario is among the world's most diverse and cosmopolitan societies.
Publicly funded schools should not be places where religious dogma is taught but places where students learn mathematics, science, history, literature, language skills, computer skills, and other academic subjects without indoctrination in one particular faith at the expense of others.
Students should receive a well-rounded education that includes an understanding of all of Ontario's major religious denominations, thereby helping young people understand the world they live in.
The reality of the demographics of Ontario is that it is multi-lingual, multicultural and multi-faith. It is also secular.
In a world where religious tensions are growing, where religious nationalism is rearing its ugly head, stressing what unites our next generation of citizens should be of paramount importance.
There is no better way to accomplish that than establishing a single, non-sectarian, secular public school system.
The Algoma District School Board, the English-language public district school board is the school district administrator for 39 elementary and six secondary schools in the Algoma District.
The Huron-Superior Catholic District School Board serves the communities of Blind River, Chapleau, Elliot Lake, Espanola, Hornepayne, Massey, Sault Ste. Marie, Wawa and White River, and administers 18 elementary and two secondary schools.
Ontario is the only province in Canada to fund four different types of school boards: public Anglophone, public Francophone, Roman Catholic Anglophone and Roman Catholic Francophone.
The amount of overlap in educational positions (especially high-ranking ones), buildings (the small town of Valley East in Greater Sudbury - population 22,300 -has four high schools, one each from the four school boards), and specialized program funding, etc. is ridiculous.
Greater Sudbury, population 165,000, has 17 different high schools while Edmonton, with 10 times the population, has 38.
The population of Edmonton is about 1,519,000 and Northern Ontario's is about half that, 780,000.
Northern Ontario has 24 school boards vs Edmonton's three.
There are 72 school boards in Ontario, including 31 English public boards, 29 English Catholic boards, four French public boards, and eight French Catholic boards.
As secular public and Catholic school boards both require public funding, there is a constant struggle between the two to acquire more money from the government at the expense of the other.
Even though approximately 74 per cent of Ontario is non-Catholic, Catholic school boards receive over 51 per cent of total public funding.
Since funding is based on enrolment numbers, Catholic schools have been increasing their non-Catholic student population.
Catholic boards are trying their best to siphon students off the public stream into the separate schools in a squabble for public funds.
In the wake of recent budget cuts, being able to keep a school surviving is becoming increasingly harder.
Wasting time and resources trying to pull students from each other's stream only puts more strain on the already tight school boards especially in remote areas.
When a local student population is divided into two streams, in this case, secular and separate, the closure of a community's schools will become more likely.
Many schools in rural Northern Ontario are half-empty as enrolment rates remain marginal or in some cases, decreasing.
Many school boards are facing funding deficits. Despite the provincial government's claims of "increased funding," many school boards argue that the funding is still insufficient to meet their needs, leading to cuts that impact the quality of education.
The Toronto Catholic District School Board is looking at a $66.5 million deficit for the upcoming school year.
The Windsor-Essex Catholic District School Board approved a budget for the next school year with a $2.3 million deficit. The Sudbury Catholic District School Board (SCDSB), the Rainbow District School Board and the Algoma District School Board are all facing deficits and plan to pull from their reserves. RELIGIOUS AFFILIATION IN ONTARIO No religion /Unaffiliated: 31.6 per cent Catholic: 26.0 per cent Protestant: 13.5 per cent Other Christian: 12.7 per cent Muslim: 6.7 per cent Hindu: 4.1 per cent Sikh: 2.1 per cent Jewish: 1.4 per cent DISTRIBUTION OF ONTARIO'S PUBLICLY FUNDED SCHOOL BOARDS English Public: 43.1 per cent French Public: 5.6 per cent English Catholic: 40.3 per cent French Catholic: 11.1 per cent Consolidating the public and Catholic school systems into secular English and French school systems would eliminate the duplication (and sometimes quadruplication) of educational delivery.
Both the Elementary Teachers'Federation of Ontario and the Ontario Secondary Schools Teachers'Federation have now called for the Ontario government to end funding of the province's Catholic schools and move toward one secular school system for each official language.
At the time of Confederation in 1867, it was a concern that the new Province of Ontario would be controlled by a Protestant majority that might abuse its power over education to take away the rights of its Roman Catholic minority.
Two major changes have made the constitutional protection of Catholic education an anachronism.
First, instead of a small minority in a province dominated by Protestants, Catholics are now one of two dominant religious constituencies within a diverse population -around 26 per cent of the Ontario population, comparable to the population of Protestants and other non-Catholic Christians combined.
Second, the public education system is no longer Protestant but secular.
Catholics do not need protection from a Protestant school system because no such system exists.
Ontario is now the only province that funds the religious schools of the Catholic faith exclusively.
The funding of only Catholic schools is discriminatory and violates the equality provision in the Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
Only one thing is standing in the way: Section 93 of the Constitution.
However, it is not something that can't be changed as other Canadian provinces removed this constitutional stipulation in the 1990s.
Both Quebec and Newfoundland were able to amend section 93 of the Constitution and eliminate their Catholic school systems.
Neither Quebec nor Newfoundland faced opposition from the federal government when changes were made because education is a provincial jurisdiction and, when most people wanted the change, the federal government did not challenge them.
Newfoundland voted by a majority of 74 per cent to end the Catholic school system, in a 1997 referendum, saving over $50 million a year.
Ontario could proceed by first holding a referendum or following in the footsteps of Quebec in 1998 and creating an exception to Section 93.
Combining Ontario's two largest school systems would save over $1.6 billion a year (the estimate in 2016, equal to $2.08 billion today), a staggering sum when you consider that Ford's Progressive Conservative government projects a deficit of $9.8 billion in 2024-2025.
Then there's the ongoing battle in Catholic school districts about how to fully accept and integrate Gay-Straight Alliances (GSAs), which promote mutually supportive relationships among students.
And, of course, there are the instances where Catholic-school officials have organized field trips to attend protests on behalf of the church's teachings against abortion.
None of this should surprise.
It's inevitable that an educational system based on the conservative teachings of the Roman Catholic Church will be in open conflict with the socially progressive direction of Canadian society. At issue is not whether such schools should exist but whether the rest of us should continue to pay for them.
An applicant for a teaching position in a Toronto Catholic school board was told that she needed a letter from a priest and proof that she was baptized.
When she spoke up to say this was a violation of the Canada Charter of Rights and Freedoms, she was frostily told that this is the law in Ontario and written in the constitution.
End of story. How does the publicly funded school system get away with discrimination based on religion in their hiring practices? With teaching jobs in short supply in the province, a non-Catholic is immediately at a disadvantage, as a third of the jobs are with Catholic boards. Ontario's Catholic school boards remain tightly tied to the Catholic Church. Hiring of teachers into Catholic schools explicitly discriminates on this basis, with Catholic school boards requiring a signed affirmation from a priest that the candidate "participates in the sacramental life of the Church" and "comes from a Catholic background."
The unspoken assumption underpinning this - that Catholic children might somehow be irreparably corrupted by contact with a non-Catholic teacher, or that only other Catholics are worthy of a child's trust and respect - is frankly disgusting.
In no other avenue of work are employers allowed to discriminate on the basis of religion like this.
In 1999, the United Nations Human Rights Committee determined that Canada was in violation of Article 26 of the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights, because Ontario's Ministry of Education discriminates against non-Catholics by continuing to publicly fund separate Catholic schools, but not those of any other religious groups.
To comply with the ruling Ontario could either extend funding to other religious schools or end funding to Catholic schools; 25 years later, the province has done neither.
In Ontario's 2007 election, then-Progressive Conservative leader John Tory argued that publicly funding only Catholic schools represents unequal treatment for all other religions.
However, Tory picked the wrong solution, proposing to fund all religions' schools.
Ontario voters rejected Tory's proposal in the 2007 provincial election - uncomfortable with the idea of state funding for Evangelical Protestant and Jewish and Muslim schools.
Tory lost the election, largely due to his mishandling of this issue.
Ever since then, both the Liberals and Conservatives, have been deathly afraid to revisit the issue, treating it as politically radioactive. Politicians, the PCs, Liberals and even the NDP, all stated during the last Ontario election campaign that they had no interest in shaking up the status quo on the issue.
As of right now, in Ontario only one party with seats in parliament, the Green Party (two seats), supports the merging of Catholic and public-school boards.
Public funding for Catholic schools has no place in 21st-century Ontario.
Ontario isn't living up to its mission of fairness with Catholic schools being allowed to take public funds to teach Catholic beliefs while any other faith-oriented schooling is simply out of luck, requiring families to pay out of pocket if they wanted different religious education.
Not only are the potential cost savings an impactful way to combat our deficit, but it's pulling Ontario back from being a province of equality and inclusion.
Funding of Ontario's Catholic schools will persist until an Ontario government has the courage to adopt the only principled solution, to do the next right thing, to eliminate public funding for Ontario's Catholic schools and re-affirm the separation of church and state in our educational system.
A call for an end to public funding of Catholic schools would be a winner on election day for the party that pushes the idea.
That's especially relevant for the Liberals and NDP, both of whom these days are promoting seemingly indistinctive views on major topics and desperately need a defining policy - a "wedge" issue - to truly differentiate themselves in the eyes of voters.
For these two parties, not only does calling for a single, publicly funded secular school system make sense financially and from a fairness standpoint, it could be a huge vote-getter, with polls consistently showing over the past two decades that well over 65 percent of those surveyed oppose funding Catholic schools.
The Liberals and NDP seem to be running scared, fearful of losing support from Catholic voters. But leaders in Quebec and Newfoundland suffered no political damage when they acted to end Catholic school funding. Indeed, Newfoundland held a referendum in 1997, in which 74 per cent of voters endorsed the move.
A similar outcome likely would happen in Ontario, according to pollsters.
Signs that the public funding of Catholic schools in Canada might be at an end aren't hard to divine.
Organized religion is losing significance, the Roman Catholic Church's reputation is at a low point, and government budgets are shrinking.
Politicians are loath to risk the ire of the powerful and vocal minority whom the separate system serves, but this is no time for cowardice.
Cynical Ontario politicians should scoop their guts up off the floor and back up their rhetoric with action.
If Catholic schools cannot meet the imperatives of safety and inclusion, they ought not to be publicly funded. They ought to be amalgamated into the public system.
The current system of funding both the public and Catholic school systems is no longer sustainable in a secular multicultural society. Down the road, we will look back and wonder why it took so long in Ontario. Dr. Peter Chow is a retired Sault Ste. Marie physician

Subject: Catholic schools; Teaching; Secularism; Population; Students; Hiring; Catholic churches; Referendums; Anglophones; Funding; Christianity; Elementary schools; School boards; Religion; School systems; Secondary schools; Teachers; Education; School districts

Business indexing term: Subject: Hiring

Document 132 of 219

Inconvenient truth about easier Liquor access

Author: Baker, Patricia

Publication info: Baker, Patricia. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/inconvenient-truth-about-easier-liquor-access/docview/3077559662/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Inconvenient+truth+about+easier+Liquor+access&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-07-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Baker%2C+Patricia&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Would you prefer better access to the purchase of beer and wine in corner stores or welcome first responders at your door in a timely fashion when you have a crisis on your hands? You are not going to get both because Doug Ford is bound and determined that alcohol in corner and grocery stores take precedence over the health, education and safety of the people of Ontario.
This latest move to enrich the pockets of his corporate friends is estimated to cost Ontario taxpayers millions of dollars, if not over a billion when the early implementation payout to the Beer Store owners is settled.
The LCBO will lose profits from their sales when corner stores and grocery outlets can sell more of the same alcohol products. What about all the employees who may lose their jobs, many of them unionized? Corner and grocery stores will not have the variety that LCBO and the Beer Store have, so shoppers will still visit both. But education, health care and housing are in bad shape and people are suffering.
Spend the billions here first and get these three houses in order. But these urgencies will not give the Ford government the gratuities and hoopla they covet that beer and wine will.
There is the answer to that. One of the biggest problems this province continues to face is the onward and upward privatization of our public health care system. It is floundering with insufficient funding, the loss of physicians, nurses and support staff to the private system for better pay and working conditions.
You can't blame them, but why try to put the public system on its knees to funnel billions into private health care that most average earning Canadians can't afford to access let alone even think about accessing.
There is no excuse for patients dying on stretchers awaiting to be seen. Nor is there an excuse for elderly patients in long term care wards in hospitals to be forced to pay $400 a day if they refuse to transfer to a care home not of their choosing.
These are human beings at the end of their lives, having served and supported this country and province. Here in northern Ontario, it takes at least an hour's travel by family to visit their loved ones in a distant facility not of their choosing. It is acceptable to the government to get them out of a hospital bed.
Some private care homes who were discovered during the COVID pandemic to be so disgusting and pathetic continue to house the elderly with the help of the Ford government.
How many elderly loved ones have died in misery in places like this, and they continue to operate? There is no excuse for wait times of over a year or more for orthopedic surgical interventions such as knee and hip replacements. This is just the tip of the iceberg as waits for other interventions, both medical and surgical are just as long, if not longer.
But for those who have the financial ability to pay for treatment in a private facility, the wait time is much shorter. Anyone who can afford to go to private health care for help is welcome to do so, it is their right.
But you just can't leave so many patients on a wait list because they can't afford alternate care or the government is underfunding hospitals and clinics to the point, they are unable to function in an efficient manner.
Millions are without a family physician. Having one serves to open doors to specialized care and diagnostic interventions. These unfortunate souls must wait for hours in emergency rooms to get prescriptions reordered, obtain a referral to a specialist or to report new symptoms that are worrying.
Their urgent need to go to ER is degrading and frustrating because they do not always require emergency care. Walk-in clinics are in short supply and those which require an appointment are booked solid before they even open for the day.
Ford should be focusing on creating and opening walk-in and group clinics which would be integrated into the public system. Open more medical schools in Ontario, especially Northern Ontario, and make it more appealing and financially worthwhile to choose family or emergency room medicine as a career.
Make it less challenging for applicants to get into medical school in the first place. This is not to minimize requirements to fast tract applicants. But clean up the application and acceptance process, streamline it, look after any backlogs and hire qualified people to achieve the most beneficial results.
There is too much bureaucracy for both domestic and international applicants. Why do we have immigrants and those without permanent residency status working two, three or four low paying jobs, when many of them are already educated in health care specialties in their homelands? Doesn't it add up to place them in the educational programs so they can become integrated in their respective specialties here in Canada, then advance them toward a type of internship which if successful would lead to registration and licence to practice without restrictions.
There are international students who come here to study in health care so they can work their way through the system and finally work at the careers they already were educated for in their homelands.
These processes do exist, but if you speak with those who are desperately trying to jump through the hoops towards success in systems that are not easy to maneuver due to backlogs that hamstring many hopes of gaining what they require to gain full time employment and eventually permanent resident status.
Domestic students have their own set of roadblocks. There are not enough seats in medical schools. Hospitals are on strict budgets which have been shrinking over the years. Staffing levels fluctuate, and services are based on funding.
Tuition costs are rising, and some programs available to students are no longer running. Colleges and universities continue to struggle with inadequate funding.
Grade schools are under enormous pressure to provide students with what is mandated in their curriculums. Class sizes are too large for teachers to provide even basic learning skills at times.
Many schools are aging facilities and unable to provide cooling for students who are now along with the rest of us experiencing the seriousness of heat and climate change.
You can't educate students and give them what they deserve, if teachers go to their classes every day with their hands tied behind their backs due to funding shortfalls and aging buildings.
Health-care professionals cannot care for patients who require medical attention if the funding falls short year after year and systems themselves are floundering with staff burnout because of the pressures they face to keep up.
If you reside in Northern Ontario, everything mentioned above in this article is compounded by distance, the availability of specialized care, transportation costs and having the ability to secure a family physician.
Paramedics are an integral part of health care as they are quite often the first to arrive at a scene where there may be casualties or victims with very life-threatening injuries. Many 911 calls are to residences where family members are suffering medical emergencies. They could be from industrial sites where injuries can be very serious and require immediate attention.
It could be an incident at a school, a life-threatening allergic reaction or trauma, a motor vehicle collision with death or life altering injuries. The list goes on and on and the significant roles these first responders play are being disrespected by a government that has other agendas.
It has become customary for their arrival times to be lengthened or dispatchers not able to answer the calls in a timely manner. Demand is high, staffing and morale are becoming very severe impediments to the smooth functioning of such a vital health-care service.
Emergency rooms are inundated with patients as well, and many of them have nowhere else to go. Paramedics who deliver patients to ER must stay with them until they are seen by the medical staff.
Patients on arrival are triaged and the more serious are attended to sooner, but those who do not meet those criteria wait along with the paramedic who brought them in.
The ambulance sits outside the doors, out of service until that patient is looked after. Conceivably, if all ambulances and crew sent out on calls all end up in this situation at one or multiple emergency sites, all are out of service.
There may only be a few ambulances and crew available for calls, or none. Hence, the backup and delay of first responder arrivals at calls becomes dangerous in a wait and see scenario on a routine basis.
The transfer of patient care to nurses and physicians on arrival at the ER is the protocol that has been followed in the past and should be the norm now. It isn't and that is a huge red flag the government of Ontario can't seem to grasp the meaning of.
Doug Ford as premiere of Ontario continues to betray the people of this province. He has funneled millions of our tax dollars to private companies, especially in health care, where the return is a two-tear system fraught with inconsistencies and downright favouritism to his wealthy corporate friends.
It seems to be becoming more of a reality that an election could be taking place in 2025, a year or so early. By reveling in his popularity and pairing this with his obsession to gain another term in power, Ford is in essence, telling us he is not done selling off our assets to fulfill any backroom deals he may have already shaken hands on.
Think about it. How can any reasonable individual think that alcohol sales should overshadow a health care system that is structurally and fundamentally crumbing? How can any reasonable individual think that alcohol sales should overshadow all our educational facilities, programs, employees and finally our students, who are the ultimate losers.
But let's make it easier for the public to buy alcohol in the very grocery stores which are gouging the public with soaring food prices. A high number of Ontarians are being forced to visit food banks to eat and feed their families.
It is a cruel and unforgivable irony that grocery stores will be able to keep food prices out of the reach of this province's children, the ones who do not have enough to eat, while at the same timeline their own pockets and those of the very wealthy by selling booze.
Just think about it before getting sick ... and your wait time at the ER is three or more hours minimum. Buy a beer on your way there to soften your anxiety.
Patricia Baker is a Sault Star district correspondent, columnist and retired Sault Area Hospital nurse

Subject: Workforce planning; Hospitals; Patients; Emergency medical care; Students; Paramedics; Beer; Schools; Health care policy; Funding; Employment; Grocery stores
