Document 151 of 219

A primer on prostate cancer

Author: Chow, Peter

Publication info: Chow, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/primer-on-prostate-cancer/docview/3053720165/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+primer+on+prostate+cancer&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-05-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Chow%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On autopsy of men with no known prostate cancer, microscopic prostate cancer is found in 20 per cent of men aged 50-59, almost 30 percent of men 60-69, 36 per cent of men 70-79 (51 per cent of Blacks 70-79) and 47 per cent of men above 80.
Prostate cancer is the most common cancer among Canadian men (excluding non-melanoma skin cancers).
About one in eight men one in six Black men including O.J. Simpson) will be diagnosed with prostate cancer in their lifetime and one in 29 will die from it.
But many, many men will die with prostate cancer without ever even knowing they had it.
In Canada, over 25,000 men will be diagnosed in 2024 with prostate cancer, 20 per cent of all new cancer cases in men. 4,600 men will die from prostate cancer, 10 per cent of all cancer deaths in men.
It is the third leading cause of death from cancer in men in Canada, after lung cancer and colorectal cancer.
The number of deaths from prostate cancer reached 45.1 per 100,000 men in 1995 and has fallen to 22.7 per 100,000.
The increase in prostate cancer incidence largely mirrors screening using the Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA) test.
The death rate for prostate cancer has decreased reflecting improved treatment.
Prostate cancer is one of the least preventable cancers, which underscores the importance of advancements in detection and treatment, but is almost 100 per cent survivable if detected before it spreads.
Prostate cancer is rare in men younger than 40, but the chance of having prostate cancer rises rapidly after age 50.
About 99 per cent of cases occur after age 50, 60 percent in men older than 65 A first-degree relative with the disease increases the risk two to three times.
The average age of diagnosis is 66; 10 per cent of men newly diagnosed with prostate cancer are under 55.
Worldwide, cancers historically restricted to older age groups are now being diagnosed in younger adults, including colorectal, breast, oesophageal, gastric and pancreatic cancers.
There's been an increase in early-onset prostate cancer in men between 15 and 40 years old.
Prostate cancer diagnosed in younger men tends to be more aggressive and more advanced with a lower rate of survival than older men.
The average five-year survival rate for prostate cancer is between 95 per cent and 100 percent for men ages 40-80. For men ages 25-34, the 5-year survival rate is 80 per cent. For men ages 20-29, it's 50 per cent. For men ages 15-25, it's 30 per cent.
Development of male-pattern baldness, age-related prostate enlargement (benign prostatic hypertrophy) and prostate cancer are all dependent upon the presence of the testes, and orchidectomy before the age of 20 prevents them.
As well, removal of the testes early in life extends male lifespan -castration at age 11 increases longevity by 11 years, at age 21 by four years.
However, I think most men would still prefer to hang on to their balls.
Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA) screening detects many cancers that would have otherwise gone undiagnosed. Ontario New Democrats proposed that the Ontario government pick up the cost of a PSA screening test ($35) but this was turned down by the Conservative government.
The PSA test is currently covered by eight other provinces.
Although only about one in 456 men under age 50 will be diagnosed, the rate shoots up to 1 in 54 for ages 50 to 59, 1 in 19 for ages 60 to 69, and 1 in 10 for men 70 and older.
Worldwide, Black men have the greatest risk of having this disease, followed by Caucasians, with the lowest incidences among Asian men.
To estimate the prevalence of undiagnosed, asymptomatic prostate cancer, studies of prostate cancer discovered incidentally at autopsy for unrelated causes of death were done among 6,024 men.
Prostate cancer was found in 20 per cent of men aged 50-59, almost 30 per cent of men 60-69, 36 per cent of men 70-79 (51 per cent of Blacks 70-79) and 47 per cent of men above 80.
This enormous prevalence, coupled with the high sensitivity of PSA screening, has led to the marked increase in the apparent incidence of prostate cancer.
Projected to the current age distribution, these data suggest roughly four million Canadian men have potentially detectable but undiagnosed prostate cancer -the vast majority of whom will die with it, not from it.
Most prostate cancers grow slowly and are confined to the prostate gland, where they may not cause any serious harm and need minimal or even no treatment.
However, some types are aggressive and can spread quickly.
Prostate cancer that's detected early - when it's still confined to the prostate gland - has the best chance for successful treatment.
The five-year net survival for prostate cancer of all stages is 93 per cent.
When detected early, the fiveyear survival rate is close to 100 per cent.
Detected late, it drops to 28 per cent. PROSTATE CANCER SCREENING Digital rectal exam If your doctor finds any abnormalities during digital rectal exam you will need further tests. Most new doctors don't do this anymore, relying on the PSA. Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA) test Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA) is produced by both normal prostate cells and prostate cancer.
So, it's normal for a small amount of PSA to be in your bloodstream.
However, if a higher-than-usual level is found, it may indicate prostate infection, inflammation, enlargement (Benign Prostatic Hypertrophy) or cancer. 17.8 per cent of men screened using PSA testing will have an unnecessary biopsy due to false positives.
Prostate Cancer Canada encourages men in their 40s to get their first PSA test.
PSA testing is not recommended for most patients over age 70 or who have a life expectancy of less than 10 years.
If they are Black or have a family history or a genetic predisposition such as BRCA1 or BRCA2, they should consider having a PSA test starting at age 40. PSA LEVELS - 0 to 4 ng/mL is safe. - 4.0 to 10.0 ng/mL is suspicious and is associated with a 25 percent chance of having prostate cancer. - For men under 50, PSA levels over 2.5 ng/mL may be cause for concern. - For men over 60, however, PSA levels up to 4.0 ng/mL are considered normal. The amount of naturally occurring PSA in your blood gets higher as you age. - 10.0 ng/mL is associated with a 50 percent chance of having prostate cancer.
False positives test results are common with PSA screening -only about 25 percent of men who have a prostate biopsy after an elevated PSA are found to actually have prostate cancer. IF YOUR PSA IS HIGH Usually the first thing doctors will do to follow up on a high PSA level is another PSA test. If the PSA level is still high, your doctor may proceed to one of these follow-up procedures: - PCA3 Test - SelectMDx test - Transrectal Ultrasound (TRUS) - MRI - bladder exam (Cystoscopy) - Prostate biopsy Obviously, cancers are diagnosed that would never have produced any clinical symptoms meaning that hyperzealous PSA screening may over-diagnose prostate cancer with startling frequency, leading to needless interventions and surgeries.
It is estimated that between 75 and 100 men undergo unnecessary treatment, surgery and/or radiation, for every life saved.
It is not just early detection we want -we need early prediction -prediction about who will go on to develop clinical disease, metastatic spread and death.
Early detection by PSA helps tell us what and where and when, but not with whether. P CA3 TEST The PCA3 Test is a urine-based molecular test that detects the presence of Prostate Cancer Antigen 3 (PCA3).
PCA3 is a gene that is over-expressed specifically in prostate cancer cells rather than all prostate cells, and, if present in urine, is an indication of prostate cancer.
Benign enlargement of the prostate will not cause elevated PCA3 unlike PSA.
The PCA3 test is performed on a urine sample.
Just prior to collecting the sample, the doctor will perform a digital rectal exam (DRE) to release cells from the prostate.
The PCA3 test has a sensitivity of 95 percent and a specificity of 95 per cent -meaning only five per cent rates of false-negative or false-positive results.
University Urology Associates in Toronto is one of only a few locations in Canada where the PCA3 test is available. The PCA3 test costs $385. You'll have a fee for at least one office visit or consultation, as well. WHEN SHOULD YOU BE TESTED F OR P CA3? - When you have an abnormal PSA level, with or without a negative biopsy result - If you have already had a negative biopsy result - If you have a high cancer risk, even if you have a low PSA level - To watch for any progression of a low-grade cancer if you are doing active surveillance - To monitor your cancer, if you have already had treatment for prostate cancer Do you need a prostate biopsy? What I would do is get a PSA. If the PSA is elevated, then get a PCA3. If the PCA3 is normal a biopsy most likely is not necessary, avoiding a very painful procedure.
If the PCA3 is elevated, either get a prostate biopsy or follow up with serial PCA3 tests.
Would I go to Toronto, pay $385 for a PCA3 test and another $200 for the consultation just to avoid a prostate biopsy? In a heartbeat.
PROSTATE CANCER MONIT ORING If your PSA levels continue to be elevated yet a previous biopsy showed no cancer, you may choose to monitor your prostate health using the PCA3 tests and avoid further biopsies (they're very unpleasant) unless there's a meaningful change in your test results.
If you were found to have prostate cancer, PCA3 levels can help evaluate the aggressiveness of the tumour -the higher the PCA3, the more aggressive the cancer. SELE CTMDX SelectMDx is a urine test that measures two cancer-related genetic biomarkers, HOXC6 and DLX1.
Combined with other clinical factors like total PSA and age, the test produces a likelihood score of discovering clinically significant disease (defined as Gleason Score equal to or more than 7 ).
This provides your urologist with another important piece of information to consider when recommending whether a biopsy is required and potentially avoid an unnecessary and very painful procedure.
The test has sensitivity of 76 percent but a very high specificity, accurate 98 percent of the time -meaning a false-negative rate of 24 percent, but a false-positive rate of only 2 per cent.
In the U.S., the current price for the SelectMDx test is $500 USD. PROSTATE BIOPSY A prostate biopsy is most commonly done by the transrectal method through the rectum.
Ultrasound is usually used to look at the prostate gland and guide the biopsy needle.
However, ultrasound doesn't allow visualization of prostate tumours, so a biopsy might miss the tumour.
Now, it's become possible to instead perform an MRI-guided biopsy.
MRI lets doctors see most prostate tumours and detection of prostate cancer has significantly increased -a 57 per cent improvement. GLEASON SCORES The Gleason score is the single most important prognostic indicator for prostate cancer and plays a significant role in treatment planning. This score is based on how aggressive the cancer looks under a microscope.
The pathologist looks at the cancer cells in the prostate and assigns a score on a scale of three to five from two different locations, first the area where the cancer is most obvious and then the next most obvious area of growth. The pathologist then gives each area a score from three to five with a more aggressive appearance receiving a higher score.
The scores are added together to come up with an overall score between 6 and 10.
For example, the most common grade of the cells in a tissue sample may be grade 3 cells, followed by grade 4 cells in less common areas.
The Gleason score for this sample would be Gleason 3+4=7. Cancers will score 6 or more. A score of 7 means the cancer is intermediate, and a higher score (8 to 10) means the cancer is more likely to grow and spread.
Someone with a Gleason score of 7 that comes from adding 3 + 4 has a less aggressive cancer than someone with a Gleason score of 7 that comes from adding 4 + 3 because the person with a 4 + 3 =7 grade has more grade 4 cells than grade 3 cells.
The lowest Gleason score is 6, which is a low-grade cancer.
A Gleason score of 7 is a medium-grade cancer, and a score of 8, 9, or 10 is a high-grade more aggressive cancer.
Doctors look at the Gleason score in addition to stage to help plan treatment.
For example, Active Surveillance or Watchful Waiting) may be an option for someone with a small, localized tumour, low PSA level, and a Gleason score of 6 or 7.
People with a higher Gleason score may need more aggressive treatment.
Gleason scoring by pathologists is a labour-intensive, error-prone subjective process suffering from observer variability, with reported Gleason score discordance ranging from 30 percent to 53 per cent.
Increased consistency and accuracy of Gleason grading lies in the field of Artificial Intelligence, where Computer-aided Gleason grading has been developed to assign Gleason grades in an automatic and reproducible manner.
AI assistance improves tumour detection, review time, and interpathologist agreement. CANCER STAGING The stage of the cancer is done by the T, N, and M classifications.
T is tumour status, the size of the cancer in the prostate, N is any spread to the regional lymph nodes and M is any distant metastatic spread.
Staging also includes the PSA level and Gleason Grade Group.
Stage I and Stage II: The cancer is found only in the prostate.
Stage III: The cancer has broken through the capsule of the prostate gland and is locally advanced outside the prostate gland.
Stage IV: The cancer has spread to lymph nodes and/or other parts of the body.
After the results of the biopsy are back, the next step is to sit down with a urologist who specializes in prostate cancer or an oncologist, or preferably, both, to discuss the course of action.
Decision making on treatment (or none, just Active Surveillance or Watchful Waiting) is subtly much more complicated than people realize. Some urologists will automatically push for surgery -"If you have a hammer, everything looks like a nail."
Since the advent of PSA screening, there has been a lot of overly aggressive treatment, with many negative prostate biopsies, surgeries, and radical prostatectomies resulting in high incidences of total incontinence (Depends!) and total impotence (penile implants!).
It is estimated that between 50 and 100 men undergo unnecessary treatment, surgery and/or radiation, for every life saved.
Consult a urologist specializing in prostate cancer or an oncologist, or better yet, both, before deciding on a course of treatment. WAIT AND WAT CH Thirty years after it began, a 2019 landmark study, published in the New England Journal of Medicine, that followed 690 Swedish men since they were diagnosed with localized prostate cancer, showed that the disease will not cause harm to the majority of men who have it, and that aggressive treatment is warranted only for men with a high risk of spread.
Half of the men had their prostates removed to get rid of the cancer, and half were put on "Active Surveillance." Of those who died, 77 per cent died of something other than prostate cancer.
According to the study, those men who had a Radical Prostatectomy at the start of the study lived an average of 2.9 years longer than men who got no therapy.
But 45 per cent of men treated with surgery regretted, often bitterly, their prostate cancer surgery afterwards.
Over-treatment is an issue because Radical Prostatectomy and similar therapies often cause permanent side effects, most commonly total impotence and urinary incontinence.
In Sweden today, 80 percent of men with newly diagnosed prostate cancer are not treated, but "Actively Surveilled," to make sure their tumour is not becoming more dangerous.
"Active Surveillance" includes regular checkups, whereas with "Watchful Waiting," follow-ups were deferred until a man had symptoms. The majority who are diagnosed today are diagnosed very early from PSA detection and have usually low-risk disease.
They will very likely be over-treated if they are treated immediately.
Gleason 6 disease almost never develops into aggressive cancer requiring treatment and patients with Gleason 6 cancer can avoid treatment such as Surgery and Radiation, and their side effects Most men with Gleason 7 cancers will not die from the cancer, but rather, with it.
Watchful Waiting -means follow-ups are deferred until a man has symptoms.
Active Surveillance -aims to avoid unnecessary treatment of harmless cancers while still providing timely treatment for men who need it.
Active surveillance formerly involved having regular PSA tests, MRI scans and sometimes biopsies to watch for any signs of progression.
Now PCA3 levels can be followed to tell us how aggressive the cancer is and whether it is progressing.
Men undergoing active surveillance will have delayed any treatment-related side effects, and those who eventually need treatment will be reassured that it was necessary.
I am 76 and if I were diagnosed with localized Gleason 7 prostate cancer, I would opt for Active Surveillance.
Active treatment of prostate cancer will be another topic. Dr. Peter Chow is a retired Sault Ste. Marie physician

Subject: Autopsies; Urine; Antigens; Life expectancy; Age; Medical screening; Black people; Prostate cancer; Biopsy

Document 152 of 219

Trudeau, Singh have led parties to 50-year-low poll numbers: study

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-singh-have-led-parties-50-year-low-poll/docview/3053720056/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%2C+Singh+have+led+parties+to+50-year-low+poll+numbers%3A+study&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-05-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new data analysis from the Angus Reid Institute has combed through 50 years of political polls and uncovered some historical trends and oddities. Most recently, it found that the popularity of Canadian federal party leaders is at a five-decade low. And that's not one of them - it's all of them. UNP OPULAR LEADERS Data gleaned from ODESI - the Ontario Data Documentation, Extraction Service and Infrastructure Initiative - found that, going back to 1974, there has never been a time when the leaders of the three main federal parties have polled as low as this year.
As of April 2024, the pollster determined, the most popular federal party leader, Pierre Poilievre of the opposition Conservatives, has a net rating of -12 points, calculated by combining favourability with unfavourabililty in polling numbers.
And he's the most popular. New Democratic Party Leader Jagmeet Singh has a net rating of -14, which is his worst in seven years as party leader. And Prime Minister Justin Trudeau of the governing Liberals has a net approval rating that is downright chilly at -38. TRUDEAU TRENDING D OWN The prime minister's numbers become even more dire when one looks at the trending direction. In data from this decade, Liberal and NDP leaders start out with net positive numbers of around 10 in 2020, while the Conservative leader (then Erin O'Toole) has a rating of -43.
However, by the following year, Trudeau had dropped to -9 while Singh was at just 6, and O'Toole had risen to -28.
The next three years saw the Conservative numbers increase in each poll (though never crossing into positive territory) while Trudeau and Singh continued to sink ever lower.
NEW DEMOCRAT S REACH ALL-TIME L OW Historically, the New Democrats have almost never strayed into negative territory, and remain relatively popular in opinion polls, even though they have never won a federal election.
"In 2011 Jack Layton (NDP), Michael Ignatieff (Liberal) and Stephen Harper (Conservative) were all in negative territory, but the intensity of dislike towards Layton was relatively slight," the Angus Reid Institute notes in its analysis. "In the late 1980s both John Turner (Liberal) and Brian Mulroney (Conservative) were heavily disapproved of, but (NDP) Ed Broadbent soared in public opinion polls."
More recently, however, numbers for the NDP leader (Singh since 2017) have been heading down and staying down. After positive figures in 2020 and 2021, the NDP fell to -1 the following year, and then an even zero. In the latest numbers, Singh has the distinction of holding the worst rating ever, at -14.
Why so low? "It appears that the NDP's supply and confidence agreement with the deeply unpopular Liberal government has increased Singh's profile and influence enough that he, too, is now garnering considerable criticism," the Institute suggests. CHRETIEN LEADS THE PACK At the other end of the popularity ladder is Liberal Jean Chretien, who in 1994, early in his 10 years as prime minister, scored a record-setting 42 on the scale.
In fact, throughout his time as prime minister, he consistently pulled in positive numbers, dipping as low as 14 and as high as 34. Liberal numbers wouldn't fall below zero again until 2003, the same year Paul Martin took over as prime minister and party leader.
TALE OF TWO TRUDEAUS In 1974, when data collection began, Liberal leader Pierre Trudeau had already been prime minister for six years, and his first number was a respectable 14, though still lower than the NDP's David Lewis (17) and the Conservatives'Robert Stanfield (18). But the bloom was clearly off the rose; in subsequent years his popularity would plummet.
In 1976 he scored a dismal -25 during a period of high inflation, before struggling back to numbers around zero through the rest of the decade, and then a drop in 1981 from which he never recovered. That year saw Trudeau post a -19, which sank to -34 and then -33. In 1985 the Liberal numbers rebounded to -3, but they were no longer Trudeau's Liberals, as John Turner had taken the helm.
A similar fate seems to be befalling Trudeau's son, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. In 2014, the first year after he took the party reins, he scored a rating of 7. Two years later, as prime minister, his score was 29, and it stayed positive for each of the next two years.
Since 2021 it has been mired in negative numbers. And just as Singh has brought the New Democrats to its lowest rating since 1974, Trudeau's current -38 is a low-water mark over the same stretch of time.
In its analysis, the Angus Reid Institute notes: "Prime Minister Trudeau scores his lowest rating in his run as Liberal leader in April, with just 28 per cent approval. What these historic data show is usually the decline of one federal leader engendering the rise in favourability for an alternative; see the elder Trudeau's fall and the corresponding rise of Brian Mulroney. Our fractured and divisive politics, however, appear to have created an era where all leaders can remain under water at the same time."

Subject: Conservatism; Popularity; Prime ministers

People: Mulroney, Brian; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Trudeau, Singh have led parties to 50-year-low poll numbers: study

Document 153 of 219

Separation of law enforcement, politics ensures 'fair and thoughtful'nonbiased approach to justice

Author: Baker, Patricia

Publication info: Baker, Patricia. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/separation-law-enforcement-politics-ensures-fair/docview/3051788249/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Separation+of+law+enforcement%2C+politics+ensures+%27fair+and+thoughtful%27nonbiased+approach+to+justice&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-05-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baker%2C+Patricia&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The trial of Umar Zameer, the man accused of first-degree murder in the death of Toronto police officer Jeffrey Northrup, on July 2, 2021, is over. Zameer's acquittal, a relief to both he and his family, has brought forward many more questions about the charges brought against him as well as the prosecution's path towards achieving a conviction of this man.
He is now free, after he and his family lived or should we say existed, within a threeyear nightmare of persecution and incarceration. The world around him including the prosecution, had intentions to prove in a court of law that he had deliberately run down this police officer.
It all started as Canada Day celebrations at the downtown square in Toronto July 1, 2021, were winding down. The Zameer family had gone to their vehicle in the parking garage and after settling in, he, his pregnant wife and young son were approached by two plain clothes police officers.
Although these officers testified in a court of law that they had repeatedly identified themselves as police officers, Zameer and his wife adamantly denied this. They said the pair began yelling and thumping their vehicle, so the family believed they were being threatened and attacked by gang members.
Their reaction was to try and escape in fear of their safety, so Zameer drove the family vehicle toward the exit to flee to safety but was stopped by an unmarked police van. After reversing his vehicle, an apparent contact with Northrup took place, sending him to the ground. When Zameer accelerated, the officer was run over.
Zameer said he thought he had gone over a speed bump and didn't know he had hit Northrup until he was stopped at the gate and arrested. His wife had tried to explain what had happened in statements to police, but was shocked when a short time later, then police chief James Ramer, told reporters that Northrup's death was deliberate.
Apparently, there had been a stabbing in the area earlier, which the police were investigating. The perpetrator had been described as a man with brown skin; Zameer fit into this description. But even after his wife explained what had happened to the attending officers, their attempts to escape from fear of being attacked were misinterpreted as guilt.
Racial profiling seemed to have played a major role in the development of what came to be a tragedy for Zameer rather than a crime.
When news of Zameer's release on bail several months after his arrest was publicized, Ontario Premier Doug Ford called this completely unacceptable. Then Toronto Mayer John Tory sympathized with Ford's exclamation.
The presumption of innocence literally went out the window for Zameer, as Ontario's top politicians sided with Toronto police, who by then may have been under scrutiny themselves in this case.
The police witnesses had testified under oath at Zameer's trial, that Northrup was standing in front of Zameer's vehicle when he was hit. This evidence was contested by a police force reconstructionist, which then led Zameer's defence lawyer to allude to police collusion.
Further to the deconstructionist's testimony was that Northrup had been side swiped in a blind spot when Zameer drove his vehicle to the exit gate of the parking garage. He was not in front where he could have been seen by Zameer.
In the end, the OPP have been asked to investigate if grounds exist for the possibility of perjury on the part the police officers involved in Zameer's arrest and subsequent charge of first-degree murder.
This is yet to be proven in a court of law.
In the end Zameer was acquitted, but a dark cloud continues to hover over this horrific tragedy and ultimate miscarriage of justice in his arrest, and charge of first-degree murder. This three-year nightmare ruined his reputation, his life and his place in this country as a valued human being who only wanted to live in peace and work hard.
When he was granted bail while awaiting trial, red flags were already in the wind about whether the prosecution's assertion that Zameer had deliberately and intentionally run down this police officer, ultimately killing him.
It has been reported that judges were waving red flags because they believed that the prosecution's case was based on their argument that Zameer deliberately ran down the police officer "runs contrary to logic and common sense," Justice Jill Copeland wrote when she released him on bail.
His release on bail while awaiting trial caused a firestorm of accusations and disbelief that an individual charged with first degree murder of a police offer could be granted bail.
He was labelled a "cop killer," was fired from his accounting job, had to sell off his home and miss the birth of his daughter while bills continued to pile up. The family lost friends as well as their credibility as productive citizens of Canada.
All this, as an innocent man, charged but not yet convicted in a court of law for first degree murder. Where was the presumption of innocence in all of this, are we not innocent until proven guilty? Do politicians feel they can contest the rulings of an independent justice system just to gain political points? Our justice system is completely independent from the provincial, territorial and federal governments.
This case is a perfect example of why it needs to stay that way. Keeping law enforcement and politics independent of each other ensures a fair and thoughtful nonbiased approach to justice.
That is true democracy. This case sends the impression that we are not, as it may depend on who you are, what you look like and whose life in the chain of command and societal importance you have impacted.
To make matters worse, the derogatory statements made by three high level politicians were far too premature to be valid assertions of the accused's constitutional right to a fair trial.
Other statements, which effectively nailed Zameer to the proverbial wall were said in haste long before much of the factual information required to come to a sensible and fair conclusion had been released to the public.
Instead, this man endured a pre "trial" by fire, tarred and feathered by friends, strangers, lawyers and politicians.
This has been a nightmare, no question. But what led up to this was a tragedy, with the Zameer family being in the wrong place at the wrong time, having led to a nightmare in the making.
Zameer and his pregnant wife were new immigrants to Canada and on that fateful day life changed in an instant with a Toronto police officer dead and Zameer charged with first degree murder.
But the case is not completely closed, with the arresting officers and witnesses'testimony apparently differing from what the reconstruction team established as to where Northrup was standing when impact occurred.
These witnesses are innocent until proven otherwise. But as they begin living with this information in the public realm, their reputations perhaps in question, sleepless nights spent worrying about what is yet to come, every one of us should stop and gain some valuable insight into what it must feel like to be under investigation for possible wrongdoing but knowing full well you are innocent.
Think about it before you condemn someone to hell before a trial, it could be any one of us trying to prove innocence when everyone around you wants your head on a platter.
Umar Zameer would not have been the first Canadian to go to the prison of the wrongly convicted. Too many have been incarcerated for years, for crimes they did not commit.
Thanks is not enough for this legal team who left no stone unturned to get to the truth, the reconstruction team who put forward evidence that contradicted police witnesses'testimony, judge and jury who listened to the evidence from both sides and came to the only conclusion based on evidence ... acquittal.
We must not forget the other casualty; a valued and apparently well-respected police officer who lost his life. Whether the investigation into collusion comes to the conclusion that it played a part in all of this or it didn't, this man's death could be remembered for all the wrong reasons.
Patricia Baker is a Sault Star district correspondent, columnist and retired Sault Area Hospital nurse

Document 154 of 219

Trudeau's bullying helped crush his popularity

Author: Staples, David

Publication info: Staples, David. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeaus-bullying-helped-crush-his-popularity/docview/3051787795/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%27s+bullying+helped+crush+his+popularity&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-05-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Staples%2C+David&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's reputation is in tatters. David Coletto, chief executive of Abacus polling, reports the Conservatives have more support, 44 per cent, than the coalition of the Liberals at 23 per cent and Jagmeet Singh's NDP at 17 per cent, combined, and the polls are unlikely to change with Trudeau as leader.
"What I've learned from 20 years of public opinion research into Canadian politics: You cannot persuade people or lead them when they don't trust you and think your time has passed," Coletto said on social media. "Any fight the Liberal government has is bound to fail as long as this is how people feel about it and its leader ... Reputation is everything."
Coletto's point got me thinking not only about Trudeau, but about recent allegations against Alberta Premier Danielle Smith for uncaring and authoritarian policies.
What is the issue that has sunk Trudeau's reputation? His failed economic policy tops the list, but second is his propensity for bullying and authoritarian governing. We've seen it in the way he demonizes opponents, hammers outcasts from his own party, and scoffs at our constitution and courts.
Of course, Trudeau's authoritarian streak was there from the beginning. Remember Trudeau's 2014 dictate that no longer would anti-abortion candidates be tolerated in Liberal ranks, not to mention Trudeau's now infamous pro-Chinese statement from 2013. Asked which country he most admired, Trudeau said, "There's a level of admiration I actually have for China, because their basic dictatorship is allowing them to actually turn their economy around on a dime and say we need to go green, we need to start, you know, investing in solar."
An authoritarian line runs to Trudeau's unrelenting agenda to tax and thwart Alberta's oil and gas economy. Even our federal courts have become alarmed, ruling against Trudeau's plastics ban and industrial assessment policy.
Far from being humbled by the court decisions, Trudeau rushes from issue to issue, from a new, discriminatory cap on Alberta's oil and gas emissions to his federal housing policy, stomping on provincial rights as if they were a mosquito on his arm rather than Canada's foundational agreements. He was last heard threatening on the housing file: "I'd always rather work with provinces, but if we have to, I will go around them and be there for Canadians."
My own wake-up call on Trudeau's character came in 2017 when his government brought in a rule that no group could get government funding for student summer job grants unless it signed an attestation confirming it respected abortion rights. I'm pro-choice, but I respect the rights of conscience of others. Trudeau evidently did not, then went down the same path forcing discriminatory race and sex quotas into government business contracts and research grant agreements.
We might also remember Trudeau's ugly blowups with former Liberal Party insiders like Jody Wilson-Raybould, who complained she finally quit his cabinet because: "I just wanted it to end. The condescension. The belittlement. The lack of seriousness. The lack of commitment to the truth."
Finally, there's Trudeau's demonizing of opponents as racists or misogynists over basic policy or personal disagreements.
Trudeau's most unforgivable authoritarian act was his support of banks freezing the personal accounts of anti-lockdown protesters.
As for Smith, she's done well on several hot-button issues to avoid acting the bully. On two tricky files, drug addiction and the medical gender transition of children, she's brought in far less permissive policies than the progressive left pushes, but she's kept public support by focusing her rhetoric on what's best for homeless drug addicts and for vulnerable gender-dysphoric children facing monumental adult decisions.
Last week, Smith crossed a line, coming down too aggressively on the authority of elected municipal councillors, then cutting funding for our poorest transit users. At once, an outcry exploded over her being uncaring and a bully.
On the transit issue, Smith reversed herself at once. It now looks like her government is open to walking back some disagreeable measures in its municipal governance reform.
We are all, quite rightly, on high alert for bullies, for the authoritarian streak of powerful people. Only arrogant politicians destined to go down in infamy fail to tone down their own bullying tendencies. dstaples@postmedia.com

Subject: Authoritarianism; Bullying; Court decisions; Abortion; Reputations

Document 155 of 219

Trudeau turns labour vote right

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-turns-labour-vote-right/docview/3050016381/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+turns+labour+vote+right&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-05-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Workers of the world, unite! This is your moment. Across the globe, parties are courting the labour vote as life gets more unaffordable, technology takes over more jobs and everything seems more uncertain. Traditionally enamoured with the left, unionized workers are turning to the right as progressive parties dive down policy rabbit holes that have little to do with improving their members'working conditions, but much to do with identity politics and social engineering.
Here in Canada, it is no different. Federal and provincial conservative parties are gaining support among blue-collar voters. In the most recent Ontario election, several private sector unions, including the Laborers'International Union of North America (LIUNA), endorsed Doug Ford's Conservatives, a first for the party. Federally, Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are gaining traction in traditional NDP territory, from British Columbia to Ontario, as workers flip their votes from orange to blue.
But it's not just the NDP that is feeling the pain. The federal Liberals are losing support too, due to growing working-class opposition to the carbon tax. In what could be the biggest policy reversal of the decade, even the NDP says the tax is too expensive. Why? Because polls show the worse off a voter is, the more likely they are to vote Conservative. Even 36 per cent of public sector union workers would back the Tories, compared with 26 per cent each for the Liberals and NDP.
So the counter-campaign has started. Two weeks ago, the head of the Canadian Labour Congress, Bea Bruske, warned Poilievre is "a fraud" and not to be trusted due to his voting records on workers' rights.
Then last week, a second salvo: the "milestone" announcement of a new Honda electric vehicle battery plant in Alliston, set to create "over 1,000 well-paying manufacturing jobs" and boost the Canadian auto industry. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was all smiles, and no wonder: the plant is right in the Liberals'sweet spot, producing green energy for cars and good jobs for unionized workers.
Except there's a fly in the ointment: foreign workers. And it's turning a good news announcement into yet another boondoggle for the Liberals.
On April 10, Sean Strickland, executive director of Canada's Building Trades Unions, wrote Trudeau a letter over concerns about foreign workers taking Canadian jobs at another EV project, the NextStar plant in Windsor, a venture between Chrysler's Stellantis and Korean firm LG. Strickland said foreign workers are building the plant while Canadian workers, including 180 local millwrights and ironworkers, "are being sidelined without consequence." In response, the government says only 70 of the 2,000 workers currently at the site are not Canadian.
But the union says 900 South Korean workers are set to come to Windsor during the installation phase of the battery plant's development. Asked by Strickland about the new Honda deal on Monday, Trudeau said "it's part and parcel of it that we expect that the construction, the installation, the maintenance be done by Canadians as much as is humanly possible."
That answer didn't satisfy either the NDP or the Conservatives. The same day, the Tories tabled a motion in the House of Commons to get the details of the Honda deal and see just how many Canadian jobs have been guaranteed. The Liberals have said they cannot release the contracts because they contain sensitive business information that could jeopardize future EV plant deals.
But with $5 billion of taxpayers'money on the line, surely the government could demand that Honda hire local workers? Alas, you would be mistaken. Canada's history is littered with similar auto deals gone bad, where workers ended up sidelined after public funds piled in. In 2018, General Motors shuttered its Oshawa plant and put 2,500 workers out of work, despite promising in 2016 to keep things running until 2020. At the time, GM still owed the government an undisclosed amount of its $10.5-billion taxpayer bailout received in 2009.
The Liberals should be careful not to oversell the Honda deal and be fully transparent on the terms. Unless Trudeau can satisfy concerns that the plant isn't just an election-friendly photo op, and will actually employ Canadians, workers may well be asking: who's the fraud now? National Post

Location: Canada

Document 156 of 219

UCP bends Alberta authorities to its will

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ucp-bends-alberta-authorities-will/docview/3049129516/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=UCP+bends+Alberta+authorities+to+its+will&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New rules for Alberta towns and cities make it clear who's the boss. It's the United Conservative Party government, not local councils or the voters who elect them.
Under proposed laws unveiled Thursday, the province will be able to step into a local council and fire individual members. he province can also can overturn a local bylaw on any subject, widely expanding its existing power to block only land-use laws.
In another echo of post-pandemic grudges, the UCP appears to take control over any municipal orders on public health measures.
These are the latest moves in a widespread consolidation of provincial power.
Step by step, local authorities are being nudged into line as the UCP lays down the building blocks of sovereignty.
Premier Danielle Smith has stepped into university research funding, adding a requirement for more conservative ideology.
Annoyed by Ottawa striking deals with towns and cities, a UCP bill would make any agreement invalid if the province isn't directly involved.
That seems to be aimed mainly at Calgary and Edmonton, which constantly annoy the UCP with bilateral federal deals.
But the bill reaches into the smallest municipality, education boards, universities, health authorities, and Crown and public agencies.
No public authority in Alberta dares to deal with the federal government unless provincial watchers are on hand to stamp approval.
That's a heavy load of bureaucracy from a government that loves to boast about its department of red tape reduction.
Thursday's announcements relate to local elections, councils and political funding, all based on the premise that the province is supreme.
That's true in law, of course; but the province usually has been a benevolent parent, tolerant of local diversity and even a certain amount of opposition.
That era is fading fast, despite Municipal Affairs Minister Ric McIver's promise that the province will rule with a gentle hand.
The most striking measure - drawing up rules for civic political parties - is driven by UCP dreams of sweeping bigcity progressives from power.
That will only happen in Edmonton and Calgary. McIver says smaller centres are free to allow parties if they like, but there's little interest.
Another reason, I'd suggest, is that most councils in rural Alberta are already comfortably loaded with UCP supporters.
But Conservative governments, from Progressive Conservative to United Conservative, always have failed to wedge their own loyalists into the mayors'offices in Calgary and Edmonton. This they find deeply irritating.
The ultimate slap was Edmonton's election of Amarjeet Sohi, a former Trudeau Liberal cabinet minister.
In Calgary, they're stuck with progressive Jyoti Gondek, who in 2021 handily defeated conservative challenger Jeromy Farkas.
In 2001, Dave Bronconnier became mayor after running federally for the Liberals in 1997 and getting soundly thumped.
Next came Naheed Nenshi.
In 2010, he defeated conservative McIver, who moved on to provincial politics and introduced the measures Thursday.
In 2017, conservative backers put up local businessman Bill Smith, a former president of the provincial PC party, who finished 30,000 votes behind Nenshi.
It's a sorry history. But now, big-city parties might work for the UCP.
Gondek and the current council majority brought in measures, including blanket zoning and the bag bylaw, that were never mentioned in the 2021 campaign.
Parties would make the stakes much clearer, the theory goes, by releasing platforms saying what they would and would not do with a majority. And that would draw out conservative-minded voters who previously had no idea for what their ward candidates stood.
But there's no missing what goes on here. The UCP is massaging the whole public sector into fit shape for loyal service. Don Braid's column appears regularly with Postmedia. X: @DonBraid

Subject: Local elections; Councils; Voters

Location: Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

Identifier / keyword: rules; alberta; towns; cities; clear; united

Universities warn student activists encampments will not be tolerated; Warnings comes after protests set up camp at McGill University in Montreal

Author: Baig, Fakiha

Publication info: Baig, Fakiha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/universities-warn-student-activists-encampments/docview/3049128331/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Universities+warn+student+activists+encampments+will+not+be+tolerated%3B+Warnings+comes+after+protests+set+up+camp+at+McGill+University+in+Montreal&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Baig%2C+Fakiha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two Ontario universities are warning anyone planning to follow the lead of pro-Palestinian student activists in the U.S. and elsewhere in Canada that setting up encampments on campus will not be tolerated.
The University of Toronto and the University of Ottawa say they understand tensions are high, many people are feeling anxious due to the scale of suffering in the Middle East and peaceful protests are central to campus and democratic life. But, the universities say they have clear policies that state the use of their facilities without proper authorization can carry serious consequences and protests need to be organized within the limits of university policies and the law.
"U of T's lands and buildings are private property, though the university allows wide public access to them for authorized activities," Sandy Welsh, vice-provost of students, wrote in an email sent out to the student community on Sunday.
"Unauthorized activities such as encampments or the occupation of university buildings are considered trespassing. Specifically, our Code of Student Conduct prohibits intentional damage to university property, unauthorized entry and use of university property contrary to instructions, disruptions of university activities, and other offences to property and persons."
Eric Bercier, the associate vice-president of student affairs at the University of Ottawa, said the university had heard of a protest planned for Monday and warned that the setup of any encampment would carry serious consequences.
"While peaceful protest is permitted in appropriate public spaces on campus according to our policies and regulations, encampments and occupations will not be tolerated," Bercier wrote in a statement posted on the university's website.
"We ask everyone to thoughtfully consider their responsibilities and the wellbeing of our entire community. We continue to call on all members of our community to treat each other with respect and empathy as they interact in our classrooms, common spaces, and online."
The warnings from the University of Ottawa and the University of Toronto come after an encampment was set up by pro-Palestinian student activists on the grounds of McGill University in Montreal, where student activists from Concordia University are also participating.
Student activists at McGill have said they are demanding the school divest from Israeli companies that are "complicit in the occupation of Palestine." They also expect the school to cut academic ties with Israeli institutions. McGill has said the camp violates both school policies and the law.
Campuses in the United States have seen a wave of protests in recent weeks linked to the Israel-Hamas war.
On Monday, Columbia University escalated its stance against protesters on its New York campus by sending an ultimatum for students to sign a form and leave the encampment by the afternoon or face suspension.
Early protests at Columbia, where demonstrators set up tents in the centre of the campus, sparked pro-Palestinian demonstrations across the U.S. Students and others have been sparring over the Israel-Hamas war and its mounting death toll. Many students are demanding their universities cut financial ties with Israel.
When asked Monday about the planned sit-in at the University of Ottawa, Premier Doug Ford said at an unrelated news conference that Ontario is known for its inclusivity.
"You know what I wish? I wish everyone gets along," Ford said. "Sure, there's conflicts around the world. They want to voice their opinion. And that's their democratic right. Do it peacefully. That's all I ask." The Canadian Press, with files from The Associated Press

Document 158 of 219

Ferrochrome smelter not what doctor orders to attract MDs to Sault

Author: Chow, Peter

Publication info: Chow, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ferrochrome-smelter-not-what-doctor-orders/docview/3047480970/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ferrochrome+smelter+not+what+doctor+orders+to+attract+MDs+to+Sault&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Chow%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With no exaggeration Sault Ste. Marie is Naturally Gifted, one of the most beautiful places on Earth. Nestled at the hub of Lakes Superior, Michigan and Huron, in the middle of the largest store of freshwater in the world - the Great Lakes - it boasts some of the most picturesque and diverse landscapes one could imagine.
For years, Sault Ste. Marie offered numerous well-paying industrial jobs at Algoma Steel, the tube mill and St. Marys Paper, spawning a large and prosperous middle class.
The city in the 1960s had Canada's second highest per capita income year after year, trailing only Oakville, Ont., with its Ford automotive plant.
With its strategic location at the hub of the three largest Great Lakes, the future for Sault Ste Marie seemed limitless.
That Golden Age has faded. The steel plant faced greater global competition and labour-saving technological change. The workforce dropped from over 9,000 in the 1970s to around 2,700 today. As the Internet devastated the demand for paper, St. Marys Paper died.
Sault Ste Marie could have/should have become another Traverse City, Mich., on the St. Mary's.
Obviously, that didn't happen, largely because of self-inflicted wounds by our city leaders.
In 2018, when Noront, a relatively unknown mining company sought to develop recently discovered mineral deposits in the Ring of Fire in the vast wetlands of James Bay, and went looking for a potential site to process (smelt) ferrochrome, Sault City Council went into overdrive.
According to Sault MPP Ross Romano, it was he who first reached out to Noront when he was a city alderman.
Romano's 2017 byelection victory for the Ontario PC Party after David Orazietti resigned meant that Noront had a vocal advocate in Queen's Park even before Ford Nation filled it with a healthy majority government.
When Doug Ford became the premier in 2018, Noront had support in all the right places.
Ford vowed "to hop on that bulldozer himself" if that's what it would take to break ground on new Northern roads.
Noront dangled promises to the city with the proposed ferrochrome production plant -millions in economic development and hundreds of "jobs with up to $100.000 in pay."
Romano said an estimated 500 workers would be needed to build the facility, then 400 permanent jobs at the plant once operational, with another 1,200 spinoff jobs as a result.
City council unanimously passed a motion to be the potential smelter site.
A charm offensive was swiftly started, and it was hard to tell the difference between the city and Noront, both engaging in overlapping PR campaigns to recruit local cheerleaders and assuage any naysayers. Of course, it's easy to appreciate the desperation and eagerness.
In Sault Ste. Marie, as in countless other cities in Canada, established industries are waning, and economic diversification is a perpetual challenge. Youth go away to university and never return, lured by opportunities elsewhere. Skilled workers from outside the city are difficult to attract.
The population dropped from 84,000 in the 1980s to 66,000 in 2016.
The municipal tax base is stretched thin to provide spending and service levels that evolved when there was a lucrative industrial tax base; property taxes have been rising for years.
Social problems like addictions, mental health crises, ridiculous levels of minor crime, homelessness, poverty and "deaths of despair" -drug overdoses, suicides and alcohol-related deaths -are becoming both more prevalent and more difficult to address.
The city's submission reflected the incredible deference paid to industry due to economic desperation, a misplaced confidence in environmental regulations and enforcement, and most importantly, a seeming disregard for the health and safety of our community and the environment.
According to ferrochrome's most ardent supporters, Ontario has robust enough environmental regulations to keep the environment (and us) safe.
But even a cursory look at how industrial polluters are held accountable - including Algoma Steel - reveals that the regulatory regime is deeply flawed.
Eagerness often comes at the cost of careful consideration.
A delegation of Sault Ste.
Marie's most important representatives went to Toronto and then mayor Christian Provenzano hand-delivered the submission with pride.
Noront selected Sault Ste. Marie in May 2019 is its preferred location for a high-tech electric arc furnace smelting operation and entered into a 99-year lease with Algoma Steel to use a brownfield site (with a history of pollution) west of the steel plant to build a new ferrochrome smelter facility - the first of its kind in North America.
A deciding factor was access to a Great Lakes port to cheaply float in raw materials like coal and fluxes for the smelter, and ship outbound ferrochrome product to stainless steel producers in the U.S.
Ore would be trucked down a planned 300-kilometre road to Nakina in northwestern Ontario and then to a ferrochrome plant in the Sault.
In the minds of those in that delegation, this was a tremendous victory.
But for many local residents, including almost all our most respected health professionals, it was the first step to potentially rendering the city uninhabitable due to the environmental and health risks.
An open letter to the media expressing these concerns was signed by 52 local physicians. Batchewana First Nation and the Sault Tribe of Chippewa Indians both declared their opposition to the project.
Sault Ste Marie desperately wants the jobs that the proposed ferrochrome production facility would bring. But hexavalent chromium (Cr-6) is the inevitable by-product of chromium manufacturing. Along with other toxic heavy metals, including nickel, lead and vanadium.
The proposed ferrochrome production plant will produce thousands of tonnes of toxic waste contaminated with Cr-6 which is a known genotoxic carcinogen. Inhalation of ferrochrome furnace dust increases the risk of squamous cell cancer of the lung and ingestion of contaminated water is associated with cancers of the oral cavity and gastrointestinal tract. As well there are a myriad of other serious health problems related to Cr-6 exposure.
"To all those people who have concerns .... I would say 'Please, do not let yourself be governed by fears of what we do not know.'" -Ross Romano Cr-6 gained notoriety through the movie Erin Brockovich starring Julia Roberts, the true story of a law clerk who led a lawsuit against the Pacific Gas and Electric Company (PG&E) over the devastating health effects of Cr-6 on the town of Hinkley, Calif.
From 1952 to 1966, PG&E dumped about 370 million gallons (1,400 million litres) of Cr-6-tainted wastewater into unlined ponds around the town of Hinkley.
In 1996 PG&E settled for $333 million, the largest settlement for a direct-action lawsuit in U.S. history. After settling the last claims in 2008, PG&E began buying up homes in Hinkley in 2010.
Many residents took the offer and left town and their homes were promptly bulldozed by PG&E, the population dwindling to the point that the last elementary school in the town closed in 2013 By 2016, Hinkley became a toxic ghost town, its groundwater and soil contaminated by Cr-6 to this day. PG&E estimates it will take 50 years for the contamination to be cleaned up.
Noront Resources initially selected Sudbury as well as the Sault as final candidates for the site of the proposed smelter. Sudbury sent a delegation including the mayor and the chief of Wahnapitae First Nation to Tornio, Finland, site of the Outukumpu ferrochrome production plant, whose technology is what Noront is proposing. The mayor of Sudbury initially enthusiastically backed the plant, but a large group of activists organized protests against it.
Sudbury essentially said no to the ferrochrome plant. Sixty years ago, Sudbury wasn't much bigger than the Sault.
We laughed because its barren industry-scarred landscape resembled the moon so much that NASA sent astronauts to train there. Since then, Sudbury has consistently made smarter decisions than the Sault.
Its economy diversified from just mining and smelting and it, not the Sault, became the regional centre in financial and business services, tourism, health care and research, education and government for Northeastern Ontario. Its population has grown to almost three times ours.
It has a Costco, not just Dollar stores. The moonscape is even green again.
At the invitation of concerned citizens, Tom Price, a retired engineer who had set up the world's first chrome recycling plant in Pennsylvania, hosted an educational presentation in Sudbury in March 2018 on the Tornio plant in Finland (when Sudbury was still in the running). The first thing Price noticed was that no one in Tornio lived within 2.5 kilometres of the chrome plant.
The proposed site for the Sault is west of the steel plant well inside city limits.
In a statement dripping with civic concern, Noront stated it wants the smelter inside the city as that "will reduce overall smelter costs by one-third." The vast majority of Saultites live directly downwind and down river (east) of the proposed smelter site.
Despite the latest technologies in the Tornio plant, all the land around it was contaminated with Cr-6 (along with nickel, lead and vanadium, all toxic heavy metals) by air emissions more than 3 kilometres downwind.
Tornio is by the sea, so it has a lot of fog and a lot of rain, a lot more than here. Particulate matter (Cr-6 dust) gets picked up by fog and rain and drops down rapidly, within a couple of kilometres.
Here in the Sault, the smelter plant will have four large smokestacks, so the fine particles of Cr-6 will be dry and will travel a lot farther and contaminate a much larger area. In Tornio the smelter is on an ocean-side peninsula. Each year a quarter tonne of effluent waste from the smelter's arc furnace, which contains Cr-6, is washed away and dissipates in the ocean.
The Tornio plant draws 30 million cubic metres of water per year from nearby rivers as a once-through cooling medium and then dumps it into the sea. Noront is planning on using the St. Mary's River the same way.
The waters of the Gulf of Bothnia around Tornio are among the most polluted in the world with a witch's brew of toxic chemicals and heavy metals. The Baltic Sea has the world's largest ocean dead zone, devoid of marine life and fish. In the same way, the Noront plant could affect the waters of the entire Great Lakes basin, home to 35 million people. PFASs, particularly PFOS, are used as mist suppressants that are used in chromium smelting to prevent air emissions of toxic metal fumes (Cr-6).
Locating a chrome smelter here will guarantee PFAS in the entire Great Lakes basin. PFAS in the tap water was the reason the campground at Pointe des Chênes was shut down.
Once fully operational, Price estimates 110 tonnes of emission per year will be released into the air. These airborne emissions will contain Cr-6 as well as nickel, lead and vanadium; 20,000 tonnes per year of contaminated waste will end up in tailings ponds.
Inevitably these will leak and flood and Cr-6 will end up in the groundwater, just a matter of how soon and how much.
Sault, Michigan is still dealing with toxic waste containing Cr-6. In that city, the source of Cr-6 was a leather tannery that processed animal hides from the 1800's until 1958.
The site was found by the EPA (the Environmental Protection Agency) in 1988 to be toxic enough to qualify for the Superfund (the U.S. government program to clean up of the most toxic waste sites in the country).
Cr-6 still persists in the soil and the drinking water.
According to an interactive map of Cr-6 "hotspots" across the U.S., the tap water in Soo Michigan tests for levels of Cr-6 that are over 10 times the California public health goal set after the "Erin Brockovich" affair. Compared to other cities in Ontario, Sault Ste. Marie already has increased rates of cancers of the lung, bronchus, bladder, kidney, renal pelvis, and esophagus. A 2019 study showed that the rate of Acute Myeloid Leukemia was 50 per cent higher in Sault Ste. Marie than in the rest of Canada.
The rate was more than double the Canadian average for those living in the P6C area code around Algoma Steel. Cr-6 is a known carcinogen, produced even in the cleanest ferrochrome smelting production.
Research demonstrates that exposure increases the risk of multiple cancers: liver, kidney and female genitourinary, lung, lip, oral cavity, pharynx, stomach, female breast, female colon, prostate, and leukemia.
Industry in Ontario has a history of breaking environmental pollution regulations, getting slapped on the wrist with trivial fines, and then continuing to pollute ...
And then receiving financial stimulus packages from government that make a total mockery of the environmental fines. Industries have also been able to circumvent environmental regulations with exemptions from these regulations (Algoma has exemptions on benzene and particulate matter).
From 2010 to 2017, 32 companies across Canada that violated environmental rules were fined $15 million, but the penalties were not just offset but dwarfed by government largesse of more than $2.6 billion.
Essar Steel Algoma 2010-2017 -$78,362,926 funding, $159,750 penalties -and that was before it was announced that Algoma Steel Inc. is getting $420 million in federal funding to support its transition to electric-arc furnaces In effect, taxpayers paid the firms'fines -if and when they were even imposed.
In 2022, Algoma discharged more than five tons of zinc, 13 tons of ammonia, which is toxic to aquatic life; 211 pounds of lead, which is considered unsafe at any level in drinking water; 1.5 tons of manganese, which damages the nervous system at high level; and 109 tons of nitrate, which has been linked to cancer, reproductive problems and birth defects when found in drinking water.
Over the past decade, the company has poured into the St. Mary's River more than 430 tons of ammonia, nearly 3 tons of benzene, 4,600 pounds of lead, 18 tons of manganese, 2,367 tons of nitrates and 55 tons of zinc.
This is on top of the air pollution Algoma Steel spews from its stacks every year -in 2022, more than 6 tons of benzene (a carcinogen), more than 1,000 tons of nitrogen dioxide and more than 170 tons of particulate matter, along with other harmful air contaminants.
The three biggest industrial emitters of CO2 in Ontario are all steel plants, accounting for more than 40 per cent of all industrial greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in the province, more than the oil refinery, forestry, mining and chemical sectors combined.
We have bans on second-hand cigarette smoke, yet Algoma has exemptions to spew, nonstop, benzene and particulate matter, both classified as carcinogenic by the International Agency for Research on Cancer.
On June 9, 2022 an oil spill from Algoma Steel into the St. Mary's River occurred, 20,000 litres, halting commercial traffic and causing the Sault Chippewa Tribe to advise its tribal members with water intakes on Sugar Island to use alternative sources of water.
Echo Bay's water treatment plant was shut down for 18 days as a result of the spill, requiring drinking water to be trucked in. No fine has ever been imposed.
Premier Doug Ford has made mining the Ring of Fire a key priority in building an end-to-end electric vehicle manufacturing chain in Ontario.
Ontario is coming off two big - and expensive - wins in reaching that goal by landing deals with Volkswagen and Stellantis for electric vehicle battery plants in southern Ontario. Ford envisions the metals supplying those plants coming from Northern Ontario and heading south for refining and processing.
"Northern Ontario holds the key to economic growth across the province, a generational opportunity to transform our economy."
Over the past 60 years, Sudbury has consistently made the right decisions looking beyond the short-term. Sault Ste Marie should learn.
As Greta Thunberg would say, "Try counting your money holding your breath!!" As we desperately try to recruit new physicians to our city, the future with a ferrochrome smelter spewing chromium-6 isn't exactly a strong selling point. Short-term profit for long-term loss -something for Sault Ste Marie to ponder. Dr. Peter Chow is a retired Sault Ste. Marie physician

Subject: Drug overdose; Mayors; Environmental regulations; Heavy metals; Metallurgy

Location: United States--US; Canada; Finland

Company / organization: Name: Pacific Gas & Electric Co; NAICS: 221122

Document 159 of 219

Poll shows budget a bust for Trudeau Liberals

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poll-shows-budget-bust-trudeau-liberals/docview/3046279767/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poll+shows+budget+a+bust+for+Trudeau+Liberals&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If the federal government's latest budget was an attempt to boost Liberal fortunes, it was a bust.
A big, expensive, divisive bust that will ruin our economy for years to come while getting Prime Minister Justin Trudeau none of the support for which he had hoped.
The latest poll from Ipsos found most people didn't like the budget or didn't care and Trudeau isn't getting a boost in support.
Just 10 per cent of respondents who knew anything about the budget said it would help them personally, compared to 37 per cent who felt the budget would hurt them and 53 per cent who were neutral. Asked if the budget would make them more or less likely to vote Liberal, eight per cent said more likely, 34 per cent said less likely and 58 per cent said there was no change.
Ipsos asked Canadians to rate the budget with two thumbs up, two thumbs down or a shrug of the shoulders and the results aren't good for the Trudeau Liberals. Just 17 per cent gave the budget two thumbs up, 40 per cent gave it two thumbs down and 43 per cent shrugged their shoulders.
Even among younger voters to whom this budget was meant to appeal, Gen Z and Millennials, the reaction was far more negative than positive.
This shows up in party support numbers as well - Ipsos is now showing a 19-point lead for Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives over Trudeau's Liberals. The Conservatives would take 43 per cent of the vote nationally if an election were held today, the Liberals 24 per cent and the NDP 19 per cent.
Trudeau has been courting Gen Z and Millennials, his budget mentioning each of them multiple times, but it isn't working.
Among Gen Z, 38 per cent would vote Conservative, 28 per cent would vote for the NDP and the Liberals are in third at 27 per cent.
Millennial voters also are not impressed with Trudeau with 40 per cent saying they would vote Conservative, 24 per cent Liberal and 23 per cent NDP.
Regionally, Poilievre's Conservatives hold a significant lead in every part of the country except Quebec, where they are tied for second with the Liberals.
In Ontario, where the Liberals hold 75 seats, the Conservatives hold an 11-point lead, 41 per cent to 30 per cent support.
The Liberals won all those Ontario seats taking just 39 per cent of the vote in the last election to the Conservatives at 35 per cent, meaning, outside Toronto, there wouldn't be very many Liberals in Ontario if these numbers hold.
This budget was supposed to be Trudeau's attempt to claw his way back to relevancy.
Trudeau increased program spending by more than $30 billion compared to last year. He's promising to take care of every aspect of your life using tax dollars and it isn't resonating with voters.
Maybe the problem isn't a lack of spending or government announcements. Maybe the problem is Trudeau. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Millennials; Generation Z; Budgets; Voters

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets

Company / organization: Name: Ipsos; NAICS: 541910

Document 160 of 219

Ford reversals remind us our voices have power

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-reversals-remind-us-our-voices-have-power/docview/3042413714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+reversals+remind+us+our+voices+have+power&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Something Premier Doug Ford probably doesn't get enough credit for: He knows how to backtrack. As compliments go, it's undeniably backhanded, yet it's meant with genuine appreciation.
The world is full of politicians who won't back down from a policy, even in the face of intense criticism. The folksy premier certainly is capable of digging in his heels with great tenacity, and his bully-boy tactics can be audacious, but when the people object loudly enough and long enough, he has shown he can listen.
After months of resistance, Ford's Conservatives have agreed to back an NDP motion declaring intimate partner violence (IPV) an epidemic. This kind of aboutface is democracy at its best.
We saw it in 2019, when the government announced an under-baked overhaul of the Ontario Autism Program. Changes meant to reduce waiting lists would have booted thousands of kids from government-funded therapy, and doled out reduced funding based on family income rather than each child's needs. The public outcry was loud, sustained, and successful: the government reversed course on most of its changes.
We saw it again in 2022 when the province imposed a four-year contract on education workers, building in the notwithstanding clause to override their right to strike. The modestly paid workers walked out, and unions flexed the threat of a general strike. Within days, Bill 28 was not merely unanimously repealed; it was "deemed for all purposes never to have been in force."
Of course, there was no bigger reversal than the flip-flip-flop-flop drama over Greenbelt development. The premier clung far too long to a developer-friendly plan to rezone protected areas for housing development. Environmentalists hollered blue murder. As details of the preferential selection process emerged through two investigations, even steadfast Conservatives joined the chorus.
Ford finally acknowledged he'd made a mistake and said he was "very, very sorry." The government not only rolled back the zoning changes, but wrote the Greenbelt's boundaries into law.
Declaring IPV an epidemic is a largely symbolic gesture, but it signals a significant multi-system response to a lethal and ongoing health crisis. The measure topped a list of 86 recommendations by an inquest into a triple femicide in Renfrew County in 2015.
The government's shamefully dismissive response, completed last June, scoffed IPV "would not be considered an epidemic as it is not an infectious or communicable disease."
Perhaps they'd forgotten about the opioid epidemic, which is neither infectious nor communicable, and nevertheless has killed more than 40,000.
Across Canada, a woman is killed by her intimate partner every six days. The Ford government has devoted significant resources to fighting IPV and supporting survivors; its refusal to act on the inquest's top recommendation has been frankly baffling. Sustained pressure has come from dozens and dozens of municipalities, from Toronto to Sault Ste. Marie, which have passed motions declaring IPV an epidemic, urging the province to follow suit.
It's never too late to do the right thing. Reversals don't happen every day. But they happen enough to remind us that our voices have power.
This is the essence of democracy. It's not only about voting at four-or five-year intervals. We can effect change as informed and engaged citizens. There are more platforms to raise awareness than ever before. The pre-digital standbys also still work: old-school protest, letters to the editor, and contacting your MPP's constituency office.
The premier has forged ahead with some controversial policies - carving up swaths of Ontario Place for a private spa springs to mind - but he also has proven he can hear us, when we speak loudly enough. write.robin@baranyai.ca

Identifier / keyword: something; premier; probably; enough; credit; knows
