Document 161 of 219

Ford's PCs hold commanding lead, Liberals steal NDP support

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-pcs-hold-commanding-lead-liberals-steal-ndp/docview/3039704500/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+PCs+hold+commanding+lead%2C+Liberals+steal+NDP+support&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If an election were held today, Doug Ford's PC Party would win another big majority government. The latest poll from Liaison Strategies also suggests Bonnie Crombie's Liberals would form the Opposition and regain official party status at Queen's Park.
Oh, and the NDP, they'd be relegated to third place once again.
The telephone poll of 1,280 eligible Ontario voters finds that 40 per cent would back Ford's PCs, 30 per cent would back Crombie's Liberals but just 18 per cent would back the NDP under Marit Stiles. Ford's support is pretty much where it was in the last election, when his party won 83 seats, but Crombie has been able to move the Liberals up since taking over as leader in December, but only at the NDP's expense.
In the last two elections, the Liberals haven't been able to even win enough seats to be considered a recognized party in the legislature. Under the official rules, the nine Liberal MPPs are considered independents and given limited resources and time in question period compared to the NDP.
But like they did under former leader Andrea Horwath and continue to do under Stiles, the NDP has squandered their opportunity to cement themselves as the left-of-centre choice in Ontario. In every province west of Ontario, there is essentially no Liberal Party to speak of and elections are between the NDP and parties representing what is often called a free market coalition.
Had the Ontario NDP played their cards right starting in 2018, they could have ensured the same in Ontario. Instead, the Ontario Liberal Party is showing signs of coming back to life.
Not that the Crombie's Liberals should be popping champagne corks yet.
When Steven Del Duca took over as Liberal leader in March 2020, they saw a bounce in the polls. There were periods in 2020, 2021 and even early 2022 where the Del Duca Liberals were leading Ford and his PC Party and then the campaign happened and the Liberals ended up at 24 per cent, 16 points behind Ford.
It's always important for political types to remember voters are fickle, polls can change and campaigns matter. Those are three rules that apply to every party, including Ford, who appears to be sitting pretty at the moment.
Beyond the 40 per cent voter support there are other good news numbers for Ford in this poll.
Just shy of half of voters, 49 per cent, say the federal carbon tax should be scrapped. The environment isn't the issue it was before for the Liberals or NDP with 62 per cent saying pollution has decreased in the province compared to five years ago.
In a head-to-head question on which issue is more important, 54 per cent said affordability while just 25 per cent said the environment and 21 per cent said they didn't know.
Which makes Bonnie Crombie's recent comments to an Empire Club luncheon in Toronto problematic.
During a fireside chat after her speech last week, Crombie was asked about her promises to put more money into health care and education and how she'd make sure the government, if she were running it, could pay for them.
"Well, it's very interesting, because I've been thinking about what exactly the government is spending money on other than gimmicks and freezing the gas tax and giving you a rebate for your stickers and making them so you don't have to pay for them," Crombie said.
"That's really reducing the fiscal capacity and I keep thinking, would people really miss that or is that money that could be better invested in education and our health-care system?" Calling tax cuts and fee cuts a reduction in the fiscal capacity is a tell, Crombie thinks government should have that money instead of voters. Given that her party is trying to win a byelection in the suburban seat of Milton, these comments will hurt the party.
Crombie has done well taking support from the NDP, but she's likely taken as much as she can there. To win, she'll need to peel voters away from Ford's PCs and she won't do it by promising to increase taxes and fees on commuters.
Such promises are music to Doug Ford's ears.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Polls & surveys; Voters; Elections

Identifier / keyword: election; party; another; majority; government; latest

Title: Ford's PCs hold commanding lead, Liberals steal NDP support

Publication title: Sault Star; Sault Ste. Marie, Ont.

Document 162 of 219

Effectiveness of most common gender-affirming treatments for adolescents not 'well understood'

Author: Chow, Peter

Publication info: Chow, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/effectiveness-most-common-gender-affirming/docview/3038013850/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Effectiveness+of+most+common+gender-affirming+treatments+for+adolescents+not+%27well+understood%27&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Chow%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has declared his opposition to the use of puberty blockers for transgender children.
This aligns with his belief that children shouldn't make adult decisions until they become adults. This viewpoint is controversial, with critics arguing that it ignores the rights of transgender children and their need for gender-affirming treatment.
The discussion was sparked by Alberta Premier Danielle Smith's plan to ban puberty blockers and hormone therapy for children under 15 who have not already begun such treatments.
This proposed ban has been met with furious protests from those in the "woke" trendy lefty crowd.
I have to say that this is one time I have to agree with Smith and Poilievre.
The effectiveness and side effects of the most common gender-affirming treatments for adolescents are not well understood.
If this were any other situation, physicians would do what we usually do - we are skeptical. We push for better data, we are open and transparent about what we know and what we don't know about medications. (And we have to be in pediatrics because there are so few trials done on children.)
Adults fancying themselves experts in gender medicine and mental health, celebrating and promoting an ideology of diversity and fluidity in gender identity and sexual orientation, are wreaking havoc on an entire generation, encouraging them to "follow their feelings," even when they're risking permanent and irreparable harm by doing so -such as infertility? Gender dysphoria is a feeling of intense discomfort with one's sexual identity.
Once a rare diagnosis, it has exploded throughout all the rich Western countries over the past decade, by over 7,000 per cent.
Some, including Robert F.
Kennedy Jr., nephew of President John F. Kennedy, who is running as an independent for the U.S. Presidency and who is known for promoting unfounded conspiracy theories, speculates it is related to the explosion of hormone-disrupting chemicals that we have been increasingly exposed to over the past 50 years, attributing this as well to the increased diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorder as well as the more than 60 per cent decrease in average sperm counts since 1970, with the rate of decrease doubling since 2000.
This time he may not be that wrong.
Fifty years ago: Girl: "I feel like I want to be a boy."
Parent: "Well, you're not, you're a girl."
End of discussion. Now, gender-affirming treatment pioneered in the Netherlands involves acknowledging patients'dysphoria about a mismatch between their body and their sense of self and, after a psychological assessment, offering some of them a combination of puberty blocking drugs, opposite-sex hormones and sometimes gender-affirming surgery to try to ease their discomfort.
In many countries, but in America most of all, and now in Canada, the treatment is controversial, yet another front in the culture wars.
Many on the left characterize critics of gender-affirming care as callously disregarding extreme distress and even suicides among adolescents with gender dysphoria, in their determination to "erase" trans people.
Of course, it is very "woke" among the trendy lefty crowd to celebrate and promote diversity and fluidity in gender identification and sexual orientation.
Critics on the right, meanwhile, accuse proponents of being so hellbent on promoting gender fluidity and transition that they "groom" vulnerable children - a term usually applied to pedophiles.
Most medical authorities in the U.S. and Canada support gender-affirming care.
But this treatment is life-changing and can lead to infertility.
And those in Britain, Finland, France, Germany, Italy, Norway and Sweden, while supporting psychotherapy as a first step, have serious misgivings about the pharmacological and surgical elements of the treatment.
A 2020 Finnish review concluded that gender reassignment in children is "experimental," and that treatment should seldom proceed beyond psychotherapy.
Swedish authorities found that the risks of physical interventions "currently outweigh the possible benefits."
In Britain, a review found that gender-affirming care had developed without "the normal quality controls that are typically applied when new or innovative treatments are introduced."
In 2022, France's National Academy of Medicine advised doctors to proceed with drugs and surgery only with "great medical caution" and "the greatest reserve."
Last month, England announced children with gender dysphoria will no longer be routinely prescribed puberty blockers at publicly funded gender clinics after the country's National Health Service concluded insufficient evidence exists to support their safety.
Hormone blockers will be available only to children enrolled in study trials, and those already receiving treatment.
Canada is at loggerheads with its European counterparts. Across Europe, many countries now believe that the evidence is still lacking and that such gender-affirming interventions should be used sparingly and need further study.
The Europeans are right.
The fact that some patients are harmed is not in doubt.
Some are "detransitioners."
They have changed their minds and bitterly regret the irreversible effects of their treatment and are angry at doctors who, they say, rushed them into it.
Studies of trans patients in America's armed forces found that between 15 per cent and 30 per cent of them stopped treatment within a few years. The protocol for transitioning teenagers, developed in the Netherlands in the 1980s consists of: puberty blockers, cross-sex hormones and surgery.
The most used puberty blockers are Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone agonists, which suppress the production of sex hormones, including testosterone and estrogen -injectable Leuprolide (Lupron) or oral Elagolix.
Regular injections of puberty blockers suppress the production of sex hormones and stop the physical changes that come with puberty - breasts, broadening hips and menstrual periods in a child born female, voice deepening, body and facial hair, muscle mass and other changes in a child born male.
The idea is that suspending unwanted sexual development can give patients time to think about their dysphoria, and whether or not they wish to pursue more drastic interventions.
Hormone blockers are intended to be given once puberty starts - on average, at age 10-and-a-half for children born female, and 11-anda-half in those born male.
Patients who decide to proceed with their transition are then prescribed cross-sex hormones, testosterone or estrogen.
Males given estrogen will see the development of breasts and alterations to how fat is stored in the body.
Giving testosterone to females boosts muscle growth and causes irreversible changes such as deepening the voice, altering the bone structure of the face and the growth of facial hair.
Under the original Dutch protocol, surgery was permitted only after a patient turned 18, although in some places mastectomies occur at a younger age.
Male patients can have artificial breasts implanted.
Then consideration of bottom surgery.
The effectiveness and side effects of the most common gender transition treatments are not well understood.
The justification for puberty blockers put forth by its advocates is that by delaying puberty, they give youth identifying as transgender "more time to explore their gender identity, before changes happen to their body that can't be reversed."
But the data available show that 99 per cent of adolescents prescribed puberty blockers decide to proceed to cross-sex hormones.
The question is whether puberty blockers themselves affect the developing brain to make adolescents more likely to want to continue medical transition.
The male brain is functionally and even structurally different than the female brain and puberty blockers may stop the normal childhood and adolescent development of this sexual dimorphism.
Puberty blockers might essentially solidify feelings of being the opposite gender, by stopping normal hormonal surges that drive sexuality and gender identity through the early teen years, locking children into further treatment.
Even the Dutch researchers admit, "One cannot exclude the possibility that starting puberty blockers in itself makes adolescents more likely to continue medical transition."
Seventy per cent or more of the young people seeking treatment for gender dysphoria suffer from mental health problems which formerly were absolute contraindication for treatment.
But now, mental health issues are becoming a reason to proceed with transitions, rather than to stop them.
We are now told that if we don't address young people's mental health distress caused by gender dysphoria with transition, they will kill themselves.
The original Dutch protocol emphasized the need for careful screening and assessments, as do official guidelines in most countries.
But despite the guidance, there is persistent evidence that it is not being followed in practice.
Many patients in the U.S. and Canada reported similarly brief examinations, fast-tracking children onto blockers.
A London, Ont., clinic encouraged family doctors to consider starting children identifying as trans on puberty blockers even before their first appointment at the clinic.
Wait times are long, according to a form letter sent to doctors, and blockers may lessen distress in the children waiting to be seen.
The charged atmosphere has made it very difficult to separate science from politics.
If this were any other situation, physicians would do what we usually do - we are skeptical. We push for better data, we are open and transparent about what we know and what we don't know about medications. And we have to be in pediatrics because there are so few trials done on children.
European medical systems have concluded that more research and data are needed before physical treatments for adolescents with gender dysphoria can become routine.
Further research could possibly lead to guidelines like those already used in Canada and the U.S.
But it is impossible to justify the current recommendations about gender-affirming care in North America based on the existing data.
The rest of the world is waking up to the dangers of lifelong medicalization of patients with gender-affirming treatments that inflict irreversible damage to preteens and teens.
Perhaps, when the proper trials are complete, the proponents of gender-affirming care will be proven correct.
The right policy is therefore the one Britain's National Health Service and the Karolinska Institute in Sweden have adopted: Promote psychotherapy and reserve puberty blockers and cross-sex hormones for studies in which patients would be enrolled in well-run clinical trials.
It's not often I agree with Doug Ford.
Ford has accused Ontario school boards of "indoctrinating" students on issues of gender identity and sexual orientation.
"It's not up to the teachers, it's not up to the school boards to indoctrinate our kids," he said.
Last summer, parents and staff were pressuring the Toronto District School Board to stop allowing parents to opt their children out of drag queen events such as Drag Queen Story Hour.
The argument put forward is no longer that it should be permissible to have drag queen shows in schools, but that parents should be barred from opting out their own children even if they are uncomfortable with such performances.
Proponents of Drag Queen Story Hour suggest it captures the gender fluidity of childhood and gives kids glamorous, positive and unabashedly queer role models.
Why is introducing kids to drag queens so important to some people? Jonathan Hamilt, who co-founded the New York chapter of Drag Queen Story Hour, says the program strives to "capture the imagination and play of gender fluidity of childhood and gives kids glamorous, positive, and unabashedly queer role models."
"Children are inundated with implicit imagery from media about what is 'boy'and what is 'girl.'" Hamilt feels drag is "an opportunity for children to get creative and think outside the boxes we silly adults have crafted for them."
"In spaces like this, kids are able to see people who defy rigid gender restrictions and imagine a world where people can present as they wish, where dress up is real," the Drag Queen Story Hour's website states. He is explicitly declaring that the goal of Drag Queen Story Hour is to promote notions of gender fluidity and malleability to children.
This ideology's foundations started with Queer Theory, the academic discipline born in 1984 with the publication of Gayle S. Rubin's essay Thinking Sex: Notes for a Radical Theory of the Politics of Sexuality.
Queer theory is a field in gender and sexuality studies that examines the nature of sexuality-based and gender-based normativity and how society defines and polices the concepts of heterosexuality, homosexuality, and gender and sexual identities -Queer theory aims to deconstruct what is "acceptable or normal."
I feel three major things shouldn't be pushed on small children: Sex/gender identity/orientation;Religion;Partisan politics.
I don't consider it abuse, but it falls under brainwashing.
Until a child reaches puberty -11 on average in girls, 12 in boys -they are extremely impressionable.
Do children necessarily have to have all of the same political, social or even spiritual beliefs as their parents? In order for them to be able to form their own authentic self, they should be doing age-appropriate activities and never have the pressure of adult-minded issues.
Small children shouldn't be forced into adult situations. Dr. Peter Chow is a retired Sault Ste.
Marie physician

Subject: Hormones; Females; Gender identity; Teenagers

Location: Netherlands; United States--US; Canada; France; United Kingdom--UK

Identifier / keyword: federal; conservative; leader; pierre; poilievre; declared

Document 163 of 219

Jennings 'cautiously optimistic'as bill moves forward to declare IPV epidemic

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/jennings-cautiously-optimisticas-bill-moves/docview/3038013802/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jennings+%27cautiously+optimistic%27as+bill+moves+forward+to+declare+IPV+epidemic&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Caitlin Jennings has been heard, says her father.
Dan Jennings was in Queen's Park earlier this week to hear the debate on Bill 173, a bill designed to declare IPV an epidemic in Ontario.
"It was overwhelmingly powerful for me," he said the day following the hearing.
Jenning's daughter Caitlin was killed in London, Ont., on July 5 by her partner, David Yates, who has since been charged with second-degree murder. The NDP bill 173 passed second reading, moving the call to declare IPV an epidemic one step closer.
Jennings called it a small step forward.
"I'm cautiously optimistic," he said. "I was pleasantly surprised the (Progressive) Conservatives decided to back the bill although there were not a lot of them there at the time, maybe a dozen or so."
He was disappointed Premier Doug Ford and Sault MPP Ross Romano were not present.
Romano was unavailable late Thursday for comment.
Sault Ste. Marie has had its eyes opened to IPV over the past year.
In addition to Caitlin's story, the community has mourned for Angie Sweeney, who was killed Oct. 23 by her former partner, Bobbie Hallaert. The gun yielding Hallaert also shot and killed his three young children and injured his ex-wife, Kim Rose, before turning the gun on himself.
Romano had organized a community candlelight vigil following last fall's tragic events, which led to the death of four, in what he called a path toward healing after the incident took so many lives.
While Ontario's NDP and supporters, which included survivors and victims'family members, were hoping the bill would go directly to Royal Assent, It will now go before a committee for review.
Jennings said if given the opportunity, he will tell Caitlin's story at the committee hearings.
"Even if it hurts to tell her story, I have said that I will do it over and over again to make sure she is heard," Jennings told The Sault Star. "We've been knocked down really hard, but we are rebounding and fighting for change, and we are not going to stop telling her story until we get that change."
Jennings said he was amazed by the number of survivors and families present at Queen's Park and the stories shared amongst the group.
Jennings, along with Brian Sweeney - Angie's father - and many others, are continuing to advocate for the federal government to pass Bill 332 - which would enact amendments to the Criminal Code to include coercive control of an intimate partner.
City council unanimously passed a resolution earlier this week calling on the federal government to pass the bill.
Advocates hope that the resolution will get passed in other municipalities to sway the federal government to make the change.
Statistics show that in 2019, of the 107,810 people aged 15 and over who experienced intimate partner violence, 79 per cent were women.
Data show 55 per cent of women who experienced physical or sexual intimate partner violence feared a partner at some point.
Between 2014 and 2019 in Canada, there were 497 victims of intimate partner homicide, and 870 per cent were women.
Jennings remains hopeful that the bill will become law by Canada Day - which is just a few days shy from the first anniversary of Caitlin's death.

Subject: Domestic violence; Murders & murder attempts

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: caitlin; jennings; heard; father; earlier; debate

Document 164 of 219

Multi-plex homes 'good options' for many Ontarians: NDP's Stiles

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/multi-plex-homes-good-options-many-ontarians-ndps/docview/3034935263/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Multi-plex+homes+%27good+options%27+for+many+Ontarians%3A+NDP%27s+Stiles&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All levels of government need to be actively engaged in ensuring communities have the safe and affordable housing they need, charge Ontario's NDP.
Opposition Leader Marit Stiles says the Ontario government is "out of touch" with the needs of Ontarians and Premier Doug Ford needs to step and show leadership to help create the housing that Ontarians need.
The federal and provincial governments are embroiled in a funding battle over $357 million geared toward new affordable housing units.
The federal government is threatening to withhold that funding from the province until Ontario shows progress has been made on its 2018 bilateral agreement to create 19,660 new affordable housing units.
Canada's Housing Minister Sean Fraser says only about one-third of that number has been created. He says said Ontario has not provided a clear path to build more units, even after a one-year extension, and the lack of progress is also impacting national housing targets. He's penned a letter to the province noting the funds will be withheld until a proper housing plan is in place.
NDP Opposition leader Marit Stiles says her party continues to advocate for more inclusiveness, more housing options and for more leadership from the government.
Premier Doug Ford has said he doesn't want to allow the construction of four-plexes as a right across the province, for fears it will disrupt single-family dwelling neighbourhoods.
The Liberals and Green Party has called on Ford to allow for four-plexes without requiring additional municipal approvals, as per a recommendation in the government's housing affordability task force.
Stiles called the premier's comments "insulting".
"For a lot of people, that is a good option and many of us have lived in multiplex situations and there is nothing wrong with that," she told The Sault Star in a recent interview.
The NDP has taken the position that legalized four-storey multiplexes will unlock affordable housing options across Ontario, something the NDP has been calling on for years. Sault Ste. Marie's CEO of the District Social Services Administration Board recently expressed his concern about the federal-provincial government dispute, particularly because it will impact DSSABs across Ontario, including locally.
To err on the side of caution, Mike Nadeau has recommended to the DSSAB board that a capital plan for 2024-25 be paused until social services is confident that the funding will flow. The capital plan included building a new four-plex on London Street and renovating other units in the system.
"There are four less units today than we were working on yesterday," Nadeau said. In addition, the capital funding is also used to assist other not-for-profits with repairs like roofs, new elevators or other renovation projects.
In total, the specific capital project budget line totals $1.3 million and Nadeau said that's only one of several budget lines that includes government transfers that flow to the DSSAB.
With most of the DSSAB funding coming from government sources, the only other options are to leverage debt or increase municipal levies, both of which Nadeau knows are not realistic.
"There really is no plan B while this dispute continues," he said, adding that there is concern the dispute between the higher levels of government could be a long one.
Stiles called the local situation unfortunate and unfair.
"It shows these decisions are affecting people at every corner of this province and I think it is incumbent upon the government to show some leadership here," she said.
Stiles hopes that the dispute will end soon because Ontario badly needs that federal money to help with the affordable housing crisis.
She also believes that it is time the Ontario government get more actively involved in the building of new opens across the province, so that the burden isn't left entirely on the private sector.
"We really believe that we can't leave this all up to the private sector. The government needs to build housing options, like it did up until 1995, and we need to get back to that," she said.
Last fall, the NDP unveiled a proposal to create an organization called Homes Ontario where non-market value homes can be created as co-ops, homes for seniors, and others.
The NDP wants to see public land and resources unlocked for the creation of new affordable homes.
Homes Ontario would leverage provincial funding, land and policy tools and work with the federal and municipal governments, to ensure an adequate supply of non-market homes that are affordable and meet the needs of households of all incomes, ages, family sizes, abilities and cultures, the NDP document reads.

Subject: Leadership; Affordable housing; Funding

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Affordable housing

Document 165 of 219

'How we can sit there and just do nothing?'; Establish more safe consumption sites, including in Sault St. Marie: Ontario NDP

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-we-can-sit-there-just-do-nothing-establish/docview/3034000209/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27How+we+can+sit+there+and+just+do+nothing%3F%27%3B+Establish+more+safe+consumption+sites%2C+including+in+Sault+St.+Marie%3A+Ontario+NDP&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's NDP Health Critic says there is no reason why Ontario shouldn't approve more applications for safe consumption sites to help tackle the addiction crisis in the province.
France Gelinas said there is no reason why the province should limit the number of safe consumptions to 21 as addiction issues run rampant across Ontario.
In addition, Gelinas says evidence shows that the services provided within a safe consumption site are saving lives and helping individuals connect with other community services and organizations that they may need.
Gelinas told The Sault Star that while she feels for the life lost at a Toronto-area safe consumption site last year which spurred the pause in applications, there was no need for the Ontario government to pause the application process of others.
Ministry of Health spokesperson Hannah Jenson said the ministry launched a review of all 17 provincial safe consumption sites following the incident and those reviews are ongoing, while applications continue to remain on pause.
"The two are not connected," Gelinas said of the stray bullet that killed a woman outside a safe consumption site. "When it comes to mental health and addiction, those people need care. They don't need to be discriminated against. They need easier access to the services that exist so they can get healthy again."
Jenson said Ontario's budget last week announced an additional $396 million over three years for mental health and addiction services.
"This includes $124 million to support the Addictions Recovery Fund which will fund more addictions recovery beds and new mobile mental health clinics," she said in an emailed response.
"This builds on our $525-million investment since 2019 in a range of addiction treatment services and supports," some of which will be used in both Sudbury and Timmins.
Gelinas's comments come on a visit to Sault Ste. Marie with NDP Opposition Leader Marit Stiles to visit the Group Health Centre and other community healthcare organizations to learn more about the physician shortage and other health care concerns in the community.
Gelinas confirmed Sudbury's safe consumption site closed last Friday due to a lack of government funding.
Known as 'The Spot,'the facility has been lobbying for provincial funding for almost two years. That funding has not materialized.
A second facility in Timmins, also not one of the province's approved 17 sites, was also set to close its doors when at the eleventh-hour local agencies partnered together to keep the centre open - at least for now. In March, Premier Doug Ford told reporters during an unrelated press conference in Essex, Ont., that the review of safe consumption sites is almost complete, but he stressed the Ontario government plans to keep its safe consumption site cap at 21. There are currently 17 sites across the province that have been approved by the provincial government.
Sault Ste. Marie Mayor Matthew Shoemaker has been lobbying for a safe consumption site and healthcare partners have gathered to pen an application and examine issues and costs including potential locations, who would operate the facility and how much operational funding it would need.
"We believe that supervised consumption sites help save lives but if this government doesn't believe that, then they need to offer us an alternative," Shoemaker said.
"We are open to anything," he said after the Ontario budget was released and did not include either more licences or a line specific to allocating more funding for supervised consumption sites.
Data show that on average there are two overdose deaths per week in Sudbury and about one per week in Sault Ste. Marie.
Opioid deaths are still the highest across Northern Ontario, including Sault Ste. Marie.
A recent provincial report issued by the Chief Coroner's Office says there were 55 confirmed and probable opioid deaths recorded in 2022 in Algoma and Sault Ste. Marie, just shy of the 58 recorded the year prior. Of those deaths, 44 were identified from Sault Ste. Marie, the rest spread throughout the district.
The overall rate that encompasses the Algoma Public Health region is the third highest in the province -48 deaths per 100,000 citizens.
For Ontario, that death number drops to 17.6 deaths per 100,000 people, some of whom believe it is because there are more services in southern Ontario.
"How we can sit there and just do nothing, is beyond me," Gelinas said.
She said it is unacceptable that the government is not providing the funding needed to help those who need it most.
Stiles called the situation "heartbreaking" for the families who are impacted by drug addiction and mental health issues.
"The government could absolutely be funding as many of these sites as they want to. They've just chosen not to," Stiles said.
She said the implications and trauma on communities and individuals affected is real and the NDP will continue to hold the Ontario government accountable.
The Ontario government has provided funding for various services in communities including mobile crisis response teams, safe beds or short-term accommodations for persons in crisis, opioid against therapy programs, youth wellness hubs and Rapid Access Addiction Medicine clinics.
Jenson said the province is also implementing the Addictions Recovery Fund, one-time funding of $90 million over three years to boost capacity in addictions services, adding 204 new addiction treatment beds in the North.

Subject: Mental health; Addictions; Funding; Lobbying; Crisis intervention; Narcotics

Document 166 of 219

MPP Romano, province doing 'very best to thwart'Sault getting safe consumption site; 'Stigmatizing'drug use and debating 'right and wrong'is not the answer

Author: Chow, Peter

Publication info: Chow, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpp-romano-province-doing-very-best-thwartsault/docview/3033999943/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+Romano%2C+province+doing+%27very+best+to+thwart%27Sault+getting+safe+consumption+site%3B+%27Stigmatizing%27drug+use+and+debating+%27right+and+wrong%27is+not+the+answer&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Chow%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Representative democracy, the misguided belief in the collective wisdom of individual ignorance, is a system in which many ignorant foolish people vote for unqualified cynical dimwits.
The fundamental premise of representative democracy - that voters can accurately assess their own best interests - is thus exposed as completely untenable.
We know that a vast swath of people lacks the interest, motivation and/or cognitive capacity necessary to become adequately informed about important matters and thus merely vote for whoever promises them the biggest helping of free ice cream.
In the last Ontario election in 2022, it was basically Premier Doug Ford running against himself, so 57 per cent of eligible voters refused to vote.
Ford was elected by 17.8 per cent of all eligible voters; 56 per cent of the 60,818 eligible voters didn't vote in Sault Ste. Marie in 2022 and 33,791 eligible voters looked at the choices locally and, echoing Peggy Lee's Is That All There Is?, voted with their feet and stayed home.
Sault Ste. Marie MPP Ross Romano won with a landslide of 21 per cent of eligible voters.
The second problem with representative democracy is that the first part of the phrase - representative - is misleading.
Although the original concept was for citizens within a riding to elect a person who'd represent within a central chamber whatever they thought their interests to be, the reality quickly proved to be utterly different.
Those elected to represent their districts quickly discovered that several hundred individual voices, each wanting something different from most of the other voices around them, resulted in a chaotic clamour.
So, individuals began to form groups around somewhat common interests, and those groups developed into political parties.
But to attain some slender measure of cohesion it was inevitable that the great number of individual interests became condensed down into a very small number of interests around which the Party could form some reasonable consensus.
And from there it was an exceedingly short step from attempting to represent one's district to becoming the salesman for one's party.
So, far from representing the specific interests of one's district such as the Sault, a politician such as Ross Romano is a person whose tenure in Queen's Park depends on the success with which he can sell the Ontario Progressive Conservative party's policies to his district, regardless of the harm such said policies may have on his riding.
In the four years since the beginning of the pandemic in 2020, 65 people have died of COVID-19 in the Sault. (COVID-19 contributed to but was not the underlying cause of death in 56 people.)
In stark contrast, more than 3 times as many, over 215 young people have died of opioid overdoses in SSM in that same time.
Whereas COVID killed mostly senior citizens, drug overdoses killed young people, teens, people in their 20s and 30s and 40s.
Many, many more life-years lost.
Christian Provenzano, former mayor of Sault Ste. Marie, said, "Frankly, this is a health-care issue."
"And the health care that people badly need is not available for them."
He said government must act to prevent more harm, rather than "waste time stigmatizing" drug use and debating right and wrong."
Sault Ste. Marie Police Service Chief Hugh Stevenson, in a written statement issued in July 2021, has shown his support for the establishment of a local supervised safe consumption site to help people with drug addiction along the road to recovery.
Sault Ste. Marie is applying for a supervised drug consumption and treatment site.
But our faithful MPP, Ross Romano, is doing his very best to thwart this even as the dead bodies annoyingly keep piling up -over 254 since he became MPP IN 2017.
Government must act now to prevent more harm, rather than waste time stigmatizing drug use and debating right and wrong.
Too many obituaries of the sudden and unexpected passing of young people in their teens or 20s.
For my son, daughter and nephew, seeing so many people they know die is incredibly painful.
We've all lost so many people that are close to us and people that we've known, because people are dying every day while people are sitting around arguing about the morality of what to do.
This remains a public health crisis without any adults in charge, and communities across Ontario are desperate for action grounded in evidence.
The evidence-based medical recommendations are there but are being stymied by the ugly face of political ideology.
In July this year, a passerby, a 44-year-old woman, was killed by a stray bullet from a fight in the area around one of the province's supervised consumption sites in east-end Toronto.
The Ford government is using the shooting as a "scapegoat" to impose an indefinite moratorium on any new supervised drug consumption and treatment sites.
Including Sault Ste Marie. Suppose someone was shot in front of Princess Margaret Hospital on University Avenue in Toronto.
Would they declare a moratorium on cancer treatment all across Ontario?At least five sites have been waiting for provincial approval, some for more than four years - and they will have to continue to wait, according to Michael Tibollo, the associate minister of mental health and addictions, another genius.
Windsor and Sudbury have opened temporary sites with municipal funding in the meantime, but Sudbury's municipal funding is set to expire at the end of the year.
When asked about the pending closure of Sudbury's site in Question Period, Tibollo cited the Toronto shooting.
"Unfortunately, with the situation that we had at Leslieville, it's forced us to look at the consumption and treatment sites to determine the impact it is having on public safety as to where they're located," Tibollo answered.
This received a standing ovation from the PC caucus.
This standing ovation from the PC party was nauseatingly disgusting and shameful.
The PC party is showing its true colours.
Conservatives have always been cold if not openly hostile to harm reduction, medication-assisted treatment with methadone and suboxone and supervised drug consumption and treatment Sites, let alone decriminalization of drugs and assuring a clean drug supply for addicts.
This is ignoring the evidence-based recommendations of the medical community, the addiction medicine community, the law enforcement community, and Health Canada.
But Conservatives are unable to overcome their natural proclivity for judgement and punishment and slow to understand -on their usual timetable, about 10 years after the rest of us -that criminalizing drugs doesn't work.
Meta-analysis of multiple medical studies has shown that dead opioid abusers tend to not do so well in rehab but on the positive side, the recidivism rate is zero.
The Sault had the second highest opioidrelated mortality rate in Ontario in 2022.
Northern Ontario has more than three times the mortality rate of the province.
In the face of the opioid crisis, Timmins's supervised consumption and treatment site has demonstrated its effectiveness as an evidencebased solution to mitigate harm and save lives.
Between July 2022 and December 2023, the site saw a total of 38,758 visits from 1,569 individuals.
Timmins experienced a 20 per cent reduction in opioidrelated emergency calls in its first year of operation.
In 2022, Timmins experienced the first reduction in its growing opioid-related death rate since 2018, dropping from the highest in Ontario, 80.2 per 100,000 in 2021 to 53.5 per 100,000 in 2022.
By preventing overdose deaths, reducing emergency room visits, and curbing the spread of infectious diseases, SHST ultimately saves taxpayer dollars while improving community health outcomes. Dr. Robert Maloney, Algoma's Rapid Access Addiction Medicine former lead physician, and currently involved with SAH's Addiction Medicine Consult Team, said: supervised consumption sites "are" treatment programs.
These facilities substantially reduce overdose deaths and lengthy hospitalizations due to injectionassociated infections. (HIV and Hepatitis C) Moreover, these facilities offer an effective gateway for hard-core substance users to engage in additional opportunities for support, programming and treatment.
The Sault's new Inpatient detox facility won't be nearly enough.
This is the Level 3 Withdrawal Management Facility SAH submitted as its proposal way back in 2017, over seven years and 254 drug overdose deaths ago.
An inpatient withdrawal unit, when the best medical evidence favours outpatient withdrawal management programs, so clients can continue with work or education.
The best medical evidence strongly recommends against a strategy involving inpatient withdrawal management alone, since this approach leads to increased rates of overdose deaths in comparison to providing no treatment at all.
Rapid inpatient withdrawal tapers invariably lead to relapse and an increased likelihood of death.
Abstaining for any length of time lowers their tolerance to opioids and when they do relapse, they are much more likely to die of overdose.
"I think there is a bit of a lack of clarity at all levels, and it really wasn't (initially) clear to me," Maloney said.
"Most of us kind of understood it would be all services under one roof. That's not exactly what was awarded."
Maloney said this latest bid to stem drug-related deaths here is not residential rehab treatment.
Drug detox and drug withdrawal management are typically performed in a combined drug treatment/drug rehab center such as Homewood Health Center, in Guelph, Ont., or Camillus Centre, in Elliot Lake.
Mayor Matthew Shoemaker vented his frustration about the lack of a supervised consumption site, stating that he or Christian Provenzano have written to the Ontario government about support for the addiction crisis on 10 different instances since 2019.
No supervised consumption and treatment site, and no anonymous, free fentanyl testing service.
Empty platitudes and mendacious prevarications are all we get in Sault Ste Marie.
Ross Romano should be deeply ashamed. He has the rare ability to irrepressibly jump from one failure to the next with no iota of embarrassment or loss of enthusiasm.
Since 2017, he has also been the head cheerleader for the location of a Ferrochrome smelter smack-dab inside the city spewing carcinogenic chromium-6 downwind onto the core of the downtown and the bulk of the residential neighbourhoods.
A strong selling point in recruiting new physicians to the city.
Speaking of which, Romano says he's "cautiously optimistic" that a plan will be in place before May 31 that will ensure the 10,000 de-rostered patients will have access to primary care services.
The first maxim in medicine is, "Primum, non nocere" -"First, do no harm."
Romano and the Ontario PCs have blown up that silly notion.
No supervised consumption site, no harm reduction with widespread medicationassisted treatment, no anonymous, free fentanyl testing service in the city -three measures that alone would save lives right away. Dr. Peter Chow is a retired Sault Ste. Marie physician

Subject: Emergency medical care; Drug withdrawal; Drug overdose; Shootings; Addictions; Democracy; Voters; Mortality; Substance abuse treatment; COVID-19; Stigma; Drug use; Narcotics

Document 169 of 219

Trudeau could be leading party over the cliff

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-could-be-leading-party-over-cliff/docview/3032876642/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+could+be+leading+party+over+the+cliff&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This past week, the deputy governor of the Bank of Canada said, "You know those signs that say, 'In an emergency, break the glass?'Well, it's time to break the glass." Carolyn Rogers was talking about the economy, but easily could have been talking about the Liberal Party and its leadership.
Surely, people in senior positions at Liberal HQ can read the polls and assess that things are not good.
The latest Ipsos poll has Pierre Poilievre and his Conservatives leading Justin Trudeau's Liberal nationally by 18 points. The Conservatives are at 41 per cent nationally, compared to the Liberals are at 23 per cent, while in Ontario, the lead is 19 points, in British Columbia, it's 20 points, and it's the same in Atlantic Canada.
It's not just Ipsos: The latest Abacus poll also has an 18-point lead for the Conservatives; Nanos gives the Conservatives a 15-point lead; Mainstreet, a 20-point lead; and Leger, a 16-point lead. All these polls show the Conservatives leading across different age categories and in every region of the country other than Quebec.
Someone must have told Trudeau it's time to go.
Well, in fact, I've been told several people have told him that his time is up, even close family have raised the issue. Depending on who you talk to, Trudeau is either listening and will leave, or is determined to stay to fight Poilievre.
We don't know what he will do. What is clear is that many Liberal MPs are going to head for the exits and seek greener pastures outside of the world of politics.
Reportedly, as many as 70 Liberal MPs have decided not to run in the next election, that's 44 per cent of the current caucus.
This is not the kind of normal turnover due to retirement that we see in all parties ahead of a pending election, this is a stampede for the exit. Not before collecting their MPs'pension after six years of service, though Yes, Canadians have noticed that in an attempt to stay in power until October 2025, the Trudeau government will move the election back by one week. If the minority Parliament lasts until that point, it will mean 80 MPs who were elected in 2019 will qualify for their pension.
Among the 80 are 32 Conservative, 22 Liberal, 20 Bloc and six NDP MPs. The Conservatives aren't worried about losing their seats right now, but the others are, so they will do what they can, and vote for whatever is put in front of them to get those annual pensions, ranging from $32,000 to $49,000 a year.
It may not be enough to get rich on, but it's a nice cushion as you look for other work. And plenty of Liberals especially will be looking for new work.
The seat models for the current polling show the Conservatives winning more than 200 seats, but the Liberals could drop as many as 100 seats. One longtime Liberal worries that his party will be reduced to their strongholds in Toronto and Montreal, with just a smattering of seats outside these two cities.
It's happened before. In 1984, when Brian Mulroney won the biggest majority ever, the Liberals were reduced to 40 seats. In 2011, when Stephen Harper won government and the NDP the opposition, the Liberals wound up with 34 seats.
If the line continues, Trudeau could be leading his party into the same kind of embarrassing territory.
Someone in the Liberal Party needs to break the glass. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 170 of 219

Province must act on GHC proposed solutions: NDP's Stiles

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-must-act-on-ghc-proposed-solutions-ndps/docview/3032876102/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+must+act+on+GHC+proposed+solutions%3A+NDP%27s+Stiles&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Group Health Centre is still an innovative and attractive model of care, but government needs to provide the tools and resources it needs to stabilize and grow, charge Ontario's New Democrats.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Health Critic France Gelinas (MPP Nickel Belt) met with Sault Ste. Marie health-care leaders Wednesday and will return to the Ontario legislature to continue lobbying Premier Doug Ford and the Progressive Conservative government for ongoing funds and legislative changes that will ensure Ontarians have access to primary health-care providers.
Stiles said she believes in the importance and innovative work the GHC is doing, and she remains concerned with the expected derostering of 10,000 patients on May 31. "What's missing is the government. They need to listen to what the GHC is proposing, and they need to move forward quickly to ensure people here have access to great care," Stiles said.
Gelinas said more physicians are needed across Ontario, but they, too, require a work-life balance and need to work in an environment where their skills are properly utilized.
"When they're spending 20 hours in front of a computer to complete reports that are needed and then 20 minutes to complete a referral, it's all very discouraging," she said. "That's why we are not retaining primary care physicians."
Gelinas said changes need to be made in the system to reduce the administrative burden on family physicians, and government needs to provide proper funding to ensure they run effectively.
The NDP says the GHC submitted a proposal to the government as early as last June but has still not received a response - or the additional funding they requested. The existing funding model is not helping the GHC, and, in fact, is leaving them with an additional financial burden.
The NDP has proposed solutions that include reducing the administrative roles of physicians by directing more money toward administration. They've recommended changing legislation that reduces the need for multiple sick notes, standardizes insurance forms and creates a centralized referral system across the province.
"The government voted down our proposal completely, which would have freed up physicians to provide two million more patients in the province of Ontario with physicians," Stiles told The Sault Star in an interview. "This government needs to listen to these solutions."
While Ford formally announced the creation of a new medical school to focus on training family physicians at Vaughan's York University Wednesday, Gelinas said the money would have been better spent expanding NOSM University's student numbers. It currently has spots for 64 students.
"They would be willing, ready and able to take 100 more medical schools this fall," she said. "They receive thousands of applications, and they can take way more students. We don't have to wait to build a new school down south and 98 per cent of the NOSM's graduates remain in the north."
Last week's Ontario budget provided additional funding for colleges to expand nursing and other healthcare programs.
Gelinas said there was a program available that provided funding to hospitals to train new graduates, but that program was dismantled and has now been restarted with only a fraction of the funding it once had.
In addition, primary care has not seen an increase to its base budget in 12 years, she said.
Ontario's NDP remains committed to public health care.
Private health care, they argue, costs more and the increasing number of emergency room closures, lack of primary care physicians and hospital closures are a result of a lack of funding from the province.
"It's by design that this has been done. The government is ready to help a few people, a few investors, make money off the back of sick people," Gelinas said.
Sault MPP Ross Romano said he continues to work with a local health-care task force and remains optimistic that a solution will be found before the May 31 derostering deadline.
Stiles counters that Romano shouldn't give people false hope if the government doesn't plan on providing the necessary funding or changes in the system.
"The solutions have been on the minister of health's desk for months and to say a solution will come in health care, even if it is a good idea, nothing happens overnight," Gelinas said. "Now is the time to put in place the solutions that have already been identified by the GHC."
Stiles said the Progressive Conservative government is simply "out of touch with reality" and the government hasn't found any other better solution.
In January, GHC announced it would drop 10,000 patients without family care providers, and another 6,000 remain at risk.
Provincial stats indicate there are about 2.3 million Ontarians without a primary care provider.

Subject: Primary care; Funding; Health care; Medical referrals; Physicians

Document 171 of 219

Ontario government need to act on GHC proposed solutions: NDP's Stiles

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-government-need-act-on-ghc-proposed/docview/3031768513/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+government+need+to+act+on+GHC+proposed+solutions%3A+NDP%27s+Stiles&amp;title=The+Sault+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Sault+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Group Health Centre is still an innovative and attractive model of care, but government needs to provide the tools and resources it needs to stabilize and grow, charge Ontario's New Democrats.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Health Critic France Gelinas (MPP Nickel Belt) met with Sault Ste. Marie health-care leaders Wednesday and will return to the Ontario legislature to continue lobbying Premier Doug Ford and the Progressive Conservative government for ongoing funds and legislative changes that will ensure Ontarians have access to primary health-care providers.
Stiles said she believes in the importance and innovative work the GHC is doing, and she remains concerned with the expected derostering of 10,000 patients on May 31. "What's missing is the government. They need to listen to what the GHC is proposing, and they need to move forward quickly to ensure people here have access to great care," Stiles said.
Gelinas said more physicians are needed across Ontario, but they, too, require a work-life balance and need to work in an environment where their skills are properly utilized.
"When they're spending 20 hours in front of a computer to complete reports that are needed and then 20 minutes to complete a referral, it's all very discouraging," she said. "That's why we are not retaining primary care physicians."
Gelinas said changes need to be made in the system to reduce the administrative burden on family physicians, and government needs to provide proper funding to ensure they run effectively.
The NDP says the GHC submitted a proposal to the government as early as last June but has still not received a response - or the additional funding they requested. The existing funding model is not helping the GHC, and, in fact, is leaving them with an additional financial burden.
The NDP has proposed solutions that include reducing the administrative roles of physicians by directing more money toward administration. They've recommended changing legislation that reduces the need for multiple sick notes, standardizes insurance forms and creates a centralized referral system across the province.
"The government voted down our proposal completely, which would have freed up physicians to provide two million more patients in the province of Ontario with physicians," Stiles told The Sault Star in an interview. "This government needs to listen to these solutions."
While Ford formally announced the creation of a new medical school to focus on training family physicians at Vaughan's York University Wednesday, Gelinas said the money would have been better spent expanding NOSM University's student numbers. It currently has spots for 64 students.
"They would be willing, ready and able to take 100 more medical schools this fall," she said. "They receive thousands of applications, and they can take way more students. We don't have to wait to build a new school down south and 98 per cent of the NOSM's graduates remain in the north."
Last week's Ontario budget provided additional funding for colleges to expand nursing and other health-care programs.
Gelinas said there was a program available that provided funding to hospitals to train new graduates, but that program was dismantled and has now been restarted with only a fraction of the funding it once had.
In addition, primary care has not seen an increase to its base budget in 12 years, she said.
Ontario's NDP remains committed to public health care.
Private health care, they argue, costs more and the increasing number of emergency room closures, lack of primary care physicians and hospital closures are a result of a lack of funding from the province.
"It's by design that this has been done. The government is ready to help a few people, a few investors, make money off the back of sick people," Gelinas said.
Sault MPP Ross Romano said he continues to work with a local health-care task force and remains optimistic that a solution will be found before the May 31 derostering deadline.
Stiles counters that Romano shouldn't give people false hope if the government doesn't plan on providing the necessary funding or changes in the system.
"The solutions have been on the minister of health's desk for months and to say a solution will come in health care, even if it is a good idea, nothing happens overnight," Gelinas said. "Now is the time to put in place the solutions that have already been identified by the GHC."
Stiles said the Progressive Conservative government is simply "out of touch with reality" and the government hasn't found any other better solution.
In January, GHC announced it would drop 10,000 patients without family care providers, and another 6,000 remain at risk.
Provincial stats indicate there are about 2.3 million Ontarians without a primary care provider.

Subject: Primary care; Funding; Health care; Medical referrals; Physicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Physicians

Document 173 of 219

How the Liberals blew their credibility on the carbon tax

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-liberals-blew-their-credibility-on-carbon-tax/docview/3031003560/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+the+Liberals+blew+their+credibility+on+the+carbon+tax&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Once a government loses its credibility on an issue, it's hard to recover it.
That's what has happened to the Trudeau Liberals on their carbon tax.
Their loss of credibility began when they claimed they'd come up with a tax that, due to rebates, would leave 80 per cent of Canadian households paying it better off, which never passed the smell test.
That's because what they didn't say is this was only the fiscal impact of the carbon tax - dollars in, dollars out - not its negative impact on the Canadian economy.
That only happened when independent, non-partisan Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux examined the carbon tax.
He said the government was right to say 80 per cent of households end up better off when only its fiscal impact is considered.
But when you factor in its negative impact on the economy, Giroux estimated 60 per cent of households paying the federal carbon tax are already worse off today, rising to 80 per cent in Nova Scotia in 2025, in Ontario in 2026, in Manitoba in 2029 and in Alberta and P.E.I. in 2030.
Giroux reported that the carbon tax would lower Canada's GDP and reduce business investment and labour income for skilled and unskilled workers. He also noted the government doesn't remit the GST paid on top of the carbon tax back to those paying it.
Ever since, the Liberals and their supporters, frantic to discredit Giroux, have been throwing up red herrings and irrelevant arguments, not to discredit his analysis of the fiscal impact of their carbon tax - because Giroux agrees with the Trudeau Liberals on that - but his added analysis of its economic impact.
They complained Giroux didn't factor in the cost of other things the Trudeau government might have done to lower emissions in place of a carbon tax, ignoring that the PBO's role is to assess government policies that actually exist.
They said the PBO didn't factor in future savings due to new technologies to fight climate change, nor the cost of doing nothing because of increasingly severe weather.
This ignored that what the PBO was assessing was the economic impact of Trudeau's carbon tax that began at $20 a tonne of industrial greenhouse gas emissions in 2019, is now $65 a tonne and will rise on April 1 to $80 a tonne, on its way to $170 a tonne in 2030.
We don't know what the Liberal plan is after 2030, and as Giroux has noted, claiming the carbon tax will save money because of technological innovation or less severe weather in Canada by 2030 is unrealistic.
It's also absurd. Contrary to his critics'claims, Giroux has examined the long-term impact of climate change on the Canadian economy, estimating that, "recent increases in temperature and precipitation combined with future changes in weather patterns will reduce Canada's real GDP by 5.8 per cent in 2100."
The issue, Giroux said, is that even if we were able to reduce our emissions to net zero by tomorrow, it would have no impact on the weather in Canada because our emissions, at 1.5 per cent of the global total, aren't enough to materially impact climate change.
Reducing our emissions will give us the moral high ground in urging major emitters like China to do the same, but the idea that anything Canada does alone through Trudeau's carbon tax is going to mean fewer wildfires, floods and storms in Canada, is absurd. lgoldstein@postmedia.com

Subject: Economic impact; Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Cost control; Credibility; Emissions; Carbon; Households; Climate change; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Economic impact Gross Domestic Product--GDP Cost control Environmental tax

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: government; loses; credibility; issue; recover; happened

Document 174 of 219

Algoma U gets fewer international student permits

Author: Kelly, Brian

Publication info: Kelly, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/algoma-u-gets-fewer-international-student-permits/docview/3031003002/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Algoma+U+gets+fewer+international+student+permits&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Kelly%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario will prioritize its newly reduced number of international undergraduate student study permits to post-secondary institutions that offer in-demand programs such as in the skilled trades, child care and health care, the province said Wednesday.
Nearly all of the permits will go to publicly assisted colleges and universities with private career colleges receiving none.
The federal government announced earlier this year it would slash the number of international student permits it would hand out with Ontario seeing its allotment cut in half.
"We are protecting the integrity of our province's post-secondary education system by attracting the best and brightest international students to Ontario to study in areas that are critical to our economy," Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop said in a statement.
"We have been working with post-secondary institutions to ensure international students are enrolled in the programs to support a pipeline of graduates for in-demand jobs."
Post-secondary institutions, especially colleges, in the province turned increasingly to international students after Premier Doug Ford's government cut tuition by 10% in 2019 and froze it there. The tuition freeze remains in place, the province said last month.
Last month, Dunlop gave a $1.3-billion funding lifeline to post-secondary institutions, but the colleges and universities said they needed double that amount. Many post-secondary institutions are running deficits this year.
Ontario's budget this week indicated that the lost international student revenue in the college sector, whose finances show up on the province's books, will total about $3 billion over two years.
The province said 22 of 23 universities will keep study permit applications at 2023 levels, but fewer will be allocated to Algoma University, which is based in Sault Ste. Marie. And 11 of 24 colleges will keep the allotment of applications at the 2023 level. Conestoga College in Kitchener and colleges with public-private partnerships will see the largest decline, the province said.
Algoma University is in "a strong, stable financial position," said president Asima Vezina in a statement.
"Our strategic planning has been designed to adapt to this change, aligning with our plan to moderate growth from 2024 onwards," she said.
The two-year cap on international study permits "is not expected to impact" the post-secondary institution's staffing numbers. Algoma, said Vezina, will review recent announcements by the provincial and federal governments "and analyze how the adjustment will impact future Thunderbirds, our institution, and the communities we serve."
Colleges Ontario CEO Marketa Evans said colleges are pleased their institutions will receive the bulk of the international study permit applications, but they are still left in the lurch.
The federal government's student visa changes have already resulted in the "collapse" of the spring cohort at public colleges and there will be a "severe impact" on the fall term with revenue losses in the hundreds of millions of dollars, Evans said.
"This has resulted in significant efforts to reduce costs, as colleges are not permitted to run unfunded deficits," she wrote in a statement.
"The consequences include immediate program suspensions and a pause on capital investments that include investments in student housing."
Universities are being responsible in their recruitment and management of international students, said Steve Orsini, president of the Council of Ontario Universities.
"The decision to cap international undergraduate allocations at 2023 levels will restrict the ability for universities to modestly increase enrolment, which will exacerbate financial pressures on the sector," Orsini said in a statement.
Federal Immigration Minister Marc Miller has said the cap is in response to a recent surge in international students and is meant to curb bad actors from taking advantage of high tuition fees while providing a poor education.
"Eager to review the details of this announcement in more depth, but this appears to be a very reasonable response by the government of Ontario," Miller said on social media Wednesday.
Alex Usher, president of Higher Education Strategy Associates, said the plan is "more promising than I would have expected.
"It sounds to me like they have done the right thing, which is they're reserving the biggest losses for the people who have public-private partnerships, plus Algoma, plus Conestoga, which are the institutions that didn't go the PPP route, but did build whole new campuses just for international students," he said.
"There's some basic procedural fairness there." with files from The Sault Star

Subject: Public private partnerships; Tuition; Foreign students; Colleges & universities; Permits; Education
