Document 175 of 219

Is the carbon tax costing you or making you money?; Both sides have been extensively citing the PBO's reports to justify their stances

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-carbon-tax-costing-you-making-money-both-sides/docview/3031002949/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+the+carbon+tax+costing+you+or+making+you+money%3F%3B+Both+sides+have+been+extensively+citing+the+PBO%27s+reports+to+justify+their+stances&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-04-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As one MP put it this week, Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux has been in the news "almost as much" as Taylor Swift these days, as the parties debate his studies of the federal carbon tax's impact on Canadians' wallets.
Both Liberals and Conservatives have been extensively citing the PBO's reports in attempting to justify their respective stances on the carbon tax, which went up April 1, and the governments tax rebates.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau claims the reports say eight out of 10 families get more from the rebate than they pay in carbon taxes. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre cites the PBO in his claim that "a majority of households will see a negative impact."
The truth is, Liberals and Conservatives are both telling a different version of the truth - with some important caveats.
Giroux's appearance at the House of Commons Government Operations Committee last Wednesday may have been overshadowed by the Saskatchewan premier's testimony, but MPs had pointed questions for the budget watchdog.
Conservative MP Phil Lawrence asked Giroux whether there is ultimately "more money coming into Canadians'pockets or leaving their pockets" for those where the backstop applies.
"If one looks at the fiscal impact, that is the amount of the carbon tax paid directly, indirectly and the GST that applies on these embedded direct carbon taxes paid minus the carbon rebate, most families are better off - we estimate around 80 per cent," said Giroux.
Last year's analysis from the PBO indeed stated that "most households will see a net gain" in Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario, Prince Edward Island, and Newfoundland and Labrador by the fiscal year 2030-31 - once the tax reaches $170 per tonne of carbon.
The only exception was in Nova Scotia, where households in the third, fourth and fifth highest income quintiles would pay more than they receive in carbon rebates.
The PBO noted that the tax minus the rebates is "broadly progressive," meaning that lower-income households would benefit more than higher income households.
However, the numbers changed once the PBO included the economic impacts of the introduction of the carbon tax. The assumption is that the tax will have had an effect on some sectors of the economy, and therefore family incomes.
"Then, we find that most Canadian families, in provinces where the federal backstop regime is in place, will see a small negative impact of the carbon tax," Giroux told MPs.
The same analysis mentioned above indeed mentioned that "most households will see a net loss" by the fiscal year 2030-2031 since they will be paying more in the federal fuel charge and GST, and it is assumed that they will be receiving lower incomes.
On average, households in Alberta would be paying $2,773 in net losses and those in Ontario would be paying $1,820 by 2030-31, according to the PBO's estimates. Lower-income households would continue to benefit from a net gain, while higher-income households would pay more.
But the PBO's conclusions on the economic impacts of the carbon tax have been challenged by Liberals and by climate think tanks, who claim that he failed to account for the costs of climate change.
The Canadian Climate Institute, for example, accused the PBO of being "misleading" for failing to consider "economic benefits of carbon pricing and the costs of climate inaction, both in terms of stabilizing the climate and competing in a global economy racing to net zero."
And this week, hundreds of economists signed an open letter saying that a national carbon price is the cheapest way to cut the most emissions, while disputing arguments put forward by Poilievre that the carbon price is driving up inflation and the cost of living.
Poilievre dismissed them as "so-called experts" and pledged to listen to the "common people."
However, the PBO did attempt to account for the cost of climate change in a report back in 2022. The analysis noted that extreme weather had already reduced Canada's GDP by 0.8 per cent between 1981 and 2021 and that the GDP could be further reduced by five per cent by 2100. But the report noted that "accounting for the cost of climate change is not straightforward."
Liberal MP Irek Kusmierczyk challenged Giroux this week and said it "boggles the mind" that the PBO did not account for the costs of climate change in its carbon tax analysis.
Giroux said that his mandate is to estimate the cost of government proposals, and encouraged other entities like think tanks or the government itself to disclose their numbers if they want to venture in a cost-benefit analysis of the carbon tax.
"To my knowledge, there hasn't been that much work undertaken on the cost-benefit analysis, because it's a complicated field that requires lots of data spanning several years," he said.
"So that's why even though it would be ideal to have the cost-benefit analysis, the benefits are not always very tangible, and not easily measurable." calevesque@postmedia.com

Subject: Rebates; Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Think tanks; Cost benefit analysis; Costs; Carbon; Households; Climate change; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates Gross Domestic Product--GDP Cost benefit analysis Costs Environmental tax

Document 176 of 219

CSIS warned of 'foreign interference'in Toronto riding during 2019 election;; Chief electoral officer had 'high confidence'that race was free and fair

Author: Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/csis-warned-foreign-interferencein-toronto-riding/docview/3020312141/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=CSIS+warned+of+%27foreign+interference%27in+Toronto+riding+during+2019+election%3B%3B+Chief+electoral+officer+had+%27high+confidence%27that+race+was+free+and+fair&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-03-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.3&amp;au=Nardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's chief electoral officer said Canadian Security Intelligence Service raised "allegations of foreign interference" in the nomination contest of a Toronto-area riding during the 2019 election, but he ultimately determined "no action could be taken."
Speaking to the Public Inquiry into Foreign Interference Thursday, Stéphane Perrault said he has "high confidence" that the 2019 and 2021 federal elections were free and fair.
The same day, the Commissioner of Canada Elections - the country's election investigative watchdog - told the inquiry that her office struggles to investigate foreign interference complaints.
Commissioner Caroline Simard and her top investigator, Mylène Gigou, listed several reasons for those struggles, including a lack of resources for costly foreign interference investigations, challenges with accessing evidence and tracing money, and a lack of useful legal tools.
"I would expect that these are not just challenges that are unique to our office, but likely for most law enforcement agencies as well," Gigou told the inquiry.
Former commissioner Yves Côté also said that there are vast legal exceptions to "undue" foreign interference provisions in the Canada Elections Act. For example, it is legal for a foreigner to tell a Canadian how they should vote in an election.
Perrault's testimony at the inquiry and a summary of an interview with inquiry lawyers two weeks ago revealed that CSIS came to him "unsolicited" in 2019 with concerns about foreign interference allegations in the riding of Don Valley North, in Toronto.
"During the 2019 federal election, he discussed with the Canadian Security Intelligence Service (CSIS) allegations of irregularities related to the voting in the nomination contest in the riding of Don Valley North, Ontario," reads a summary of a Perrault's pre-hearing interview.
"At that point, Mr. Perrault determined that no action could be taken."
The Elections Canada head declined to say which party's nomination contest was the target of the allegations. But Don Valley North is represented by Han Dong, who was elected for the Liberals in the 2019 elections but stepped down from caucus after reports by Global News last year that he allegedly told a Chinese diplomat to delay the release of Michael Kovrig and Michael Spavor, being held prisoner in China, because it would benefit the Liberal party.
Dong has denied the allegations and filed a lawsuit against Global News.
In a report published last year, former special rapporteur on foreign interference David Johnston disputed Global News's story but did note there were "irregularities" discovered in Dong's nomination process in 2019.
He wrote that there was a "well-grounded suspicion that the irregularities were tied to the (People's Republic of China) consulate in Toronto, with whom Mr. Dong maintains relationships."
Perrault told commission counsel that after a routine audit of the Don Valley North nomination contest after the 2019 election, he referred the file to the Commissioner of Canada Elections, the country's election investigative body, for "other reasons that may be unrelated" to CSIS's allegations.
The document cited "accuracy, completeness or failure to meet filing deadlines."
Simard, the Commissioner of Canada Elections, told the inquiry that her office's investigation into Dong's nomination race is ongoing and is focused on compliance and enforcement of the Canada Elections Act political financing regime.
She also revealed that she is investigating an unspecified "electoral contest" in the Greater Vancouver Area.
Perreault frequently declined to answer specific questions about CSIS's allegations in Don Valley North, citing national security concerns, to the obvious displeasure of some parties'lawyers in the room.
Perrault said that as far as Elections Canada's mandate is concerned, he was fully satisfied that the last two federal elections were free and fair.
An undated briefing document from Canada's Security and Intelligence Threats to Elections (SITE) Task Force to Perrault that was tabled at the inquiry Thursday also revealed that security agencies appear to be monitoring attempted foreign interference by Pakistan.
It is one of the first, if not the first, time a Canadian government document identifying Pakistan as a potential source of foreign interference is made public.
But what level risk (if any) posed by Pakistan as well as any suspected methods, interests and targets are all redacted.
SITE was established in the lead-up to the 2019 election to co-ordinate intelligence and information between Canada's intelligence agencies, the RCMP and Global Affairs Canada.
In the undated briefing note, SITE says that "challenges remain" in dealing with foreign interference by China, Russia, India, Iran and Pakistan, though there have been improvements.
It noted that the People's Republic of China (PRC) was the task force's biggest concern and that the sophistication and intensity of its foreign interference (FI) activities in Canada "outpaces" that of other hostile countries.
"The PRC is highly capable and motivated against Canada, and acts in a sophisticated, pervasive and persistent manner in carrying out Fl activities against all levels of Canadian government and civil society. PRC Fl threat actors are pragmatic and tend to pursue paths of least resistance," the document said.
"The PRC continues its efforts to cultivate relationships with current MPs and influence their views on issues of strategic importance to the PRC."
The document notes that other state actors, including India, Russia and a third redacted country, had "not yet demonstrated a significant threat to the election process."
But Indian officials continued to act against Canadian politicians, democratic processes and the Indo-Canadian community in order to promote a positive image of their home country or counter "perceived threats to India" within Canada, reads the note.
SITE noted that Russia could, but at the time of the briefing note had not, engaged in foreign interference activities in Canada. It's efforts generally focused on cyber capabilities as well as discrediting democratic institutions and using disinformation to create social "wedge" issues. cnardi@postmedia.com

Subject: Nominations; Task forces; Canada Elections Act; Intelligence services; Elections

Location: China; Pakistan; Russia; Canada; India

Company / organization: Name: Elections Canada; NAICS: 921190; Name: Canadian Security Intelligence Service; NAICS: 922190, 928110

Document 177 of 219

Health care gets budget cash; No consumption site for city

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/health-care-gets-budget-cash-no-consumption-site/docview/3013922639/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Health+care+gets+budget+cash%3B+No+consumption+site+for+city&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-03-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Health care is getting billions of new dollars from the Ontario government over the next few years.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy released the 2024 spring budget Tuesday. The $214.5 billion budget is the province's largest ever.
It focuses on key issues plaguing the province including affordability, health care and infrastructure, among other things.
From funding a new medical school at Vaughan's York University to training and recruiting new workers, health care appeared to be the winner of Premier Doug Ford's budget this year.
While York University will get $9 million to launch a medical school, there was no word on any funding for Algoma University or Northern Ontario School of Medicine University's plan to establish a campus in Sault Ste. Marie.
That's not surprising, said Mayor Matthew Shoemaker.
"We are just about to launch a business case study, so I understand that we are certainly in the early stages of this plan yet, and likely too early to get the attention of the provincial budget," Shoemaker said.
"But what bewilders me with this announcement is that NOSM has the highest rate of students going into family medicine - 55 per cent - so why not provide NOSM with additional student spaces and focus on a place that already develops family practitioners, especially given that the needs in the North are more acute," he said. "It's unlikely that we will see huge benefits from a school in Vaughan."
Sault MPP Ross Romano said Algoma U and NOSM U still have work to do before any funding is considered.
"But I'm very optimistic for the future that the business case will be positive," he said.
Romano said he's been working hard on the file and believes there are some natural synergies to work together and the necessity to increase health resources across the region is something known and understood by the Ontario government.
"When we look at the specifics of health care in the budget, specifics to the North, I think we show a demonstrated commitment to building our health-care resources throughout the region," he said.
The province is again increasing its funding to provide more primary health care teams and increase their capacity.
Sault Ste. Marie received $1.1 million to increase capacity at two centres - a total of 4,450 spaces - last month.
The provincial funding will increase to $546 million, allowing communities to access a further $466 million in net new dollars, to be distributed over the next two years, Romano said.
"I feel good about seeing those kinds of investments being made given our health-care situation," Romano said.
In addition, he said, Northern Ontario will be able to access a further $50 million to stabilize and build its health-care capacity and connect people to primary care providers and train registered nurses and registered practical nurses.
"Those funds to train more individuals will then connect into the funds to increase capacity that creates the clinics," Romano said.
"I think these are all programs that will help us attract and retain more resources in our community."
Sault College President David Orazietti said there were a few positives in the provincial budget, including the investment to fund more spaces in health-care programs, including nursing and RPN seats.
But to do that, hospitals need additional funding to hire those nurses.
Hospitals will receive a four per cent increase in their budgets - mainly designed to reduce surgery wait times.
Orazietti said he's pleased the learn and stay program will also continue.
He said it is an important part of funding for students to embark upon careers in the health-care sector. The budget allocates $50 million over three years to recruit trained health-care workers to Northern communities.
"That's positive and obviously there are training opportunities there because those health-care programs we already offer," he said.
Mental health and addictions were also addressed in the budget, with more funding allocated to the issues plaguing communities across the province, including Sault Ste. Marie.
The government has earmarked $400 million over three years for mental health and addictions. Romano said Sault Ste. Marie has received its fair share of that funding in recent years, part of a path to wellness journey that is expected to continue.
"It's something that we've been working on for a very long time and something that I think we'll continue to work on," he said.
Shoemaker said he's been working to connect with Michael Tibollo, associate minister of addictions and mental health, for some time, specifically to get help for the community.
It was noted there was no additional funding - or licences - for supervised consumption sites in the budget.
"We believe that supervised consumption sites help save lives but if this government doesn't believe that, then they need to offer us an alternative," Shoemaker said. "We are open to anything," Shoemaker said he was disappointed, but not surprised, not to see supervised consumption sites addressed in the budget but believes the city will have a strong application for one of the four licences available, when that application process reopens.

Subject: Provincial budget; Mental health; Addictions; Funding; Health care; Nurses; Northern communities

Document 178 of 219

$42M spent on firearms confiscation program; Four years since Liberals announced sweeping changes to Canada's gun law

Author: Passifiume, Bryan

Publication info: Passifiume, Bryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/42m-spent-on-firearms-confiscation-program-four/docview/2986752408/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%2442M+spent+on+firearms+confiscation+program%3B+Four+years+since+Liberals+announced+sweeping+changes+to+Canada%27s+gun+law&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-03-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.3&amp;au=Passifiume%2C+Bryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Four years after the Trudeau Liberals announced sweeping changes to Canada's gun laws, the government has so far spent $42 million on a federal firearms confiscation program that doesn't yet exist.
In a response to an order paper question filed by Sen. Don Plett in September, Public Safety Canada revealed that $41,904,556 has been spent so far on the government's "firearms buyback program," and that 60 department employees are working on the project.
"This is a boondoggle, and it hasn't even begun," Plett said Friday during the Senate question period, directing his question at government representative Sen. Marc Gold.
"How can your government have spent $42 million on this, when not a single firearm has been bought back?" As well, the documents reveal that IBM has been awarded a $2.27-million contract to develop, design and implement the program.
That figure is nearly double what that contract was worth when Public Safety Canada announced the firm's involvement in the program in 2020.
In the documents, the RCMP say it is managing a team of 15 full-time employees devoted to the gun grab.
As well, Service Canada has assigned two employees to the program, and a response from Public Services and Procurement Canada said it has devoted "the equivalent of 5.825 full-time employees" to the project.
Last April, the federal government commenced the first phase of the program by entering into a $700,000 agreement with the Canadian Sporting Arms and Ammunition Association to confiscate so-called "assault rifles" from retailers.
That program is expected to start later this year.
Work on Phase 2, which involves confiscating legally purchased firearms from their licensed owners, began earlier this year.
The government issued an invitation to qualify earlier this year, seeking vendors interested in collecting and destroying the over 1,500 gun models summarily banned by the federal government under the May 1, 2020 order-in-council.
That order-in-council instantly reclassified around 1,500 popular firearm models to prohibited status, including so-called "military-style" or "assault" firearms.
Initially meant to happen by April 2022, amnesty for licensed owners was extended until Oct. 23, 2023, and again until 2025.
The government estimates that between 10,000 and 15,000 newly prohibited firearms are in the hands of Canadian businesses, and anywhere between 125,000 to 200,000 are owned by licensed individuals.
"These estimates could vary as the number of affected non-restricted firearms is unknown," the invitation remarked.
As many crime guns are committed with prohibited firearms smuggled into Canada from the United States - and therefore unlawful for Canadian licence-holders to own - critics say the government is preoccupying itself with an expensive program whose affect on Canada's crime epidemic will be negligible.
National Post contacted Public Safety Canada for comment.
Firearms policy expert Tim Thurley said that with such an unprecedented program, there's no question it will be expensive.
"Consider, for example, the logistics of confiscating firearms in rural and remote areas, where some of these firearms - such as the Ruger Mini-14 - were widely used by hunters, farmers and recreational or competitive sport shooters," he said.
"Expect the costs to climb significantly further as the business and personal stages of the program are implemented and as more firearms are prohibited."
He said he expects the cost to stretch into the billions.
Simon Fraser University Prof.
Gary Mauser mused that nearly $42 million is a lot of money to spend for what he said amounts to "grandstanding" by the federal government.
"Ottawa is not having much success with their efforts on this file," he said.
"Ontario has refused to co-operate with future confiscations. Saskatchewan has asked to join the appeal. Requests for co-operating organizations have fallen flat."
The fact that the now-banned guns sat safely with their owners without incident for the past four years suggests the 2020 bans - enacted after the 2020 mass shooting in Nova Scotia - were introduced for reasons other than just public safety, Mauser said.
Last October, National Post learned Quebec-based anti-gun group PolySeSouvient was so angry that mandatory confiscation wasn't part of the government's gun control scheme it disinvited Prime Minister Justin Trudeau from future commemorations of the 1989 École Polytechnique shooting.
The government added a confiscation program later that year.
Questions are still swirling around Parliament Hill over how much the confiscation will ultimately cost taxpayers.
Government documents obtained by National Post say costs could reach as high as $2 billion, far greater than election promises suggesting the program would only cost between $400 million and $600 million.
Tracey Wilson, spokesperson for the Canadian Coalition for Firearm Rights (CFFR), told National Post that skyrocketing costs so far don't bode well for the program's future.
"Not only has not a single firearm been confiscated, the program itself doesn't even exist - not even for willing retailers anxious to off-load unsellable inventory," she said.
"The whole premise of the 2020 gun ban was that these firearms, previously approved for sporting and hunting purposes by the RCMP lab, were simply too dangerous to own. Yet four years later, they are all exactly where they've been for decades, if not generations - in the gun safes of licensed, RCMP-vetted gun owners."

Subject: Bans; Licenses; Mass murders; Public safety; Costs; Employees; Firearms

Business indexing term: Subject: Costs Employees

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: National Post; NAICS: 513110; Name: Public Safety Canada; NAICS: 922190

Identifier / keyword: years; trudeau; liberals; announced; sweeping; changes

Document 179 of 219

Councillors working with Angie's Angels urging feds to implement Bill C-33

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/councillors-working-with-angies-angels-urging/docview/2973589546/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Councillors+working+with+Angie%27s+Angels+urging+feds+to+implement+Bill+C-33&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-03-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two city councillors are working with Angie's Angels and hope to develop a resolution that can be shared across Canadian municipalities to urge the federal government to focus on ending Intimate Partner Violence.
Ward 3 Coun. Angela Caputo and Ward 2 Coun. Lisa Vezeau-Allen have connected with victim Angie Sweeney's best friends, Lindsay Stewart and Renee Buczel, to draft the motion and urge both the federal and provincial governments to take action.
More specifically, they want the federal government to pass Bill C-322 and the province to declare IPV an epidemic.
Angie's Angels was created to raise awareness of the widespread problem with IPV and urge the government to pass Bill C-332 to tighten up the criminal code and protect other victims from coercive and controlling men.
Sweeney was killed by her former partner Bob Thomas Halleart in October. Halleart then went to a second address and killed his three children, as well as injured his exwife Kim Rose, before turning the gun on himself.
Since the tragic incident that sent waves of shock across Sault Ste. Marie and beyond, many communities, including Sault Ste. Marie, have asked the province to declare intimate partner violence an epidemic.
Mayor Matthew Shoemaker was also asked to pen a letter to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, reiterating a face-to-face meeting in November, asking the Canadian government to implement recommendations of the Renfrew County Inquest that would take a stand against IPV.
Shoemaker has recently received a detailed response to its letter from Justice Minister and Attorney General Arif Virani.
The four-page letter states the Government of Canada is committed to ending gender-based violence, including IPV, in all forms and is working to address gaps in the Criminal Code.
The letter says the government's efforts and the Renfrew jury recommendation are also aligned in creating a new offence targeting coercive control.
Virani said the government supports the private member's bill C-332, An Act to Amend the Criminal Code (controlling or coercive conduct), which would strengthen Canada's legislative framework addressing IPV by proposing reforms to protect victims in intimate relationships.
Caputo publicly thanked Virani for his detailed response and efforts to address the gaps in the criminal code.
She said Canada needs to pass the private member's bill and make controlling or coercive conduct a crime.
"Angie's friends said that they believe if the legislation had been around, it could have saved (Angie's) life," Caputo told council.
Caputo said she's working to develop a motion that she hopes will get city council's support and one that can be shared with other municipalities to get their endorsement.
Strength in numbers will show the federal government that the legislation is important, and a necessary tool for the justice system, she said.
"I'll be looking for council's support in the near future on this, and hopefully other municipalities will follow us and pass the same resolution to get some change," Caputo said.
Caputo is also hoping that Ontario's Doug Ford government will also declare IPV an epidemic, something that they have failed to do to date, despite more than 100 municipalities and agencies adopting resolutions calling on the province to do just that.
"We remain hopeful that the Ford government will do that," Caputo said.
Caputo also wants to see improvements made that will keep perpetrators behind bars longer and wants to see an IPV registry, similar to a sex offender registry.
She said Sault Ste. Marie's tragedy and subsequent research has taught her that IPV can happen to anyone and doesn't discriminate against anyone.
"I was certainly aware of (IPV) but I was not aware of how prevalent it is. That's been a learning curve for me," she said. "I've also learned about the strength of women and how empowering they can be when they come together for something like this."
Vezeau-Allen noted that in 1993 the United Nations passed a declaration on violence against women and, 30 years later, some progress has been made.
"It's sad two friends have to come together to advocate for change. These are two very determined young women, and we will help them in any way we can," Vezeau-Allen said.

Subject: Domestic violence; Councils

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: councillors; working; angels; develop; resolution; shared

Document 180 of 219

N.B. premier shows the way

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/n-b-premier-shows-way/docview/2973019629/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=N.B.+premier+shows+the+way&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-03-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Brunswick's provincial government tabled its sixth consecutive balanced budget on Tuesday, which is virtually unheard of in Canada. This is a fiscal approach residents shouldn't take for granted, and policymakers across the country should adopt.
Since taking office in 2018, Premier Blaine Higgs has been a light of responsible, centre-right governance. He is not a flashy leader; rather, he's measured, determined and disciplined. His Progressive Conservatives have focused on stabilizing the province's precarious finances - turning New Brunswick from a warning sign to a success story.
These are real accomplishments.
Perhaps the biggest indicator is the parliamentary budget officer's judgment that New Brunswick's finances are now sustainable over the long term - one of only five provinces to get this nod.
Change did not happen overnight.
Instead, Higgs gradually improved the balance sheet and paid down debt - creating about $100 million of new fiscal room and reducing the impact of punishing debt-servicing charges.
More than $2.3 billion in surpluses have accumulated since the PCs took power, according to Public Accounts. (For context, New Brunswick's entire budget comes in at about $13 billion.)
This is a stark contrast to the irresponsibility we've seen in Ottawa, where the federal government has moved its goalposts over and over again to justify ever-higher spending. This year's deficit is expected to reach nearly $50 billion.
It's the kind of policy-making that will saddle future generations with the effects of today's bad decisions. New Brunswick seems to get this point in a way few other provinces do.
Higgs is not without his critics on the left, who point out that surpluses could support higher social spending and that revenue increases owe to higher federal transfers.
Yet these ideas are exactly what got New Brunswick into trouble in the first place. The Liberals'"see a dollar, spend a dollar" routine sent the province careening toward a fiscal cliff, until cooler heads decided to break that cycle.
There have also been right-leaning critics who want Higgs to make substantive reforms. While the premier has delivered modest income tax relief, he hasn't slashed tax rates for a simple reason: it would be premature to do so with so much economic uncertainty, including low growth, high interest rates and inflation still running relatively high.
To be sure, deeper cuts will eventually be needed to make New Brunswick competitive. But the premier is putting his province on a fiscal footing to be able to afford them, without plunging back into deficit.
Others have criticized Higgs's social policy agenda, specifically around gender policies in public schools. But here, too, we find his approach to be the opposite of extreme.
His government's reforms have observed a common-sense distinction between older children aged 16 and up - who can choose to use a different name and pronoun in school - and those who are younger. The latter group needs parental consent to go ahead with such changes.
Far from being a revolution, this is a good balance between individual students'freedoms and parental rights. It's a dose of maturity and reason in a highly emotional, sometimes hysterical culture war.
This is the kind of leadership we need: not only in Ottawa, but in all our provincial capitals. New Brunswick's example shows that even small, less-advantaged provinces can get ahead through good policy. It's an approach that deserves emulation to get Canada back on track.

Subject: Provinces; Budgets

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: provincial; government; tabled; sixth; consecutive; balanced

Title: N.B. premier shows the way

Publication title: Sault Star; Sault Ste. Marie, Ont.

Document 181 of 219

Trudeau's visit clarifies pharmacare options

Author: Breakenridge, Rob

Publication info: Breakenridge, Rob. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeaus-visit-clarifies-pharmacare-options/docview/2973019304/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%27s+visit+clarifies+pharmacare+options&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-03-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Breakenridge%2C+Rob&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's still unclear what the purpose was of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's visit to Calgary last week. Yes, there was an announcement about the 1.3 million seniors signed up for the dental care program, but it's unclear why our city needed to be the venue for that. Will Trudeau return to announce the 1.4-million milestone? But while the trip may have been more of a photo op or some early campaigning, the prime minister inadvertently helped vindicate a much-maligned position of the Alberta government.
No sooner had the ink dried on the Liberal-NDP agreement on national pharmacare, the Alberta government made it clear it would likely "opt out" of the program. So, it hardly should have been a surprise that pharmacare would come up in a meeting between the prime minister and Premier Danielle Smith.
However, whereas it had appeared we were destined for yet another Alberta-Ottawa skirmish, there may be common ground on this issue and a potential path to a deal.
Canada does have universal health care, but it's only "national" in the sense that it exists in all provinces and territories. Pharmacare, too, is provincial jurisdiction, so Ottawa's role is limited to dangling dollars in the hopes provinces agree to spend them a certain way.
The feds have identified which types of medications and what sort of coverage they'd like to see, but provinces are under no obligation to go along with that. Prescription drug coverage varies from province to province, so it's logical for any province to prefer a course of action that lends itself to or enhances what's already in place.
Asked last week about pharmacare and Alberta's perceived opposition, Trudeau noted his government's goal "is to make sure that the gaps in coverage that exist in every province across the country get filled in those two areas (i.e., contraceptives and diabetes medication)... That's a goal that I think everyone can agree with."
He went on to say his message to Smith was that "we want to work with you on a way that makes sense for you, that will be different from B.C., different from Quebec, different from Ontario, different from P.E.I., but we want to work with you on a system that'll make sure that everyone in Alberta is able to access coverage."
This would seem to bring Ottawa in line with Alberta's position, which all along has been that if Ottawa wants to make financial contributions aimed at expanding prescription drug coverage, Alberta would be open to negotiating such an arrangement.
If whatever deal Alberta ends up with deviates significantly from arrangements in other provinces that hew more closely to the sort of system the feds envision, it will be up to Albertans to judge whether the correct approach was taken here.
At the end of the day, Ottawa needs the provinces to make this work, and the provinces are under no obligation to take marching orders on a matter that falls under their jurisdiction. Rob Breakenridge is a Calgary radio host. rob.breakenridge@corusent.com X: @RobBreakenridge

Subject: Provinces; Jurisdiction; Prescription drugs; Prime ministers; Insurance coverage

Business indexing term: Subject: Insurance coverage

Document 182 of 219

Romano must 'act quickly'to help heal primary care woes: Steelworkers

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/romano-must-act-quicklyto-help-heal-primary-care/docview/2957826103/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Romano+must+%27act+quickly%27to+help+heal+primary+care+woes%3A+Steelworkers&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The United Steelworkers and other local union heads are demanding a meeting with MPP Ross Romano and the Minister of Health to discuss the derostering of 10,000 patients from Group Health Centre.
Mike DaPrat, president of Local 2251, the steelworker's largest union, said more than two dozen union and labour representatives have joined together and want to meet with Romano to discuss the critical physician shortage that requires immediate attention.
Romano has told The Sault Star he's happy to meet with DaPrat and others to discuss the issue.
In fact, Romano said, he's "cautiously optimistic" that a plan will be in place before May 31 that will ensure the 10,000 derostered patients will have access to primary care services.
Romano launched a task force, its first meeting held March 1, to review a vision and mission statement and develop a plan of attack to determine the root causes of the physician shortage issues and develop short and long-term solutions. Romano said the first goal is to ensure that derostered GHC patients will have access to primary care services through alternatives that are currently being explored.
"While it may not look like the same type of care we want to reach with our long-term vision, I am confident that it will meet our needs in the short-term while we develop a long-term solution," Romano said.
The long-term goal would be to ensure patients have comprehensive longitudinal care - being a member of a rostered clinic with both preventative and proactive care as well as urgent care needs and other basic prescription renewals, among other things, he said.
Romano is counting on the health-care experts to unite and achieve the desired outcome.
"I'm cautiously optimistic. I'm hopeful and I believe we can achieve what we need to achieve," he said.
DaPrat told The Sault Star that the union has learned the federal government has provided the province with $3.1 billion over three years to enhance Ontario's healthcare delivery system by certifying international doctors and implementing more nurse practitioners.
DaPrat's letter, posted to the USW Local 2251 website and Facebook page, also alleges a local doctor applied for a grant from the provincial government that would allow him to train up to 51 Internatioanl doctors that are presently in Sault Ste. Marie. It was denied. Also, allegedly, a number of health-care providers applied for a grant to employ a number of nurse practitioners who have 20 hours left until they graduate, which was also denied.
"If this is fact is the case, it must be the provincial government of which you are part of, who is denying the requests/applications. This is extremely puzzling," the letter reads.
DaPrat said the group wants to know why Premier Doug Ford and his provincial government are not moving forward to certify internationally trained physicians, including those who live in Sault Ste. Marie and the Algoma District.
The steelworkers argue that the federal funding distributed to the province should have been utilized across the province quickly to avoid situations like Sault Ste. Marie now finds itself in, he said.
Romano said the National Assessment Collaboration is charged with grading internationally trained medical graduates and the existing system is working well. That system assesses internationally trained graduates, and interviews are completed provincially and regionally where residency positions are available.
In fact, all five residency positions for primary care in Sault Ste. Marie were filled by internationally trained graduates last year.
In order to increase residency positions, more preceptors - or trainers - are needed.
Sault Ste. Marie's GHC is unique and has been considered an ultimate model of health care, so the magnitude of losing one-sixth of its rostered patients creates a much greater disruption to the system than smaller models.
Romano said the main issues seem to pinpoint administration concerns, the growing complexity of primary care and the lack of individuals wishing to practise primary care medicine.
"I do believe we can come up with a solution and I think that we have the opportunity, because of the nature and gravity of the local challenge, to have an impactful opportunity that can allow us to be leaders to find an innovative solution," Romano said.
Romano said he was first notified of the GHC's issue on Jan. 11 and was told by officials there that there is no option to change the derostering solution. He requested a letter from them noting the healthcare centre needed $11 million to help retain and attract physicians and nurse practitioners and focus on addressing the needs of the remaining patients.
"In the end, I requested that they address their concerns and answer some questions in a letter, but it was noted that this wouldn't affect the 10,000 derostered patients anyways," Romano said.
Romano said he's been in contact almost daily with the GHC since the Jan. 11 meeting.
Romano insists the task force will also focus on the long-term health of the local health-care system but needs to deal with the short-term solution first.
"The letter made it clear the solution they were looking for went beyond the 10,000 patients to be derostered on May 31 and I don't want to lose sight of those patients," he said. "I can say that on behalf of the group, everyone is committed to the short-term and the long-term plan and the ministry (of health) shares that commitment."
DaPrat says that if elected officials do not start working for the community and individuals they represent, then they will be ousted in the next election.
He is urging older steelworkers to join the Steelworkers Organization of Active Retirees (SOAR).
Steelworkers over the age of 45 can join SOAR and DaPrat wants to create a voting coalition of all seniors in Sault Ste. Marie.
"I have told the SOAR president in Hamilton that it is important we start voting for our interests and not a political party," DaPrat told The Sault Star.
"We need to better ensure that our city members of Parliament and members of provincial Parliament serve the interests of the electorate, and not the party, and if they don't serve the interests of the community, then they will be gone."
During the last provincial election, the USW Local 2251 endorsed Romano and the Progressive Conservative party, after taking the position that unions shouldn't vote with ideologies because it could minimize the interest of members.
"If Mr. Romano does not act quickly and handle this matter expeditiously, he will be gone" in the next provincial election, DaPrat charges.
Romano said his focus is on the community members who do not have access to a primary care provider and want it.
"That is the only thing I am worried about and that's all I ever will be worried about," he said.
DaPrat warns that while the provincial government is issuing a message that privatized health care will provide better service, he disagrees.
"We already have a model to look at and one that had been noticed by others," he said. "Just look at our long-term care residences, which are privatized and the mess they are in with no staff, deplorable conditions and CEO's making a ton of money."
The Group Health Centre issued letters earlier this year to slightly more than 10,000 patients who will lose access to their primary care provider and same-day clinic services after May 31.
A further 6,000 patients are at risk of also being derostered if more physicians are not recruited.
The problem isn't limited to Sault Ste. Marie. The North Shore Health Network will also see two physicians leave in June, leaving about 1,500 patients orphaned. The group is hoping temporary locum funding will bridge the gap in the short-term.
Officials estimate there are about 30,000 people in the area without a primary care provider.

Subject: Health care access; Nurse practitioners; Primary care; Provincial elections; Community; Task forces; Physicians

Document 183 of 219

Program helping Northern ERs nears end

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/program-helping-northern-ers-nears-end/docview/2957825891/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Program+helping+Northern+ERs+nears+end&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A program that helps rural and Northern Ontario hospitals avoid temporary ER closures is set to expire at the end of the month, with no signal from the government as to whether it will be extended, made permanent or simply end.
Many of the province's more isolated hospitals rely on doctors from urban areas filling shifts on what is known as a locum basis, and the Temporary Locum Program pays them a premium as an incentive.
Rural and northern hospitals have said the program has helped them keep their emergency department doors open as they struggle with shortages of local doctors, but its future is uncertain after the current expiry date of March 31.
"We are getting dangerously close to that April 1st, when we'll be without that incentive for locums," said Tim Vine, CEO of North Shore Health Network, which has three sites between Sault Ste. Marie and Sudbury.
"So, we wait with great anticipation and hope that it will be continued."
The province established the COVID-19 Temporary Summer Locum Program during the pandemic, and it expired last March 31 before the government renewed it to Sept. 30, dropping the COVID-19 from the program title. Then in September the province extended it to March 31 of this year, again under a new name, the Temporary Locum Program.
The renewal announcement last spring came two months after it had actually expired, and in that interim period the lack of funding led to four temporary closures of Thessalon's ER, which was relying entirely on locum physicians due to having no primary care physician in the community.
There are now two local physicians for that site, but it is still heavily dependent on outside doctors, Vine said. Across all three North Shore sites the network is 50 per cent locum dependent, he said.
"I'm really hopeful that...there will be an extension to the program because without it, I do fear for our ability and many small, rural hospitals'ability to continue to staff our emergency department with physicians," Vine said.
The Ontario Medical Association has also stressed the importance of the program to the Ministry of Health.
"The OMA is aware of the expiry date for the most recent extension of the Temporary Locum Program and has raised this issue with the ministry," the doctors'group said in a statement.
A spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones did not indicate what will happen after March 31.
"Last fall, our government extended the Temporary Locum Program to provide support to rural and local hospitals," Hannah Jensen wrote in a statement. "All hospitals in need of physician coverage stayed open throughout the summer."
"Our government will have more to say about the future of this program in the near future."
NDP health critic France Gelinas said the government needs to come up with a permanent solution, instead of constantly renewing the program on a temporary basis at the last minute.
"I don't know why they keep doing this," she said.
"There are no Uber physicians who are just waiting for the phone to ring so they can go up north. That doesn't exist. I mean, they book their locums a long time in advance."
The province has noted that other ways it assists those hospitals include another emergency locum program, reimbursing expenses for medical residents when on clinical assignment in northern ERs, and a virtual peer-to-peer program for rural and remote ER doctors.
That other emergency locum program is meant as a backup, said Melanie Goulet, recruitment co-ordinator for health professionals at Notre-Dame Hospital in Hearst, Ont. Having something like the Temporary Locum Program in place permanently would be helpful, she said.
"It would be nice to know we have this program and we're not always scared...are we going to have it in the next month," Goulet said.
"Schedulers are already planning six months ahead so they're already way late to give answers." The Canadian Press

Subject: Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Physicians; COVID-19

Document 184 of 219

Canada's top-down control of health-care supply has 'sharply'limited foreign recruitment

Author: Chow, Peter

Publication info: Chow, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadas-top-down-control-health-care-supply-has/docview/2957825793/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+top-down+control+of+health-care+supply+has+%27sharply%27limited+foreign+recruitment&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Chow%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: So, the president of Sault Ste. Marie's largest steelworker union says the GHC de-rostering of thousands of people in this city is a conspiracy manufactured by Premier Doug Ford in an attempt to eventually privatize health care in the province.
Nice try, but the shortage of doctors is the fault of every Ontario provincial government regardless of party, not just Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
The Ford government is actually looking good compared to its predecessors with plans to increase the number of doctors coming out of Ontario's medical schools by creating a new Scarborough Academy of Medicine and the new Toronto Metropolitan University Medical School in addition to adding 260 undergraduate and 450 residency spots.
All three main political parties, and every premier in every Ontario government since the inception of Medicare in 1968 are responsible for getting us to where we are today.
As soon as government took total control of paying doctors, suddenly there were too many doing too many operations, generating too many visits, making too much money.
Zealous politicians offered incentives for physicians to retire early or used other methods to reduce physician supply, such as limiting medical school enrolment, residency training spots, and billing numbers (which doctors need in order to get paid).
The NDP government of Bob Rae (1990-1995), decided "there were too many doctors" costing the province too much and slashed medical school enrolment in Ontario by 12 per cent.
As the Ontario Medical Association warned, a massive doctor shortage (particularly in family medicine) hit the province at the end of that decade. It has taken the last 25 years to come close to correcting that and we're not there yet -we still have fewer doctors per capita than Outer Mongolia (2.89 physicians per 1,000 inhabitants compared to 2.31 in Ontario).
In one example of topdown central planning run amok, the Rae government announced it would pay the University of Toronto up to $10 million a year to not produce as many doctors. The Mike Harris Progressive Conservative government that followed Rae announced a 10 per cent "clawback" in the fees paid to Ontario doctors, the first of several clawbacks.
Kathleen Wynne's Liberal government, 2013-2018, went to war with physicians and unilaterally imposed a 2.65 per cent cut to all doctor services (on top of the 2.5 per cent cut from 2012).
The OHIP fee today paid to your family doctor is $23.75 for a one complaint visit and $37.95 for a two or more complaints.
I got a haircut yesterday and it cost me $40.
Meanwhile, private clinics are popping up in Ontario where nurse practitioners charge patients $80 for a consultation, double what OHIP pays a doctor. The Ministry of Health reimburses pharmacies $75 for a 5-minute over-the-phone review of the medications a patient is taking.
The Wynne government went ahead with a brilliant idea and cut 50 residency training positions for doctors.
In order to sell these misguided policies, the Rae government and its bureaucrats played to the politics of envy, which the Wynne government continued to their catastrophic end in 2018.Doctors were vilified as greedy, uncaring, overpaid fat cats. The Progressive Conservatives, the Liberals, the NDP -they all have played this card over and over again ever since government in Canada took over physician payment in Medicare in 1968.
Canada stands alone among developed nations in maintaining an outright ban on private health insurance for anything covered by the Canada Health Act. This is why Canadians can purchase insurance to cover emergency dental surgery, but if they get cancer they're restricted either to getting in line - or paying cash in a U.S. hospital. None of our peer countries do this. Two-tier systems are the norm everywhere else.
Private health care exists alongside public Universal Health Coverage (UHC) in all of the countries of Europe, East Asia, Israel, Japan, South Korea, Australia, New Zealand, and the UK, despite variations, with the private sector very important in some countries, and of less importance in others.
The European experiences indicate that Universal Health Coverage (UHC) can be effectively provided with large-scale private sector provision in healthcare services.
These other countries can offer regulatory models and useful insights for Canada to consider as it advances private medical clinics.
Unfortunately, many Canadians have placed Canada's Medicare, ideologically pure from private funding, on an absurdly sacred pedestal much like many Americans view Second Amendment gun ownership rights as sacrosanct.
And the same way the NRA views any gun control legislation as the thin edge of the wedge, ideological purists in Canada view any provision whatsoever of privately-funded health services as a prelude to the horrible state of healthcare in the U.S.
The only other country that is as pure ideologically is Cuba, where physicians moonlight driving taxi.
The word profit has become taboo in any discussion of healthcare in Canada.
One surgeon in the Sault wrote to Cyril Symes, the NDP representative for Sault Ste. Marie in the House of Commons of Canada from 1972 to 1980, complaining of government clawbacks on the fee schedule.
Symes answered saying he found it "personally repugnant that anyone should seek to benefit from human sickness and suffering."
Any privately funded healthcare service is seen as the sinister prelude to U.S.-style healthcare. Non-negotiable.
End of discussion. These widescale limits on private health insurance are why governments in Canada are so uptight about certifying any new doctors, because they know that the public system will have to pay for it.
The most obvious effect of this approach is that Canada maintains a quota system at the country's 17 medical schools, a "choke point" that "limits student admissions to just under 2,900 spots for prospective doctors each year."
All 17 university schools of medicine are incredibly selective. Ontario has 6 medical schools, all of which have acceptance rates of less than 10%, most under 5 per cent -Queen's University School of Medicine 1.9 per cent, NOSM University 3.5 per cent.
Each year, provincial health departments calculate how many doctors they think they'll be able to afford, and then set med school admissions accordingly. In 2018, for instance, Quebec cut medical school admissions after they determined that "too many" doctors were graduating, costing the province too much money. No other profession works this way. Provinces don't really care how many lawyers or dentists their schools are producing, since it's not the government that pays them.
But an oncologist or a cardiologist can only realistically expect to find employment through the government, which then prompts the government to train only as many as they think they can afford to pay when they graduate.
For the 2020 scholastic year, U of T Med School was only allowed to train a quota of 8 cardiologists, 4 endocrinologists and a single clinical pharmacist.
The school also had to scale back its number of graduating family doctors, with one fewer allocation in the realm of "family medicine" than the year before. And no one wants to be a family doctor anymore anyways.
Even doctors who graduate from family medicine residencies now choose to be ER docs or hospitalists, anything but doing longitudinal family medicine. It is due to these factors that medical associations have been screaming for decades that Canada's doctor shortage, especially in family doctors, was poised to get exponentially worse. Which is where we are.
Over 6.5 million Canadians, over 3 million Ontarians with no family doctor.
By 2026, 25 per cent of all Ontarians will have no family doctor.
That's 4.4 million men, women and children.
Even if government-imposed restrictions on the number of doctors being trained in Canada are immediately removed, it won't have any impact for much of the next decade given the time it takes to train a new doctor and the coming flood of retirement of baby-boomer physicians.
Importing foreign-trained doctors has helped to keep the physician shortage from becoming an even worse catastrophe, but even then, Canada's culture of top-down control of the health-care supply has sharply limited foreign recruitment. Canada is notorious for maintaining unusually onerous requirements to certify foreign doctors. At the same time, government quotas on medical residencies have been instrumental in scaring away even Canadians who have obtained medical training abroad.
Currently, almost 3,600 Canadians are studying medicine at schools in Ireland, the UK, Australia, the U.S., the Caribbean and elsewhere.
"The messaging for so long has been that it's nearly impossible to get a bloody residency in Canada if you're an international graduate," said the CEO of the Atlantic Bridge Program, a group that slots Canadians into Irish medical schools.
"You tell people to go away long enough, and eventually, they go away."
So, sadly, that's why you aren't likely to get a family doctor anytime soon in Sault Ste Marie. Dr. Peter Chow is a retired Sault Ste. Marie physician

Subject: Canadians; Medicine; School admissions; Health care policy; Canada Health Act; Medical schools; Medicare; Physicians; Health insurance

Business indexing term: Subject: Medicare Physicians Health insurance

Location: Australia; United States--US; Canada; United Kingdom--UK

Identifier / keyword: president; sault; largest; steelworker; union; thousands

Title: Canada's top-down control of health-care supply has 'sharply'limited foreign recruitment

Document 185 of 219

DR. PETER CHOW: Canada's culture of top-down control of health-care supply has 'sharply' limited foreign recruitment

Author: Sault Star

Publication info: Sault Star. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dr-peter-chow-canadas-culture-top-down-control/docview/2957740333/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Full text: So, the president of Sault Ste. Marie's largest steelworker union says the GHC de-rostering of thousands of people in this city is a conspiracy manufactured by Premier Doug Ford in an attempt to eventually privatize health care in the province.

Document 186 of 219

Safe consumption site bid still under development; Sault hopes addictions data will warrant local site

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/safe-consumption-site-bid-still-under-development/docview/2956997265/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Safe+consumption+site+bid+still+under+development%3B+Sault+hopes+addictions+data+will+warrant+local+site&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-03-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sault Ste. Marie Mayor Matthew Shoemaker is waiting patiently for the Ontario government to complete its review of safe consumption sites and reopen its application process to approve more sites.
And he's hoping that comes with a funding commitment as well.
"Frankly, to re-open the approvals process without committing funding would represent only a small step in the right direction on a crisis in which we need leaps forward," Shoemaker said.
Premier Doug Ford told reporters during an unrelated press conference in Essex, Ont., earlier this week that the review of sites is almost complete, but he stressed the Ontario government plans to keep its safe consumption site cap at 21. There are currently 17 sites approved, although those in Northern Ontario are struggling to keep their doors open because they have not received provincial funding.
Timmins, for instance, has been funding its own site for more than a year, and that funding has now been turned over to the Timmins and District Hospital, which says it can't continue supporting the site beyond the end of March.
Simply put, municipalities don't have the resources or the mandate to fund health care, so there isn't a readily apparent stable funding source the city can access to operate a safe consumption site, Shoemaker said in an emailed response to The Sault Star. "We've seen safe consumption sites in communities such as Sudbury and Timmins struggle to maintain operations without provincial funding. "Shoemaker vows that Sault Ste.
Marie "will submit a fulsome application developed with the support of community partners and containing a plan to ensure the development of a site that will operate in a safe and responsible manner, including the availability of wraparound services to help people break the cycle of addiction," he said.
A local stakeholder group is working through a process to submit an application to the province, said CAO Tom Vair.
"We have made some progress with organization and our partners to advance the discussions, but it is still in the development at this stage," Vair said.
A timeline to submit the application hasn't been finalized and it is yet to be determined who would operate the facility.
"Our hope certainly is that given our statistics, Sault Ste. Marie would be a contender for one of those spots," Vair said of four spots still available under the government's cap to permit 21 consumption sites.
Sault Ste. Marie, like so many other communities across Ontario and the country, is struggling with addictions.
Shoemaker said the city has made its concerns well known to the provincial government -repeatedly.
In fact, there have been 10 letters to the province from the city asking for support for the mental health and addictions crisis.
The City of Sault Ste. Marie has asked the government to reopen the approval process for safe consumption sites and commit to adequate funding for those sites.
"The provincial government recently announced temporary funding for the Concurrent Disorders Intensive Day Treatment Program at the Sault Area Hospital and we're asking them to provide the dedicated base funding required for the hospital to offer the program on a permanent basis," he said. That program was suspended for a 30-month period due to lack of funding while the community continued to struggle with mental health and addictions challenges.
"The Northern Ontario School of Medicine University has the potential to develop programming that could provide badly needed care and support to community members dealing with mental health and addictions related issues, but their work is jeopardized without increased, stable funding from the province," Shoemaker said.
Data from the Office of the Chief Coroner released earlier this year show Sault Ste. Marie has higher than provincial average rates for opioid-related deaths.
The third quarter results for 2023 shows Sault Ste. Marie has twice the provincial rate for opioid-related emergency department visits, three times the provincial rate for hospitalizations and is slightly above (0.6 times) the provincial rate for deaths.
Shoemaker said he believes a safe consumption site could reduce some of those deaths and visits to hospital emergency rooms, as well as provide wrap-around services that could point users toward the help they require.
Statistics from Sudbury and Thunder Bay - communities with safe consumption sites - show decreases in emergency department visits and hospitalizations.
Shoemaker has also previously noted that the addiction and mental health problem is more challenging in Northern Ontario because the region receives fewer funding dollars and has fewer programs than its southern Ontario counterparts.
The Canadian Drug Policy Coalition has also got on board to advocate for more safe consumption sites and provincial funding.
It has penned an open letter to Health Minister Sylvia Jones and associate Minister of Mental Health and Addictions Michael Tibollo calling for emergency funding for safe consumption sites who are risking closure due to lack of funding, among other things.
"The tragedy of an isolated instance of gun violence in Toronto must not prevent people in diverse locations across the province from accessing vital health services any longer. The Ontario government's decision to stop processing applications altogether for more than seven months is punitive and irresponsible," the letter reads.
The letter references high mortality rates, including those in the Northern communities of Timmins and Sudbury where funding applications are at a standstill.
The letter requests immediate emergency funding to supervised consumption sites that risk closure, continued finding for sites and the removal of the camp on the number of funded supervised consumption sites across Ontario, among other things.

Subject: Emergency medical care; Community; Mental health; Addictions; Funding; Northern communities; Narcotics

Identifier / keyword: sault; marie; mayor; matthew; shoemaker; waiting

Title: Safe consumption site bid still under development; Sault hopes addictions data will warrant local site

Publication title: Sault Star; Sault Ste. Marie, Ont.

First page: A.1

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Mar 14, 2024

Section: Local

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Sault Ste. Marie, Ont.

Country of publication: Cana da

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 0839203X

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 2956997265

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/safe-consumption-site-bid-still-under-development/docview/2956997265/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 188 of 219

Liberals at Queen's Park show ignorance on guns and justice

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-at-queens-park-show-ignorance-on-guns/docview/2955976510/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+at+Queen%27s+Park+show+ignorance+on+guns+and+justice&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you ever needed proof that Liberals don't understand gun crime, gun control or gun policy beyond using it to scare people, Stephen Blais is walking proof.
The MPP for Orleans in Ottawa stood up to try and make a point this week on guns and judges getting tough on crime and utterly failed.
The Liberals at Queen's Park have been trying to make hay out of the fact that Premier Doug Ford appointed two people who used to work for him to the panel that vets lawyers seeking judicial appointments. Imagine that, the party that won the election appointing people who philosophically agree with them to advisory roles in the government.
This is a complete non-scandal because not only does every party do this, but they should do this. You advance the agenda you were elected on by relying on those who agree with you to get things done.
No matter that the previous Ontario Liberal government did this or that the federal Liberal government that Blais supports does this, he and his party want to make this an issue.
On Wednesday though, Blais decided to go a step further and point out that Matt Bondy, Ford's former deputy chief of staff who is now chair of the judicial appointments advisory committee, is also registered to lobby for a gun manufacturer.
"How can we believe the premier's tough-oncrime stance when he's asked the guy who sells the guns to appoint the judges?" Blais asked during question period.
And with that, he showed everyone that he knows absolutely nothing about guns.
Bondy is indeed a lobbyist for a gun manufacturer, a company out of Kitchener called Colt Canada that only makes and sells weapons to the Canadian military and law enforcement. In fact, Colt's only reason to exist with their facility in Kitchener is so that Canada has a secure domestic supply of rifles and other small arms for the Canadian military.
If Blais walked into his local gun store, he wouldn't be able to buy anything from Colt Canada. They make some fantastic firearms, but they do not sell into the retail market.
Nor are they guns found in the hands of criminals on the streets of Ottawa. No one is doing a drug deal with a C7 rifle, a C20 sniper rifle or Colt's EAGLE single-shot grenade launcher.
These are products designed for and sold to the Canadian military and police services across the country.
Colt produces the guns used by the guys who arrest the bad guys with guns. But why let the truth get in the way of scaring people? Doug Ford has expressed his frustration with judges and justices of the peace giving light sentences or letting repeat violent gun offenders out on bail for years now. This isn't a new thing for him, he's been saying this since before he was elected premier.
But now that he's appointed two people who used to work with him, and who agree with his judicial philosophy, to the panel that screens judges and the Liberals, the NDP and much of the media are losing their collectivist little minds.
Why? It's not because Liberals don't appoint like-minded people when they are in office, it's that they do it and claim they are all being non-partisan and neutral when in reality, they aren't. Ford is being upfront in what he wants.
"I'm proud to go out there and tell the people who are seeing violence in their homes, violence on the streets, violence in our subways, that we're going to get judges that are actually going to keep these criminals in jail," Ford said in response to Blais'bizarre question.
Ask the public what they care about, it isn't lobbyists, it's keeping criminals behind bars. That's something Blais'Liberal Party has been against for years. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Violence; Judicial appointments; Firearms

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: needed; proof; liberals; understand; crime; control

Title: Liberals at Queen's Park show ignorance on guns and justice
