Document 189 of 219

Health ministry won't say if it will meet to discuss urgent funding to overdose prevention site

Author: Stoffman, Nicole

Publication info: Stoffman, Nicole. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/health-ministry-wont-say-if-will-meet-discuss/docview/2955976469/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Health+ministry+won%27t+say+if+it+will+meet+to+discuss+urgent+funding+to+overdose+prevention+site&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Stoffman%2C+Nicole&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Minister of Health's office would not confirm if they would meet with the Canadian Drug Policy Coalition following the coalition's open letter calling for emergency funding to supervised consumption sites in the province.
Safe Health Site Timmins, a supervised consumption site which admitted 19 people who use drugs to treatment, reversed 130 overdoses in its first year of operations, and kept over 12,000 needles off the streets, will have to close at the end of the month without provincial funding.
The 51 signatories to the letter included four from Timmins; Mayor Michelle Boileau, Julie Nobert-DeMarchi, Executive Director, Timmins & Area Women in Crisis, Jason Sereda, President, Board of Directors, DIY Community Health Timmins, and Dr. Julie Samson, Co-lead of the addiction medicine consult service, Timmins and District Hospital.
Instead, a spokesperson for Minister of Health Sylvia Jones pointed The Daily Press to the 204 new addiction treatment beds in the North the government is funding through their Addictions Recovery Fund, and an overall five per cent increase in base funding for all community based mental health and addictions programs.
Hannah Jensen also pointed to $525 million the government has spent in Timmins on a range of addiction treatment services such as mobile crisis response teams, a rapid access addiction medicine clinic, safe beds, opioid agonist therapy, a structured psychotherapy program and the Cochrane-Temiskaming youth wellness hub.
Supervised consumption sites allow people to consume dangerous, illegal drugs with clean needles and under supervision of a health professional, reducing the risk of overdose and infection.
Because these sites are 'low-barrier'(easy to walk to with a simple intake process), evidence shows they help connect drug users to other health services and referrals.
Deaths by overdose plummeted during the site's first year of operations.
In combination with other harm reduction and treatment services offered in Timmins, deaths by overdose dropped from 40 in 2020 and 2021 to 22 in 2022.
The city has spent over $1.5 million to fund the temporary site on the understanding that once approved as a permanent site by Health Canada (granted Sept. 29, 23), it would qualify for provincial funds on an ongoing basis.
That plan was abruptly halted when, seven months ago, the province paused all approvals for permanent consumption sites following the death of Karolina Huebner-Makurat from a stray bullet outside of a supervised consumption site in Toronto.
Sudbury's supervised consumption site, The Spot, will also be forced to close at the end of March.
In its first year of operations, 256 people accessed The Spot representing 543 total visits, and 706 total consumptions of fentanyl, speedballs or methamphetamine. The Spot reversed a total of nine overdoses, none of which required emergency medical services.
"The decision this government has taken means two people a week will die in my community," said Sudbury's MPP France Gélinas (MPP Nickel Belt), and Opposition health critic with the NDP.
"The 51 people who wrote and endorsed that letter are ready to sit down ASAP with this government and explain why these centres are going to save lives. We have strong data and evidence to support the important role of supervised consumption sites," she wrote in an emailed response to a request for comment on March 7.
Gélinas said the province could fund the sites out of their $5.1 billion contingency fund for emergencies.
The Financial Accountability Office of Ontario shared the total amount of the fund in a March 6 report.
"The FAO report does not take into account the full project expenditure and is only a snapshot in time," said Hannah Jensen, spokesperson for Sylvia Jones, minister of health, when asked in an email if the minister would consider using this fund to keep Safe Health Site Timmins open.
"Following the tragic incident in July at the Riverdale CTS site, the ministry launched a review of all 17 provincial CTS sites and a third-party review led by Unity Health of the Riverdale site," Jenson wrote, referring to the acronym for Consumption Treatment Services, or permanent consumption sites.
"These reviews include consulting with Public Health, community engagement and reviewing complaints against the CTS sites. These reviews remain ongoing and will inform the next steps taken by the Ministry of Health including funding, location and application decisions. All applications remain on pause."
The main complaint Ward 3 Councillor Bill Gvozdanovic hears form residents is about discarded needles. He is concerned the site has increased the number of needles in the neighbourhood and beyond.
"My main concern is that the original plan is not working," he said, adding that Timmins asked for provincial funding long before the Toronto shooting took place. "They're saying 366 people used the injection site, but they're not telling us, based on the number of needles distributed, how many have not (used the site for consumption). And that's your data."
"I want the province to come up with a strategy that is going to help people get away from using but also keep in mind of the detriments to the community," Gvozdanovic said.
He has written a letter to Michael Tibollo, associate minister of mental health and addictions and Timmins MPP George Pirie asking them to consider each community separately.
The city has estimated it would cost $91,000 for one full-time staff to take over needle collection in town. Currently, the task is divided between the Living Space, the city's public works department, and the Porcupine Health Unit.
Over a 9-month period in 2023, 2,300 needles were collected by the Living Space and public works. That number is higher as the health unit did not have numbers available for Chief Administrative Officer Dave Landers'report to council on Dec. 12.
The PHU did not respond to an emailed request to share the number of needles collected.
A resolution is expected to go back to council late March confirming next steps for the city to take over the cleanup of needles.
In its first year of operations from July 4, 2022 to June 30, 2023 Safe Health Site Timmins welcomed 24,168 visits from 366 people.

Subject: Drug overdose; Mental health; Addictions; Funding; Substance abuse treatment

Identifier / keyword: minister; office; confirm; canadian; policy; coalition

Title: Health ministry won't say if it will meet to discuss urgent funding to overdose prevention site

Publication title: Sault Star; Sault Ste. Marie, Ont.

First page: A.5

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Mar 12, 2024

Section: Local

Document 190 of 219

Transit electrifies fleet; Funding of $7.8M helps

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/transit-electrifies-fleet-funding-7-8m-helps/docview/2954420477/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Transit+electrifies+fleet%3B+Funding+of+%247.8M+helps&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-03-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sault Ste. Marie Transit Services is getting about $7.8 million to upgrade and modernize its fleet to environmentally friendly buses.
It's believed the city will be one of the first - if not the first - in Northern Ontario to purchase new electric buses.
The funding is a joint commitment from the federal, provincial and municipal governments.
The federal government's Public Transit Infrastructure Stream will contribute $3,104,040, with the province earmarking $2,586,442. The city will contribute $2,090,173.
The funding will be used to purchase new vehicles, including electric buses and a charging station, conventional buses, paratransit buses and passenger vans.
The new fleet will help reduce air pollution, reduce fuel usage and cut maintenance costs.
Sault MP Terry Sheehan, MPP Ross Romano and Mayor Matthew Shoemaker were on hand for the joint announcement Friday at the Huron Street transit office.
"Modern and sustainable investment is very important because it also reduces powerful emissions, increases productivity and supports the supply chains and propels Canada forward in the clean, mean economy," Sheehan said.
Transit is critical to help residents and tourists get around and is vital to any community, he said.
Sheehan said transit infrastructure improvements have been a priority for the federal government since the Liberals took office in 2015 and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau made his first local infrastructure announcement at the transit facility.
Romano said the funding commitment is "a great announcement for our community" which will help provide safe, reliable transit.
"As we look at the electrification of our economy and the greening of it, this is a great environmental initiative to make these types of investment which are critical," he said.
"Getting people to places is critical. We are seeing a change of demographics here in our community and with the growth of our community, it is becoming more imperative every day that people can get to places safely and more effectively."
Romano said he's pleased the government can help the Sault dedicate more dollars to its infrastructure.
In addition, he said, the spending and new buses will help the economy, the modernization of the fleet and be better for the environment.
Shoemaker called public transit one of the most important services the city offers.
"This is going to improve our transit services in our community," he said, along with the lives of people in Sault Ste. Marie.
Communities are no longer self-contained neighbourhoods and as the Sault continues to grow geographically, it spreads out, and transit becomes more important to many, Shoemaker said.
While the cost of electric buses are more expensive at an estimated $1.2 million, director of community services Brent Lamming said a procurement process hasn't begun yet, so the exact amount of each unit is not yet known.
The cost savings will be achieved on the operational and maintenance side, he said.
The procurement of an electric bus will be the first for the Sault, and Lamming said starting slowly with one or two buses will allow transit services to properly train its staff and determine how the new buses operate, especially during the winter.
"We'll get that first unit on board, use it as a training bus and then have it deployed on the road and see how the charging station accommodates one or two (buses) before we ramp up," Lamming said.
He said there will be some thought given to when the first electric bus will be deployed, when it will be charged and whether it should be used on its busier or less frequently travelled routes.
"We will take a cautious approach and make sure we're not 100 per cent reliant on the electric bus, but we do want to introduce the technology and we've had some success already with the electric ice resurfacers at the Northern Community Centre," he said.
Romano said technology is constantly advancing.
"I think it is an improvement in technology and it will continue to improve and that is something we want to be able to lean into as we move forward into the future," he said.
Sheehan said he puts his trust in the industry experts and believes the buses will have the technology to operate in all weather conditions.
Shoemaker added he expects the transition to be gradual and he believes there will always be a need to have some traditional buses as part of the fleet to ensure buses can stay on the road.
Investment in parabuses is also important and the city sees an average of 40,000 annual bookings, he said.
Romano said that it shows our community is aging and the need for the city to provide accessible transit is important to help people get to where they want to go.
Sheehan agreed the community is aging and the parabus plays an extremely important role to transport people.
"We can be very proud as a community about where we are," he said.
Shoemaker said the buses are critical to the community, especially for those with mobility or accessibility issues.
"It's a growing need and I suspect it will continue to grow for the next 15 to 20 years," he said.
The first electric bus isn't expected to arrive in the Sault until late this year.

Document 191 of 219

Universities, colleges face hard choices

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/universities-colleges-face-hard-choices/docview/3126978115/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Universities%2C+colleges+face+hard+choices&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The good news for parents and students: Tuition at Ontario's colleges and universities won't be going up for the next three years. The bad news for the province's universities and colleges is they won't be getting as much money as they hoped for.
Minister Jill Dunlop announced this week the government of Doug Ford was putting $1.3 billion in new funding into the post-secondary education sector.
Questioned time and again about other issues, Dunlop repeated the government was investing $1.3 billion. It was as if she thought the journalists present hadn't heard her many pronouncements on the matter previously.
The news of the investment comes after a blue-ribbon panel appointed by the Ford government suggested last November tuition should go up, and urged the province to increase funding by $2.5 billion during three years. Instead, the province is offering no tuition increase and just $1.3 billion.
Opposition parties were quick to denounce the move as not enough.
"What has been promised today by the government comes nowhere close to what the blue-ribbon panel recommended was needed," NDP critic Peggy Sattler said.
Yet, while denouncing the government for not following the blue-ribbon panel, Sattler was quick to endorse the tuition freeze.
"We absolutely support a tuition freeze. Students are suffering from the cost-ofliving crisis the same way that everyone else in Ontario is," she said.
Liberal critic Adil Shamji made similar complaints, that the government didn't listen to its own panel on increased funding, while also saying he supports the tuition freeze the blue-ribbon panel opposed. That panel had called for a five per cent tuition hike starting in September, followed by automatic tuition hikes of two per cent a year afterward.
That wouldn't help anyone deal with affordability issues.
"I will not apologize for continuing to freeze tuition in this province," Dunlop said. "Let me take you back to 2019, when Ontario had the highest tuition in all of Canada."
Now, three provinces have higher university tuition than Ontario, while seven provinces have higher college tuition.
Governing is about choices, and Ford has decided raising tuition rates right now isn't the best option. Could he have given the universities and colleges more money? Obviously, he could have, and Sattler made a solid argument that if the province is going to freeze tuition, they need to offer more subsidies.
But here we are, back to choices, and is giving colleges and universities ever-increasing amounts of money a good thing? It is worth asking whether we need to keep every single university and college in this province. In 2022-23, seven of Ontario's 24 colleges had more international students, paying higher fees, than they had domestic students.
That included Sarnia's Lambton College with 82 per cent international students, Northern College in Timmins with about 80 per cent, North Bay's Canadore College at 72 per cent and Conestoga College in Kitchener-Waterloo at 71 per cent. This is part of a pattern, that despite NDP and Liberal claims, isn't all Doug Ford's fault; it started about a decade ago and really took off in around 2016.
There is no doubt the Ford government has let this get out of control. Not that Dunlop would admit that Monday; in fact, she dodged question after question, putting all the blame on Ottawa.
The reality is they are both to blame, but so are the schools which became dependent on the higher tuition paid by foreign students and now want the government at Queen's Park to bail them out.
That shouldn't happen. Governing is about choices; so, too, is running a major college or university.
Ford has given families what they need. He's given the colleges and universities some of what they asked for, but not all of it. It's time for those in charge of these institutions to start making hard choices.
It won't be pretty, but it is necessary. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 192 of 219

Mantha 'disappointed' bill defeated; Romano wants 'decisive' action

Author: Darbyson, Kyle

Publication info: Darbyson, Kyle. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mantha-disappointed-bill-defeated-romano-wants/docview/3126976270/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mantha+%27disappointed%27+bill+defeated%3B+Romano+wants+%27decisive%27+action&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Darbyson%2C+Kyle&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Algoma-Manitoulin MPP Michael Mantha's quest to pass Bill 13, the Northern Health Travel Grant Advisory Committee Act, came to a swift end Monday after it was defeated at second reading.
Mantha expressed his disappointment in a news release that was circulated shortly after the result was finalized.
"I want to thank my colleagues who supported this bill to help Northerners seeking access to medical treatment," wrote Mantha, who currently sits as an independent.
"I am disappointed in the Progressive Conservative caucus for voting against this measure to improve the lives of Northern Ontarians."
A breakdown provided on the Legislative Assembly's website indicates that Sault Ste. Marie MPP Ross Romano was one of the 73 who voted against Bill 13 on Monday.
"This proposed legislation seeks to establish yet another advisory committee to have further discussions," Romano wrote in an email to The Sault Star early Tuesday afternoon.
"I believe we need to focus on decisive action to improve the health-care system for Northern families."
Bill 13, if passed, would have formed a committee to review the Northern Health Travel Grant Program and recommend changes to the Ministry of Health.
The Northern Health Travel Grant was first established in 1985 to assist patients living in Northern Ontario who need to travel more than 100 kilometres for various medical treatments.
While the program's reimbursement rates have been subject to increases over the last four decades, Mantha said he believes the current travel grants are insufficient given the recent spike in prices for gas, food and general accommodations.
Mantha reiterated these points when Bill 13 was debated in the Legislative Assembly on Feb. 22 and received support from a couple other Northern MPPs in the process.
Lise Vaugeois (Thunder Bay-Superior North) said the inadequacies of the Northern Health Travel Grant Program keep the people in her office "very, very busy," especially when it comes to accommodating patients who must travel over 1,000 kilometres to Toronto to receive specialized care.
"The amount for kilometres is 41 cents a kilometre. It hasn't been adjusted since 2007," Vaugeois said.
"I think the hotel amount was adjusted in 2017, but there are very few places-I believe the hotel amount is ... $100 a night but only to a maximum of $500. We know it costs an awful lot more than that to stay in a hotel these days."
Sudbury MPP Jamie West also stood in support of Bill 13 last week, providing multiple examples of how he's had to help constituents navigate the bureaucratic pitfalls of the current Northern Health Travel Grant Program.
Currently, West said one of his constituents still hasn't received word on the status of his travel grant after three months. Meanwhile, another man was reportedly forced to wait more than a year to resolve an appeal after his travel grant was denied.
"This is a broken system, Speaker. We need to address it," West said in the legislature.
"The member for Algoma-Manitoulin has a very simple solution, about putting our heads together, working together, which I think the people of Ontario want us to do, where we can address this, fix this and make health care better for people across Northern Ontario."
Despite this testimony, Progressive Conservatives voiced their opposition to Bill 13 on Feb. 22, claiming that the provincial government is already doing enough to close these gaps in service for Northern residents.
MPP Robin Martin (Eglinton- Lawrence) spoke glowingly about the recent accomplishments of the Northern Health Travel Grant Program, like how the Ministry of Health allocated nearly $45 million in financial travel assistance throughout 2022-23 and has set aside over $48 million for 2023-24.
Martin also mentioned how the province is attempting to strengthen Northern health care through other efforts, such as expanding the Ornge air ambulance fixed-wing fleet from eight to 12 aircraft.
"While the opposition and independent members continue to be all talk or all about talk, our government is busy getting it done," Martin said.
Dawn Gallagher Murphy (Newmarket-Aurora) expanded on her Tory colleague's argument by bringing up the fact that the government, last year, approved three more Ontario health teams to serve Northern communities surrounding Cochrane, Sudbury and Timiskaming.
"Working together, these teams break down barriers, connecting people to care by ensuring a seamless transition from one provider to another, with one patient record and one care plan being shared between the providers," Murphy said.
Mantha introduced Bill 13 for its first reading back in the summer of 2022 and visited the Espanola Regional Hospital and Health Centre two weeks ago to raise public awareness for a second reading.
While Mantha was disappointed that the vote didn't go his way, he vowed to "keep fighting for access to health care in the North" through different channels.
"Whether it is the (Northern Health Travel Grant), our public hospitals or home care, Northerners deserve the same services and care as everyone else in our province," he wrote in a release. kdarbyson@postmedia.com The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government

Document 193 of 219

Pharmacare a scheme to keep power

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pharmacare-scheme-keep-power/docview/2933305873/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pharmacare+a+scheme+to+keep+power&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is pharmacare doomed to fail? Last week, Quebec announced it would opt out of the Liberal-NDP plan for a national pharmacare program. This week, it was Alberta's turn. Health Minister Adriana LaGrange blasted the lack of provincial consultation and accused the federal government of overstepping its jurisdiction.
"We're not going to allow Ottawa to pick and choose what coverage should be available to Albertans based on issues they find politically palatable."
Instead of taking orders from Ottawa, Alberta will take the cash.
"Give us the dollars," LaGrange said. "Allow us to enhance the programs we actually have now, rather than create more bureaucracy."
In response, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh attacked Quebec Premier François Legault and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith.
"I'm not surprised at the Conservative governments that have cut investments in health care. These are the same premiers that, when you look at their province, they made massive cuts that hurt workers and patients."
Ah, the C-word. Never mind that Legault's government doesn't go by that name: it's a convenient excuse to attack the ideology, and by extension the federal Conservatives as well. Singh is eager to savour his pharmacare "victory" and position the NDP as the true alternative to the Tories. And since he has as much a chance of gaining seats in Quebec as he does in Alberta, bashing those two governments is a low-risk strategy.
But the NDP aren't the only ones playing politics with this deal. The Liberals are as well, starting with the choice of contraceptive and diabetes medication as the first drugs to be covered.
Contraception is a relatively low-cost item for the feds: it's already fully covered in B.C. and Manitoba, and partly covered in Ontario. Most private health plans - which four in five Canadians have - pay for it as well. More importantly, the coverage appeals to a key voter demographic - women - the Liberals need to get back. The latest polls show that 36 per cent would vote Conservative, compared to 26 per cent who would vote Liberal.
As for diabetes, that is also a numbers game.
Nearly 10 per cent of Canadians - six million people - suffer from the disease, with an additional six million classified as "pre-diabetic." Rates are even higher among seniors (20 per cent) and First Nations populations on reserve (17 per cent). Almost everyone knows someone with diabetes and knows that insulin is a lifesaver.
But that doesn't mean you need universal pharmacare to cover people who can't afford it. It's like bringing in a $27-billion dollar bazooka to kill a fly. A study released last week by the Montreal Economic Institute found only 2.8 per cent of Canadians currently have no drug coverage whatsoever. Twenty-five million people have private insurance, and existing provincial plans subsidize or cover a host of medications for persons who do not, including low-income patients and seniors.
The study also confirmed that imposing a single-payer universal drug insurance plan would jeopardize the quality of coverage for at least 21.5 million people. That's because a single-payer model would not fund many medications currently covered by private plans, including innovative medicines.
Based on the experiences of New Zealand, cutting-edge drugs won't be introduced into the Canadian market at all, because a universal plan would deem them too costly. A 2019 report commissioned by Medicines New Zealand ranked the country last for access to medicines and pharmaceutical investment among 20 OECD nations. Of 304 medicines introduced between 2011 and 2017, New Zealand funded only 17. Life-saving cancer therapies and other innovative medicines were rendered unavailable.
In other words, national single-payer pharmacare will consign Canadians to the same problems as national single-payer medicare: lack of choice, rationing of care and worse outcomes. Instead of keeping what works and fixing what's broken, the NDP chose to score political points, and the Liberals bought themselves another year in power.
So kudos to Alberta and Quebec for standing up and opting out. Let's hope other provinces join them and expose this deal for what it is: a political game that leaves patients in the cold. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Diabetes; Canadians; Indigent care

Location: New Zealand; Canada; Quebec Canada

Identifier / keyword: pharmacare; doomed; quebec; announced; national; program

Document 194 of 219

City wants help with coffers; Province not covering all costs

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-wants-help-with-coffers-province-not/docview/2931245233/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+wants+help+with+coffers%3B+Province+not+covering+all+costs&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: City council wants the provincial government to do a comprehensive social and economic prosperity review to promote the stability and sustainability of municipal coffers.
Ward 2 Coun. Luke Dufour's motion noted that one-third of municipal spending in Ontario is for services in areas of provincial responsibility. Expenditures outpace provincial contributions by nearly $4 billion per year.
In Sault Ste. Marie, the municipality receives $17.88 million from the Ontario Municipal Partnership Fund, but the District Social Services Administration Board's annual levy was $20.74 million. The city was short nearly $3 million of program funding downloaded by the province.
"This cuts to the core of our conversation around this table, especially around budget time," Dufour said.
He said council understands the constraints of its budget and its reliance on property taxes and the cash-strapped taxpayers.
Municipal governments have become laser focused on saving money, but there is no feasible way property taxes can pay for the services a community needs, Dufour says.
"Our property taxes are not going to solve the mental health and addictions crisis," he said. "There is no way our property taxes are going to pay for the infrastructure growth needed to accommodate the new residents our community is expected to have over the next five or 10 years."
Dufour and city council are calling on the province to work with cities and the Association of Municipalities of Ontario to examine existing funding formulas.
AMO's 2024 pre-budget submission, Social and Economic Prosperity Review, calls on the province to sit down with municipalities and work together on a joint study of municipal finance. AMO also wants a detailed analysis of Ontario's infrastructure investment and service delivery needs. It is encouraging councils to pass resolutions urging the province to commit to the review.
Dufour's motion notes that municipalities are continuing to struggle with unprecedented population and housing growth, infrastructure challenges, health and social problems like homelessness and addressing mental health and addictions.
Inflation, rising interest rates and provincial policy decisions are also constraining municipal fiscal capacity.
The challenges are not new, Dufour noted. The long-term issue has been snowballing.
The last fiscal review was done in 2008 and examined the provincial downloaded services and related funding.
It's time for another probe, Dufour told council. He suggests the Ontario government may be amenable to talking in order to deal with issues, like housing and growth.
Dufour wants the City of Sault Ste. Marie to call on the province to have a conversation that he calls critical to the community.
"Our tax budget cannot sustain critical increases and we need this conversation," he said. "It's a longterm issue."
Ward 1 Coun. Sandra Hollingsworth said other municipalities have more revenue generating tools, an inequality and disparity that is noted in Northern communities.
"Our tools to generate revenue are so limited, but the downloads continue," she said.
Mayor Matthew Shoemaker agreed, noting the city's shortfall between the funding it receives and the downloaded provincial programs it must pay for.
"That's why the province is in good fiscal shape," he said. "It is pushing its responsibilities to the municipalities with downloaded services."
Ward 3 Coun. Angela Caputo took aim at Premier Doug Ford, who recently said that politicians should help Ontario residents and need to stop gouging them for taxes.
Caputo notes that's an interesting perspective considering it is the province that is downloading services to the municipality, forcing it to collect more taxes to pay for the services that Ontarians need.
"I was very disappointed to hear that from the premier," she said. "When he talks about politicians, he should recognize that he is a politician who needs to collect funds from folks here in Ontario to keep things going."
Caputo said it is difficult for voters to recognize what services are the responsibility of each level of government. She's hoping the resolution would help clear up misconceptions.
City council unanimously passed the resolution on Monday.

Subject: Municipal government; Mental health; Infrastructure; Councils; Funding; Politics; Municipal finance; Property taxes; Municipalities; Northern communities

Document 195 of 219

Ghc Needs Help: Ndp; Ministry touts NOSM gains

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ghc-needs-help-ndp-ministry-touts-nosm-gains/docview/2930370946/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ghc+Needs+Help%3A+Ndp%3B+Ministry+touts+NOSM+gains&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Leader of the Official Opposition is standing with the United Steelworkers and calling on the government to provide funding and more primary care providers to solve "an urgent and very scary" health care crisis in the North.
Marit Stiles and Nickel Belt MPP and Health Critic France Gelinas said the Conservatives and Premier Doug Ford have failed to address the doctor and nurse shortages in Northern Ontario and Sault Ste. Marie is the latest victim.
In January, the Group Health Centre announced the derostering of 10,000 patients because primary care physicians left their practices or retired and replacements have not been found.
"This lack of access to primary care is not a new problem in the North. New Democrats have been sounding the alarm about this growing gap for years," Stiles said at a Queen's Park media event on Wednesday. "This storm did not arrive yesterday."
She said many seniors and vulnerable people will pay the price for the years of inaction.
Stiles is calling on Ford to tell Saultites how the Ontario government will ensure there is access to primary care close to home.
"It's a basic service of the government and it is a right for all Canadians," she said.
Ministry of Health spokesperson Hannah Jensen said the ministry was notified by the GHC about the issue before it was publicly announced and has worked with the centre to find a solution.
"Finding an immediate replacement for these patients on such short notice was not achievable," she said in an emailed response to The Sault Star.
Sault MPP Ross Romano announced earlier this month the Ontario government was spending $1.1 million to expand Maamwesying North Shore Community Health Services and the Sault Community Health Centre to provide primary care for about 4,000 additional patients.
"This expansion builds on our progress which also includes launching the largest medical school expansion in over a decade, with over 100 new residency seats at the Northern Ontario School of Medicine alone and expanding the Northern Ontario Resident Streamlined Training and Reimbursement (Nor-Star) program," Jensen said.
Gelinas called the GHC "a gem in Northern Ontario" that was created by the United Steelworkers to ensure its members had access to health care.
She said there are specific solutions that will help solve the issue. The GHC has requested resources for many years, but those pleas have gone unanswered.
It is estimated that 2.2 million Ontarians don't have access to a primary care physician.
In Sault Ste. Marie, estimates of orphaned patients hover around the 35,000 mark once the derostering notices become effective May 31, stakeholders have said.
"We have a government who refuses to fund primary care in Northern Ontario," Gelinas said. "We need a government who cares and treats Northerners as equal partners."
She doesn't blame the GHC.
Instead, she's calling on the government to take action before the May 31 deadline so the letters can be rescinded.
United Steelworks Union Local 2251 President Mike DaPrat said unionized employees earmarked $800,000 to develop the GHC. Ultimately 6,000 enrollees provided $135 each to create the health-care centre, which grew with the creation of OHIP.
The issue, DaPrat said, began in 2012 when the Ontario government changed the funding and governance agreement with the GHC.
"Both governments are to blame here, both the Liberals and the Conservatives," he said.
The GHC, through an agreement with the Ministry of Health and The Algoma District Medical Group, receives funding for every patient enrolled at its facility. The funding covers physician salaries and a portion flows through to the GHC to support physician practice. The model reduced an administrative burden for physicians, said GHC communications manager Giordan Zin.
DaPrat said steelworkers never intended to be assigned to a specific doctor and be discarded once that physician retired or left. The purpose was to have continual care.
He wants to see the federal and provincial government work together to guarantee a path of citizenship for immigrants who choose to work in isolated communities in the north.
"We align more with the isolated communities where a lot of doctors don't want to come and stay," DaPrat said. "We need to be attractive, and the ministry is suppose to oversee health, not be a bureaucratic organization that doesn't care and has a one-size-fits-all solution for health care."
DaPrat said the union is meeting with the GHC to discuss the governance model and if a satisfactory conclusion isn't reached, Local 2251 has retained legal assistance to examine its options.
Gelinas said there are healthcare professionals who can work in Sault Ste. Marie, including unemployed nurse practitioners who are opening private clinics because they can't find jobs that receive government funding.
"They have the knowledge and skills to provide primary care to people in need, but the government refuses to fund them. People's health and lives are being put at risk due to this government's failure. Where are they expected to go for their prescription renewal or chronic disease management? What happens when they have a bad fall? This is unacceptable," she said.
Gelinas said it's time for Ontario to expand its funding to include many disciplines that can provide all facets of health care, from dieticians to physiotherapists and mental health workers.
"The Group Health Centre has been asking for money for this for a long time," she said.
She called the GHC model "too precious to lose" and one that is "worth the investment."
Jensen said the ministry will continue to work with partners in Sault Ste. Marie and across Ontario to ensure primary care is available to people where and when they need it.

Subject: Health care access; Primary care; Funding

Company / organization: Name: United Steelworkers of America; NAICS: 813930

Document 196 of 219

Today in history

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/today-history/docview/2928748256/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today+in+history&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In 1959, Prime Minister John Diefenbaker cancelled the Avro Arrow jet program. The decision resulted in the layoff of nearly 14,000 people at the Avro plant at Malton, Ont. Developed by A.V. Roe Canada Ltd. (commonly known as Avro Canada), the Arrow (also known as the CF-105) was an advanced, allweather supersonic interceptor jet.
In 1962, astronaut John Glenn became the first American to orbit Earth after blasting off aboard the "Friendship 7" Mercury capsule.
He completed three orbits. In 1980, U.S. President Jimmy Carter formally advised the Soviet Union the U.S. would boycott the Summer Olympics in Moscow. Canada also boycotted the Games. In 1985, the first U.S. cruise missile test took place in Canadian airspace. The unarmed missile was released from a B-52 bomber over the Beaufort Sea. The self-propelled projectile took less than an hour to reach the Primrose Weapons Range in northern Alberta.
In 1992, painter A.J. Casson, who immortalized small Ontario towns in watercolour, died in Toronto at age 93. He was the last surviving member of the Group of Seven artists. In 1993, after 21 years as an MP, Joe Clark announced he would not seek re-election. The constitutional affairs minister led the Tories for seven years and was prime minister from June, 1979 to March, 1980. He became Tory leader again in 1998 and regained a Commons seat in 2000. In 2000, Ujjal Dosanjh was elected leader of the B.C. NDP, becoming the country's first Indo-Canadian premier. But he was defeated in an election the following year by the Liberals under Gordon Campbell. In 2019, the Trudeau government voted down an Opposition motion for a public inquiry into the SNC-Lavalin scandal. Former justice minister Jody Wilson-Raybould - who was at the heart of the firestorm - abstained from voting, explaining it was not appropriate since the matter involved her personally. Some Opposition members demanded that Prime Minister Trudeau and the current Justice Minister David Lametti also abstain but they registered votes against the motion, which was defeated 160 to 134.

Subject: Prime ministers

Location: United States--US; Canada

Document 197 of 219

Shoemaker wants help in opioid fight; Province again asked to assist

Author: Della- Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della- Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/shoemaker-wants-help-opioid-fight-province-again/docview/2927936059/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Shoemaker+wants+help+in+opioid+fight%3B+Province+again+asked+to+assist&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-+Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayor Matthew Shoemaker has penned another letter to the Ontario government reiterating the need for the province to assist in the opioid crisis.
Shoemaker's two-page letter to Premier Doug Ford and Associate Minister of Mental Health and Addictions Minister Michael Tibollo was the 10th letter the city has written since March 2019.
This letter points to data released earlier this month by Ontario's Chief Coroner which shows Sault Ste. Marie has higher than provincial average rates for opioid-related calls. The third quarter results for 2023 shows Sault Ste. Marie has twice the provincial rate for opioid-related emergency department visits, three times the provincial rate for hospitalizations and is slightly above (0.6 times) the provincial rate for deaths.
"The statistics clearly demonstrate the need for Sault Ste. Marie to receive increased support from the Government of Ontario in a prompt manner," the two-page letter reads.
Shoemaker reiterates the need for government assistance to fund treatment programs, clinics and a supervised consumption site to help save lives and reverse the statistics that show alarming trends.
Shoemaker said the newest data shows a sharp increase, when by contrast, communities like Thunder Bay and Sudbury that have supervised consumption sites, both saw decreases in emergency department visits and hospitalizations.
Shoemaker said he and former Mayor Christian Provenzano have written to the government about the mental health and addictions crisis on 10 instances.
He notes it took 30 months for the province to find a "limited return" of the Concurrent Disorders Intensive Day Treatment Pilot Program, which had been paused in the summer of 2022 due to a lack of funding.
The program provides badly needed specialized care for those with complex addictions and mental health needs.
"I want to thank your government for its support but reiterate the need for the dedicated base funding required for the hospital to offer the program on a permanent basis," said Shoemaker.
He also notes the need for funding to develop a research partnership between Algoma University and the Northern Ontario School of Medicine to develop mental health and addiction solutions and the need for a supervised consumption site.
The Ontario government has paused applications for supervised consumption sites, but has said it will reopen those applications in early spring.
"As I'm sure you're aware, at the municipal level we can advocate, but we cannot put city dollars into hospitals, treatment facilities and medical schools. That funding needs to come from the Ontario government, it says so in the Constitution," Shoemaker writes.
Tibollo was in Sault Ste. Marie earlier this year for the Algoma Youth Wellness Hub and at that time said he was willing to hear suggestions on how to improve treatment and recovery programs.
"The City of Sault Ste. Marie is providing (suggestions). Moving forward, I urge the Government of Ontario to act in support of these suggestions with the zeal Minister Tibollo used to defend your government to the media," Shoemaker concludes.
Shoemaker was asked about the mental health and opioid situation on TVO's The Agenda earlier this week. Shoemaker said the file is a challenging one that the city can't get ahead of.
"We are continuing to lobby the government of Ontario," he said, noting that shelters, emergency rooms and treatment programs are full.
The problem, he said, is more challenging in Northern Ontario because the North receives less funding dollars and has fewer programs.
"Each passing month that we don't get help we need for our community, there are going to be more months with more deaths and higher statistics in Sault Ste. Marie than there are elsewhere," Shoemaker said.

Subject: Emergency medical care; Mental health; Addictions; Funding; Narcotics

Document 198 of 219

Supreme Court should have decided Bill 124 case

Author: Lille y, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/supreme-court-should-have-decided-bill-124-case/docview/2927059177/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Supreme+Court+should+have+decided+Bill+124+case&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is little doubt the fate of Bill 124 should have been decided by the Supreme Court of Canada.
The Ontario Court of Appeal issued a split decision Monday, finding the law legislating wage increases was, in part, unconstitutional.
But rather than appeal to the Supreme Court, the cowards in the government of Doug Ford announced they will repeal the wage-cap legislation imposed on public sector workers.
Justice Lise Favreau, writing the majority opinion in the Court of Appeal's 2-1 decision, twisted herself into bizarre positions to arrive at her final conclusion, one that leaves more questions than answers.
Thanks to the court, Bill 124 is invalid as far as unionized workers of the Ontario public service are concerned, but valid for non-unionized workers. The challenge to the law was brought forward by several unions, which argued Bill 124 violated their charter-protected right to collective bargaining.
"The rights protected by s. 2(d) of the charter do not apply in the same way to non-represented employees and accordingly, the Act is only unconstitutional in so far as it applies to the represented employees covered by the Act," Favreau wrote.
About 82 per cent of the Ontario public service is covered by a union, roughly 530,000 people, according to the Financial Accountability Office, while roughly 120,000 are non-union employees.
The government argued that Bill 124 had been necessary to control wages at a time when Ontario's finances were in a horrible position. The province's net debt-to-GDP ratio was above 40 per cent and more than eight per cent of the province's budget was being spent on debt charges.
Favreau ruled the fiscal restraint created by the bill could have been achieved "through good faith bargaining" and, despite the fiscal situation of the province, there was no evidence that "expediency" was required.
That's a claim Justice William Hourigan, writing a dissenting opinion, found nearly laughable.
"If a government sees an economic cliff on the horizon, courts should not require it to wait till the last moment to act," Hourigan wrote.
He was also highly critical of a statement by Favreau that she was striking the law down because, "many of whom are women, racialized and/or low-income earners, have lost the ability to negotiate for better compensation or even better work conditions."
"There are many issues to unpack in this comment. However, I will restrict myself to reminding my colleagues that 'women, racialized and/or low-income earners'pay taxes in this province, and they, too, have an interest in ensuring the responsible management of the province's finances and the protection of sustainable public services," Hourigan said.
Laws restricting wage settlements for government employees have survived many court challenges in the past. The Manitoba government passed a similar law in 2017 and had it struck down in court, but an Appeals Court overturned that ruling, declaring the law valid in 2021.
When unions tried to appeal to the Supreme Court, the justices declined to hear the case.
When the government of Stephen Harper brought in the Expenditure Restraint Act in response to the 2008 financial crisis, the law was challenged several times on similar grounds and upheld. There was a good chance Favreau's decision would be overturned if Ford's government appealed to the Supreme Court.
Citing the Expenditure Restraint Act cases, Favreau said, in that instance, the law was justified due to the economic crisis but in the case of Bill 124, things weren't bad enough to enact such measures. She was effectively saying the Ford government could have restricted wages, and it would have been constitutional, they just did it wrong.
Given the weak ruling by Favreau and the fact it contradicts existing precedent from the Supreme Court and is at odds with a recent Manitoba decision, the Ford government should have appealed. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Nonunion; Expenditures; Collective bargaining; Employees; Low income groups; Wages & salaries

Business indexing term: Subject: Collective bargaining Employees Wages & salaries

Document 199 of 219

Mantha targets benefit improvements to Northern Health Travel Grant Program; Bill 13 set for second reading

Author: Darbyson, Kyle

Publication info: Darbyson, Kyle. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mantha-targets-benefit-improvements-northern/docview/2927058455/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mantha+targets+benefit+improvements+to+Northern+Health+Travel+Grant+Program%3B+Bill+13+set+for+second+reading&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Darbyson%2C+Kyle&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the Legislative Assembly set to reconvene next week, Algoma-Manitoulin MPP Michael Mantha is renewing his push to pass a private member's bill that aims to improve the Northern Health Travel Grant Program.
Mantha made this announcement at the Espanola Regional Hospital and Health Centre on Monday, revealing that this proposed piece of legislation, if passed, will establish a committee to review the program and recommend changes to the Ministry of Health.
The independent MPP introduced Bill 13, the Northern Health Travel Grant Advisory Committee Act, back in the summer of 2022 and is excited now that the legislation is finally scheduled for its second reading on Feb. 22.
"Being able to get the treatment you need without financial barriers is at the core of our healthcare system in Canada," Mantha said in a news release. "I am calling on the Ford government to do the right thing, listen to people in the North and pass my bill next week."
The Northern Health Travel Grant program, established in 1985 under then-Premier David Peterson, is designed to assist patients living in Northern Ontario who need to travel over 100 kilometres for various medical treatments.
While the program's reimbursement rates have been subject to increases over the last four decades, Mantha believes that the current travel grants are insufficient given the recent spike in prices for gas, food and general accommodations.
"If you're from Northern Ontario and you're going to see a specialist down in Toronto or London ... a cheap hotel room is $240 and up," he told The Sault Star on Tuesday. "Your parking for the one or two days, you're looking at $30 to $50 and up plus your food."
Through consultation with his constituents, Mantha said the bureaucratic process of applying for travel grants is also ripe for reform, describing the current system as "cumbersome" and in desperate need of being streamlined.
"When you look at individuals like cancer patients, who are travelling to the Sault or travelling to Sudbury for cancer treatment, they're waiting weeks and sometimes months for their reimbursement," he said.
"This is undue hardship and undue stress on those particular individuals to getting their reimbursement done."
If Bill 13 passes, Mantha said the resulting committee will be composed of healthcare providers and residents, who will be tasked with improving the Northern Health Travel Grant program by consulting with primary care providers, Indigenous health organizations and Northern public health units.
Even though Mantha has sat as an independent MPP since being ousted from the Ontario NDP last year, due to workplace misconduct allegations, he remains confident in his ability to turn this proposed piece of legislation into law.
Not only does he have a track record of getting private member's bills across the finish line, such as the Children's Law Reform Amendment Act in 2016, but he's confident that this specific issue will appeal to lawmakers across the political spectrum.
"So again, I'm hoping that the arguments I'm bringing forward are reasonable ones and ones that are going to be seen and supported by all members," he said.
The Legislative Assembly of Ontario's first official meeting of 2024 is scheduled for this upcoming Tuesday. kdarbyson@postmedia.com The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government

Subject: Reimbursement; Legislation; Grants

Document 200 of 219

Today in history

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/today-history/docview/2924639289/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today+in+history&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In 1802, Alexander Mackenzie was knighted for being first to cross the North American continent by land, in 1793. In 1829, King's College, Fredericton - now the University of New Brunswick - was given a royal charter. In 1837, Russian poet and novelist Alexander Pushkin was killed in a duel. In 1840, Britain's Queen Victoria married Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha.
In 1841, Upper and Lower Canada were united as the Province of Canada, with Kingston as the capital. In 1846, members of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, also known as the Mormons, left Illinois and began an exodus to the American West, now Utah. They were led by Brigham Young, newly elected as their leader. In 1870, the Young Women's Christian Association - YWCA - was formed. In 1890, Russian novelist Boris Pasternak, author of Dr. Zhivago, was born in Moscow. In 1906, Prince Rupert was chosen from 15,000 entries as the name of the Grand Trunk Railway's Pacific terminal. Eleanor Macdonald of Winnipeg won $250 for suggesting the name.
In 1933, the first singing telegram was introduced by the Postal Telegraph Company in New York. In 1947, peace treaties between the Allies and some of the Axis powers were signed in Paris. Canada signed treaties with Italy, Romania, Hungary and Finland. In 1949, Arthur Miller's play "Death of a Salesman" opened at Broadway's Morosco Theater with Lee J. Cobb as Willy Loman. In 1956, Wilbert Coffin was hanged in Montreal for the murders of three American hunters, killed in the Gaspe in 1953. Many people believed he was innocent.
In 1983, the federal government agreed in principle to allow the testing of American weapons over Canadian territory. In 1991, Peru's health minister reported at least 51 deaths from cholera. An epidemic later spread across South America. In 1992, Alex Haley, the author of "Roots," died in Seattle at age 70. In 1992, an Indianapolis jury found former heavyweight boxing champion Mike Tyson guilty of rape and other sex-related charges in a 1991 incident involving a beauty pageant contestant.
In 1996, a machine scored its first victory under classic chess tournament rules as an IBM computer called "Deep Blue" beat world champion Gary Kasparov. In 2003, Inderjit Singh Reyat pleaded guilty to manslaughter in the 1985 Air India bombing that killed 329 people, and was sentenced to five years in prison. He admitted to acquiring material for a bomb that police alleged caused the mid-air explosion. Reyat had completed a 10-year sentence for his role in a second bombing the same day that killed two baggage handlers at Japan's Narita airport.
In January 2011, he was sentenced to nine years in prison for perjury. Reyat was convicted of lying at the trial of the two men who were accused and then acquitted of the Air India bombing. (In 2017, the Parole Board of Canada allowed him to leave a halfway house where he was required to stay following his release from prison in 2016.) In 2003, The World Health Organization's Beijing office received an email describing a "strange contagious disease" in Guangdong province that killed dozens within one week. The disease was later identified as SARS, or severe acute respiratory syndrome. In 2004, France's National Assembly voted overwhelmingly to banish religious emblems such as Muslim headscarves, Jewish skullcaps and large Christian crosses from state schools, a measure meant to keep tensions between Muslim and Jewish minorities out of public classrooms
In 2004, Auditor General Sheila Fraser reported massive abuses in a federal sponsorship program run by the Public Works Department that funnelled cash to Quebec advertising agencies with close ties to the Liberal Party. Prime Minister Paul Martin fired former public works minister Alfonso Gagliano as ambassador to Denmark and ordered an independent judicial inquiry into the scandal.
In 2005, Prime Minister Paul Martin testified at the Gomery inquiry into the sponsorship scandal, becoming the first sitting prime minister to have appeared in public at an inquiry since 1873. In 2005, the engagement of Prince Charles and his longtime lover, Camilla Parker Bowles, was announced. (They married two months later.) In 2009, a working U.S. commercial satellite and a derelict Russian satellite collided in space over Siberia, believed to be the first-ever crash of two intact spacecraft in orbit. In 2011, the Supreme Court of Canada ruled that in court cases where national security is an issue, the Federal Court alone, and not a trial judge, can decide what evidence can be kept secret. The issue arose in the high-profile terrorism case known as the Toronto 18, when Ontario Superior Court Justice Fletcher Dawson struck down provisions of the Canada Evidence Act.

Subject: Criminal sentences; Acquittals & mistrials; Scandals; Manslaughter; Prime ministers

Document 201 of 219

Sault Ste. Marie 'single largest community' supported regularly by Hope Air for medical travel

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sault-ste-marie-single-largest-community/docview/2923732321/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sault+Ste.+Marie+%27single+largest+community%27+supported+regularly+by+Hope+Air+for+medical+travel&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hope Air needs to ink an agreement with the provincial government to ensure it can keep pace with the increasing travel needs patients have to get the health care they require.
And Sault Ste. Marie is the single largest community in Ontario of patients that are supported by Hope Air on a regular basis, said the charitable organization's CEO Mark Rubinstein.
Hope Air provides free medical travel programs to assist patients living in numerous small, remote or rural communities across Ontario. It provides airline, hotel, meals and ground transportation programs for patients to access healthcare not available close to home.
The programs support patients who cannot afford the significant expenses associated with travelling for diagnostic testing, treatment, and follow-up care for a variety of health conditions including Cancer, Cardiovascular, Diabetes, Respiratory, Orthopedic, Mental Health, Autism, and Pediatric conditions among many others.
Patients are often transported to specialty medical care hospitals such as Sick Kids, Princess Margaret Cancer Care and Mount Sinai, among others.
About 25 per cent of Hope Air's patients are children.
Hope Air does not currently receive funding from the Ontario government.
Hope Air operates exclusively by donations in Ontario but has made inroads in other provinces to establish funding partnerships with provincial governments including British Columbia, Prince Edward Island and Newfoundland & Labrador.
It's trying to do the same in Ontario, Rubinstein said in a telephone interview with The Sault Star.
"Where you live should not determine if you live," he said.
While Hope Air has been historically able to meet the modest demand through its charitable donations, the COVID-19 pandemic changed that.
Patients travel has resumed - and skyrocketed - since the pandemic.
Hope Air made record high travel arrangements in 2023, organizing 3,367 trips to medical facilities for those who needed it most, marking an increase of 165 per cent from previous years.
While that increase is partly due to the pandemic, it's also because many are struggling with financial challenges just to make ends meet and the cost of travelling hundreds of kilometres away for medical treatment is an added burden.
"With soaring demand from families and children in need who turn to Hope Air, we must continue to find ways to answer their call. As demand grows exponentially, exceeding our available resources, we must come together - government, health care sector, private sector, donors and volunteers - to support the unique role that Hope Air plays in Ontario. Equitable access to health care - despite distance or financial means -is a fundamental right," Rubinstein said.
Hope Air is hoping to partner with the Ontario government to ensure no one is left behind. It has also completed a 50-community tour to tell Hope Air's story and ensure communities understand the impact on members if it doesn't have the funding to help with medical travel.
Rubinstein said the government is "generally supportive" and understands demand for the service isn't going to decrease.
While the lack of primary health care providers is growing in Sault Ste. Marie and other parts of the Northern Ontario, Rubinstein said government financial support ensures patients are getting the healthcare they deserve.
"This is an easy fix for life-saving issues that require travel," Rubinstein said. "We just need stable funding to help us meet the demand. There is no reason why long-distance travel should be a barrier."
Hope Air completed travel arrangements for more than 1,736 flights, facilitating access to medical care for families, individuals and children from across Ontario last year.
In Sault Ste. Marie last year, it completed 931 travel arrangements for 100 patients.
Since Hope Air's inception, 9,855 travel arrangements have been made for 904 patients in Sault Ste. Marie.
In November, city council passed a resolution which asked Mayor Matthew Shoemaker to pen a letter to the Ontario government in support of Hope Air. The letter was sent to Health Minister Sylvia Jones, MPP Ross Romano and Ontario Premier Doug Ford.
Since its inception in 1986, Hope Air has provided more than 170,000 travel arrangements for patients. The organization anticipates it will be asked to serve 18,000 patients by 2025.
Patients seeking support for medical travel needs can apply online to Hope Air at hopeair.ca or call 1-877-346-4673.

Subject: Funding; Health care; Pandemics

Document 202 of 219

Smith in Ottawa as Liberals decry 'draconian' policies

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/smith-ottawa-as-liberals-decry-draconian-policies/docview/2922713523/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Smith+in+Ottawa+as+Liberals+decry+%27draconian%27+policies&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amid accusations from the federal Liberals that she's pushing an anti-LGBTQ agenda, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith defended her new suite of policies on transgender youth during a visit to Ottawa on Monday.
Smith announced last week that her United Conservative Party government would ban puberty blockers and hormone therapy for children 15 and younger who have not already begun those treatments.
Her government plans to require that parents give their consent for kids 15 and under to go by a different name or pronoun at school. Teenagers aged 16 and 17 would be able to make such changes without permission, but schools would have to notify their guardians.
The policies have spurred several days of protest.
At an event to mark the opening of a new provincial office near Parliament Hill, Smith suggested there is no "single voice" that can speak on behalf of the entire transgender community.
She said has spoken to some transgender people who expressed concerns about children's ability to transition.
Smith defended the package of proposals as part of a "considered approach" she wants to take when it comes to children with diverse gender identities.
The goal is to ensure children are "fully informed" about the decisions they are making in the event they may regret them later in life, she said.
"We had to have a conversation about what is the appropriate ages to be able to make those life-altering decisions."
Earlier in the day, Liberal Employment Minister Randy Boissonnault said he requested a meeting with Smith to discuss the proposed changes.
Boissonnault, who is openly gay and the only Liberal cabinet minister from Alberta, said Smith is pushing "draconian" measures on some of the province's most vulnerable youth.
After some back-and-forth between their respective offices, the pair were expected to meet Monday afternoon.
The federal government's ability to ask for a Supreme Court reference on any use of the notwithstanding clause by provinces is an "important legal tool," Boissonnault noted.
"We will be playing very close attention to see what's in Premier Smith's legislation, which I hope never makes it to the floor of the (legislature)," he said.
Asked about Smith's policy at a news conference Monday, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said: "Let parents raise kids and provinces run schools and hospitals."
He did not specifically address Smith's proposals. His comments come after his office told MPs last week to "refrain" from making any comment about Alberta's specific measures, and to "flag" any media requests they receive to the office.
In Toronto, Ontario Premier Doug Ford signalled that his government would not follow Alberta in restricting the availability of surgeries and treatments for transgender youth. "No. We have a law here and we're leaving everything alone," he said, echoing previous remarks from the province's health minister.
Local advocates in Ottawa, meanwhile, were organizing an "emergency" protest downtown later Monday in light of Smith's visit to the capital.
The Alberta government office Smith opened in Ottawa is intended to help advance the province's priorities and fortify federal-provincial ties, the Alberta government said in a news release.
The Canadian Press

Subject: Children & youth; Transgender persons
