Document 203 of 219

Allow patients to pay for private care, veteran sault md says

Author: Chow, Peter

Publication info: Chow, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/allow-patients-pay-private-care-veteran-sault-md/docview/2921623253/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Allow+patients+to+pay+for+private+care%2C+veteran+sault+md+says&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Chow%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I graduated from medicine at the University of Toronto in 1971, three years after the inception of Medicare in Canada in 1968.
As soon as government took total control for paying them, suddenly there were too many doctors doing too many operations, generating too many visits, making too much money.
The medical profession, the College of Physicians and Surgeons, the Ontario Medical Association, have absolutely no influence on medical manpower numbers -it is the provincial government that has absolute control.
For the last 55 years, provincial governments have tried to cut health-care spending by limiting the number of doctors, clawing back their fees, capping their incomes and limiting the number of billing numbers (which are needed for a doctor to be paid).The Ontario government decides how many doctors it wants to pay and limits medical school enrolments accordingly.
Despite crippling physician shortages, each provincial government still imposes tight quotas on how many students are allowed to enrol at the country's 17 medical schools, a "choke point" that limits student admissions to just under 2,900 spots for prospective doctors each year.
Ontario has six medical schools, all of which have acceptance rates of less than 10 per cent, four below five per cent.
As long as the government is the sole single payer for physician services, it will be the choke point for physician numbers and the reason for the doctor shortage.
The shortage of doctors is the fault of every Ontario provincial government regardless of party, not just Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
And up to now, governments have been fine with that; all three main political parties, and every premier in every Ontario government since the inception of Medicare.
Bob Rae's NDP government, 1990-1995, slashed medical school enrolment in Ontario by 10 per cent in 1993 and the repercussions are still felt today..The Bob Rae NDP government of 1990-1995 in Ontario implemented the Barer-Stoddart report, which postulated that hordes of surplus medical school graduates were going to suck the system dry by generating scads of unnecessary medical encounters.
In one bizarre example of top-down central planning run amok, the Rae government announced it would pay the University of Toronto up to $10 million a year to not produce as many doctors.
Zealous politicians in many provinces offered incentives for physicians to retire early or contemplated other methods to reduce physician supply, such as limiting billing numbers and capping physician incomes.
Not long ago, Ontario wouldn't allow new doctors except in "underserviced" areas, such as Sioux Lookout, which medical students in Toronto always confuse with Sault Ste Marie.
Quebec currently does the same thing, forcing new graduates to work in the boondocks if they want to be paid by the province's healthcare plan.
Which is why so many family doctors in Montreal and Quebec City have opted out of the public system completely and are private.
The Mike Harris Progressive Conservative government that followed Rae pushed tax reductions and slashed public spending on health care, closed hospitals, privatized long-term care and eliminated the jobs of thousands of nurses, infamously comparing them to obsolete hula hoop makers.
In 1996, Harris announced a 10 per cent "clawback" in the fees paid to Ontario doctors for services they provide, the first of several.
The fee schedule has never been unclawed and today the present Ontario fee schedule is 70 per cent lower than the fee schedule of the Ontario Medical Association for uninsured patients..In 1997 Harris capped physician incomes and declared that doctors who cut back on the number of patients they see because they've hit their billing caps should lose their medical licences -all this while there was a physician shortage. Kathleen Wynne's Liberal government, 2013-2018, went to war with physicians in the mid 2010s.
On Feb. 1 2015, rather than opt for a fair, binding arbitration process, the Liberal government proceeded to unilaterally impose a 2.65 per cent cut to all doctor services (on top of the 2.5 per cent cut from 2012) and an arbitrary cap on total compensation to doctors.
Without a doubt, family physicians were the most heavily impacted physicians.
This meant that once patients'demand for care exceeded the allocated $12 billion, the doctors themselves were responsible for subsidizing the difference through a further claw-back. Any other profession would go on strike, but doctors realize how much our patients need us, and we would never go on strike (nor are we allowed to by our regulatory college).The only recourse doctors had was to retire or to leave the province. Which many did.
The Wynne government thought it was a good idea to cut 50 residency positions (training for doctors) and only saw the light during a deathbed confession just in time for the 2018 election. The Ontario government's contempt for family physicians resulted in, as OMA vice-chair Audrey Karlinsky put it, a cohort of six years of medical graduates not choosing family medicine when hundreds of thousands of people didn't have a family doctor.
The decision of former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberal government to cut 50 medical residency positions in Ontario over two years was done for one reason only.
That was to save money by cutting the number of doctors in a province that desperately needed them, playing on the politics of envy, painting doctors as fat cats, a tactic government uses over and over again.
Canada spends less of its total health budget on primary care than the average among Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) countries (5.3 per cent vs. 8.1 per cent).At the same time, Canada is seeing declining enrolment in family medicine as a specialty among medical students, and more graduating family physicians are choosing not to practise generalist office-based care, opting instead for being hospitalists or ER doctors.
No one wants to be a family doctor. With good reasons.
The number of Ontarians without a family doctor, as of Sept. 2022, has surpassed 2.3 million (up from 1.8 million in 2020), over 14.8 per cent of all Ontarians according to a new report.
The Ontario College of Family Physicians'latest forecast is that approximately one in four Ontarians - that's 4.4 million - will be without a family doctor by 2026.
To make up for that shortfall, and without even considering physicians on the cusp of retirement, Ontario would need approximately 1,700 new family doctors.
A retrospective study of community-dwelling seniors who were admitted to Thunder Bay Regional Health Sciences Centre examined the association between family physician status and outcomes among those with a family physician. Among the 12,033 seniors admitted to hospital between 2004, and 2013, 40.7 per cent lacked a family physician.
There was a 60 per cent higher chance of dying while in hospital plus a 21 per cent higher chance of dying in the year afterwards for those with no family doctor.
The health-care system is complicated and a family doctor is vital in navigating the system.
Canada can learn from OECD countries such as the Netherlands, Norway, the UK and Finland, where more than 95 per cent of people have a primary care clinician, near-universal insurance coverage exists, the proportion of health spending that is spent on primary care is larger than Canada's and GPs are organized better in teams and are more accountable for the care they must provide.
Ten per cent of medical doctors in Finland work solely in the private sector. They also have a right to collect user fees set at a maximum of 46 Euros.
The Netherlands offers mandatory insurance that is managed by private insurance companies and you pay a compulsory deductible for the first part of healthcare you receive under the insurance.
In Norway, citizens are responsible for paying a certain amount in user fees until they reach a certain amount of money paid outof-pocket, when they receive an exemption card for public health services, and no longer have to pay user fees for the remainder of the calendar year.
Allow patients to pay for private care if they so choose. Sweden, Finland, Germany, Australia, Denmark, Japan, Germany - basically every other rich developed country with a universal health-care system allows this option and has better outcomes than Canada.
When access to timely care cannot be provided in the publicly funded system, Canadians should be able to use private health insurance to reimburse the cost of care obtained in the private sector rather than die waiting.
Since 2013, there have been 931 patients who died in Ontario while waiting for heart surgery longer than the maximum recommended wait time. Then there are those who die while waiting for scans or surgeries that would have improved their quality of life, like a hip operation.
Over 17,000 Canadians died while waiting for surgery or a diagnostic scan in 2022-23.
Universal public healthcare is a right but one should have the option of accessing better (often more costly, more advanced medical technology such as laser cataract surgery not available on OHIP), faster, more timely care if so desired, as everyone in every other modern, industrialized wealthy country in the world can. This approach helps reduce wait times every time a patient decides to pay for private care.
Shaking up the way hospitals are funded could also help. Hospitals severely limit OR time because more surgeries are seen as a cost coming out of a pre-determined global budget.
Fund more OR time, reward hospitals for being more productive, shortening patient wait times.
Instead of giving hospitals a large cheque at the beginning of the year and hoping for the best, governments could pay them based on their performance and output.
Health-care is a right, not a privilege, but like food and shelter, it is also a commodity.
We do not mandate that everyone eat Hamburger Helper or live in row housing.
Political ideology run amok has elevated government single-payer one-tier health care to an impossible pedestal.
Clinging to an ideology of government single-payer one-tier health-care is simply misguided virtue-signalling on a national scale.
Should we be proud of a health-care system where governments abandon patients to die? And make no mistake, people will die because of not having a family doctor.
Obviously not. These international examples can inform bold policy reform in Canada to advance a vision of primary care for all.

Subject: Shortages; Health care policy; Surgery; User fees; Primary care; Health care expenditures; Medical students; Fees & charges; Medicare; Physicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Shortages Medicare Physicians

Location: Netherlands; Canada; Germany; Norway; Finland

People: Wynne, Kathleen; Rae, Bob

Document 204 of 219

Two health-care organizations share $1.1M to add patients to roster; Comes after GHC announces it's derostering 10,000 patients

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine; Ougler, Jeffrey

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine; Ougler, Jeffrey. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/two-health-care-organizations-share-1-1m-add/docview/2921623198/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Two+health-care+organizations+share+%241.1M+to+add+patients+to+roster%3B+Comes+after+GHC+announces+it%27s+derostering+10%2C000+patients&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine%3BOugler%2C+Jeffrey&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two health-care organizations have been provided with $1.1 million to provide primary healthcare to more than 4,000 people in the region.
Sault Ste. Marie MPP Ross Romano announced the funding Friday.
This is part of the province's recent $110-million announcement, geared to helping unattached patients access the health-care services that they need across the province.
The Sault Community Health Centre, operating from the new Community Resource Centre, will receive $615,000 of ongoing operational funding to serve an additional 2,275 unattached patients to its rosters.
The Maamwesying North Shore Community Health Services will receive $494,000 to help an additional 2,275 new patients within Sault Ste. Marie and regional Indigenous communities it supports across its 620-kilometre span and 12 health-care centre sites.
Romano said the goal is to provide primary health-care services to all those who want it.
"This is the beginning, not the end," Romano said during the Zoom announcement. "We still have a long way to go but I would say this is a tremendous first start to make sure people in our community who want access to primary care, will have that primary care."
Romano said he believes the announcement is "exceptionally" good news for the community, including the 18,000 patients without care for some time.
"The unattached patients (issue) is ultimately what we are aiming to solve," Romano said. "We are going to keep moving forward to make a difference in this community."
Last week, the Group Health Centre announced it would axe 10,000 patients from its list - patients whose doctors have retired or left the organization. A further 6,000 patients remain at risk in the near future.
It's estimated that there are now about 30,000 people in the area without a primary care provider.
Algoma District Medical Group, in collaboration with Group Health Centre, has "worked hard" to recruit new primary care providers, stated a letter signed by Algoma District Medical Group CEO Dr. Jodie Stewart and sent to affected GHC patients, a copy of which was obtained by The Sault Star.
"Unfortunately, recruitment has become very difficult due to the nationwide shortage of family physicians," the letter said. "This issue is not unique to Sault Ste Marie but is part of a broader healthcare crisis in Ontario. Every person deserves to have a primary care provider. Please share your story with our elected government representatives to help us make this happen."
Group Health Centre's announcement will also have a "significant" impact on Sault Area Hospital's emergency department, overall operations and wait times -as well as those patients affected and the entire community, said SAH president and CEO Ila Watson.
The region's principal healthcare facility, like other Ontario hospitals, is facing challenges in terms of high patient volumes, high acuity and staffing issues, and the addition of 10,000 patients without primary care will contribute to the hospital's already existing challenges, she said.
"We know that our community will increasingly rely on our hospital for care they should be able to access elsewhere," Watson told The Sault Star via email.
More residents without a primary care provider will also "directly translate to a significant increase" in calls for paramedic service and increased off-load delays experienced at Sault Area Hospital, which will ultimately impact ability to respond to emergencies, District of Sault Ste. Marie Social Services Administration Board said earlier this week.
Romano was asked whether the Group Health model, innovative when first created, is now outdated and needs to be revamped.
He said experts in the community will need to answer that question when he gathers stakeholders on March 1 to form a new committee designed to look at the issues that have resulted in the loss of primary care providers.
"It's those health-care experts who come to the table, that we need to ask those questions. All the questions being asked are relevant and I believe will come up in that conversation," Romano said.
The United Steelworkers Union has penned letters to Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, calling the de-rostering "unacceptable."
The letter notes it was the USW in Sault Ste. Marie that contributed financially to build and equip the GHC, an agreement that, in return, would see steelworkers have access to health care.
The USW called the de-rostering of its members and retirees a breach of contact.
They're calling on both levels of government work together and take immediate action to address the crisis.
"This significant shortage is critical and will certainly overload our hospital emergency department, which will probably result in potential closures of that department. The closest emergency room is in Sudbury, which is four hours away."
Romano said the suggested breach would be a matter for the courts to decide.
"I don't think now is the time to point fingers in any direction. Now is the time for us to look for possible ways to ensure we can get people the care they deserve," he said. Dr. Alan McLean, who leads the medical services at the CRC, said the need exceeds existing resources and the funding will stretch further to help those who have difficulty accessing health care.
Carol Eshkakogan, of the Maamwesying North Shore Community Health Services, said the funding will be used to enhance health care for unattached patients with a variety of health-care providers, including physicians, nurse practitioners, chiropractors, nurses and more.

Subject: Health care access; Hospitals; Primary care; Emergency medical care; Health care policy; Funding; Community health care; Health services

Business indexing term: Subject: Hospitals

Document 205 of 219

LETTER: Allow patients to pay for private care 'if they so choose,' veteran Sault Ste. Marie physician writes

Author: Sault Star

Publication info: Sault Star. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letter-allow-patients-pay-private-care-if-they-so/docview/2921281036/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LETTER%3A+Allow+patients+to+pay+for+private+care+%27if+they+so+choose%2C%27+veteran+Sault+Ste.+Marie+physician+writes&amp;title=The+Sault+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Sault+Star&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Sault+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I graduated from medicine at the University of Toronto in 1971, three years after the inception of Medicare in Canada in 1968.
As soon as government took total control for paying them, suddenly there were too many doctors doing too many operations, generating too many visits, making too much money.
The medical profession, the College of Physicians and Surgeons, the Ontario Medical Association, have absolutely no influence on medical manpower numbers - it is the provincial government that has absolute control.
For the last 55 years, provincial governments have tried to cut health-care spending by limiting the number of doctors, clawing back their fees, capping their incomes and limiting the number of billing numbers (which are needed for a doctor to be paid).
The Ontario government decides how many doctors it wants to pay and limits medical school enrolments accordingly.
Despite crippling physician shortages, each provincial government still imposes tight quotas on how many students are allowed to enrol at the country's 17 medical schools, a "choke point" that limits student admissions to just under 2,900 spots for prospective doctors each year.
Ontario has six medical schools, all of which have acceptance rates of less than 10 per cent, four below five per cent.
As long as the government is the sole single payer for physician services, it will be the choke point for physician numbers and the reason for the doctor shortage.
The shortage of doctors is the fault of every Ontario provincial government regardless of party, not just Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
And up to now, governments have been fine with that; all three main political parties, and every premier in every Ontario government since the inception of Medicare.
Bob Rae's NDP government, 1990-1995, slashed medical school enrolment in Ontario by 10 per cent in 1993 and the repercussions are still felt today..
The Bob Rae NDP government of 1990-1995 in Ontario implemented the Barer-Stoddart report, which postulated that hordes of surplus medical school graduates were going to suck the system dry by generating scads of unnecessary medical encounters.
In one bizarre example of top-down central planning run amok, the Rae government announced it would pay the University of Toronto up to $10 million a year to not produce as many doctors.
Zealous politicians in many provinces offered incentives for physicians to retire early or contemplated other methods to reduce physician supply, such as limiting billing numbers and capping physician incomes.
Not long ago, Ontario wouldn't allow new doctors except in "underserviced" areas, such as Sioux Lookout, which medical students in Toronto always confuse with Sault Ste Marie.
Quebec currently does the same thing, forcing new graduates to work in the boondocks if they want to be paid by the province's health-care plan.
Which is why so many family doctors in Montreal and Quebec City have opted out of the public system completely and are private.
The Mike Harris Progressive Conservative government that followed Rae pushed tax reductions and slashed public spending on health care, closed hospitals, privatized long-term care and eliminated the jobs of thousands of nurses, infamously comparing them to obsolete hula hoop makers.
In 1996, Harris announced a 10 per cent "clawback" in the fees paid to Ontario doctors for services they provide, the first of several.
The fee schedule has never been unclawed and today the present Ontario fee schedule is 70 per cent lower than the fee schedule of the Ontario Medical Association for uninsured patients..
In 1997 Harris capped physician incomes and declared that doctors who cut back on the number of patients they see because they've hit their billing caps should lose their medical licences - all this while there was a physician shortage.
Kathleen Wynne's Liberal government, 2013-2018, went to war with physicians in the mid 2010s.
On Feb. 1 2015, rather than opt for a fair, binding arbitration process, the Liberal government proceeded to unilaterally impose a 2.65 per cent cut to all doctor services (on top of the 2.5 per cent cut from 2012) and an arbitrary cap on total compensation to doctors.
Without a doubt, family physicians were the most heavily impacted physicians.
This meant that once patients' demand for care exceeded the allocated $12 billion, the doctors themselves were responsible for subsidizing the difference through a further claw-back. Any other profession would go on strike, but doctors realize how much our patients need us, and we would never go on strike (nor are we allowed to by our regulatory college).
The only recourse doctors had was to retire or to leave the province. Which many did.
The Wynne government thought it was a good idea to cut 50 residency positions (training for doctors) and only saw the light during a deathbed confession just in time for the 2018 election. The Ontario government's contempt for family physicians resulted in, as OMA vice-chair Audrey Karlinsky put it, a cohort of six years of medical graduates not choosing family medicine when hundreds of thousands of people didn't have a family doctor.
The decision of former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberal government to cut 50 medical residency positions in Ontario over two years was done for one reason only.
That was to save money by cutting the number of doctors in a province that desperately needed them, playing on the politics of envy, painting doctors as fat cats, a tactic government uses over and over again.
Canada spends less of its total health budget on primary care than the average among Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) countries (5.3 per cent vs. 8.1 per cent).
At the same time, Canada is seeing declining enrolment in family medicine as a specialty among medical students, and more graduating family physicians are choosing not to practise generalist office-based care, opting instead for being hospitalists or ER doctors.
No one wants to be a family doctor. With good reasons.
The number of Ontarians without a family doctor, as of Sept. 2022, has surpassed 2.3 million (up from 1.8 million in 2020), over 14.8 per cent of all Ontarians according to a new report.
The Ontario College of Family Physicians' latest forecast is that approximately one in four Ontarians - that's 4.4 million - will be without a family doctor by 2026.
To make up for that shortfall, and without even considering physicians on the cusp of retirement, Ontario would need approximately 1,700 new family doctors.
A retrospective study of community-dwelling seniors who were admitted to Thunder Bay Regional Health Sciences Centre examined the association between family physician status and outcomes among those with a family physician
Among the 12,033 seniors admitted to hospital between 2004, and 2013, 40.7 per cent lacked a family physician.
There was a 60 per cent higher chance of dying while in hospital plus a 21 per cent higher chance of dying in the year afterwards for those with no family doctor.
The health-care system is complicated and a family doctor is vital in navigating the system.
Canada can learn from OECD countries such as the Netherlands, Norway, the UK and Finland, where more than 95 per cent of people have a primary care clinician, near-universal insurance coverage exists, the proportion of health spending that is spent on primary care is larger than Canada's and GPs are organized better in teams and are more accountable for the care they must provide.
Ten per cent of medical doctors in Finland work solely in the private sector. They also have a right to collect user fees set at a maximum of 46 Euros.
The Netherlands offers mandatory insurance that is managed by private insurance companies and you pay a compulsory deductible for the first part of healthcare you receive under the insurance.
In Norway, citizens are responsible for paying a certain amount in user fees until they reach a certain amount of money paid out-of-pocket, when they receive an exemption card for public health services, and no longer have to pay user fees for the remainder of the calendar year.
Allow patients to pay for private care if they so choose. Sweden, Finland, Germany, Australia, Denmark, Japan, Germany - basically every other rich developed country with a universal health-care system allows this option and has better outcomes than Canada.
When access to timely care cannot be provided in the publicly funded system, Canadians should be able to use private health insurance to reimburse the cost of care obtained in the private sector rather than die waiting.
Since 2013, there have been 931 patients who died in Ontario while waiting for heart surgery longer than the maximum recommended wait time.
Then there are those who die while waiting for scans or surgeries that would have improved their quality of life, like a hip operation.
Over 17,000 Canadians died while waiting for surgery or a diagnostic scan in 2022-23.
Universal public health-care is a right but one should have the option of accessing better (often more costly, more advanced medical technology such as laser cataract surgery not available on OHIP), faster, more timely care if so desired, as everyone in every other modern, industrialized wealthy country in the world can.
This approach helps reduce wait times every time a patient decides to pay for private care.
Shaking up the way hospitals are funded could also help. Hospitals severely limit OR time because more surgeries are seen as a cost coming out of a pre-determined global budget.
Fund more OR time, reward hospitals for being more productive, shortening patient wait times.
Instead of giving hospitals a large cheque at the beginning of the year and hoping for the best, governments could pay them based on their performance and output.
Health-care is a right, not a privilege, but like food and shelter, it is also a commodity.
We do not mandate that everyone eat Hamburger Helper or live in row housing.
Political ideology run amok has elevated government single-payer one-tier health care to an impossible pedestal.
Clinging to an ideology of government single-payer one-tier health-care is simply misguided virtue-signalling on a national scale.
Should we be proud of a health-care system where governments abandon patients to die?
And make no mistake, people will die because of not having a family doctor.
Obviously not.
These international examples can inform bold policy reform in Canada to advance a vision of primary care for all.

Subject: Shortages; Health care policy; Surgery; User fees; Primary care; Public health; Health care expenditures; Medical students; Fees & charges; Medicare; Physicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Shortages Medicare Physicians

Location: Netherlands; Canada; Germany; Norway; Finland

People: Wynne, Kathleen; Rae, Bob

Company / organization: Name: University of Toronto; NAICS: 611310; Name: Organization for Economic Cooperation & Development; NAICS: 541720

Identifier / keyword: graduated; medicine; university; toronto; three; years

Document 206 of 219

NDP leadership race needs to find the ring road out of Edmonton

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-leadership-race-needs-find-ring-road-out/docview/2921034852/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+leadership+race+needs+to+find+the+ring+road+out+of+Edmonton&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Alberta NDP won't win government again unless it breaks out of its comfy northern fortress.
But the leadership race to replace Rachel Notley already is looking like loads of Edmonton, not much Calgary or anywhere else.
There could be four Edmonton MLAs in the race: Sarah Hoffman, Rakhi Pancholi, David Shepherd and Jodi Calahoo Stonehouse.
Only one Calgarian, Kathleen Ganley, seems set to run.
Nobody has formally announced yet.
Application packages were sent out Monday. The official campaign starts Feb. 5.
Last May showed the NDP won't win another election without sweeping Calgary, or having a break through in rural Alberta.
During the campaign, the party fell over itself making pitches to the city.
The central campaign office was in Calgary.
Notley made dozens of local appearances.
It almost worked. The NDP captured 14 of 26 seats in the city.
But the fatal mistake - promising to raise the business tax - showed a deep disconnect with a crucial Calgary voting bloc at that pivotal moment.
And Notley carried every single seat in Edmonton.
The question now is whether the Edmonton wing is so dominant, and the local voting members so abundant, that a geographic outsider simply can't win the leadership.
Several Edmonton candidates might split that city's vote, and a sole Calgary candidate - Ganley - could win.
There have been eight Alberta NDP leaders since 1962. The only leader from outside the capital was Rachel Notley's father, Grant, from northern Spirit River-Fairview.
With a leadership record like that, it's hard to convince people you're a real provincial party.
Since the 1960s - and even before - Alberta's conservative governments have been led by an array of pan-Alberta premiers.
Ernest Manning (Edmonton) was followed by Harry Strom (Cypress) as the final Socred leaders. Then came PC premiers Peter Lougheed (Calgary); Don Getty (Edmonton, and then Stettler); Ralph Klein (Calgary); Ed Stelmach (Fort Saskatchewan), Alison Redford (Calgary), Dave Hancock (Edmonton) and Jim Prentice (Calgary).
The UCP premiers have been Jason Kenney (Calgary) and Danielle Smith (Brooks- Medicine Hat).
The NDP needs to develop this kind of reach and variety. Falling back on another comfortable Edmontonian won't get the job done.
In the 2015 election campaign, Prentice offended many Albertans when he said: "Alberta is not an NDP province."
He lost, but he wasn't entirely wrong. Notley won with 40 per cent of the provincial vote because of the conservative split between PC and Wildrose.
Last year's strong results in Calgary have convinced many New Democrats the party is permanently on the edge of victory.
It's not. Without serious signs they're reaching out to the whole province, the NDP is doomed to stagnate.
A couple of Edmonton hopefuls told me Monday they can fully champion Calgary (and by implication, rural Alberta, too).
They try. The NDP launched the new Calgary Cancer Centre that was stalled by PC dithering. But moves like that, while welcome, often seemed like foreign aid.
The party did take two essential steps at its weekend meeting in Red Deer.
First, they abandoned the rule that gave up to 25 per cent of leadership votes to "official organizations," meaning unions.
In this race, the standard will be one member, one vote.
The party also will ignore its longtime rule saying members can't also hold cards in other parties.
The party's chief returning officer, Amanda Freistadt, says they will keep an eye out for hostiles who try to flood the party, but will assume others genuinely support the NDP.
Most of all, though, this party needs a leadership divorce from Edmonton. Don Braid's column appears regularly with Postmedia. X: @DonBraid

Subject: Leadership; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

People: Notley, Rachel

Document 207 of 219

'I am going to focus on actual solutions'; Sault MPP Romano to convene committee to examine physician shortages

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/i-am-going-focus-on-actual-solutions-sault-mpp/docview/2921034798/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I+am+going+to+focus+on+actual+solutions%27%3B+Sault+MPP+Romano+to+convene+committee+to+examine+physician+shortages&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: City council is calling on the physician recruitment and retention committee to expand its mandate to ensure its focus is on attracting primary care providers, including nurse practitioners and physician assistants.
But the cry for more provincial government help and changes to the system was also echoed by city councillors, many who still remain shocked at the news that the Group Health Centre is de-rostering 10,000 patients as a result of a loss of primary care physicians and the inability to attract new ones.
Mayor Matthew Shoemaker was first to address the physician recruitment and retention motion penned by Ward 4 Coun. Stephan Kinach and Ward 1 Coun. Sandra Hollingsworth Monday.
"As we all know, health care is a provincial issue and one we will not be able to single-handedly resolve, however we do play a role in physician recruitment and it's something we have an obligation to provide, not just for physician recruitment, but the best recruitment for primary health care we can provide in our community," Shoemaker said.
He said it is logical to expand the role of the physician recruitment and retention committee to ensure they are also looking at recruiting nurse practitioners and nurse practitioner assistants, at least to help in the short term, given the large number of residents without primary care providers.
Carrie Stewart, manager of the physician recruitment and retention committee, did not return a phone call to The Sault Star.
It's estimated that about 30,000 Sault Ste. Marie residents do not have a primary care provider.
Group Health Centre announced recently it was de-rostering 10,000 patients, with another 6,000 patients at risk to be let go in the near future.
Shoemaker said that a NOSM presence in Sault Ste. Marie would go a long way to ensuring there are additional providers in the community, experience the community and hopefully establish their practices here.
Ward 5 Coun. Corey Gardi called the GHC announcement "awful news," especially in a community with an aging population.
He put the blame squarely on the provincial government.
"Let's not lose sight of the fact... this is entirely the purview of the provincial government. The federal government sources the funds, but this shouldn't come as a shock to many, specifically to Mr. (Doug) Ford or any other member of provincial Parliament who represents this community or any other. It was years in the making and now it's coming to a head all at once," Gardi said, supporting the move to expand the committee's terms of reference.
Sault MPP Ross Romano told The Sault Star that he is surprised the committee's existing mandate doesn't include the recruitment of nurse practitioners and assistants and believes that mandate expansion is an important good start.
"Obviously we're a little behind the eight ball right now and these are some trying times for our community, so I hope they're going to be able to ramp up those recruitment efforts very quickly," he said.
But finger pointing, he said, is not helpful.
"Any member of the municipal government, especially one who has been there for one term already, knows it is the responsibility of the municipal government to address recruitment, and to only now bring a resolution to focus on primary care into the recruitment efforts, is unfortunate," Romano said. "I am not going to play a finger-pointing game. I am going to focus on actual solutions. That's what our community deserves and needs."
Romano has reached out to health-care providers in the community and is working on establishing a committee with the experts to pinpoint the specific issues that have caused the severity of the primary care provider loss in Sault Ste. Marie.
Information he received says two are retiring and three others are moving into another capacity but staying in the community.
He wants the committee to focus on the actual issues that need to be addressed, whether it be recruitment, incentives, the delivery of primary care or the red tape that may discourage primary care providers.
"We need to ask the right questions and know what it is we are really trying to address," he said.
He wants to ensure that Ministry of Health representatives are included in the committee to help achieve the committee's goal - to ensure there are enough primary care providers for those who want one.
His vision also includes physicians and representatives from Sault Area Hospital, GHC, Superior Health Family Team, the Nurse Practitioner Led Clinic and the City of Sault Ste. Marie, among others.
Ward 4 Coun. Stephan Kinach told city council medical schools are not graduating enough students to keep up with Canada's growth and there are residency spots that are going unfilled.
Kinach wants to see a more grassroots approach to lobby the province to do more to ensure Canadians have primary care providers. He noted that the problem isn't confined to Sault Ste. Marie or Northern Ontario. Toronto has 305 unfilled family medicine positions, Ottawa has 171 and Hamilton has 114 positions available.
Kinach noted a primary care physician is a link for entire medical care for an individual, including referrals to specialists or others.
Those referrals are now being sought through emergency departments, driving up waiting times, and clogging the system with people who do not have emergencies.
Ward 1 Coun. Sandra Hollingsworth said other communities are already jumping on board, urging higher levels of government to step in and assist.
As a registered nurse, Hollingsworth says she sees many patients at the walk-in clinics who do not have family doctors and haven't been properly examined for several years, something that is detrimental to their health.
Romano said the Minister of Health recognizes that "this is a significant challenge, a very tough time for our community. I know I have the minister's office attention on this, and I know they want to help."

Document 208 of 219

Student cap will hit schools' finances hard; Colleges, universities rely on international students to balance books

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/student-cap-will-hit-schools-finances-hard/docview/2918434571/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Student+cap+will+hit+schools%27+finances+hard%3B+Colleges%2C+universities+rely+on+international+students+to+balance+books&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberals'cap on international students could hit some universities and colleges in Ontario particularly hard, as schools there have become reliant on the higher tuition fees those students pay to keep their books balanced.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller announced the cap on Monday, saying his department will issue no more than 360,000 new visas this year, 35 per cent lower than last year, and keep them at that number for two years.
The visas will be handed out proportionally to provinces based on their population, which will force even larger cuts at some schools in Ontario. Schools in the province have been aggressive about bringing in international students and the new cap could mean a 50 per cent reduction in the number of new students schools can admit. Miller was still working with provinces on specific allocations, but Ontario, British Columbia and Nova Scotia were expected to have to make the biggest reductions. Other provinces that were not as aggressive about recruiting could actually be able to increase the number of international students they bring in.
The federal government specifically exempted Masters and PhD students from the change, but otherwise will leave the decision on how to use allocations to provincial governments, forcing premiers to pick whether to send more international students or dollars to schools.
Mikal Skuterud, an economics professor at the University of Waterloo, said provinces that have to reduce their international student numbers should see which schools produce the best long-term financial outcomes for graduates, which is data the government already has.
"Avoid the politics, look at the data, let the data do the talking. I really would encourage them to do that."
Skuterud said the international student program became a way to bring in low-wage labour in many cases, which it never should have been. "The student program is not about plugging holes in low skilled labour markets, but that's kind of what it's become in a lot of ways."
He said the federal decision to lower the number of hours an international student can work, brought in last fall, should also help.
Alongside the labour market implications, provincial governments will have to decide how to handle the impact on universities and colleges, which, especially in Ontario, have become reliant on international student money.
Ontario's auditor general Bonnie Lysyk warned in 2022 that the provinces'institutions were becoming too reliant on international students to keep their finances in order.
"A high reliance on international student enrolment by universities poses risks outside of the Ministry's and the universities'control," she said Lysyk examined the system as a whole with detailed audits of some schools.
She found many Ontario schools relied heavily on international students to make their finances work. Algoma University in Sault Ste. Marie, Ont., for example has a satellite campus in Brampton where 90 per cent of students were international students. She found that the Brampton campus was responsible for half the school's enrolment and 65 per cent of its revenue.
After Laurentian University sought creditor protection, Lysyk examined that school's books and found they took a significant hit when Saudi Arabia pulled students from the school during a diplomatic dispute with Ottawa.
"Laurentian lost 130 students from Saudi Arabia. These students would have paid an estimated $3 million in tuition revenues and ancillary fees over the full length of their degrees," she said.
Steve Orsini, President and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities said the cut to international students is coming at a time when universities are struggling with a tuition freeze and no new money coming from the provincial government.
"Before (Monday's) announcement, there were at least 10 universities facing sizable operating deficits, totalling $175 million this year and growing to $273 million next year," he said.
Orsini said universities have been more responsible about bringing in international students and called on the government to give them more time to adapt.

Subject: Provinces; Tuition; Foreign students; Schools; Colleges & universities; College campuses; Labor market

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities Labor market

Potential Conservative candidates gear up for 2025 federal election

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/potential-conservative-candidates-gear-up-2025/docview/2916290889/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Potential+Conservative+candidates+gear+up+for+2025+federal+election&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At least two area residents have indicated their interest to carry the Conservative Party of Canada banner into the next federal election for the Sault Ste. Marie riding.
Michael Gassi, of Sault Ste.
Marie, and Cameron Ross, of Richard's Landing, have both filed their applications to be contestants in the 2025 federal election.
A third person, Sonny Spina, has said he would be open to seeking the nomination.
New to politics, Gassi said it was during the COVID-19 pandemic that local and national issues seriously caught his attention and he has become concerned with what is occurring around the world.
"I advocate for the values of liberty, faith, family, morals freedom and the promise of a healthy future for our children and grandchildren," he told The Sault Star.
Crime, homelessness and poverty, increased during the pandemic resulting in the dire situation Sault Ste. Marie, Canada and other G7 countries are in now, he said.
"I've become very disenchanted with where our country is moving, and when I was approached about seeking the nomination, it didn't take too long for me to decide," he said.
Ross, who ran for office in both 2004 and 2008, said, "there is a need for adult leadership at the federal level of Canadian politics."
He said he believes that there is optimism in Conservative circles and it's time for a change of government that will offer a fresh approach that focuses on issues such as housing, food affordability, resource development and national security, among other things.
Ross said he believes he has the experience in retail politics, a background in business and a knowledge of Northern Ontario.
His military background, rising to the rank of colonel, also demonstrates his strong leadership qualities needed to win the Sault Ste. Marie and Algoma riding, he said.
Even more importantly, Ross said he lives in Central Algoma - in Richards Landing --and knows the region well.
He said he believes that will be a bonus to him as the riding's geographical makeup grows for the next federal election, to stretch from Hearst and White River to Elliot Lake.
The 64-year-old has been twice elected to municipal government and is serving as a councillor in St. Joseph Township.
Spina, a Ward 1 city councillor, was the Conservative candidate for Sault Ste. Marie in the 2019 and 2021 federal elections. He placed second to Liberal incumbent MP Terry Sheehan both times. In the 2021 election he lost by just 247 votes.
He's not ruling out a third try.
"I'm always an advocate for people of Sault Ste. Marie," he said. "I always look at where I can have the greatest impact for people in the Sault."

Document 210 of 219

Cold shatters opposition to nuclear power

Author: Staples, David

Publication info: Staples, David. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cold-shatters-opposition-nuclear-power/docview/2916290287/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cold+shatters+opposition+to+nuclear+power&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Staples%2C+David&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Albertans were reminded yet again when temperatures crashed below -35 C this weekend which energy sources we can count on to save us from freezing.
One of the great public services provided by a private citizen comes from Ian Mackay, an oilfield information technology specialist in Lacombe, who scrapes data from the website of the Alberta Electric System Operator (AESO), the provincial power grid operator.
Due to Mackay's real-time reports on social media, we know that on Jan. 13 - when the average temperature was -33 C and the provincial government sent out a plea to reduce consumption to avoid rotating blackouts - Mackay reported that gas was supplying 80.5 per cent of our electricity, coal 7.9 per cent, biomass, 2.9 per cent, hydro, 2.5 per cent, solar, 1.3 per cent, and wind, 0.99 per cent.
Such was Mackay's distress about this close call that he vented on social media: "Being asked to save electricity in one of the coldest places on the planet that also happens to sit on one of the largest energy supplies in the world is insane. We should be one of the wealthiest, most advanced societies in the world. Instead, we are all sitting in the dark with our fingers crossed that heat stays on."
But just as we're in crisis, a sensible plan is emerging, with a huge step coming Monday with Edmonton's Capital Power and the Ontario Power Generation (OPG) announcing a partnership to do a deep dive into bringing cutting-edge nuclear technology to bolster power Alberta's grid.
Nuclear has reliably supplied about 60 per cent of Ontario's electricity for decades. OPG is working with four private companies on a new, small modular reactor (SMR), similar to ones employed for decades on American warships and subs, but now envisioned for commercial use. They are to be built to the same specifications, completed in a factory, ready to be shipped, plugged into the grid and put to use, thus cutting down on cost, manufacturing delay, and regulatory issues.
OPG has four SMRs to be completed in 2028, each putting out 315 megawatts. By comparison, the new Cascade gas plant near Edson, Alta., will produce 900 MW.
At the media conference to announce Capital Power's nuclear ambitions, company president and chief executive Avik Dey spoke of the need for reliable energy that can be dispatched whenever it's needed, noting not all forms of energy will be able to provide this necessary benefit in a net-zero environment, just hydro, geothermal, nuclear and natural gas abated with carbon sequestration.
Dey talked about having an operating SMR in Alberta between 2030 and 2035.
The first SMR is expected to be costed out by January 2025, said Ken Hartwick, OPG's president and chief executive. "We are very convinced that this is very competitive with wind, solar and batteries, a combination of those, and on days like this past weekend, will actually work."
Ontario Energy Minister Todd Smith noted while other mega-projects have struggled with cost, the nuclear industry is proving itself with multibillion-dollar refurbishments of existing CANDU reactors in Ontario coming in on budget and ahead of time.
Asked if anti-nuclear sentiment might stop this progress, Nathan Neudorf, Alberta's minister of affordability and utilities, said: "We have, especially of late, seen a dramatic shift in the general public's acceptance."
Neudorf pointed out that nuclear presents a unique circumstance, with the government of Alberta and the Trudeau Liberals accepting it is a solid option.
The NDP in Alberta is on the fence with nuclear, similar to the Ontario NDP. "I think partly because there's the realization that we absolutely need nuclear if we intend to hit the climate change targets that have been set."
In 21/2 years in his post, Smith said he hasn't had one critical opposition question on nuclear in question period.
The case for nuclear is so strong in Canada, its critics have gone as inactive as solar and wind on a frozen Alberta day. dstaples@postmedia.com

Subject: Electricity; Electricity distribution; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Document 211 of 219

Falling into the 'population trap'

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/falling-into-population-trap/docview/2916290043/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Falling+into+the+%27population+trap%27&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's immigration policies are damaging the economy. That's the finding of Canada's top bank economists, who issued a dire warning to the federal government. By admitting 455,000 new permanent residents and more than 800,000 non-permanent residents last year, Canada is on the brink of recession, or worse.
"I'll put it bluntly: We've fallen into the population trap," said Stéfane Marion, chief economist at National Bank of Canada. An increase in the standard of living is no longer possible because "you don't have enough savings to stabilize your capital-to-labour ratio."
We also learned the government was warned two years ago by its own officials about high immigration causing a housing shortage - and ignored those warnings.
Asked about this, Housing Minister Sean Fraser replied, "I don't think anybody needs a briefing note to understand that having more people in the housing market impacts the housing market, but having gone through in consecutive years the process of building Canada's immigration levels plan, I can tell you that there are an enormous number of factors that go into them."
OK then, so what are those factors? Why did the government decide immigration levels should increase at this pace? Why does it continue to allow hundreds of thousands of foreign students to work unlimited hours? Why did it admit so many people into the country in such a short time? The classic answer is we need to increase the labour supply. Businesses are short of workers. Canadians are not having enough children to replace them. That's the argument of the Century Initiative, the business community and immigration advocates.
But simply pulling in adult immigrants and temporary workers is not the same as boosting fertility in the existing population. Those workers have needs that are very different from those of children.
They won't live under their parents'roof; they need their own housing. They won't be ferried about by their parents; they need independent transportation. They will be buying adult levels of food, clothing and other necessities. In short, they will be competing with other adults for basic needs, and that increased demand means two things: price inflation and resource scarcity. Which is exactly what Canada is now experiencing.
Fraser is right. You don't need a study to figure this out. It's simple common sense. So again, why did the government do this - especially after it was warned not to by its own bureaucrats? The cynic in me sees one reason: votes.
Immigrants tend to settle in cities. City dwellers tend to vote for "progressive" parties. In Canada, since the 1960s, that has translated to increased Liberal support. According to research done at Western University, a oneunit shift in the "urbanity score" for a riding produces an increase of eight percentage points in the Liberal party's vote share in that riding. No surprise then, that in the 2021 election, the Liberals won 109 of the 150 most urban ridings, while the Conservatives took only 23.
Immigrants also are grateful to the party that brings them in. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau learned this from his father. Pierre Elliott Trudeau took credit for the citizenship of millions of Canadians, even though many had come to Canada under previous governments. Why? Because they took their oath under his watch. And when it came to elections, the immigrant communities of the day - Italians, Greeks, Haitians, Vietnamese - tended to vote Liberal.
The implications of Justin Trudeau's replication of this policy are dire. Studies show they are turning Canadians against immigration: even 62 per cent of current immigrants think we're letting in too many people. They are impoverishing Canadians, both current and newcomer, according to the bank study. And they are also helping fuel Quebec separatism, as francophones look with alarm at rising immigrant populations in the rest of Canada. By the end of the century, Quebec risks becoming a bit player in Confederation, with only 15 per cent of its population.
This cannot continue. Instead of flooding the country with newcomers, the government needs to boost domestic productivity. That's a harder fix - and one that won't give them more votes. But then again, if Canadians can't afford a decent life, the Liberals may not get their votes, either. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Canadians; Immigrants; Immigration

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: immigration; policies; damaging; economy; finding; economists

Document 212 of 219

'Petition' of support circulates at Poilievre event in Sault Ste. Marie

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/petition-support-circulates-at-poilievre-event/docview/2915466701/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Petition%27+of+support+circulates+at+Poilievre+event+in+Sault+Ste.+Marie&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hugh Dekever makes no bones about it.
He is a Conservative Party of Canada supporter, and he applauds Pierre Poilievre and his efforts to make life more affordable for Canadians.
The Sault Ste. Marie resident turned to other like-minded Saultites Sunday during Poilievre's visit to the community as part of his Axe the Tax tour of Northern Ontario.
Dekever created his own 'axe'and worked the crowds Sunday, asking area residents to sign it.
He wanted to present the axe to Poilievre to show support for him, the party, and his plan to rid of the carbon tax if elected.
"The carbon tax affects everything we do," Dekever told The Sault Star. "It's making it more expensive for us with everything, our transportation, our heating and our home budgets," he said.
The retired Dekever said he's on a fixed budget and every dollar counts, but noted that it is not only retired people who are filling the pinch, but young families and everyone who drives a vehicle, heats their home or buys groceries.
Dekever, a member of the local ratepayer's association, said he supports green technology and believes government needs to focus on, and invest in, companies and industries that practise green technology.
"They're the type of jobs we need here. They're better paying jobs, much better than tourism jobs," he said. "That's where government investment needs to be."
Poilievre's Sault Ste. Marie stop was the last of his Northern Ontario appearances, which included Thunder Bay, Timmins, Sudbury and North Bay before moving on to Quebec.
The tour, a pre-election form rally, promises to scrap the carbon tax if elected during the next federal election, set for 2025.
The Conservative Axe the Tax rallies have been held across Canada as a drive against the Liberal government's carbon tax. They argue the Liberals'inflationary carbon tax is nothing but a tax plan driving up the price of gas, groceries and home heating.
The governing Liberals have defended the carbon tax as a price on pollution that helps address the worsening climate crisis.
Canada's next federal election is expected in October 2025.

Document 213 of 219

Poilievre promises to axe carbon tax if elected; Pitches 'green' projects such as small modular nuclear reactors, 'incentivizing' carbon capturing storage

Author: Della-Mattia, Elaine

Publication info: Della-Mattia, Elaine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-promises-axe-carbon-tax-if-elected/docview/2915466650/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+promises+to+axe+carbon+tax+if+elected%3B+Pitches+%27green%27+projects+such+as+small+modular+nuclear+reactors%2C+%27incentivizing%27+carbon+capturing+storage&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Della-Mattia%2C+Elaine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre stomped into Sault Ste. Marie Sunday as part of his Northern Axe the Tax tour, warning voters on the potential of what may come if the Liberals are re-elected.
Most importantly, he said, is that voters need to be aware that the Liberal government will quadruple the carbon tax, making it even more expensive for Canadians to heat their homes, drive their cars and purchase groceries.
All this, he said, while Prime Minister Justin Trudeau spent his holiday season jetting off to a luxury resort for "a free vacation at a $9,000 per night resort, gifted to him by his billionaire friends, burning jet fuel that we pay for. He's a high-carbon, high-tax hypocrite."
In contrast, Poilievre said seniors are having difficulties heating their homes and paying for groceries.
Poilievre says Trudeau's plan, passed by the Liberal government including Sault MPP Terry Sheehan, will quadruple that carbon tax.
Instead, Poilievre offers a solution that will lower the cost of low-carbon alternatives rather than raising the costs of fossil fuels.
"That means greenlighting green projects like small modular nuclear reactors, which can supply base load, conditions free, clean green electricity onto our grid," he said. "Quick approvals of hydro electric dams, incentivizing carbon capturing storage so companies can return oil and gas to the ground and approve tidal energy off the east coast Atlantic Canada," is the right solution.
"These projects can supply us with green carbon alternatives and are low priced," he said.
Poilievre said quicker mining development is needed so that electric vehicles, currently made in China, and are burning coal to make them, can be created here to allow Canada to supply the technology and the energy for a clean green environment.
The existing federal government plan is nowhere near reaching its 2030 emission reduction target, he said.
Poilievre said the federal government's 'pause'on the carbon tax was only imposed after his own caucus was protesting, along with some areas of the country.
Poilievre said his solution was to treat all Canadians fairly to pause the tax for everyone.
"But no, Terry Sheehan turned his back on the people of Sault Ste. Marie and voted with Trudeau to quadruple the carbon tax on home heating," he said.
Ward 1 Coun. Sonny Spina, who attended the media event, said he believes the carbon tax should be erased in order to help families live day to day.
"Taxes like this are not helping Canadians and they certainly are not helping Northern Ontario. We need to do everything we can to make life more affordable for people. We, in Northern Ontario, have no choice by to heat our homes in the winter time and its costing us more to do that."
Poilievre's message has been the same at other stops across the North, including Thunder Bay, Timmins, North Bay and Sudbury.
Poilievre's solution and a "common sense" government will axe the carbon tax on heat, food, gas and farmers "forever," he said.
He said his plan is to also cut spending, balance the budget and bring down inflation and interest rates.
He also promised to reduce income tax and develop a plan that would see cities urged to approve residential developments quicker by offering incentives to those who meet or exceed targets and penalties to those who fail.
Poilievre said a Conservative government will sell 6,000 federal buildings, thousands of acres of federal land and Toronto's CBC building in order to build more affordable homes.
"We're going to get the government out of the way to build the homes that our youth will be able to afford, and they will be in safe neighbourhoods," he said.
The plan to make streets safe, including going after criminals and leaving lawful hunters and sports shooters alone.
"Make no mistake, if Trudeau is reelected he will ban your hunting rifle. He tried to do it and the only reason he backed off is because of the pressure we put on," Poilievre said. "If he gets a chance, you'll use your hunting rifle and that will not do anything to fight crime."
Poilievre also said he'll turn the table on crime by changing laws that will mean repeat offenders will stay in jail as opposed to getting out on bail and reoffending, especially those who are violent offenders, in order to keep communities safe.
Poilievre's visit attracted about 150 party faithful's at a meet-andgreet event Sunday morning at Algoma's Water Tower Inn.

Calling Code Blue in Ontario emergency departments; Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/calling-code-blue-ontario-emergency-departments/docview/2913343389/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Calling+Code+Blue+in+Ontario+emergency+departments%3B+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The average wait time in the emergency department at Montford Hospital, Ottawa was 19 hours in the week before Christmas.
While most emergency departments may not be experiencing such extensive wait times, unparalleled ER wait times have been in the news. At the end of November Sault Area Hospital reported that its ED was "experiencing a high volume of patients, sicker patients, staffing shortages and limited inpatient beds." While hospitals prioritize the most urgent cases, other patients have long waits -in hallways, waiting rooms, empty corners ... wherever space can be found.
Medical examinations are often done in plain view and hearing range of people nearby. Patients, already suffering, experience loss of privacy and dignity in such situations.
Along with overcrowding and long wait times in emergency departments, there is the crisis in care caused by the closures of emergency departments altogether.
Some have closed for days at a time. In Algoma, the Thessalon public hospital temporarily closed its emergency department several times last summer. Some ERs in rural areas, such as in Minden, have closed permanently.
It is not just an inconvenience to have to drive further to the next emergency department. When prompt treatment matters, lives may be in jeopardy. As well, numerous people without family doctors are forced to seek primary medical care, including prescription renewals, at an emergency department. ER closures and lengthy wait times affect access to care and health outcomes. This particularly affects seniors who often deal with issues related to health and transportation. In northern Ontario, where distances between emergency departments can be hundreds of kilometres and weather conditions frequently hamper travel, ER closures disproportionately put Northern lives at risk.
Staffing shortages and hospital funding cuts are largely responsible for ER closures and long wait times. Recent Ontario legislation to publicly fund the expansion of private hospitals has resulted in less funding for public hospitals. Tax payer funded private clinics draw staff from underfunded public hospitals and further deplete hospital staff, thereby contributing to the hospital crisis.
In addition, for-profit staffing agencies, promising nurses better working conditions and better pay, entice them from the strained public system. Hospitals, desperate for more nurses, are hiring agency nurses and paying them often twice as much or more as they pay their staff nurses. Many agency nurses are former staff nurses who have left untenable work environments and poorer paying positions in public hospitals. But they are not part of a hospital "team" of regular workers who can provide continuity of care to their patients. Last year the number and cost of agency hours increased dramatically. "Costs to the public purse more than tripled to $174 million from $38 million." (Toronto Star, Oct. 19, 2023) At the same time as the government flows millions of tax payer dollars to independent health facilities (private clinics) and private agencies, it is reducing its funding to public health care. Ontario funds its hospitals at the lowest rate in Canada.
Without sufficient funding and lacking staff, wait times will continue to increase, hospital emergency rooms will continue to close and operating rooms, that we have paid for, will continue to sit empty on weekends and evenings.
What is the government's long range, comprehensive plan to keep our public hospitals functioning? Without a working plan, Doug Ford's 2018 promise to "end hallway health care" continues to ring hollow. Marie DellaVedova, Political advocacy representative, RTOERO District 3 Algoma

Subject: Health care access; Workforce planning; Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Departments; Funding; Nurses; Labor shortages

Business indexing term: Subject: Workforce planning Hospitals Labor shortages

Document 215 of 219

Amid declining rights, youth provide hope

Author: Mulcair, Tom

Publication info: Mulcair, Tom. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/amid-declining-rights-youth-provide-hope/docview/2913343138/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Amid+declining+rights%2C+youth+provide+hope&amp;title=Sault+Star&amp;issn=0839203X&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Mulcair%2C+Tom&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sault+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The decline of rights in Quebec is alarmingandconcernsusall.
With Bill 21, the Coalition Avenir Québec government brazenly attacked religious minorities.
The justification for this overt discrimination is swaddled in high-sounding claptrap about "laïcité," the separation of church and state.
It's hard to see it as anything other than the crass politics of exclusion, playing to the intolerance of the lowest common denominator. Laïcité has been spray-painted on to give this discrimination a superficial sheen.
Elsewhere, Premier François Legault has attacked the constitutionally guaranteed rights of the English-speaking minority to control and manage its school boards.
Legault also unilaterally changed the founding 1867 Constitution, the BNA Act, and removed the equality of English and French before the courts. Shamefully, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau did nothing to challenge him.
Bill 96 contains outrageous provisions allowing for warrant less search and seizure of computers and cellphones by the vocabulary constabulary.
Like Bill 21, it has an ostensibly virtuous purpose: it's about protecting and preserving the French language in Quebec. Except the numbers show the French language is doing just fine, thank you. But it's become an undebatable truth that French is in such desperate need of discriminatory legislation that we'll become another Louisiana without it.
Like Bill 21 before it, Bill 96 has, for now, been peremptorily shielded by using the notwithstanding clause to remove charter rights.
We can presume it's precisely because those limits on rights are not demonstrably justifiable in a free and democratic society that Legault uses the notwithstanding clause.
When Ontario Premier Doug Ford considered using the notwithstanding clause, Trudeau flew into a state of high dudgeon and Ford backed down. Removing rights in Quebec is apparently in a different category. Trudeau actually made things worse with Bill C-13 that reduced the rights of the English linguistic minority, leading to Marc Garneau's resignation as a Liberal MP.
Back in Quebec, during the study of his mammoth reform, Health Minister Christian Dubé brought in a clause that could eviscerate language guarantees for bilingual health and social service institutions. Thanks in part to the great work of the Opposition Liberals, Dubé beat a hasty retreat, but it did nothing to assuage fears.
In Bill 96, the ability to hire bilingual staff to ensure services in English in those institutions has been curtailed. Bureaucratic interpretations only have made things worse and the McGill University Health Centre has caved and made board presentations in French only.
Legault also has betrayed a 47-year-old deal with anglophone parents who choose to send their kids to French school. The law says their kids are deemed to have studied in English for purposes of admission in the future, but Legault has broken that long-standing rule by diktat and refused to issue eligibility certificates to many.
The recent attacks on McGill and Concordia universities supposedly were necessary because too much English was being heard on the streets in Montreal. Think about that for a second. Can you imagine how that will be perceived by tourists? Bienvenue au Grand Prix de Montréal, please don't speak English in the streets. We can't stand it.
Since leaving politics, I've had the good fortune to teach at the Université de Montréal. The next generation of Quebec leaders I've met there gives me the greatest hope. While the likes of Legault, French Language Minister Jean-François Roberge and Higher Education Minister Pascale Déry appear to be in a race to the bottom against the Parti Québécois, young Quebecers are overwhelmingly open, tolerant and respectful of differences. They are the greatest hope for our future together. Tom Mulcair, a former leader of the federal NDP, served as minister of the environment in the Quebec Liberal government of Jean Charest.

Subject: French language; Bilingualism

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada
